**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain how **zero drift** and **span drift** affect the calibration curve of an instrument. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermistor-based temperature indicator has a full-scale range of **200 °C** with output voltage range of **0 to 5 V**. Initial zero reading (at 0 °C) is **0.02 V**. After 12 hours, the zero reading is **0.08 V**. The full-scale reading at 200 °C initially was **5.00 V**, but after 12 hours it became **5.15 V**. Calculate:
   (i) Zero drift and percentage zero drift
   (ii) Span drift and percentage span drift
   (iii) Net error at 100 °C after 12 hours **(7 marks)**

---

**MODEL SOLUTION — Question B**

**(a) Sketch Explanation:**

A calibration curve plots output $y$ vs input $x$.

- **Ideal line:** Passes through origin with slope $K$.
- **With Zero Drift:** Entire line shifts **vertically** (parallel shift). New equation: $y = Kx + \Delta z$.
- **With Span Drift:** Slope changes from $K$ to $K + \Delta K$. Line pivots about origin.
- **Combined Drift:** Both intercept and slope change.

*(Sketch to be drawn by student — marks: 2 for ideal line, 2 for zero drift, 2 for span drift, 1 for combined)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Range: 0 to 200 °C; Output: 0 to 5 V
- Initial zero: $z_0 = 0.02$ V at $t = 0$
- After 12 hr: $z(12) = 0.08$ V
- Initial FS: $y_{FS}(0) = 5.00$ V
- After 12 hr FS: $y_{FS}(12) = 5.15$ V

**(i) Zero Drift:**

$$\Delta z = 0.08 - 0.02 = 0.06 \text{ V}$$

$$\%\text{Zero Drift} = \dfrac{0.06}{5} \times 100 = 1.2\,\%$$

**(ii) Span Drift:**

The span (full-scale deflection above zero) initially was:
$$S_0 = y_{FS}(0) - z_0 = 5.00 - 0.02 = 4.98 \text{ V}$$

After 12 hours:
$$S_{12} = 5.15 - 0.08 = 5.07 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta S = 5.07 - 4.98 = 0.09 \text{ V}$$

$$\%\text{Span Drift} = \dfrac{0.09}{4.98} \times 100 \approx 1.807\,\%$$

**(iii) Net Error at 100 °C after 12 hours:**

Theoretical output at 100 °C (assuming linear): midway → 2.50 V (ideal from zero).
Initial sensitivity: $K_0 = \dfrac{4.98}{200} = 0.0249 \text{ V/°C}$
After 12 hr sensitivity: $K_{12} = \dfrac{5.07}{200} = 0.02535 \text{ V/°C}$

Ideal output at 100 °C: $2.50$ V
Actual output at 100 °C (12 hr later):

$$y_{actual} = K_{12} \cdot 100 + z(12) = 0.02535 \times 100 + 0.08 = 2.535 + 0.08 = 2.615 \text{ V}$$

Net error at 100 °C:

$$e = 2.615 - 2.50 = 0.115 \text{ V}$$

$$\% \text{ Error at 100°C} = \dfrac{0.115}{5} \times 100 = 2.3\,\%$$

*(7 marks — distribution: 2 marks for i, 2 marks for ii, 3 marks for iii)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing full-scale output with full-scale input** — students often use $x_{FS}$ (input) instead of $y_{FS}$ (output) in percentage drift calculation. Always normalize by **output full scale**.
> 2. **Mixing zero drift with zero error** — zero error is a *constant offset*; zero drift is a *time-varying* offset.
> 3. **Forgetting to subtract the initial zero offset** when calculating true span in span-drift problems.
> 4. **Units inconsistency** — time may be in seconds, minutes, hours, or days. Always state the unit.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — examiners award marks for explicitly stating that ambient temperature is assumed constant during the test interval.

---

### 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Zero Stability** = constancy of zero output over time when input is zero.
- ✅ **Zero Drift** = any deviation from original zero reading → loss of zero stability.
- ✅ Zero drift causes a **parallel shift** of the calibration curve; span drift changes the **slope**.
- ✅ **Formula to remember:** $\%\text{Zero Drift} = \dfrac{\Delta z}{y_{FS}} \times 100$
- ✅ **Zero Stability Index** = % Zero Drift per unit time.
- ✅ Common causes: temperature, aging, supply variation, humidity, vibration.
- ✅ Remedies: thermal compensation, auto-zero circuits, regulated supply, periodic calibration.
- ✅ Distinguish **zero error** (constant) from **zero drift** (time-varying).
- ✅ Numerical problems require identifying $y_{FS}$, $z(0)$, $z(T)$, and time interval clearly.
- ✅ Always state **units** in the final answer — % FS/hr or mV/hr.
- ✅ **Auto-zero** and **REL/Relative** modes in digital instruments are practical implementations of zero-drift compensation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Loading Effect and System Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Loading Effect and System Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Loading Effect and System Response

## 1.1 Loading Effect — Core Definition

The **Loading Effect** in any measurement system refers to the alteration of the original physical quantity (the measurand) caused by the act of connecting the measuring instrument to the system under test. Because no instrument is ideal, it invariably draws some form of energy from the process being measured, thereby disturbing the very quantity it is meant to measure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Loading effect is the disturbance created in the system being measured due to the insertion of the measuring device. The deviation between the true value of the measurand and the value indicated by the instrument is termed the **Loading Error (e_L)**.*

Mathematically, the loading error is expressed as:

$$e_L = \frac{X_{measured} - X_{true}}{X_{true}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $X_{true}$ — actual value of the quantity in the undisturbed system
- $X_{measured}$ — value indicated by the instrument after connection

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket-and-Scoop" Analogy

Imagine a tank of water (the **system**). The actual water level is your "true measurement." Now you dip a large measuring cup (the **instrument**) into the tank. The cup is so big that the water level *drops* significantly the moment you insert it. What you read in the cup is **not** the original level — it is a disturbed, smaller value. The bigger the cup relative to the tank, the larger the **loading error**.

- A *voltmeter* with low internal resistance is like a large cup.
- A *voltmeter* with very high internal resistance is like a tiny spoon — minimal disturbance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:**
> An ideal instrument should have **infinite input impedance** (for voltage/force-like measurements) and **zero output impedance** (for current-like measurements), making its loading effect approach zero.

---

## 1.2 System Response — Core Definition

**System Response** is the behaviour of the output of a measurement system as a function of time when the input (or the measurand) is varied in a known manner. A measurement system rarely reproduces its input instantly; instead, it takes time to settle, may overshoot, oscillate, or lag behind.

The classification of measurement systems by their dynamic order is foundational in KTU 2024 syllabus:

| Order | General Differential Equation | Mechanical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Zero-Order** | $y = Kx$ | Ideal lever, ideal spring scale |
| **First-Order** | $\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = Kx$ | Liquid-in-glass thermometer, RC circuit |
| **Second-Order** | $\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\dfrac{dy}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2}y = K\omega_{n}^{2}x$ | Mass-spring-damper, LCR circuit, accelerometer |

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath Tub" Analogy

Think of the measurement system as a bathtub:
- **Zero-order**: The moment you turn the tap (input), the water (output) reaches the new level *instantly* — no delay, no overshoot.
- **First-order**: The water rises gradually, approaching the new level smoothly without ever exceeding it (like a slowly filling tub).
- **Second-order (underdamped)**: The water sloshes back and forth, overshoots the target level, oscillates a few times, and then settles — like stepping into a tub and watching the water wobble.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** In KTU 2024 questions, the *standard test inputs* used are **Step**, **Ramp**, **Impulse**, and **Sinusoidal (frequency response)**. Always identify the input shape *first*, then pick the correct response equation.

---

## 1.3 Why Loading Effect and System Response Matter

- They determine the **static accuracy** (via loading error) and the **dynamic fidelity** (via system response) of any measurement.
- They define the **bandwidth**, **time constant**, and **damping characteristics** of an instrument.
- They are essential for **transducer selection**, **calibration**, and **signal conditioning** in real industrial plants.

> [!WARNING]
> A common KTU confusion: **Loading Effect is a static phenomenon**, while **System Response is a dynamic phenomenon**. Do not mix their equations or interpretations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Loading Effect — Theoretical Analysis

When an instrument of impedance $Z_m$ is connected across a source of impedance $Z_s$, the measured value differs from the true open-circuit value.

### Case 1 — Voltmeter (Parallel / Shunt Loading)

A voltmeter is connected in **parallel** with the source. By the voltage divider rule:

$$V_{measured} = V_{true} \times \frac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m}$$

Hence the **loading error in percentage**:

$$e_L^{\text{(parallel)}} = \left( \frac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m} - 1 \right) \times 100\% = -\frac{Z_s}{Z_s + Z_m} \times 100\%$$

- The **negative sign** indicates the measured value is always **less than** the true value.
- To minimise this error, $Z_m \gg Z_s$ (instrument impedance must be very large compared to source impedance).

### Case 2 — Ammeter (Series Loading)

An ammeter is connected in **series**. By current divider:

$$I_{measured} = I_{true} \times \frac{Z_s}{Z_s + Z_m}$$

$$e_L^{\text{(series)}} = \left( \frac{Z_s}{Z_s + Z_m} - 1 \right) \times 100\% = -\frac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m} \times 100\%$$

- To minimise this error, $Z_m \ll Z_s$ (instrument impedance must be very small).

### Case 3 — Temperature Measurement (Thermal Loading)

A thermometer of heat capacity $C_t$ is placed in a fluid of heat capacity $C_s$. If the system holds $C_s \gg C_t$, the disturbance is small. Otherwise:

$$e_L^{\text{(thermal)}} = \frac{T_{measured} - T_{true}}{T_{true}}$$

This depends on **specific heat**, **mass**, and **time of contact**.

---

## 2.2 System Response — Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Zero-Order System

$$y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

- Pure proportionality — **no dynamics, no time lag, no loading distortion**.
- Frequency response is **flat (constant) for all frequencies** — ideal.
- Static sensitivity = $K$.

### 2.2.2 First-Order System

Governing equation:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)$$

Where:
- $\tau$ — **Time constant** (seconds)
- $K$ — Static sensitivity (gain)

**Step Input Response** ($x(t) = X$ for $t \geq 0$):

$$y(t) = KX\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Key checkpoints (frequently asked in KTU):

| Time | Response (% of final value) |
|---|---|
| $t = \tau$ | 63.2% |
| $t = 2\tau$ | 86.5% |
| $t = 3\tau$ | 95.0% |
| $t = 4\tau$ | 98.2% |
| $t = 5\tau$ | 99.3% |

**Ramp Input Response** ($x(t) = at$):

$$y(t) = K\left(at - \tau a + \tau a \cdot e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

The **dynamic error** (lag) for large $t$ is constant: $e = K\tau a$.

**Sinusoidal (Frequency) Response**:
$$y(t) = \frac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega\tau)^{2}}} \cdot X_{0} \sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$ and the **amplitude ratio** is $\dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^{2}}}$.

**Bandwidth**: Defined as the frequency at which amplitude ratio drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (-3 dB point). For a first-order system: $\omega_{BW} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$.

### 2.2.3 Second-Order System

Standard form:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\frac{dy}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2}y(t) = K\omega_{n}^{2}x(t)$$

Where:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{M}}$ — natural (undamped) frequency
- $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KM}}$ — damping ratio
- $M$ — mass / inertia, $B$ — damping, $K$ — stiffness

**Step Input Response** (under-damped, $0 < \zeta < 1$):

$$y(t) = KX\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \sin\!\left(\omega_{d} t + \phi\right)\right]$$

Where:
$$\omega_{d} = \omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}} \quad\text{and}\quad \phi = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}{\zeta}\right)$$

**Damping Cases:**

| Case | Condition | Step Response Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Over-damped | $\zeta > 1$ | Slow rise, no overshoot, two real roots |
| Critically damped | $\zeta = 1$ | Fastest rise without overshoot |
| Under-damped | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Oscillates, overshoots, then settles |
| Undamped | $\zeta = 0$ | Sustained oscillation at $\omega_n$ |

**Time Domain Specifications (Under-damped Step Response):**

| Specification | Formula |
|---|---|
| **Rise Time** $t_r$ | $\dfrac{\pi - \phi}{\omega_{d}}$ |
| **Peak Time** $t_p$ | $\dfrac{\pi}{\omega_{d}}$ |
| **Maximum Overshoot** $M_p$ | $e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ |
| **Settling Time** $t_s$ (2%) | $\dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ |
| **Settling Time** $t_s$ (5%) | $\dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_{n}}$ |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| \# | Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Loading error (parallel / voltmeter) | $e_L = -\dfrac{Z_s}{Z_s + Z_m}$ | $\vert e_L \vert \to 0$ when $Z_m \gg Z_s$ |
| 2 | Loading error (series / ammeter) | $e_L = -\dfrac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m}$ | $\vert e_L \vert \to 0$ when $Z_m \ll Z_s$ |
| 3 | First-order step response | $y(t) = KX(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Asymptote at $KX$ |
| 4 | First-order time constant | $\tau = RC$ or $\tau = L/R$ | seconds |
| 5 | First-order frequency ratio | $\dfrac{Y}{X} = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | First-order phase lag | $\phi = \tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$ | radians |
| 7 | Second-order natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$ | rad/s |
| 8 | Second-order damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Second-order damped frequency | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 10 | Peak overshoot | $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | fraction (×100 for %) |
| 11 | Peak time | $t_p = \pi/\omega_d$ | seconds |
| 12 | Settling time (2% band) | $t_s = 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$ | seconds |
| 13 | Rise time (under-damped) | $t_r = (\pi - \phi)/\omega_d$ | seconds |
| 14 | Bandwidth (1st order) | $\omega_{BW} = 1/\tau$ | rad/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** In process industries (chemical, power, manufacturing), the dynamic response dictates how fast a sensor can track changes. A thermometer with $\tau = 60\,\text{s}$ cannot reliably measure rapid temperature transients. Selecting a transducer with appropriate $\tau$ or $\omega_n$ is therefore a design-critical decision.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Zero-order systems** model ideal static calibration — needed for precision balances and pressure gauges.
- **First-order systems** dominate **thermal** and **liquid-level** measurements (e.g., RTD in a furnace, boiler drum level).
- **Second-order systems** govern **mechanical vibrations**, **seismic accelerometers**, and **LVDT dynamics** — critical for structural health monitoring of bridges, machine tool chatter detection, and crash-test instrumentation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Loading Error for a Voltmeter

**Given:** A Thevenin source of EMF $V_{th}$ with internal (source) impedance $Z_s$ drives a load. A voltmeter of impedance $Z_m$ is connected across the load terminals.

**Find:** The loading error $e_L$ in percentage.

### Step 1 — Write the Thevenin equivalent

The Thevenin equivalent is a voltage source $V_{th}$ in series with $Z_s$. The open-circuit (true) voltage across the terminals is:

$$V_{true} = V_{th}$$

### Step 2 — Connect the voltmeter

When the voltmeter of impedance $Z_m$ is connected, the total circuit impedance seen by the source is:

$$Z_{total} = Z_s + Z_m$$

### Step 3 — Apply the voltage divider rule

The voltage across the voltmeter (the measured voltage) is:

$$V_{measured} = V_{th} \cdot \frac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the loading error

By definition:

$$e_L = \frac{V_{measured} - V_{true}}{V_{true}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = \frac{V_{th} \cdot \frac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m} - V_{th}}{V_{th}} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = \left( \frac{Z_m}{Z_s + Z_m} - 1 \right) \times 100\%$$

Taking the common denominator:

$$e_L = \left( \frac{Z_m - (Z_s + Z_m)}{Z_s + Z_m} \right) \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\,e_L = -\frac{Z_s}{Z_s + Z_m} \times 100\%\,}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign proves that the voltmeter *always* reads a value smaller than the true value, because the current drawn by the meter produces an additional voltage drop across $Z_s$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Step Response of a First-Order System

**Given:** A first-order system with transfer relation $\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y = Kx$ subjected to a step input $x(t) = X$ for $t \geq 0$ with initial condition $y(0) = 0$.

**Find:** The complete response $y(t)$ and the time to reach 95% of final value.

### Step 1 — Substitute the step input

For $t \geq 0$, $x(t) = X$ (constant). The differential equation becomes:

$$\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = KX$$

This is a linear, first-order ODE with constant coefficients.

### Step 2 — Rearrange into standard form

$$\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}y = \frac{KX}{\tau}$$

### Step 3 — Identify the integrating factor

The integrating factor is:

$$\mu(t) = e^{\int \frac{1}{\tau} dt} = e^{t/\tau}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply through and integrate

$$e^{t/\tau}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}y = \frac{KX}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}$$

The left-hand side is an exact derivative:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left( y \cdot e^{t/\tau} \right) = \frac{KX}{\tau}e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$y(t) e^{t/\tau} - y(0) = \frac{KX}{\tau} \int_{0}^{t} e^{u/\tau} du$$

With $y(0) = 0$:

$$y(t) e^{t/\tau} = \frac{KX}{\tau} \cdot \tau\left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$

$$y(t) e^{t/\tau} = KX\left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $y(t)$

$$y(t) = KX\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\,y(t) = KX\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)\,}$$

### Step 6 — Find the 95% rise time

We need $y(t_{95}) = 0.95\,KX$:

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t_{95}/\tau}$$

$$e^{-t_{95}/\tau} = 0.05$$

$$t_{95} = -\tau \ln(0.05) = \tau \cdot 2.996 \approx 3\tau$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Damping Ratio of a Mass–Spring–Damper

**Given:** A second-order mechanical system with mass $M$, damping coefficient $B$, and spring stiffness $K$, driven by force $f(t)$. The displacement $y(t)$ is the output.

**Find:** Expression for $\zeta$ and $\omega_n$.

### Step 1 — Apply Newton's second law

$$M \frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + B\frac{dy}{dt} + Ky = f(t)$$

### Step 2 — Convert to standard form

Divide the entire equation by $M$:

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + \frac{B}{M}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{K}{M}y = \frac{1}{M}f(t)$$

### Step 3 — Compare with the canonical second-order form

$$\frac{d^{2}y}{dt^{2}} + 2\zeta\omega_{n}\frac{dy}{dt} + \omega_{n}^{2}y = K_{s}\omega_{n}^{2}x(t)$$

By direct comparison:

$$\omega_{n}^{2} = \frac{K}{M} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}$$

$$2\zeta\omega_{n} = \frac{B}{M} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta = \frac{B}{2M\omega_{n}} = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\frac{K}{M}}, \qquad \zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}\,}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Maximum Overshoot of a Second-Order System

**Given:** Under-damped step response $y(t) = KX\left[1 - \dfrac{e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\sin(\omega_{d} t + \phi)\right]$.

**Find:** Peak overshoot $M_p$.

### Step 1 — Identify the peak

Peak occurs at $t = t_p$ when the derivative $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = 0$. For an under-damped system:

$$t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{d}} = \frac{\pi}{\omega_{n}\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $t_p$ into $y(t)$

At $t = t_p$, $\omega_{d} t_{p} = \pi$, so $\sin(\pi + \phi) = -\sin\phi = -\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}$.

Also, $e^{-\zeta\omega_{n}t_p} = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$.

$$y(t_p) = KX\left[1 - \frac{e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}} \cdot \big(-\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}\big)\right]$$

$$y(t_p) = KX\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\right]$$

### Step 3 — Subtract the steady-state value

$$M_p = \frac{y(t_p) - y(\infty)}{y(\infty)} = \frac{KX\left[1 + e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\right] - KX}{KX}$$

$$\boxed{\,M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}\,}$$

For percentage overshoot, multiply by 100.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sample Loading-Error Calculation

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A voltmeter with internal resistance $R_m = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ is used to measure the voltage across a circuit with Thevenin source resistance $R_s = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$. Find the loading error.

$$e_L = -\frac{R_s}{R_s + R_m} \times 100\% = -\frac{5{,}000}{5{,}000 + 50{,}000} \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = -\frac{5{,}000}{55{,}000} \times 100\% = -9.09\%$$

**Conclusion:** A meter of higher input impedance (say, $5\,\text{M}\Omega$) would reduce this to $e_L \approx -0.1\%$, which is why modern digital multimeters advertise $10\,\text{M}\Omega$ input resistance.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Sample First-Order Time Constant

**Numerical Example:**

A thermometer follows a first-order model with $\tau = 12\,\text{s}$ and is placed in a bath suddenly changed to a new temperature. Find the time to reach 98.2% of final reading.

$$t_{98.2\%} \approx 4\tau = 4 \times 12 = 48\,\text{s}$$

If $\tau$ is too large, the thermometer is **sluggish** — a clear disadvantage in process control loops where fast feedback is needed.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Sample Second-Order Specification

**Numerical Example:**

A second-order accelerometer has $\omega_n = 250\,\text{rad/s}$ and $\zeta = 0.3$. Compute $M_p$, $t_p$, and $t_s$ (2% band).

**Peak overshoot:**

$$M_p = e^{-0.3\pi/\sqrt{1-0.3^2}} = e^{-0.3\pi/\sqrt{0.91}} = e^{-0.3 \times 3.298} = e^{-0.9894} \approx 0.372 = 37.2\%$$

**Damped frequency:**

$$\omega_{d} = 250\sqrt{1-0.09} = 250 \times 0.954 = 238.5\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Peak time:**

$$t_p = \frac{\pi}{238.5} = 0.0132\,\text{s} \approx 13.2\,\text{ms}$$

**Settling time (2%):**

$$t_s = \frac{4}{0.3 \times 250} = \frac{4}{75} = 0.0533\,\text{s} \approx 53.3\,\text{ms}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Loading Effect in a Measurement Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source / System Under Test]
    B[Thevenin Equivalent Vth Zs]
    C[Switch - Instrument ON]
    D[Measuring Instrument Zm]
    E[Output Indication]
    F[Disturbance Arrow - Loading]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    F -.-> B
    F -.-> D
```

**How to read it:** The dashed line from F to B and D represents the **loading effect** — the connection of the instrument perturbs both the source and the indicator.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — First-Order System Step Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step Input x = X]
    B[Integrator Block 1 divide by tau]
    C[Summing Junction]
    D[Output y of t]
    E[Feedback Loop gain 1]

    A --> C
    C --> B
    B --> D
    D --> E
    E --> C
```

**Reading aid:** This is a feedback-style block representation of $\tau \dot y + y = Kx$. The block represents the operator $\dfrac{1}{\tau s + 1}$ — i.e., a first-order lag.

---

## 4.3 Classification Tree — Order of a Measurement System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measurement System]
    A --> B[Zero Order]
    A --> C[First Order]
    A --> D[Second Order]

    B --> B1[Pure gain K - no dynamics]
    C --> C1[One energy storage element]
    C --> C2[Thermometer, RC circuit]
    D --> D1[Two energy storage elements]
    D --> D2[Mass spring damper, LCR]
```

---

## 4.4 Step Response Curve — Damping Cases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ResponseCurves
        direction LR
        R1[Overdamped zeta greater than 1 - slow rise no overshoot]
        R2[Critically Damped zeta equals 1 - fastest non oscillatory]
        R3[Underdamped 0 zeta less than 1 - overshoot oscillation]
        R4[Undamped zeta equals 0 - sustained oscillation]
    end

    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> R4
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualisation tip for exams:** When asked to compare damping cases, draw all four curves on a single set of axes with the *same* $\omega_n$. The under-damped case has the **highest peak**, the critically-damped case has the **shortest settling time without overshoot**, and the over-damped case is the **slowest**.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Second-Order Closed-Loop System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Input x of t]
    SUM[Summing Junction]
    F1[Forward Path omega n squared over s squared plus 2 zeta omega n s plus omega n squared]
    Y[Output y of t]
    FB[Unity Feedback]

    X --> SUM
    SUM --> F1
    F1 --> Y
    Y --> FB
    FB --> SUM
```

This is the standard canonical second-order closed-loop block with unity feedback and **open-loop transfer function**:

$$G(s) = \frac{\omega_{n}^{2}}{s^{2} + 2\zeta\omega_{n} s + \omega_{n}^{2}}$$
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Define loading effect in a measurement system. Explain with an example how the internal impedance of a measuring instrument affects the measurement accuracy.**

**Model Answer:**

The **loading effect** is the disturbance introduced into a system under measurement when an instrument is connected to it, because the instrument draws some energy from the system and thereby alters the original value of the measurand. The deviation between the indicated value and the true value is called the **loading error**.

**Example:** Consider a voltmeter of internal resistance $R_m$ connected across a source with Thevenin resistance $R_s$. The true open-circuit voltage is $V_{th}$, but the voltmeter reads:

$$V_m = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_m}{R_s + R_m}$$

If $R_m$ is not sufficiently large compared to $R_s$, a significant voltage drop occurs across $R_s$, and the meter under-reads. Hence, a high input-impedance voltmeter minimises loading error and improves measurement accuracy.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Distinguish between a first-order and a second-order measurement system. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | First-Order System | Second-Order System |
|---|---|---|
| Differential equation | $\tau \dot y + y = Kx$ | $\ddot y + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot y + \omega_n^2 y = K\omega_n^2 x$ |
| Energy storage elements | One | Two |
| Step response | Monotonic exponential rise to steady value | May rise monotonically or oscillate and overshoot |
| Key parameter | Time constant $\tau$ | Natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$ |

**First-order examples:** (1) Liquid-in-glass thermometer, (2) RC low-pass filter.
**Second-order examples:** (1) Mass-spring-damper accelerometer, (2) LCR circuit / seismometer.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**A first-order temperature measuring system has a time constant of 8 s. It is initially at 30 °C and is suddenly placed in a bath maintained at 200 °C.**

#### (a) Derive the response equation $T(t)$ and find the temperature reading after 12 s. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

The governing equation for a first-order thermal system is:

$$\tau \frac{dT}{dt} + T(t) = T_{surrounding}$$

Substituting $\tau = 8$ s and $T_{surrounding} = 200$ °C:

$$8 \frac{dT}{dt} + T = 200$$

The general step response of a first-order system is:

$$T(t) = T_{final} + (T_{initial} - T_{final})e^{-t/\tau}$$

Plug in $T_{final} = 200$ °C, $T_{initial} = 30$ °C, $\tau = 8$ s:

$$T(t) = 200 + (30 - 200)e^{-t/8}$$

$$\boxed{\,T(t) = 200 - 170\,e^{-t/8}\,}$$

At $t = 12$ s:

$$T(12) = 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-12/8} = 200 - 170 \cdot e^{-1.5}$$

$$e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

$$T(12) = 200 - 170 \times 0.2231 = 200 - 37.93$$

$$\boxed{\,T(12) = 162.07\,^\circ\text{C}\,}$$

#### (b) Determine the time required for the thermometer to read 99% of the final temperature. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

We require $T(t_{99}) = 0.99 \times 200 = 198$ °C.

$$198 = 200 - 170\,e^{-t_{99}/8}$$

$$170\,e^{-t_{99}/8} = 2$$

$$e^{-t_{99}/8} = \frac{2}{170} = 0.01176$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$-\frac{t_{99}}{8} = \ln(0.01176) = -4.4427$$

$$t_{99} = 8 \times 4.4427 = 35.54\,\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\,t_{99} \approx 35.5\,\text{s}\,}$$

**Alternative shortcut (KTU examiner tip):** 99.3% of final value corresponds to $t = 5\tau = 5 \times 8 = 40$ s, which is the standard answer for *within* 1% tolerance. The exact $t_{99}$ above (35.5 s) is more precise.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**A voltmeter with internal resistance 200 kΩ is used to measure the voltage across a circuit branch which has a Thevenin equivalent voltage of 10 V and source resistance 5 kΩ.**

#### (a) Compute the loading error in percentage and the actual reading of the voltmeter. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

The measured voltage (voltage divider):

$$V_m = V_{th} \cdot \frac{R_m}{R_s + R_m} = 10 \times \frac{200{,}000}{5{,}000 + 200{,}000}$$

$$V_m = 10 \times \frac{200{,}000}{205{,}000} = 10 \times 0.9756 = 9.756\,\text{V}$$

Loading error:

$$e_L = \left(\frac{V_m - V_{th}}{V_{th}}\right) \times 100\% = \left(\frac{9.756 - 10}{10}\right) \times 100\%$$

$$e_L = -0.0244 \times 100\% = -2.44\%$$

$$\boxed{\,V_m = 9.756\,\text{V}, \quad e_L = -2.44\%\,}$$

**Negative sign** indicates the voltmeter reads *less* than the true value, confirming the loading effect.

#### (b) A second-order accelerometer used in vibration testing has $\omega_n = 400$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.25$. Calculate the percentage overshoot, peak time, and 2% settling time. Comment on whether this damping is suitable for an accelerometer. (7 marks)

**Solution:**

**Percentage overshoot:**

$$M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$$

$$\sqrt{1 - 0.25^2} = \sqrt{0.9375} = 0.9682$$

$$M_p = e^{-0.25\pi / 0.9682} = e^{-0.8110} = 0.4445 = 44.45\%$$

**Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 400 \times 0.9682 = 387.3\,\text{rad/s}$$

**Peak time:**

$$t_p = \frac{\pi}{\omega_d} = \frac{3.1416}{387.3} = 0.00811\,\text{s} \approx 8.11\,\text{ms}$$

**Settling time (2% band):**

$$t_s = \frac{4}{\zeta\omega_n} = \frac{4}{0.25 \times 400} = \frac{4}{100} = 0.04\,\text{s} = 40\,\text{ms}$$

$$\boxed{\,M_p \approx 44.45\%, \quad t_p \approx 8.11\,\text{ms}, \quad t_s \approx 40\,\text{ms}\,}$$

**Comment on suitability:** An overshoot of 44.45% is too high for a measurement accelerometer, which is meant to faithfully reproduce the input waveform. A **damping ratio close to 0.6–0.7** (slightly under-damped) is generally preferred for accelerometers to balance bandwidth and waveform fidelity. Hence, this system is **over-oscillatory** and would distort the measured vibration signal.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Sign of loading error:** Many students write the loading error as a positive number and lose 1 mark. It is always **negative** for both voltmeter and ammeter.
> 2. **Final value vs. measured value confusion:** In first-order step response, students often forget the $(T_{initial} - T_{final})$ term and write $T(t) = 200(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Damping ratio formula:** A very common error is writing $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{\sqrt{MK}}$ (missing the factor of 2 in the denominator). The correct form is $\zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{MK}}$.
> 4. **M_p unit:** $M_p$ is a *fraction* unless explicitly stated. Writing "$M_p = 44.45$" instead of "$M_p = 44.45\%$" is a 1-mark deduction in some valuation keys.
> 5. **Settling-time band:** Always state the band (2% or 5%) used. The formula $\dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}$ is for 2%; for 5% it is $\dfrac{3}{\zeta\omega_n}$.
> 6. **Do not confuse "static sensitivity $K$" with "stiffness $K$":** In second-order mechanical systems, $K$ denotes stiffness (N/m). In first-order or zero-order electrical/thermal models, $K$ is a static gain (dimensionless or V/°C). They are different physical quantities, and using one in place of the other is a frequent conceptual error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Know for KTU ESE):**

- **Loading effect** = disturbance caused by the measuring instrument. $e_L = (X_m - X_{true})/X_{true} \times 100\%$.
- **Voltmeter** → high input impedance. **Ammeter** → low input impedance.
- **Zero-order system** → $y = Kx$, no dynamics.
- **First-order system** → $\tau \dot y + y = Kx$. Step response: $y(t) = KX(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$. Time constant $\tau$ = time to reach 63.2% of final value.
- **5τ rule** → 99.3% of final value is reached at $t = 5\tau$.
- **Second-order system** → $\ddot y + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot y + \omega_n^2 y = K\omega_n^2 x$.
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/M}$. **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = B/(2\sqrt{MK})$.
- **Damping cases:** $\zeta > 1$ (over-damped), $\zeta = 1$ (critical), $0 < \zeta < 1$ (under-damped), $\zeta = 0$ (undamped).
- **Peak overshoot** $M_p = e^{-\zeta\pi/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ (under-damped only).
- **Peak time** $t_p = \pi/\omega_d$, where $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- **Settling time** $t_s = 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$ for 2% band.
- **Bandwidth (first-order)** = $1/\tau$ rad/s.
- **Always quote the band** (2% or 5%) when stating settling time.
- **Negative loading error** means the instrument under-reads.
- **An ideal instrument** has zero loading effect and instantaneous (zero-order) response — in practice, both are unachievable, and engineering trade-offs are made.
- For accelerometers, $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.7$ is ideal; for galvanometers, $\zeta \approx 0.6$–$0.8$ provides critical damping or slight under-damping.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Static Performance Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Static Performance Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Static Performance Characteristics"?

In the context of KTU's PCPET503, every measurement instrument is a *transducer system* — a device that converts a physical input (mechanical, thermal, electrical) into a readable output. To judge whether an instrument is "good" or "bad," engineers use two broad categories of performance attributes:

- **Static characteristics** — behaviour when the input quantity is either *constant* (steady-state) or *varying so slowly* that the dynamic (transient) effects are negligible.
- **Dynamic characteristics** — behaviour when the input is *rapidly time-varying* (covered in Module 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Static Performance Characteristics):**
> Static performance characteristics are the set of inherent attributes of a measurement system that describe its *steady-state* response — i.e., how faithfully it represents the input signal when the input is either held constant or changes so slowly that inertial, damping, and thermal-transient effects can be ignored. They are evaluated by a **static calibration** experiment, where known, precisely controlled inputs are applied in a step-wise or ramp-wise (quasi-static) manner, and the corresponding outputs are recorded.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Bathroom Weighing Scale

Imagine you step onto a **digital bathroom scale** every morning:

| What you do | What the scale "shows" | Which characteristic is being tested |
|---|---|---|
| Stand still on the scale; weight doesn't change | The reading is **stable and constant** | **Drift / Stability** |
| Stand still; your actual weight is 70 kg, scale shows 70.2 kg | A small, fixed error | **Accuracy** |
| Step off, step on, step off, step on — same weight | The reading comes back to ~70.2 kg | **Repeatability (Precision)** |
| Ask your brother (same weight) to use the scale | He gets 70.4 kg | **Reproducibility** |
| Add 1 kg weight (now 71 kg) — scale jumps by 1.0 kg | Output change per input change | **Sensitivity** |
| Slowly creep onto the scale from 60→71 kg | The needle/screen moves in equal, evenly-spaced steps | **Linearity** |
| Push down on the scale, then release | Reading takes a moment to return to 70.2 kg (or stays higher) | **Hysteresis / Dead Band** |
| The scale shows 70.2 kg even when you are 70.0 kg | The smallest weight it can "see" is 0.1 kg | **Resolution / Threshold** |

A *static performance test* is essentially doing all of the above in a controlled metrology lab — but with traceable reference standards instead of a bathroom floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCPET503 Module 1):**
> The official Module 1 explicitly demands the study of:
> *(i) Static performance characteristics, (ii) Errors in measurements, (iii) Statistical analysis of measurement data, and (iv) Uncertainty analysis.*
> The static characteristics we cover here are the foundation for the error and uncertainty analysis in the upcoming sub-modules.

### 1.2 The Master List of Static Characteristics

For KTU 2024, the following set is the *de-facto canonical list* expected in the exam:

1. **Accuracy**
2. **Precision** (Repeatability + Reproducibility)
3. **Sensitivity** (and Inverse Sensitivity / Scale Factor)
4. **Range** and **Span**
5. **Resolution** (and **Threshold**)
6. **Linearity** (and **Non-linearity / Conformity**)
7. **Hysteresis** (and **Dead Band / Dead Zone**)
8. **Drift** (Zero Drift, Sensitivity Drift)
9. **Stability**
10. **Static Stiffness**
11. **Loading Effect / Input Impedance**

Each is treated rigorously in the next sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generalised Block Representation

A measurement system is mathematically modelled as a **two-port static operator**:

$$q_o = f(q_i, t, \text{environmental parameters})$$

For the static case (time-invariant, no transient dynamics), the operator reduces to a *static mapping*:

$$q_o = f(q_i)$$

A "perfect" instrument would obey $q_o = K \cdot q_i$ (purely linear, infinite resolution, no error). Every static characteristic below quantifies the *deviation* of the actual $f(q_i)$ from this ideal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used throughout these notes (per KTU notation):**
> - $q_i$ = input quantity (the measurand)
> - $q_o$ = output quantity (the reading)
> - $q_{true}$ = true value of the measurand (an idealised reference)
> - $q_m$ = measured value
> - $e$ = static error
> - $K$ = static sensitivity (gain)
> - Subscripts $(\uparrow)$ and $(\downarrow)$ denote readings taken during *increasing* and *decreasing* input sweeps, respectively.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Accuracy
- **Definition:** The closeness of a measured value to the *true* value of the measurand.
- **Quantitative form:** Usually expressed as **percentage of full-scale (FS)** or **percentage of true value (reading)**.

$$e_a = q_m - q_{true}$$

$$\text{Accuracy} = \left(1 - \frac{\vert e_a \vert}{q_{true}}\right) \times 100\,\%$$

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{q_{true} - \vert e_a \vert}{q_{true}} \times 100\,\%$$

- **Inaccuracy (more common in datasheets):** The maximum error expressed as % of FS output.
- **Why it matters:** KTU exam problems on accuracy almost always test the *difference between % of reading and % of FS* — a classic trap question.

### 2.2.2 Precision
- **Definition:** The degree of *mutual agreement* (repeatability) among a set of independent measurements of the same quantity under *specified* conditions.
- **Two sub-types:**
  - **Repeatability** — same instrument, same observer, same lab, same day, short time interval.
  - **Reproducibility** — different instrument, observer, lab, or time interval.

$$S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_{mi} - \bar{q})^2} \quad \text{(sample standard deviation)}$$

$$\text{Precision index} = \frac{2S}{\text{Full-Scale Output}} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Accuracy vs Precision — KTU's favourite conceptual question:**
> *Accuracy* is **closeness to truth** (single point). *Precision* is **closeness of repeated readings to each other** (statistical spread). An instrument can be precise but inaccurate (systematic bias), or accurate on average but imprecise (random scatter).

### 2.2.3 Sensitivity
- **Definition:** The ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input, at a *quasi-static* operating point.

$$K = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i} = \frac{dq_o}{dq_i}\bigg|_{q_i = q_0}$$

- **Inverse Sensitivity / Scale Factor:**

$$K' = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{\Delta q_i}{\Delta q_o}$$

- For a linear system, $K$ is constant; for a non-linear system, $K$ varies with the operating point.
- **Units:** [output units] / [input units] — e.g., mV/°C, mm/N.

### 2.2.4 Range and Span
- **Range:** The minimum to maximum input over which the instrument is designed to operate.
  - e.g., 0 °C to 200 °C.
- **Span (Full-Scale Input, FSI):** The *algebraic difference* between the maximum and minimum of the range.

$$\text{Span} = q_{i,\max} - q_{i,\min}$$

- **Full-Scale Output (FSO):** The corresponding change in output.

$$\text{FSO} = q_{o,\max} - q_{o,\min} = K \cdot \text{Span}$$

> [!TIP]
> Many KTU problems specify "% of FS" — students often confuse *FS* (Full-Scale input) with *FSO* (Full-Scale output). For a linear instrument, FSO = K × Span.

### 2.2.5 Resolution and Threshold
- **Resolution:** The *smallest resolvable* change in input that produces a *perceptible* change in output.

$$R = \frac{\text{FS input}}{2^N - 1} \approx \frac{\text{FS input}}{2^N}$$

where $N$ = number of bits in the ADC / display.

- **Threshold:** The *smallest input* that produces any detectable output at all (a "dead-zero" concept).
- **Example:** A 12-bit ADC on a 10 V range:
$$R = \frac{10\text{ V}}{4095} \approx 2.44\text{ mV}$$

### 2.2.6 Linearity (and Non-linearity)
- **Linearity:** The closeness of the calibration curve to a *specified straight line* over its range.
- **Two reference lines are used in KTU practice:**
  1. **Best-fit straight line** (least-squares regression) — highest linearity, smallest non-linearity error.
  2. **End-point straight line** — drawn between the calibration points at minimum and maximum input.
  3. **Theoretical / Terminal straight line** — drawn through zero and the nominal full-scale point.
- **Non-linearity (deviation from straight line):**

$$\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{\max \vert q_o - q_{o,\text{line}} \vert}{\text{FSO}} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The KTU Trap:** A problem may give you a calibration table and ask "the linearity of the instrument." The answer depends on *which reference line* the examiner assumes. Always state your assumption.

### 2.2.7 Hysteresis
- **Definition:** The maximum difference in output for the *same input*, obtained by approaching it from *opposite directions* (increasing vs decreasing sweeps).

$$e_h = \vert q_{o,\uparrow}(q_i) - q_{o,\downarrow}(q_i) \vert_{\max}$$

- **Hysteresis error as % of FSO:**

$$\%H = \frac{e_h}{\text{FSO}} \times 100\,\%$$

- **Causes:** Internal friction, backlash in linkages, magnetic hysteresis in ferromagnetic cores, elastic after-effect in materials.
- **Dead Band / Dead Zone:** The *range of input* values over which the output does *not change* at all when the input is varied in one direction and then reversed. Numerically related to hysteresis but expressed in *input* units.

### 2.2.8 Drift
- **Definition:** The slow, undesired change in output over time when the input is held constant.
- **Two flavours:**
  - **Zero Drift** — change in output at zero (or any fixed) input. Often due to thermal effects on the null.
  - **Sensitivity Drift** — change in the slope $K$ of the calibration curve. Due to ageing, temperature, component degradation.
- **Mathematically:**

$$\text{Zero Drift} = \frac{q_o(t_2) - q_o(t_1)}{q_o(t_1)} \times 100\,\%$$

### 2.2.9 Stability
- **Definition:** The ability of an instrument to retain its performance characteristics *over a long period* without recalibration.
- Quantified by the magnitude of drift over a stated time interval (e.g., "stability: ±0.1% of FS per 6 months").

### 2.2.10 Static Stiffness
- **Definition (for force / displacement / pressure instruments):** The ratio of a small change in *effort* (force, pressure) to the resulting change in *flow* (displacement, deformation) — i.e., the *resistance to deformation* of the sensing element at static equilibrium.

$$K_s = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta x}\bigg|_{\text{static}}$$

- Higher static stiffness → less deformation → higher natural frequency → better dynamic behaviour (links directly to Module 2).

### 2.2.11 Loading Effect
- **Definition:** The disturbance an instrument *introduces* into the measurand by virtue of being connected to it. e.g., a voltmeter draws current; a thermometer draws heat.
- Quantified by **Input Impedance** (for electrical) or **Input Stiffness** (for mechanical). High input impedance / stiffness → small loading effect.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table — it covers ~70% of the numerical questions in KTU Module 1.

| # | Characteristic | Core Formula | Common Units / Form |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Static Error | $e = q_m - q_{true}$ | Same as measurand |
| 2 | Accuracy (% of true) | $\left(1 - \dfrac{\vert e \vert}{q_{true}}\right) \times 100$ | % |
| 3 | Accuracy (% of FS) | $\left(1 - \dfrac{\vert e \vert}{q_{i,\max}}\right) \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Sensitivity | $K = \dfrac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$ | Output / Input |
| 5 | Inverse Sensitivity | $K' = \dfrac{1}{K}$ | Input / Output |
| 6 | Span | $\text{Span} = q_{i,\max} - q_{i,\min}$ | Input units |
| 7 | Full-Scale Output | $\text{FSO} = q_{o,\max} - q_{o,\min}$ | Output units |
| 8 | Resolution | $R = \dfrac{\text{FS input}}{2^N - 1}$ | Input units |
| 9 | Non-linearity | $\dfrac{\max \vert q_o - q_{o,\text{line}} \vert}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | % of FSO |
| 10 | Hysteresis error | $e_h = \max \vert q_{o,\uparrow} - q_{o,\downarrow} \vert$ | Output units |
| 11 | % Hysteresis | $\dfrac{e_h}{\text{FSO}} \times 100$ | % of FSO |
| 12 | Dead Band | $q_{i,\text{dead}} = \dfrac{e_h}{K}$ | Input units |
| 13 | Zero Drift | $\dfrac{q_o(t_2) - q_o(t_1)}{q_o(t_1)} \times 100$ | % over time |
| 14 | Sample Std. Dev. (Precision) | $S = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum(q_{mi} - \bar q)^2}$ | Same as output |
| 15 | Static Stiffness | $K_s = \dfrac{\Delta F}{\Delta x}$ | N/m, N/mm |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the design of a pressure gauge, you *deliberately* trade-off static stiffness (high) against range (low), because a stiffer diaphragm deflects less, hence gives a smaller output per unit pressure. This trade-off cascades into sensitivity, resolution, and natural-frequency (Module 2) considerations. Real engineering is a Pareto optimisation across all these static (and dynamic) characteristics.

---

## 2.4 Logical Flow of "How to Characterise an Instrument Statically"

A board-exam favourite: describe the *experimental procedure* of a static calibration.

1. **Select reference standards** (e.g., dead-weight tester for pressure, NABL-traceable voltage source for voltage).
2. **Apply known inputs** in *discrete, equally-spaced steps* from $q_{i,\min}$ to $q_{i,\max}$ (up-scale sweep).
3. **Record outputs** at each step *only after steady state* is reached (this is the *static* part).
4. **Reverse the input** in equal steps back to $q_{i,\min}$ (down-scale sweep).
5. **Repeat** the full cycle multiple times to obtain repeatability statistics.
6. **Plot** $q_o$ vs $q_i$ — this is the *calibration curve*.
7. **Compute** the static characteristics (sensitivity from slope, hysteresis from up-down spread, non-linearity from deviation, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Below are **three fully-worked derivations** that KTU examiners have historically used (or are highly likely to use) as the 7-mark sub-parts in Part B questions.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Non-linearity as a Percentage of FSO

**Statement (KTU standard form):** For a calibration curve whose measured outputs $q_{oi}$ deviate from a best-fit straight line $q_{o,\text{line}}(q_i) = a + b q_i$ (where $a$ and $b$ are intercept and slope from least-squares regression), derive the expression for percentage non-linearity.

**Given data:** $n$ calibration points $(q_{i1}, q_{o1}), (q_{i2}, q_{o2}), \ldots, (q_{in}, q_{on})$.

**Step 1 — Define the deviation at any calibration point:**
The vertical distance between the measured point and the reference straight line is

$$\delta_i = q_{oi} - q_{o,\text{line}}(q_i) = q_{oi} - (a + b q_{ii})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the slope $b$ and intercept $a$ from least squares (derivation continues in 3.2 below):**

**Step 3 — Define the maximum non-linearity as the *worst-case* deviation:**

$$D_{\max} = \max_{i=1,\ldots,n} \vert \delta_i \vert = \max_i \vert q_{oi} - (a + b q_{ii}) \vert$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (FSO):**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Non-linearity (\%)} = \frac{D_{\max}}{\text{FSO}} \times 100 = \frac{\max_i \vert q_{oi} - (a + b q_{ii}) \vert}{q_{o,\max} - q_{o,\min}} \times 100\;}$$

**Interpretation:** A non-linearity of 0.5% of FSO means the worst calibration point deviates from the best-fit straight line by only 0.5% of the total output swing. Smaller is better.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Least-Squares Best-Fit Slope and Intercept (for non-linearity)

This is the most-asked derivation in KTU Module 1, so it is reproduced in full.

**Step 1 — Define the residual (the "error" we minimise):**
For each calibration point, the *residual* between the measured output $q_{oi}$ and the value $a + b q_{ii}$ predicted by the straight line is

$$r_i = q_{oi} - (a + b q_{ii})$$

**Step 2 — Define the sum of squares of residuals (the "loss function"):**

$$S(a, b) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[ q_{oi} - a - b q_{ii} \right]^2$$

**Step 3 — Minimise $S$ w.r.t. $a$ and $b$ (set partial derivatives to zero):**

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial a} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[ q_{oi} - a - b q_{ii} \right] = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial b} = -2\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_{ii}\left[ q_{oi} - a - b q_{ii} \right] = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand the two normal equations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_{oi} = n a + b \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_{ii}$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} q_{ii}\, q_{oi} = a \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_{ii} + b \sum_{i=1}^{n} q_{ii}^2$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the standard mean notations:**

$$\bar q_i = \frac{1}{n}\sum q_{ii}, \quad \bar q_o = \frac{1}{n}\sum q_{oi}$$

$$S_{ii} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (q_{ii} - \bar q_i)^2, \quad S_{io} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (q_{ii} - \bar q_i)(q_{oi} - \bar q_o)$$

**Step 6 — Solve the normal equations (using the centred forms):**

From the first normal equation, $a = \bar q_o - b\,\bar q_i$.
Substituting into the second and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;b = \frac{S_{io}}{S_{ii}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_{ii} - \bar q_i)(q_{oi} - \bar q_o)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(q_{ii} - \bar q_i)^2}\;}$$

$$\boxed{\;a = \bar q_o - b\,\bar q_i\;}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back into the non-linearity formula of §3.1 to obtain the final % non-linearity.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Converting Hysteresis Error from Output Units to Input Units (Dead Band)

**Statement:** Given a transducer of static sensitivity $K$, derive the expression for *dead band* (also called *dead zone*) in *input* units from the *hysteresis error* $e_h$ measured in *output* units.

**Step 1 — Definition of hysteresis error in output units:**

$$e_h = \max_i \vert q_{o,\uparrow}(q_{ii}) - q_{o,\downarrow}(q_{ii}) \vert$$

This is the *maximum* vertical gap between the up-sweep and down-sweep calibration curves, expressed in output units (e.g., mV, mm).

**Step 2 — Convert this to equivalent input units using sensitivity:**
The sensitivity $K = \Delta q_o / \Delta q_i$ tells us how many output units correspond to one input unit. Therefore, an output difference of $e_h$ corresponds to an *equivalent input difference* of

$$q_{i,\text{dead}} = \frac{e_h}{K}$$

**Step 3 — Express as a percentage of span:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Dead Band (\% of Span)} = \frac{e_h / K}{q_{i,\max} - q_{i,\min}} \times 100 = \frac{e_h}{K \cdot \text{Span}} \times 100\;}$$

**Step 4 — Special case (linear instrument, $K$ = constant):**
If $K$ is constant, this becomes a simple division. For non-linear instruments, $K$ is evaluated at the operating point, and the formula gives a *local* dead band.

**Worked numerical example (typical KTU style):**
A load cell has a span of 0–1000 N and a sensitivity of 2 mV/V at 10 V excitation. The maximum hysteresis observed between up- and down-sweep is 4 mV. Find (i) the hysteresis as % of FSO, (ii) the dead band in N.

$$\text{FSO} = K \times \text{Span} = 2\,\tfrac{\text{mV}}{\text{V}} \times 10\,\text{V} \times \frac{1000\,\text{N}}{1} = 20{,}000\,\text{mV}\text{ over the 1000 N span}$$

$$\%H = \frac{4}{20{,}000} \times 100 = 0.02\,\%\text{ of FSO}$$

$$\text{Dead Band} = \frac{e_h}{K} = \frac{4\,\text{mV}}{2\,\text{mV/V} \times 10\,\text{V}} = \frac{4}{20}\,\text{N} = 0.2\,\text{N}$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love this kind of "one formula, two answers" question. Practice it until it becomes reflex.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Characteristic

| Situation in the problem | Use this characteristic | Key formula to invoke |
|---|---|---|
| Reading 70.2 kg on a 70.0 kg standard | **Accuracy / Error** | $e = q_m - q_{true}$ |
| Same measurement repeated 10 times, all near 70.2 kg | **Repeatability / Precision** | $S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(q_{mi}-\bar q)^2}$ |
| Different lab measures 70.4 kg on the same standard | **Reproducibility** | (qualitative + statistical comparison) |
| Calibration table given | **Sensitivity, Linearity, Hysteresis, Drift** | $K$, non-linearity, $e_h$, drift |
| ADC with $N$ bits on a given range | **Resolution** | $R = \text{FS}/(2^N - 1)$ |
| Output rises/falls by itself at constant input | **Zero Drift** | $\Delta q_o / q_o \times 100$ |
| Output scale is non-linear (e.g., square-root flow) | **Linearity / Non-linearity** | best-fit or end-point line deviation |
| Spring/diaphragm-type element | **Static Stiffness** | $K_s = \Delta F / \Delta x$ |
| Voltmeter changes the circuit under test | **Loading Effect / Input Impedance** | qualitative + impedance ratio |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Static Calibration Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Standard<br/>Traceable Source] -->|Known Input qi| B[Instrument<br/>Under Test]
    B -->|Output qo| C[Readout / Display<br/>Indicator]
    B -->|Disturbance| D[Environment<br/>Temperature Humidity]
    C --> E[Calibration Record<br/>qo vs qi]
    E --> F[Static Characteristics<br/>Computed]
    F --> G1[Accuracy]
    F --> G2[Sensitivity K]
    F --> G3[Linearity]
    F --> G4[Hysteresis]
    F --> G5[Drift]
    F --> G6[Resolution]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Reference Standard** is a calibrated, traceable input source — the *truth* against which the instrument is judged.
- The **Instrument Under Test (IUT)** is the device whose static characteristics are being evaluated.
- The **Readout/Display** is the output indicator; sometimes the IUT and readout are integrated.
- The **Environment** introduces thermal, humidity, vibration, and EMI disturbances that the calibration *must control* — otherwise drift and zero-shift contaminate the results.
- The **Calibration Record** is the dataset $(q_i, q_o)$ used to compute all the static characteristics.
- The **six characteristic blocks** on the right are computed from the same dataset using the formulas of Section 2.

---

## 4.2 Tree Diagram — The Static-Characteristic Family

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Static Performance<br/>Characteristics] --> A[Accuracy]
    S --> B[Precision]
    B --> B1[Repeatability]
    B --> B2[Reproducibility]
    S --> C[Sensitivity]
    C --> C1[Static Sensitivity K]
    C --> C2[Inverse Sensitivity 1 by K]
    S --> D[Range and Span]
    S --> E[Resolution]
    E --> E1[Threshold]
    S --> F[Linearity]
    F --> F1[Best Fit Line]
    F --> F2[End Point Line]
    F --> F3[Theoretical Line]
    S --> G[Hysteresis]
    G --> G1[Dead Band]
    S --> H[Drift]
    H --> H1[Zero Drift]
    H --> H2[Sensitivity Drift]
    S --> I[Stability]
    S --> J[Static Stiffness]
    S --> K[Loading Effect]
```

This tree is the **mental model** every KTU student should internalise — it has appeared, in some form, in nearly every previous ESE question paper for this course.

---

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — How Characteristics Relate During Instrument Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Choose Sensing<br/>Element] --> B{Static Stiffness<br/>High or Low}
    B -->|High| B1[Small Deflection]
    B -->|Low| B2[Large Deflection]
    B1 --> C[Low Sensitivity]
    B2 --> D[High Sensitivity]
    C --> E[Needs High Gain<br/>Amplifier Stage]
    D --> F[Good Resolution<br/>at Low Range]
    E --> G[Possible Noise<br/>Amplification]
    D --> H[Possible Non<br/>Linearity]
    F --> I[Limited Dynamic<br/>Range]
    H --> I
    G --> I
    I --> J[Final Instrument<br/>with Paretolistic<br/>Static Response]
```

**Engineering meaning of the diagram:** Choosing the static stiffness of a sensing element (e.g., a Bourdon tube or diaphragm) immediately forces trade-offs in sensitivity, resolution, linearity, and dynamic range. This is the **designer's Pareto front** — the conceptual reason why KTU expects students to know *how* the static characteristics inter-relate, not just their isolated definitions.

---

## 4.4 Hysteresis Loop — Visualised

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input rising<br/>qi increases] --> P2[Output Curve<br/>Up Sweep]
    P3[Input falling<br/>qi decreases] --> P4[Output Curve<br/>Down Sweep]
    P2 --> P5[Gap eh = max vertical<br/>separation]
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Dead Band<br/>eh by K input units]
```

In a *real* graph, the up-sweep and down-sweep curves form a **hysteresis loop**; the maximum vertical gap $e_h$ between them, divided by FSO, gives the percentage hysteresis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCPET503, Module 1]`
**Define the following static performance characteristics of a measurement system:**
**(i) Sensitivity, (ii) Resolution, (iii) Hysteresis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~80 words each):**

> **(i) Sensitivity (1 mark):** It is the ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input, evaluated at a quasi-static operating point, i.e., $K = \Delta q_o / \Delta q_i$. It represents the *gain* of the instrument.
>
> **(ii) Resolution (1 mark):** It is the smallest *resolvable* change in the input that produces a perceptible change in the output. For digital instruments it is governed by the number of bits: $R = \text{FS input} / (2^N - 1)$.
>
> **(iii) Hysteresis (1 mark):** It is the maximum difference in output for the *same* input when approached from opposite directions (up-sweep vs down-sweep), $e_h = \max \vert q_{o,\uparrow} - q_{o,\downarrow} \vert$. Expressed as % of FSO.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCPET503, Module 1]`
**Distinguish between (a) Accuracy and Precision, and (b) Repeatability and Reproducibility, with one suitable engineering example for each pair.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1.5 marks per pair):**

> **(a) Accuracy vs Precision (1.5 marks):**
> *Accuracy* is the closeness of a measured value to the *true value* (a single-point concept). *Precision* is the closeness of *repeated measurements* to each other (a statistical-spread concept). An example: a voltmeter reading 10.02 V on a 10.00 V standard every time is *highly precise* but *inaccurate*; another instrument whose readings scatter randomly between 9.95 V and 10.05 V is *more accurate on average* but *less precise*.
>
> **(b) Repeatability vs Reproducibility (1.5 marks):**
> *Repeatability* refers to variation when measurements are made by the *same* operator, on the *same* instrument, in the *same* lab, over a *short* time interval. *Reproducibility* refers to variation when *any one* of these conditions changes (different operator, instrument, lab, or longer time gap). Example: a CNC machine's diameter measurements on ten consecutive shafts (repeatability) vs diameters of the *same* design produced on three different CNC machines in three different plants (reproducibility).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### 📋 Question A — 14 Marks

> `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Adapted for PCPET503 Module 1]`
> **(a)** With a neat sketch of a typical static calibration set-up, explain the procedure of conducting a *static calibration* of a pressure gauge using a *dead-weight tester*. List the static performance characteristics that can be determined from such a calibration. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pressure gauge is calibrated against a standard using the following data. The nominal full-scale input is 10 bar. The gauge has an output in mV via a strain-gauge bridge. Compute the **(i)** static sensitivity, **(ii)** non-linearity as % of FSO, and **(iii)** hysteresis as % of FSO. **(7 marks)**

| Input pressure $q_i$ (bar) | Output (up-sweep) $q_{o,\uparrow}$ (mV) | Output (down-sweep) $q_{o,\downarrow}$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 9.8 | 10.0 |
| 4 | 20.0 | 20.2 |
| 6 | 30.0 | 30.3 |
| 8 | 40.2 | 40.4 |
| 10 | 50.0 | 50.0 |

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a) — Static Calibration of a Pressure Gauge (7 marks)

**Sketch (2 marks):**
Dead-weight tester → calibrated pressure input $P$ → connected hydraulically to the gauge under test → gauge output (mV) read on a digital voltmeter or indicator. The environment (temperature, vibration) must be controlled.

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. Assemble the dead-weight tester, fill with hydraulic oil, bleed out air, and place the *certified* weights on the piston.
2. Apply incremental pressure in equal steps (e.g., 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 bar). At each step, allow the system to reach *steady state* (no drift in output) and record the gauge reading.
3. Reverse the procedure: reduce the pressure in the *same* steps back to 0, recording readings at each step.
4. Repeat the full cycle 3–5 times to obtain repeatability statistics.
5. Plot output $q_o$ vs input $q_i$ for up-sweep and down-sweep (two curves).

**Static characteristics obtainable (2 marks):**
Accuracy, precision (repeatability, reproducibility), sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, threshold, resolution, drift (if repeated over time), span, and range.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computations (7 marks)

**(i) Static sensitivity (2 marks):**
The sensitivity is the *best-fit slope* of $q_o$ vs $q_i$. Since the data is essentially linear, we use the *average* of up- and down-sweep at each pressure (or use end-point line). Using **end-point line** (the KTU-friendly method):

$$K = \frac{q_{o,\text{final}} - q_{o,\text{initial}}}{q_{i,\text{final}} - q_{i,\text{initial}}} = \frac{50.0 - 0.0}{10 - 0} = 5.0\,\text{mV/bar}$$

**(ii) Non-linearity (2.5 marks):**
Using the *end-point* straight line as the reference: $q_{o,\text{line}} = 5.0 \, q_i$.

| $q_i$ (bar) | $q_o^{\text{avg}}$ (mV) | $q_{o,\text{line}} = 5 q_i$ | $\delta = q_o - q_{o,\text{line}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.1 | 0.0 | +0.1 |
| 2 | 9.9 | 10.0 | −0.1 |
| 4 | 20.1 | 20.0 | +0.1 |
| 6 | 30.15 | 30.0 | +0.15 |
| 8 | 40.3 | 40.0 | +0.3 |
| 10 | 50.0 | 50.0 | 0.0 |

Maximum deviation: $\vert\delta\vert_{\max} = 0.3\,\text{mV}$ at $q_i = 8$ bar.

$$\text{FSO} = q_{o,\max} - q_{o,\min} = 50.0 - 0.0 = 50.0\,\text{mV}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Non-linearity} = \frac{0.3}{50.0} \times 100 = 0.6\,\%\text{ of FSO}\;}$$

**(iii) Hysteresis (2.5 marks):**
At each input, compute $\vert q_{o,\uparrow} - q_{o,\downarrow} \vert$:

| $q_i$ (bar) | $\vert q_{o,\uparrow} - q_{o,\downarrow} \vert$ (mV) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.2 |
| 2 | 0.2 |
| 4 | 0.2 |
| 6 | 0.3 |
| 8 | 0.2 |
| 10 | 0.0 |

Maximum hysteresis: $e_h = 0.3\,\text{mV}$.

$$\boxed{\;\text{Hysteresis} = \frac{0.3}{50.0} \times 100 = 0.6\,\%\text{ of FSO}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often forget to specify the *reference straight line* used in non-linearity. Always state "with respect to the **end-point** straight line" (or "best-fit", as the case may be). Without this, the answer is **incomplete → 1 mark deducted**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** In part (b)(iii), students sometimes compute hysteresis at *one* input value only (e.g., at 6 bar). KTU requires the **maximum** hysteresis *across the entire range*. Failing to scan all data points → **half the marks lost**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** Computing FSO incorrectly (using input span × K is a valid shortcut, but be consistent with units). Mixing mV with V will yield a 0.06% answer instead of 60% — a classic sign error.

---

### 📋 Question B — 14 Marks *(Alternative to Question A — Internal Choice)*

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 — Reconstructed for PCPET503 Module 1]`
> **(a)** Define and explain the following static performance characteristics with appropriate equations and significance: **(i)** Accuracy, **(ii)** Threshold, **(iii)** Static stiffness, **(iv)** Loading effect. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A load cell is rated for 0–500 N with a sensitivity of 3 mV/V when excited by a 12 V supply. A 0–10 V digital indicator is connected to its output.
> **(i)** Find the full-scale output in mV and V.
> **(ii)** If the indicator's ADC is 12-bit, determine the resolution in N.
> **(iii)** During a calibration, the maximum hysteresis observed is 6 mV. Compute the dead band in N and as a percentage of the span. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a) — Definitions (7 marks — 1.75 marks each)

**(i) Accuracy:** The closeness of a measured value to the *true* value. Expressed either as a percentage of the true value or as a percentage of the full-scale reading.
$$A = \left(1 - \frac{\vert e \vert}{q_{true}}\right) \times 100\,\%$$
Significance: tells us how *systematically* correct the instrument is; high accuracy is essential for commercial weighing, billing, and safety-critical measurement.

**(ii) Threshold:** The smallest input that produces a *detectable* output at all. Below this input, the output remains zero. Significance: defines the "dead-zero" of an instrument; it is the lower bound of useful input range.

**(iii) Static stiffness:** For mechanical sensors (springs, diaphragms, Bourdon tubes), it is the ratio of a small change in applied *effort* to the resulting change in *flow* (displacement) at static equilibrium:
$$K_s = \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta x}\bigg|_{\text{static}}$$
Significance: high stiffness → small deflection → high natural frequency (good for dynamic response, Module 2) but lower sensitivity (Pareto trade-off).

**(iv) Loading effect:** The disturbance introduced by the measurement instrument *into* the measurand. A voltmeter draws current; a thermometer draws heat. Quantified by the input impedance (electrical) or input stiffness (mechanical). Significance: an ideal instrument has *infinite* input impedance and *zero* loading effect.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Full-scale output (2 marks):**

$$\text{FSO (mV)} = K \times V_{exc} \times \text{Span} = 3\,\frac{\text{mV}}{\text{V}} \times 12\,\text{V} \times 500\,\text{N}$$

Wait — reinterpreting sensitivity correctly. Sensitivity of 3 mV/V at full-scale (per V of excitation, per V of rated output) is the *dimensionless* gain. In practice the load cell produces:

$$\text{FSO} = K \times V_{exc} = 3\,\frac{\text{mV}}{\text{V}} \times 12\,\text{V} = 36\,\text{mV} = 0.036\,\text{V}$$

The 0–500 N range corresponds to 0–36 mV.

**(ii) Resolution in N (2.5 marks):**

$$R_{\text{indicator}} = \frac{10\,\text{V}}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442\,\text{mV} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V}$$

Convert to equivalent input force:
The full-scale of 500 N produces 36 mV; therefore, $1\,\text{mV} \leftrightarrow 500/36 = 13.889\,\text{N}$.

$$R_{\text{force}} = R_{\text{indicator}} \times \frac{\text{Span}}{\text{FSO}} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V} \times \frac{500}{0.036}$$

$$R_{\text{force}} = 2.442 \times 10^{-3} \times 13{,}888.9 = 33.92\,\text{N}$$

$$\boxed{\;R \approx 33.9\,\text{N}\;}$$

**(iii) Dead band (2.5 marks):**

Sensitivity in *output / input* form: $K = \text{FSO} / \text{Span} = 36\,\text{mV} / 500\,\text{N} = 0.072\,\text{mV/N}$.

$$\text{Dead Band} = \frac{e_h}{K} = \frac{6\,\text{mV}}{0.072\,\text{mV/N}} = 83.33\,\text{N}$$

As percentage of span:

$$\%\text{Dead Band} = \frac{83.33}{500} \times 100 = 16.67\,\%$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{Dead Band} = 83.3\,\text{N} \approx 16.67\,\%\text{ of Span}\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #4 (Question B specific):** Students often confuse the two different "sensitivities" of a load cell — the *dimensionless* sensitivity (mV/V, given by manufacturer) and the *calibrated* sensitivity (mV/N, derived). Mixing these leads to a factor-of-12 error (the value of $V_{exc}$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #5:** Resolution depends on the *whole signal chain* — sensor, amplifier, ADC. Forgetting the indicator's resolution and only considering the sensor's leads to a non-physical answer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #6:** "Dead band" is asked in *input* units, while "hysteresis" is asked in *output* units. The conversion is **always** a division by the (calibrated) sensitivity $K$. Confusing these two is a very common 2-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep):**

- ✅ **Static performance characteristics** describe the **steady-state** behaviour of an instrument — no time / dynamic effects.
- ✅ **Accuracy** = closeness to truth; **Precision** = closeness of repeated readings to each other. *An instrument can be precise without being accurate.*
- ✅ **Sensitivity** $K = \Delta q_o / \Delta q_i$. **Inverse sensitivity** $K' = 1/K$. $K$ is *constant* for a linear instrument, *variable* otherwise.
- ✅ **Range** = min-to-max input; **Span** = algebraic difference. **FSO** = $K \times$ Span.
- ✅ **Resolution** of an $N$-bit ADC: $R = \text{FS} / (2^N - 1)$. **Threshold** = smallest detectable input (zero-floor).
- ✅ **Linearity** = closeness of calibration curve to a straight line. Reference line can be best-fit, end-point, or theoretical. **Non-linearity** = max deviation / FSO $\times 100\%$.
- ✅ **Hysteresis** $e_h$ = max vertical gap between up- and down-sweep curves (output units). **Dead band** = $e_h / K$ (input units).
- ✅ **Drift** = slow change in output at constant input. **Zero drift** is at zero input; **sensitivity drift** is a change in $K$. Often caused by temperature.
- ✅ **Stability** = ability to retain calibration over time = inverse of drift.
- ✅ **Static stiffness** $K_s = \Delta F / \Delta x$ — high $K_s$ ⇒ small deflection ⇒ low sensitivity (trade-off).
- ✅ **Loading effect** = disturbance caused by the instrument on the measurand; quantified by input impedance / stiffness. Ideal: zero loading.
- ✅ The **calibration curve** is the master plot: $q_o$ vs $q_i$ with *up-sweep* and *down-sweep* drawn together — from it, all static characteristics (except drift) can be computed.
- ✅ **Units trap:** always distinguish between *output units* (for hysteresis) and *input units* (for dead band); convert via $K$.
- ✅ **% trap:** "Accuracy" is usually asked as **% of reading** or **% of FS** — read the question twice.
- ✅ KTU Module 1 frequently tests the **best-fit straight-line derivation** (least squares) — memorise the two normal equations.
- ✅ The **order of preference** for the linearity reference line in industry: best-fit (smallest non-linearity) > end-point > theoretical. But KTU often uses **end-point** for simplicity.

> **End of Module 1 — Static Performance Characteristics — PCPET503 — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors and Their Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Errors and Their Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an "Error" in Measurement?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> An **Error** is defined as the *deviation* of a measured value from the *true value* (or the conventionally accepted value) of the measurand. Mathematically, if $x_m$ is the measured value and $x_t$ is the true value, then:
> $$E = x_m - x_t$$

Every measurement—no matter how sophisticated the instrument—carries an inherent uncertainty. **No measurement is absolutely perfect**, because instruments have finite resolution, environments are never perfectly stable, and human senses (or sensors) always introduce some noise.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The Arrows-on-Target Intuition)

Imagine you are shooting arrows at a bullseye target:

| Scenario | What happens | Engineering Term |
|----------|--------------|------------------|
| Arrows cluster **tightly together**, but far from the bullseye | Result is **repeatable**, but **wrong on average** | **Precise but NOT Accurate** → caused by *Systematic Error* |
| Arrows are **scattered all around** the target, average lands near the bullseye | Individual shots are off, but on average the aim is right | **Accurate on average, but NOT Precise** → caused by *Random Error* |
| Arrows scatter *AND* are far from the bullseye | Both repeatability and truth are bad | **Gross Error** (Blunders) |
| Arrows hit the bullseye consistently | The ideal | **Both Accurate and Precise** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** A measurement system should aim to be both *accurate* (close to the true value) and *precise* (reproducible). Errors are the *quantitative description* of the failure to achieve this dual goal.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Truth: True Value vs. Measured Value

- **True Value ($x_t$):** The actual physical quantity—often unknowable in absolute terms. In practice, we substitute it with a *conventionally true value* maintained by national standards (e.g., **NABL, NIST, ISO 17025**).
- **Measured Value ($x_m$):** The reading obtained from the measuring instrument.
- **Error ($E$):** The algebraic difference $x_m - x_t$.

### 1.4 Why Study Errors in Quality Control?

In a **Quality Control (QC)** environment (e.g., ISO 9001:2015, Six-Sigma), we cannot *guarantee* a part's dimension; we can only *state a probability* that it lies within a tolerance. This is possible **only** if we characterize the error structure of the measurement system. Hence, error classification is the **foundational prerequisite** for statistical process control (SPC), measurement system analysis (MSA), and gauge R&R studies.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Master Classification of Errors (KTU Board Favourite)

The universally accepted classification for PCPET503 is the **three-tier taxonomy**:

1. **Gross Errors (Blunders / Human Errors)**
2. **Systematic Errors (Deterministic / Bias Errors)**
3. **Random Errors (Residual / Accidental Errors)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic for Board Exam — "GSR" :** **G**ross, **S**ystematic, **R**andom — study them in this order; it matches most KTU question-paper flow.

---

### 2.2 Sub-Classification of Systematic Errors

Systematic errors are *reproducible* and *biased* in nature. They have a **non-zero mean** and arise from identifiable causes:

| Sub-Type | Source | Remedy |
|----------|--------|--------|
| **Instrumental Errors** | Imperfect design, calibration drift, loading effect, zero error, ageing | Periodic calibration, zero-adjustment |
| **Environmental Errors** | Temperature, humidity, pressure, vibration, magnetic field | Controlled environment, compensation, thermal shielding |
| **Observational Errors** | Parallax, wrong reading habit, interpolation bias, personal equation | Use digital readouts, training, proper viewing |

---

### 2.3 Statistical Foundations of Random Errors

When systematic and gross errors are eliminated, the remaining scatter is governed by the **Theory of Probability**. The **Normal (Gaussian) Distribution** is the workhorse model:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

- **$\mu$** = population mean (true value in the limit of large $n$)
- **$\sigma$** = population standard deviation
- Approximately **68.27 %** of readings lie in $[\mu \pm \sigma]$, **95.45 %** in $[\mu \pm 2\sigma]$, and **99.73 %** in $[\mu \pm 3\sigma]$ (the famous **3-sigma rule**).

> [!NOTE]
> **3-Sigma Rule (Empirical):** In a well-behaved measurement, the *probability of an error exceeding $3\sigma$ is less than 0.3 %*. This is the basis for *confidence intervals* in calibration certificates.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print This!)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Notation | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|----------|------|
| 1 | Absolute Error | $\delta x = x_m - x_t$ | $x_m$ = measured, $x_t$ = true | Same as measurand |
| 2 | Relative Error (Fractional) | $\varepsilon_r = \dfrac{\delta x}{x_t}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Unitless |
| 3 | Percentage Error | $\varepsilon_\% = \dfrac{\delta x}{x_t}\times 100$ | Multiplying by 100 | % |
| 4 | Arithmetic Mean | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{1}{n}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = number of readings | Same as measurand |
| 5 | Deviation | $d_i = x_i - \bar{x}$ | $d_i$ is signed | Same as measurand |
| 6 | Variance | $\sigma^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2$ | $n-1$ = Bessel's correction | (unit)² |
| 7 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})^2}$ | Sample SD | Same as measurand |
| 8 | Probable Error (50 % confidence) | $r = 0.6745\,\sigma$ | Median of absolute errors | Same as measurand |
| 9 | Standard Error of Mean | $S_m = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | Uncertainty of the mean | Same as measurand |
| 10 | Range | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Quick spread indicator | Same as measurand |
| 11 | Sum Error (independent vars) | $\Delta y = \sqrt{\Delta x_1^{\,2} + \Delta x_2^{\,2}}$ | Errors add in quadrature | Same as $y$ |
| 12 | Product/Quotient Error | $\dfrac{\Delta y}{\vert y\vert} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\Delta x_1}{x_1}\right)^{\!2} + \left(\dfrac{\Delta x_2}{x_2}\right)^{\!2}}$ | Relative errors add in quadrature | Unitless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Rule for KTU Board:** Never confuse *Standard Deviation ($\sigma$)* with *Standard Error of Mean ($S_m$)*. $\sigma$ describes *one* reading; $S_m$ describes *the average of $n$* readings, and it is $\sqrt{n}$ times smaller.

---

### 2.5 Accuracy, Precision, Resolution, Sensitivity — The "A-P-R-S" Quad
