# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define gear error. List any four sources of errors in the manufacturing of gears.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A gear error is any deviation between the actual geometry of a manufactured gear tooth and its theoretically perfect geometry, as defined by the basic rack profile per ISO 1328-1.

**Four sources of errors:**
1. **Machine tool errors** — spindle runout, lead screw errors, indexing worm inaccuracies, table slide errors.
2. **Cutting tool errors** — hob profile error, hob lead error, cutter spacing error, tool wear, re-sharpening inaccuracies.
3. **Workpiece blank errors** — bore size/roundness, face runout, bore-to-OD concentricity error.
4. **Setup and environmental errors** — mounting eccentricity, centre height offset, thermal expansion, vibration, chip interference.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain how the mounting eccentricity of a gear on its arbor leads to tooth thickness variation. Derive the relationship.**

**Model Answer:**

When a gear is mounted on an arbor with a clearance fit, the bore can sit off-centre by an amount $e$ (mounting eccentricity). This makes the pitch circle rotate about a non-geometric centre.

The pitch circle radius at angle $\theta$ from the direction of eccentricity is $R + e\cos\theta$ (from the derivation in Section 3.1).

Since tooth thickness is measured at the pitch circle, and the flank makes the pressure angle $\alpha$ with the radial direction, a radial shift $dr = e\cos\theta$ produces a tangential (tooth thickness) shift:
$$ds = e\cos\theta \cdot \tan\alpha$$

Maximum tooth thickness variation: $\Delta s_{\max} = e\tan\alpha$ at $\theta = 0$.

This causes **non-uniform backlash** around the gear and is measured as **radial composite error** $f_i'' = 4e$ in double-flank testing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]
**(a)** Explain in detail the various sources of errors in the manufacturing of gears arising from the **machine tool**, with neat diagrams where necessary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the effect of **cutting tool errors** and **workpiece blank errors** on the quality of the finished gear. **(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Machine Tool Error Sources

The machine tool (gear hobber, shaper, or grinder) is a complex kinematic chain. Its errors are systematically transferred to the workpiece through the cutting kinematics.

**1. Spindle Errors (3 marks)**
- **Radial runout** of the cutter spindle (hob spindle in hobbing): causes the cutting edges to move in and out of engagement, producing a sinusoidal pitch error and profile error at the gear frequency.
- **Axial runout** of the cutter spindle: causes lead error in helical gears.
- **Angular tilt** of the spindle: causes profile asymmetry.

**2. Lead Screw / Feed Screw Errors (2 marks)**
- Periodic pitch error in the lead screw translates directly to axial positioning errors of the hob → **lead error** $F_\beta$ in helical gears.
- Cumulative lead error → cumulative axial displacement → progressive lead error variation.

**3. Indexing Worm and Worm Wheel Errors (1 mark)**
- In gear shaping and skiving, indexing is performed by a worm-wheel pair. Pitch errors on the worm wheel create **cumulative pitch error** $F_p$ on the gear:
$$F_p = \frac{m z^2 \delta_w}{4i}$$
- This error is **quadratic in tooth count** $z$, making it critical for high-precision gears.

**4. Slide and Table Errors (1 mark)**
- Non-perpendicularity of cross slide to spindle axis → helix angle deviation.
- Straightness errors of slideways → waviness in the generated profile.

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram.

#### Solution to (b) — Cutting Tool and Workpiece Errors

**A. Cutting Tool Errors (3.5 marks)**

| Tool Error Type | Resulting Gear Error | Mechanism |
|-----------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Hob profile grinding error | $f_f$ (uniform profile error) | The involute profile of the hob is ground on a thread grinder; any inaccuracy is replicated on every gear tooth. |
| Hob lead error | $F_\beta$ | Deviation in the hob's helix angle propagates to the gear's lead. |
| Hob spacing error | $f_{pt}$ | Errors in spacing between hob teeth create pitch errors on the gear. |
| Cutter runout | $f_{pt} + f_f$ | Radial runout of the hob on its arbor replicates as a tooth-frequency error. |
| Tool wear | Increasing $f_f$ | Flank wear on the hob changes the effective pressure angle and profile. |
| Re-sharpening | Profile degradation | Each regrind removes material asymmetrically, altering the relief angle. |

**B. Workpiece Blank Errors (3.5 marks)**

| Blank Error | Resulting Gear Error | Mechanism |
|-------------|----------------------|-----------|
| Bore size error | Press/loose fit issues | Affects mounting; if too loose, mounting eccentricity increases. |
| Bore roundness | Periodic $f_{pt}$ | The bore's out-of-roundness, when rotated, causes periodic radial displacement. |
| Face runout (TIR) | $F_\beta$ varying around gear | Face not perpendicular to bore → gear sits tilted → lead error varies sinusoidally. |
| OD-to-bore concentricity | Severe pitch + profile error | The pitch circle is concentric with the bore, but the addendum circle is not → addendum runout and tooth-depth variation. |
| Hardness variation | $f_f$ + tool wear | Inconsistent hardness causes differential cutting forces and tool deflection. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Not classifying the errors** into M-T-W-E; merely listing them as a paragraph. → **Lose 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Skipping the diagram** of the hob/gear cutting setup. → **Lose 2 marks.**
> 3. **Confusing $f_i'$ (tangential composite) with $f_i''$ (radial composite)** — the 14-mark question often tests this distinction. → **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 4. **Not mentioning workpiece errors** (only discussing tool and machine) — a classic KTU trap. → **Lose 3 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting units** ($\mu m$ vs $mm$) in numerical sub-parts. → **Lose 1 mark.**

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**(a)** With the help of suitable sketches, explain how **mounting eccentricity** and **workpiece face runout** act as sources of errors in gear manufacturing. Derive the relationship between mounting eccentricity and the resulting double-flank composite error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spur gear has **$z = 40$ teeth**, **module $m = 5$ mm**, **pressure angle $\alpha = 20°$**. It is mounted on an arbor with a **mounting eccentricity of $e = 15\ \mu m$**. Calculate:
  - (i) The radial runout $f_r$.
  - (ii) The double-flank composite error $f_i''$.
  - (iii) The maximum tooth thickness variation $\Delta s_{\max}$.
  - (iv) The pitch circle radius $R$.
**(7 marks)**

#### Solution to (a) — Mounting Eccentricity and Face Runout (7 marks)

**Mounting Eccentricity (3.5 marks):**

When a gear is mounted on an arbor using a clearance fit, the centre of the bore $C$ is displaced from the rotational axis $O$ of the arbor by an amount $e$ (the mounting eccentricity). The pitch circle (which is geometrically concentric with the bore) thus rotates about an axis that is offset by $e$ from its own centre.

**Mathematical Treatment:** (Refer to Section 3.1 derivation)

The radial distance of any point $P$ on the pitch circle from the true axis $O$ is:
$$r(\theta) = R + e\cos\theta$$

The **total radial runout** is the difference between maximum and minimum:
$$f_r = r_{\max} - r_{\min} = 2e$$

In a **double-flank composite test**, the contact shifts on both flanks as the gear rotates, so the measured total error is:
$$f_i'' = 4e$$

**Workpiece Face Runout (3.5 marks):**

The face of the gear blank may not be perfectly perpendicular to the bore axis. This is quantified as face Total Indicator Reading (TIR). When the blank is mounted on the arbor, this face runout causes the gear's axis to wobble or sit at a tilt.

**Effects:**
- Causes the **helix angle** (or the lead direction) to vary sinusoidally around the gear → produces a **lead error** $F_\beta$ that varies tooth-to-tooth.
- Creates **profile error** $f_f$ that varies around the gear.
- The face runout TIR directly adds to the radial runout, increasing the composite error:
$$f_i'' = 4e + 2 \cdot f_{\text{face runout}} \cdot \tan\beta$$
where $\beta$ is the helix angle (for spur gears, $\beta = 0$, so face runout only affects lead and profile).

#### Solution to (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $z = 40$ teeth
- $m = 5$ mm
- $\alpha = 20°$
- $e = 15\ \mu m = 0.015$ mm

**Calculations:**

**(i) Pitch Circle Radius $R$:**
$$R = \frac{m \cdot z}{2} = \frac{5 \times 40}{2} = 100\ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Radial Runout $f_r$:**
$$f_r = 2e = 2 \times 15\ \mu m = 30\ \mu m = 0.030\ \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Double-Flank Composite Error $f_i''$:**
$$f_i'' = 4e = 4 \times 15\ \mu m = 60\ \mu m = 0.060\ \text{mm}$$

**(iv) Maximum Tooth Thickness Variation $\Delta s_{\max}$:**
$$\Delta s_{\max} = e \cdot \tan\alpha = 15 \times \tan 20° = 15 \times 0.3640 = 5.46\ \mu m$$

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|----------|--------|-------|------|
| Pitch Circle Radius | $R$ | 100 | mm |
| Radial Runout | $f_r$ | 30 | $\mu m$ |
| Double-Flank Composite Error | $f_i''$ | 60 | $\mu m$ |
| Max Tooth Thickness Variation | $\Delta s_{\max}$ | 5.46 | $\mu m$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students use $e$ in mm directly without converting to $\mu m$ (or vice-versa). Always state the unit conversion explicitly: $15\ \mu m = 0.015\ mm$. The standard KTU answer format requires units in every line.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

- [x] **M-T-W-E Mnemonic** — Machine, Tool, Workpiece, Environment — every gear error traces to one of these four.
- [x] **Four ISO error symbols** must be memorised: $f_{pt}$, $F_p$, $f_f$, $F_\beta$.
- [x] **The 4× Amplification Rule:** $f_i'' = 4e$ for double-flank composite error due to mounting eccentricity. This is the **single most-tested** formula.
- [x] **Tooth thickness variation** due to eccentricity: $\Delta s_{\max} = e \tan\alpha$.
- [x] **Pitch circle radius:** $R = mz/2$ — fundamental for any numerical.
- [x] **Cumulative pitch error** scales as $z^2$ — high-tooth-count gears demand ultra-precise indexing.
- [x] **Hob profile error** is replicated on every tooth of the gear (uniform error) — distinguishes it from spindle runout (sinusoidal tooth-frequency error).
- [x] **Face runout** primarily causes **lead error** $F_\beta$, not pitch error.
- [x] **Centre height error** is a classic cause of **involute profile error** $f_f$.
- [x] **Units trap:** Always quote in $\mu m$ for ISO-grade analysis; convert explicitly.
- [x] **Composite errors** integrate all M-T-W-E sources: $f_i'$ (tangential) and $f_i''$ (radial) are the **catch-all** measurements.
- [x] **Quality grades:** ISO Q3 = aerospace; Q5–Q7 = automotive; Q9–Q12 = industrial (lower number = better).
- [x] **KTU-favoured answer structure:** Definition → Classification Diagram → M-T-W-E explanation → Formulas → Conclusion. Always include a labelled diagram — it secures 2 marks even if the text is weak.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Hook:** *"Mount on a tilted cake in a hot vibrating kitchen with a worn-out knife on a shaky table."* — Machine (shaky table), Tool (worn knife), Workpiece (tilted cake), Environment (hot vibrating kitchen). Cover the M-T-W-E and you'll cover all sources of errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Tooth Thickness: Gear Tooth Vernier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Tooth Thickness: Gear Tooth Vernier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gear Tooth Vernier (Gear Tooth Caliper):** A precision linear measuring instrument, equipped with two mutually perpendicular vernier scales, specifically designed to measure the **chordal tooth thickness** of a gear tooth at the **pitch circle** and the **addendum chord height** (the perpendicular distance from the top of the tooth to the pitch-circle chord). It is the standard KTU-recommended direct measuring instrument for verifying tooth thickness conformity during gear manufacturing and quality inspection.

The instrument complies with **IS 2707 / ISO 1328** measurement practice for involute spur gears operating on the constant chord principle.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Plain English" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pizza Slice Caliper:** Imagine slicing a pizza. The *crust arc length* at the rim is the **arc thickness** (a curved measurement along the pitch circle). But a measuring tape cannot easily follow that arc. So instead, you take a **straight-line ruler** and measure the *straight chord across the crust* — this is the **chordal tooth thickness (W)**. The **Gear Tooth Vernier** is essentially that "ruler," but instrumented with vernier slides and aligned at the exact pitch-circle height where every tooth must have a specific standardized width.

In real engineering, manufacturers don't measure the *arc* because:
1. The arc is mathematically inconvenient (involute curvature).
2. A chord measurement taken consistently at the *pitch circle* gives a **repeatable**, **calibration-friendly** value.
3. Quality control inspectors can verify thousands of gears quickly with this simple two-jaw device.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Parameters (KTU 2024 Notation)

- **Module (m)** — the fundamental gear size parameter, in **mm**.
- **Number of Teeth (z)** — total teeth on the gear.
- **Pitch Circle Diameter (D)** = $m \times z$, in **mm**.
- **Pressure Angle ($\phi$)** — typically **20°** for standard ISO gears.
- **Addendum (a)** = **1 × m** (standard full-depth teeth).
- **Dedendum (b)** = **1.25 × m**.
- **Chordal Tooth Thickness (W)** — measured value at pitch circle.
- **Addendum Chord Height (h)** — vertical distance from tooth tip to chord.
- **Circular Pitch (p$_c$)** = $\pi \times m$, in **mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All KTU numerical problems assume **20° pressure angle**, **full-depth involute system**, unless explicitly stated otherwise in the question stem.

## 1.4 Why Chordal Thickness (and not Arc Thickness)?

| Property | Arc Thickness | Chordal Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| Geometric nature | Curved (along pitch circle) | Straight-line (chord) |
| Measurement difficulty | Cannot be measured directly | Measured directly by vernier jaws |
| Repeatability | Operator-dependent | High (jaws seat on flanks) |
| KTU Exam standard | Theoretical only | **Measured & Computed** |

This dual advantage makes the gear tooth vernier the **single most important handheld inspection tool** in a gear production shop.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Gear Tooth Vernier

The instrument has **four essential components**:

1. **Beam (Main Scale)** — a precision-ground rectangular bar carrying the **horizontal main scale** graduated in **mm**. This scale reads the **addendum chord height (h)** through a fixed vertical reference edge.
2. **Sliding Jaw (Horizontal Vernier)** — slides along the beam and carries the **horizontal vernier scale** (typically reading **0.02 mm**). This jaw reads the **chordal tooth thickness (W)**.
3. **Vertical Slide (Depth Gauge Block)** — moves perpendicular to the beam, mounted on a sliding carrier. It carries the **vertical main and vernier scales** to read tooth-tip height.
4. **Contact Anvils** — two precisely machined jaws — one **fixed**, one **movable** — that contact the involute flanks of the tooth.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The two vernier readings correspond to *two different physical quantities*: the **horizontal vernier** = tooth thickness (W), the **vertical vernier** = tooth depth / addendum chord (h). Many students lose marks by confusing them.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Tooth Engagement:** The two jaws straddle a single tooth. The anvils seat against the **involute flank surfaces** on either side of the tooth at the pitch circle height.

**Step 2 — Horizontal Reading:** The horizontal vernier (toothed thickness scale) directly indicates the **chordal tooth thickness W** in mm (e.g., $W = 15.36$ mm).

**Step 3 — Vertical Reading:** The vertical vernier indicates the **addendum chord height h** — the height from the top land of the tooth down to the chord between the two contact points on the involute flanks.

**Step 4 — Comparison:** These measured values are compared against the **theoretical calculated values** obtained from gear geometry (formulas in §2.3).

**Step 5 — Acceptance Decision:** If W (measured) lies within the **tolerance band** ($\pm$ tolerance as per ISO 1328 grade), the tooth is accepted. Otherwise, the gear is rejected.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. Every KTU gear tooth vernier problem requires at least 2 of these formulas.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $D = m \cdot z$ | mm | Base formula |
| 2 | Pitch Circle Radius | $R = D/2 = mz/2$ | mm | Half of D |
| 3 | Circular Pitch | $p_c = \pi m$ | mm | Arc between adjacent teeth |
| 4 | Arc Tooth Thickness (theoretical) | $t_a = \dfrac{\pi m}{2}$ | mm | At pitch circle, half of circular pitch |
| 5 | **Chordal Tooth Thickness** | $W = D \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) = mz \sin\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2z}\right)$ | mm | **Main KTU formula** |
| 6 | **Addendum Chord Height** | $h = R\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right)\right] = \dfrac{mz}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2z}\right)\right]$ | mm | Vertical reading on vernier |
| 7 | Constant Chord (alternative method) | $W_c = m \cos\phi \cdot \cos(\text{inv}^{-1}\dots)$ | mm | Not commonly asked at KTU UG |
| 8 | Half-Chord Triangle | $W/2 = R \sin(90^\circ/z)$ | mm | Right-triangle geometry |
| 9 | Tolerance (ISO 1328 Grade 8) | $\pm\, 0.04\,m + 0.04\sqrt{D}$ | mm | Acceptable W deviation |
| 10 | Module from chord & z | $m = \dfrac{W}{z \sin(90^\circ/z)}$ | mm | Reverse calculation |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, the absolute-value bars and slash fractions have been replaced with parentheses and $\frac{}{\ }$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax — but the **mathematical values are identical**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

- **Automotive gearboxes:** Every gear tooth is inspected before assembly to prevent NVH (noise, vibration, harshness) issues.
- **Aerospace transmissions:** Tooth thickness deviation directly affects contact ratio and load distribution.
- **Mass production:** A gear tooth vernier can inspect 500+ teeth/hour — far faster than coordinate measuring machines (CMMs).
- **Field inspection:** Portable, no power supply required, used on shop floors and assembly lines.
- **Statistical Process Control (SPC):** W readings feed into $\bar{X}$-R control charts for gear manufacturing lines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chordal Tooth Thickness (W)

### Geometric Setup
Consider a single gear tooth on the pitch circle of diameter **D**. The tooth subtends an angle **2α** at the gear center, where:

$$\alpha = \dfrac{360^\circ}{2z} = \dfrac{180^\circ}{z}$$

So half the tooth angle is:

$$\dfrac{\alpha}{2} = \dfrac{90^\circ}{z}$$

### Right Triangle Construction
Drop a perpendicular from the gear center **O** to the tooth thickness chord **AB**. This perpendicular bisects the chord (a property of isoceles triangles formed by radii).

Let:
- $OA = OB = R = D/2$ (pitch radius)
- $AB = W$ (chordal thickness)
- Half-chord $= W/2$
- Half-angle at O $= 90^\circ/z$

From right-triangle trigonometry:

$$\sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) = \dfrac{W/2}{R}$$

### Step-by-Step Algebraic Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) &= \dfrac{W/2}{R} \\[6pt]
W/2 &= R \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \\[6pt]
W &= 2R \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \\[6pt]
W &= 2 \cdot \dfrac{D}{2} \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \\[6pt]
W &= D \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \\[6pt]
W &= mz \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \quad \text{(since } D = mz\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **master KTU formula for chordal tooth thickness**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Addendum Chord Height (h)

### Geometric Setup (continuing from §3.1)
From the same isoceles triangle, the perpendicular from **O** to chord **AB** has length:

$$\text{Apothem} = R \cdot \cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right)$$

This apothem extends from the gear center to the **chord line**. The **addendum chord height (h)** is the distance from this chord line UP to the tip of the tooth, which lies at radius $R_a = R + a = R + m$ (addendum circle radius).

Therefore:

$$h = R_a - R\cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right)$$

### Standard Simplification
For ISO standard gears, the tooth tip is at the **addendum circle**, but for the **chord at the pitch circle**, KTU uses the standard approximation where $h$ is measured from the tooth tip to the **pitch-circle chord**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= R - R\cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \\[6pt]
h &= R\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
h &= \dfrac{mz}{2}\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right)\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks use the variant $h = R_a - R\cos(90°/z)$ where $R_a = R + m$. Both forms are accepted at KTU — read the question stem carefully to identify which reference the examiner wants.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
A spur gear has **module $m = 6$ mm** and **number of teeth $z = 24$**. Calculate:
(a) The chordal tooth thickness **W**.
(b) The addendum chord height **h**.

### Solution — Part (a): Chordal Tooth Thickness W

Given: $m = 6$ mm, $z = 24$ teeth.

**Step 1 — Compute pitch circle diameter:**

$$D = m \cdot z = 6 \times 24 = 144 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the half-tooth-angle:**

$$\dfrac{90^\circ}{z} = \dfrac{90^\circ}{24} = 3.75^\circ$$

**Step 3 — Convert to radians (for calculator):**

$$\dfrac{\pi}{2z} = \dfrac{\pi}{48} = 0.06545 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chordal thickness formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= D \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right) \\[6pt]
W &= 144 \times \sin(3.75^\circ) \\[6pt]
W &= 144 \times 0.06540 \\[6pt]
W &= 9.418 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{rounded to } 9.42 \text{ mm})
\end{aligned}
$$

### Solution — Part (b): Addendum Chord Height h

**Step 5 — Compute pitch radius:**

$$R = D/2 = 144/2 = 72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the chord height formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= R\!\left[1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{90^\circ}{z}\right)\right] \\[6pt]
h &= 72 \times \left[1 - \cos(3.75^\circ)\right] \\[6pt]
h &= 72 \times \left[1 - 0.99786\right] \\[6pt]
h &= 72 \times 0.00214 \\[6pt]
h &= 0.154 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Answer

> **W = 9.42 mm** and **h = 0.154 mm**

### Cross-Check (Inverse Method)
Verify using $h = R - \sqrt{R^2 - (W/2)^2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 72 - \sqrt{72^2 - (9.418/2)^2} \\[6pt]
h &= 72 - \sqrt{5184 - 22.18} \\[6pt]
h &= 72 - \sqrt{5161.82} \\[6pt]
h &= 72 - 71.846 \\[6pt]
h &= 0.154 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods agree — calculation is verified.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Variations

| Problem Type | Given | To Find | Key Formula Pair |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type 1 | $m, z$ | $W, h$ | $W = mz\sin(90°/z)$; $h = (mz/2)[1-\cos(90°/z)]$ |
| Type 2 | $W, z$ | $m$ | $m = W/[z\sin(90°/z)]$ |
| Type 3 | $W, h, z$ | Verify $D$ | $D = W/\sin(90°/z)$; check $h$ against formula |
| Type 4 | $D, h$ | $z, m$ | Solve $z$ from $h = (mz/2)[1-\cos(90°/z)]$ numerically |
| Type 5 | Tolerance band | Acceptance | Compare measured $W$ vs. ISO 1328 grade limit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Gear Tooth Vernier

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gear Tooth Under Test] --> B[Fixed Anvil Left Flank Contact]
    A --> C[Movable Anvil Right Flank Contact]
    
    B --> D[Horizontal Vernier Slider]
    C --> D
    
    D --> E[Chordal Thickness W Reading - mm]
    
    A --> F[Vertical Depth Probe]
    F --> G[Vertical Vernier Slide]
    G --> H[Addendum Chord Height h Reading - mm]
    
    E --> I[Computed Theoretical W]
    H --> J[Computed Theoretical h]
    
    I --> K[Comparison with ISO 1328 Tolerance]
    J --> K
    
    K --> L{Within Tolerance?}
    L -->|Yes| M[ACCEPT Gear Tooth]
    L -->|No| N[REJECT Gear Tooth]
    
    O[Module m] --> I
    P[Teeth Count z] --> I
    O --> J
    P --> J
```

## 4.2 Geometric Construction Diagram (Mermaid Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O((Gear Center O)) -->|Radius R| A[Pitch Circle Point A]
    O -->|Radius R| B[Pitch Circle Point B]
    A ---|Chord W| B
    O -->|Apothem| M[Midpoint of Chord M]
    M -.->|h| T[Tooth Tip T]
    
    style O fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T fill:#ff6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Legend of Geometry:**
- **O** = Gear center (origin)
- **A, B** = Pitch-circle points where jaws contact flanks
- **T** = Tooth tip (top land center)
- **M** = Midpoint of chord AB
- **R** = Pitch radius = $mz/2$
- **W** = Chordal thickness = AB
- **h** = Addendum chord height = MT

## 4.3 Instrument Component Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MainBeam["MAIN BEAM ASSEMBLY"]
        BM1[Horizontal Main Scale - mm graduations]
    end
    
    subgraph HorizontalSlider["HORIZONTAL VERNIER ASSEMBLY"]
        HV1[Movable Jaw with Vernier Scale]
        HV2[Locking Screw]
        HV3[Anvil Contact Face]
    end
    
    subgraph VerticalSlider["VERTICAL DEPTH ASSEMBLY"]
        VV1[Vertical Main Scale]
        VV2[Vertical Vernier Scale]
        VV3[Depth Probe Tip]
    end
    
    subgraph FixedJaw["FIXED JAW ASSEMBLY"]
        FJ1[Fixed Anvil Contact Face]
        FJ2[Beam Reference Edge]
    end
    
    MainBeam --- HorizontalSlider
    HorizontalSlider --- VerticalSlider
    MainBeam --- FixedJaw
    
    HV3 <-->|Engages gear tooth flanks| GearTooth((Gear Tooth))
    VV3 -->|Measures tip-to-chord height| GearTooth
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Interpretation:**
> - **Horizontal Vernier Reading** → Chordal Tooth Thickness **W** (in mm)
> - **Vertical Vernier Reading** → Addendum Chord Height **h** (in mm)
> - Always quote readings to the **least count** of the vernier (typically **0.02 mm**).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define chordal tooth thickness and addendum chord height. Why is the gear tooth vernier preferred over direct arc-thickness measurement in industrial inspection?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Chordal Tooth Thickness (W):** It is the straight-line distance measured at the pitch circle between the two involute flanks of a gear tooth, measured along a chord perpendicular to the tooth's radial centerline. Mathematically, $W = mz \sin(90°/z)$.

> **Addendum Chord Height (h):** It is the perpendicular distance from the top of the tooth (tip) to the chord joining the two flank contact points at the pitch circle. Mathematically, $h = (mz/2)[1 - \cos(90°/z)]$.

> **Why Preferred:** Arc thickness cannot be measured directly because the pitch circle follows the involute curvature. A chord can be measured by a simple two-anvil vernier. The gear tooth vernier gives a **direct, repeatable, and calibration-friendly** measurement without needing complex CMM setups. *(1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for justification.)*

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: A gear tooth vernier has a main scale graduated in mm and a vernier with 50 divisions matching 49 mm. What is its least count? If it reads 12.00 mm on the horizontal scale, what is the measured chordal thickness?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Least count calculation:**

$$\text{Least Count} = \dfrac{\text{Smallest main scale division} - \text{Smallest vernier division}}{1}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{LC} = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{50} = 0.02 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation of reading:**
A direct reading of **12.00 mm** means the vernier zero aligns exactly with the 12-mm main scale mark.

$$\therefore W = 12.00 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Note on least count application:**
Any non-coincident vernier reading would be expressed as: Main Scale Reading + (Coinciding Vernier Division × LC), e.g., $12.00 + (24 \times 0.02) = 12.48$ mm. *(1 mark for the methodology explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Compulsory Choice A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working principle of a Gear Tooth Vernier. A spur gear has 40 teeth and a module of 4 mm. Determine the chordal tooth thickness and addendum chord height. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**

1. **Beam (Main Scale):** A rectangular, hardened-steel rule graduated in mm. Carries the horizontal main scale.
2. **Fixed Jaw:** Permanently attached at one end of the beam. One anvil is fixed.
3. **Movable Jaw with Vernier:** Slides along the beam, carries the horizontal vernier (LC = 0.02 mm typically). Second anvil is on this jaw.
4. **Vertical Depth Slide:** Mounted on a carriage, carries a vertical main scale and vernier, with a depth probe at the bottom.

**Working (3 marks):**

1. The tooth to be measured is placed between the two anvils.
2. The anvils seat against the involute flanks at the pitch circle.
3. The horizontal vernier reading directly gives **W**.
4. The vertical vernier reading gives **h**.
5. These are compared against theoretical values from gear geometry to assess quality.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $z = 40$ teeth, $m = 4$ mm.

**Step 1 — Pitch Circle Diameter:**

$$D = m \cdot z = 4 \times 40 = 160 \text{ mm} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 2 — Half-Tooth Angle:**

$$\dfrac{90^\circ}{z} = \dfrac{90^\circ}{40} = 2.25^\circ \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 3 — Chordal Tooth Thickness:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= D \cdot \sin(2.25^\circ) \\[4pt]
W &= 160 \times 0.03927 \\[4pt]
W &= 6.283 \text{ mm} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Addendum Chord Height:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= R\!\left[1 - \cos(2.25^\circ)\right] \\[4pt]
R &= D/2 = 80 \text{ mm} \\[4pt]
h &= 80 \times \left[1 - 0.99923\right] \\[4pt]
h &= 80 \times 0.000772 \\[4pt]
h &= 0.0617 \text{ mm} \quad (2 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{W = 6.283 \text{ mm}, \quad h = 0.0617 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Explain the principle of chordal tooth thickness measurement. The measured chordal thickness of a gear tooth is 7.85 mm. The gear has 30 teeth and module 5 mm. Check whether the tooth conforms to the theoretical specification, and compute the deviation in percentage. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Principle Explanation (7 marks)

The chordal tooth thickness is measured at the **pitch circle** because:

1. **Pitch circle is the theoretical reference** where tooth thickness and tooth space are equal (for standard gears).
2. **The involute flanks are tangent to the base circle** — hence at the pitch circle, the contact geometry is well-defined for transmission of motion.
3. The measurement chord is taken perpendicular to the tooth's radial centerline so that the flanks are symmetrically contacted.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

- Step 1: Place the tooth between the two vernier anvils.
- Step 2: Ensure anvils contact the involute flanks, not the tip or root.
- Step 3: Read **W** from the horizontal vernier.
- Step 4: Compare against the theoretical value calculated from the gear's module and tooth count.
- Step 5: The deviation indicates whether the tooth has been correctly machined.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Conformity Check (7 marks)

**Given:** Measured $W_{meas} = 7.85$ mm, $z = 30$, $m = 5$ mm.

**Step 1 — Theoretical Chordal Thickness:**

$$D = m \cdot z = 5 \times 30 = 150 \text{ mm} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$\dfrac{90^\circ}{z} = \dfrac{90^\circ}{30} = 3^\circ \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{theory} &= D \cdot \sin(3^\circ) \\[4pt]
W_{theory} &= 150 \times 0.05234 \\[4pt]
W_{theory} &= 7.851 \text{ mm} \quad (3 \text{ marks})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Deviation Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta W &= W_{meas} - W_{theory} \\[4pt]
\Delta W &= 7.85 - 7.851 \\[4pt]
\Delta W &= -0.001 \text{ mm} \quad (1 \text{ mark})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Deviation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Deviation} &= \dfrac{\Delta W}{W_{theory}} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{-0.001}{7.851} \times 100 \\[4pt]
&= -0.0127\% \quad (1 \text{ mark})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** The deviation is only $-0.0127\%$, well within the manufacturing tolerance band (typically $\pm 0.05\%$ for ISO Grade 8 gears). **The tooth CONFORMS to specification.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing degrees vs radians:** Always verify whether your calculator is in DEG mode before pressing `sin`. A common student error yields $W = 5.16$ mm instead of $7.85$ mm when radian mode is accidentally left on.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve the angle:** Use $90°/z$, **NOT** $180°/z$. The half-tooth-angle formula is the most-skipped step.
> 3. **Confusing W with arc thickness:** $W \neq \pi m/2$. The arc thickness equals $\pi m/2$ but the **chord** is shorter by a factor of $\sin(90°/z)$.
> 4. **Missing the vernier least count application:** If a reading is given as "main scale 12, vernier coinciding at 24," the answer is $12.00 + (24 \times 0.02) = 12.48$ mm. Students often write just 12 mm.
> 5. **No units in final answer:** Always append **mm** to every numerical answer.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch:** A neat diagram of the gear tooth geometry is worth **2 marks** in a 14-mark question. Always draw the center O, pitch radius R, chord W, and chord height h.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Gear Tooth Vernier (Module 2)**

### Key Concepts
- [x] Gear Tooth Vernier measures **chordal thickness W** and **addendum chord height h**.
- [x] Two perpendicular verniers: **horizontal** = W, **vertical** = h.
- [x] Pitch circle is the reference for measurement (not tip, not root).
- [x] Standard pressure angle = **20°**, full-depth teeth unless specified.

### Must-Memorize Formulas
- [x] $D = mz$
- [x] $W = mz \sin(90°/z)$ ← **Master formula**
- [x] $h = (mz/2)[1 - \cos(90°/z)]$
- [x] Arc thickness = $\pi m/2$ (for reference only)

### Construction Components
- [x] Beam + horizontal main scale
- [x] Movable jaw + horizontal vernier
- [x] Vertical depth slide + vertical vernier
- [x] Two contact anvils (fixed + movable)

### Numerical Problem Workflow
1. Compute $D = mz$.
2. Compute half-angle = $90°/z$.
3. Apply sine (for W) and cosine (for h).
4. Quote to **2 decimal places** in **mm**.

### Industrial Relevance
- [x] Used in gear QC labs worldwide.
- [x] Complies with **ISO 1328** accuracy grades.
- [x] Faster than CMM for high-volume inspection.

### Common KTU Errors to Avoid
- [x] Degree/Radian calculator mode confusion.
- [x] Using $180°/z$ instead of $90°/z$.
- [x] Confusing chord with arc.
- [x] Omitting the sketch in 14-mark answers.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"At the pitch circle, divide by z, take sine of 90/z, multiply by D — that's W!"*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constant Chord Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constant Chord Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Constant Chord Method** is a graphical–analytical technique used for the quantitative assessment of **surface roughness (texture)** from a magnified two-dimensional surface profile. In this method, the surface profile trace is divided into a series of **equal horizontal segments (chords of constant length)** drawn parallel to the mean reference line. Within each chord, the **peak-to-valley height (hᵢ)** is measured, and the **arithmetic mean** of these heights over the entire assessment length is taken as the representative roughness value of the surface.

It is classified under the **"M-System" type of roughness measurement** in classical metrology literature, as described in **IS 3073:1992** and **ISO 4287:1997** equivalents. The reference line is generally taken as a straight line passing through the **highest peak** of the profile and parallel to the general direction of the trace (a horizontal "ceiling" line through the crest of the profile).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **Analogy — Measuring the "Roughness" of a Mountain Range:**
>
> Imagine you are standing far away from a mountain range and tracing its silhouette on a transparent sheet. Now, instead of trying to find an "average" line through the middle of the trace (which is what the Mean Line Method does), you simply draw a horizontal ruler (a chord) of fixed length across the silhouette, slide it horizontally step-by-step, and at each position measure the **vertical drop from the highest point to the lowest point** within that horizontal window.
>
> You repeat this for a fixed number of equal-width windows that cover the whole mountain trace. The **average of all those vertical drops** is your "roughness score" — that is exactly the Constant Chord Method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Constant Chord Method is one of the **three classical graphical methods** of roughness assessment taught in KTU Module 2, alongside the **Mean Line (CLA / Ra) Method** and the **Envelope Method**. It is a *quick, no-instrumentation-required* technique that can be performed on a **Talysurf / Profilometer trace** using a transparent overlay.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Vocabulary)

- **Chord (C):** A fixed horizontal length drawn parallel to the mean line; its length is predetermined and constant for a given measurement.
- **Peak-to-Valley Height (hᵢ):** The vertical distance between the highest crest and the lowest trough lying within the iᵗʰ chord.
- **Assessment Length (L):** The total length of the profile over which the average is computed. As per **IS 3073**, the assessment length is normally **five times the sampling length**.
- **Sampling Length (l):** The reference length over which the surface texture is evaluated to filter out waviness.
- **Reference Line:** A straight line parallel to the mean line, drawn at the level of the **highest peak** of the profile.

> [!NOTE]
> The chord length is typically taken as **one-fifth of the sampling length**, ensuring at least **5 chords** per sampling length and **25 chords** per assessment length — this is the **5-chord-per-sampling-length convention** used in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

The procedure for evaluating surface roughness by the Constant Chord Method follows a strict sequence, which KTU examiners frequently test in descriptive 7-mark questions:

1. **Obtain the magnified surface profile trace** from a stylus-type profilometer (e.g., Talysurf).
2. **Draw the mean reference line** (geometric center line of the profile) using the area-above = area-below criterion.
3. **Draw the Constant Chord Reference Line (CCRL)** — a straight line parallel to the mean line that just *touches the highest peak* of the profile.
4. **Select a fixed chord length `C`** as per IS 3073 (typically `C = l / 5`, where `l` is the sampling length).
5. **Mark off successive chords** of length `C` along the entire assessment length `L`, all parallel to the mean line.
6. **Within each chord**, identify the highest crest and the lowest trough; record the peak-to-valley height `hᵢ` for the iᵗʰ chord.
7. **Compute the arithmetic mean** of all `n` peak-to-valley heights.
8. **Report the result** as the **Constant Chord Roughness Value, R₍c₎**, in micrometres (μm).

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Method

- **Why a chord and not the mean line?** The chord acts as a fixed "ceiling" of reference. By anchoring the reference at the highest peak (rather than at the true mean), the method **inflates** the measured heights slightly, but it provides a quick, **graphically intuitive** value that is reproducible without needing to compute the precise mean line area-balance.
- **Why average multiple chords?** Averaging cancels out localized irregularities and gives a statistically stable single-number descriptor of the texture.
- **Why fixed chord length?** A constant chord ensures that the *spatial resolution* of the measurement is uniform across the profile, making the result independent of where the profile happens to have tall or short peaks.

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- Used in **shop-floor inspection** where quick roughness estimates are needed without sophisticated electronic profilometers.
- Applied in **comparative surface-finish grading** during acceptance testing of machined parts.
- Serves as a **teaching/reference technique** in metrology labs to introduce students to graphical surface assessment.
- Particularly useful for **rough and coarse surfaces** (Ra > 6.3 μm) where the chord-based sampling is more practical than fine stylus-based averaging.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Constant Chord Roughness Value | $R_c = \dfrac{1}{n} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i$ | Arithmetic mean of all peak-to-valley heights `hᵢ` over `n` chords. |
| 2 | Total Assessment Length | $L = 5 \times l$ | As per IS 3073, assessment length = 5 × sampling length. |
| 3 | Chord Length | $C = \dfrac{l}{5}$ | Each chord is one-fifth of the sampling length. |
| 4 | Number of Chords per Assessment Length | $n = \dfrac{L}{C} = 25$ | Standard KTU/IS value for a fully compliant measurement. |
| 5 | Standard Sampling Lengths (l) | $0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0, 25.0$ mm | Selected based on the **Ra class** of the surface (IS 3073 table). |
| 6 | Relation to Rz (10-point height) | $R_c \approx R_z$ (approximate) | For nominally regular profiles, R₍c₎ approaches the 10-point mean roughness. |
| 7 | Unit | $\mu m$ (micrometre) | Always express roughness in **μm** unless stated otherwise. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Many students mistakenly use the **mean line** as the reference inside each chord. The correct reference is the **horizontal line through the highest peak** (the Constant Chord Reference Line), not the mean line.

### 2.5 Comparison with Sister Methods (High-Yield KTU Table)

| Aspect | Constant Chord Method | Mean Line Method (CLA / Ra) | Envelope Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference Line | Through **highest peak** ∥ to mean | True **mean line** (area balance) | Two lines **tangent to peaks & valleys** |
| Height Measured | Peak-to-valley per chord | Deviation from mean | Mean of 5 highest peaks − 5 lowest valleys |
| Effort | Lowest (graphical) | Moderate (graphical/electronic) | Highest (multiple tangent lines) |
| Result Magnitude | Higher than CLA | Lowest | Highest |
| KTU 2024 Marks Weightage | High (frequently in 7-mark Qs) | Very High (industry standard) | Moderate |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Constant Chord Roughness Formula

Starting from the definition, let the surface profile be divided into `n` equal chords of constant horizontal length `C`. In each chord `i`, the **peak-to-valley height** `hᵢ` is measured vertically as the distance between the highest crest and the lowest trough enclosed within that chord.

$$
h_i = y_{\text{peak},i} - y_{\text{valley},i} \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, n
$$

where:
- $y_{\text{peak},i}$ = vertical ordinate of the highest peak inside chord `i`
- $y_{\text{valley},i}$ = vertical ordinate of the lowest valley inside chord `i`

By the **arithmetic-mean definition** of a roughness parameter:

$$
R_c = \frac{h_1 + h_2 + h_3 + \dots + h_n}{n}
$$

Using summation (sigma) notation, which is the standard form expected in KTU answer scripts:

$$
R_c = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i
$$

Substituting the explicit form of each `hᵢ`:

$$
R_c = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( y_{\text{peak},i} - y_{\text{valley},i} \right)
$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** of the Constant Chord Method.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
The magnified surface profile of a turned mild-steel shaft is divided into 10 equal chords of constant length 2 mm. The peak-to-valley heights `hᵢ` (in μm) measured within each chord are recorded as follows:

$$
h_1 = 12, \quad h_2 = 18, \quad h_3 = 15, \quad h_4 = 22, \quad h_5 = 16, \quad h_6 = 20, \quad h_7 = 14, \quad h_8 = 19, \quad h_9 = 17, \quad h_{10} = 21
$$

Determine the **Constant Chord Roughness Value (R_c)** in μm. Also report the assessment length and verify whether the sampling length complies with **IS 3073** for a typical machined surface (Ra 0.8 – 1.6 μm class).

---

**Step 1 — Identify the number of chords `n`.**

$$
n = 10
$$

**Step 2 — Sum all the peak-to-valley heights.**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{10} h_i = 12 + 18 + 15 + 22 + 16 + 20 + 14 + 19 + 17 + 21
$$

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{10} h_i = 174 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Constant Chord Method formula.**

$$
R_c = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i = \frac{1}{10} \times 174
$$

$$
R_c = 17.4 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the assessment length and sampling length.**

Given chord length `C = 2 mm`, the total length covered by 10 chords is:

$$
L_{\text{covered}} = n \times C = 10 \times 2 = 20 \; \text{mm}
$$

For IS 3073 compliance, the assessment length should be `5 × sampling length`. For a machined surface in the Ra 0.8 – 1.6 μm class, the standard sampling length is **`l = 2.5 mm`**, so:

$$
L = 5 \times l = 5 \times 2.5 = 12.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

Since the covered length (20 mm) exceeds the standard assessment length (12.5 mm), the measurement is **acceptable** (it actually covers a bit more than the minimum required assessment length, which is conservative and acceptable).

**Step 5 — Final Result.**

$$
\boxed{R_c = 17.4 \; \mu m}
$$

### 3.3 Quick-Reference Analytical Matrix for Common Problem Patterns

| Problem Type | Knowns | To Find | Key Formula | KTU Marks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Direct computation | `hᵢ` values, `n` | `R_c` | $R_c = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum h_i$ | 3 – 7 |
| Reverse calculation | `R_c`, `n`, partial `hᵢ` | Missing `hᵢ` | $h_{\text{missing}} = n R_c - \sum h_i$ | 7 |
| Assessment-length check | `C`, `n`, surface class | Standard `l`, `L` | $L = 5 l$, $C = l/5$ | 7 – 14 |
| Comparison with Ra / Rz | `R_c` value | Approx. Ra / Rz | $R_c \approx R_z$, and $R_a \approx 0.4 R_z$ | 7 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Obtain magnified surface profile from Talysurf] --> B[Step 2: Draw mean reference line area balance criterion]
    B --> C[Step 3: Draw Constant Chord Reference Line through highest peak]
    C --> D[Step 4: Select chord length C equals l divided by 5]
    D --> E[Step 5: Mark off n equal chords along assessment length L]
    E --> F[Step 6: Measure peak to valley height hi in each chord]
    F --> G[Step 7: Sum all hi values]
    G --> H[Step 8: Compute Rc equals sum divided by n]
    H --> I[Step 9: Report Rc in micrometres and verify IS 3073 compliance]
    I --> J[Final Output: Constant Chord Roughness Value Rc]
```

### 4.2 Schematic Description (For Exam Sketches)

When drawing the surface profile diagram in a KTU exam answer, structure the figure as follows (imagine it as a landscape with the horizontal axis as distance and the vertical axis as amplified surface height):

1. **Bottom horizontal line** → represents the **mean reference line** (dotted/dashed).
2. **Top horizontal line** (slightly above the highest peak) → represents the **Constant Chord Reference Line** (solid).
3. **A series of irregular wave-like crests and troughs** between the two lines → represents the **surface profile trace**.
4. **Vertical double-arrow segments** within each chord → represent the measured `hᵢ` values.
5. **Small horizontal brackets** of equal length at the bottom → mark off the **chord divisions** of constant length `C`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always label the *highest peak* explicitly in the diagram and show the **Constant Chord Reference Line** as a *solid horizontal line* through that peak. Examiners award 1–2 marks for a clearly labeled, neat profile diagram.

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Choosing a Reference Length (IS 3073 Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Identify surface Ra class] --> Q{Class of Ra in micrometres}
    Q --> R1[Ra 0.1 to 0.2: l equals 0.08 mm]
    Q --> R2[Ra 0.2 to 0.4: l equals 0.25 mm]
    Q --> R3[Ra 0.4 to 0.8: l equals 0.8 mm]
    Q --> R4[Ra 0.8 to 1.6: l equals 2.5 mm]
    Q --> R5[Ra 1.6 to 3.2: l equals 8.0 mm]
    Q --> R6[Ra 3.2 to 12.5: l equals 25 mm]
    R1 --> S[Chord length C equals l divided by 5]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    R6 --> S
    S --> T[Number of chords n equals 25 over assessment length]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> *Define the Constant Chord Method of surface roughness measurement. Mention the formula used.*

**Model Answer:**
The Constant Chord Method is a graphical technique of assessing surface roughness in which the magnified surface profile is divided into a number of equal horizontal chord lengths parallel to the mean reference line. Within each chord, the peak-to-valley height `hᵢ` is measured vertically, and the average of these heights over the assessment length gives the **Constant Chord Roughness Value (R_c)**.

$$
R_c = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i
$$

where `n` is the number of chords (typically 25 over the assessment length). The reference line is taken through the **highest peak** of the profile, parallel to the mean line. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> *State two advantages and one limitation of the Constant Chord Method.*

**Model Answer:**
- **Advantage 1:** It is a simple, quick, and purely graphical method requiring no electronic computation — ideal for shop-floor inspection.
- **Advantage 2:** It can be performed on a *Talysurf trace* using only a transparent overlay and ruler.
- **Limitation:** It tends to *overestimate* the roughness value compared to the CLA (Ra) method because the reference is anchored at the highest peak rather than the true mean line, and it is *operator-dependent* in identifying crests and troughs. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]**
> *(a) [7 Marks]* Explain the step-by-step procedure of the Constant Chord Method for surface roughness measurement, with a neat labelled sketch of the profile.
>
> *(b) [7 Marks]* A surface profile trace is divided into 8 equal chords. The peak-to-valley heights measured (in μm) are: **5, 9, 7, 12, 8, 10, 6, 11**. Calculate the **Constant Chord Roughness Value (R_c)** and the **standard deviation** of the readings.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure and Sketch — 7 marks**

1. **Record the magnified profile** (typically 1000×–10000× vertical magnification) from a Talysurf profilometer. (1 mark)
2. **Draw the mean reference line** such that the area above the line equals the area below the line. (1 mark)
3. **Construct the Constant Chord Reference Line (CCRL)** — a horizontal line parallel to the mean line, drawn to *just touch the highest peak* of the profile. (1 mark)
4. **Select a fixed chord length `C`** as per IS 3073, i.e., $C = l / 5$, where `l` is the sampling length. (1 mark)
5. **Mark off `n` successive chords** of length `C` along the assessment length `L = 5 l`. (1 mark)
6. **In each chord, identify the maximum crest and the minimum trough**, and measure the vertical distance `hᵢ` between them. (1 mark)
7. **Compute** $R_c = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum h_i$ and report in μm. (1 mark)

*(Neat labelled sketch showing the mean line, CCRL, profile crests/troughs, chord divisions, and the measured `hᵢ` arrows — additional 1 mark allocated within the 7.)*

**(b) Numerical Computation — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Number of chords, $n = 8$.

**Step 2:** Sum of heights:

$$
\sum h_i = 5 + 9 + 7 + 12 + 8 + 10 + 6 + 11 = 68 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 3:** Constant Chord Roughness Value:

$$
R_c = \frac{1}{n} \sum h_i = \frac{68}{8} = 8.5 \; \mu m
$$

**Step 4:** Standard deviation. The standard deviation of a sample of size `n` with mean $R_c$ is given by:

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (h_i - R_c)^2}
$$

Compute the deviations and their squares:

| i | $h_i$ | $h_i - R_c$ | $(h_i - R_c)^2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 5  | -3.5 | 12.25 |
| 2 | 9  |  0.5 |  0.25 |
| 3 | 7  | -1.5 |  2.25 |
| 4 | 12 |  3.5 | 12.25 |
| 5 | 8  | -0.5 |  0.25 |
| 6 | 10 |  1.5 |  2.25 |
| 7 | 6  | -2.5 |  6.25 |
| 8 | 11 |  2.5 |  6.25 |
| **Σ** | — | — | **42.00** |

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{42.00}{8 - 1}} = \sqrt{\frac{42}{7}} = \sqrt{6} \approx 2.449 \; \mu m
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{R_c = 8.5 \; \mu m \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma \approx 2.45 \; \mu m}
$$

---

> **Q3(B). [KTU University Exam – May 2023 | KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern] — Alternative Choice**
> *(a) [7 Marks]* Compare the **Constant Chord Method**, **Mean Line Method (CLA)**, and **Envelope Method** of surface roughness assessment in a tabular form, highlighting the reference line, measured quantity, complexity, and relative magnitude of result.
>
> *(b) [7 Marks]* For a ground steel surface, the assessment length is 12.5 mm. If IS 3073 recommends a chord length of one-fifth the sampling length, determine the **sampling length**, the **chord length**, and the **number of chords** to be used. If the mean peak-to-valley height works out to 3.8 μm, compute the roughness value $R_c$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table — 7 marks**

*(The student is expected to reproduce the comparison table from Section 2.5 of these notes. Award 1.5 marks per correctly filled row × 4 rows + 1 mark for neatness.)*

| Aspect | Constant Chord | Mean Line (CLA) | Envelope |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference line | Through **highest peak** ∥ mean | True mean line (area balance) | Tangent to **peaks** AND **valleys** |
| Measured quantity | Peak-to-valley per chord | Deviation from mean | Mean of 5 highest peaks + 5 lowest valleys |
| Complexity | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| Result magnitude | Intermediate-to-high | Lowest | Highest |

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks**

**Step 1:** Given assessment length $L = 12.5 \; \text{mm}$.

By IS 3073:

$$
L = 5 \times l \quad \Rightarrow \quad l = \frac{L}{5} = \frac{12.5}{5} = 2.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2:** Chord length:

$$
C = \frac{l}{5} = \frac{2.5}{5} = 0.5 \; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 3:** Number of chords:

$$
n = \frac{L}{C} = \frac{12.5}{0.5} = 25
$$

**Step 4:** Roughness value $R_c$. Since the mean peak-to-valley height $\bar{h} = 3.8 \; \mu m$:

$$
R_c = \bar{h} = 3.8 \; \mu m
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{l = 2.5 \; \text{mm}, \quad C = 0.5 \; \text{mm}, \quad n = 25, \quad R_c = 3.8 \; \mu m}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Wrong Reference Line:** A *very* common mistake is using the **mean line** as the reference for measuring `hᵢ` in the Constant Chord Method. The correct reference is the **line through the highest peak** (CCRL). Marks deducted: 2 – 3 per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing Constant Chord with Envelope Method:** Students often confuse the **Constant Chord Method** (one horizontal reference line) with the **Envelope Method** (two tangent lines). Read the question carefully.
> 3. **Unit Errors:** Always express roughness in **μm**. Writing the answer in mm or m will cost 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the 5-Chord Convention:** When sampling length and chord length are not given, use $C = l/5$ and $n = 25$. Examiners often specify "as per IS 3073" precisely to test this convention.
> 5. **Incomplete Diagrams:** A diagram without a labelled **CCRL** and the measured `hᵢ` arrows loses 1–2 marks. A picture is worth a thousand words — *and* 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The Constant Chord Method divides the surface profile into **equal horizontal chord lengths** and averages the peak-to-valley heights.
- **Governing Formula:**

$$
R_c = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} h_i
$$

- **Reference Line:** A **horizontal line through the highest peak** of the profile (NOT the mean line). This is the *single most distinguishing feature* of the method.
- **IS 3073 Conventions:**
   - $C = l / 5$
   - $L = 5 l$
   - $n = 25$ chords per assessment length (standard)
- **Standard Sampling Lengths:** 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0, 25.0 mm — selected based on the Ra class of the surface.
- **Result Trend:** $R_a < R_c < R_{\text{envelope}}$ (CLA < Constant Chord < Envelope).
- **Units:** Always **μm**; convert carefully from mm if the profile data is given in mm.
- **Tools Needed:** Magnified profile (Talysurf), transparent overlay, ruler, marker.
- **Advantages:** Quick, graphical, no electronic computation, shop-floor friendly.
- **Limitations:** Overestimates roughness, operator-dependent, less precise than Ra from a profilometer.
- **Comparison Memory Hook:** *"Constant Chord = Constant Ceiling"* — the ceiling (reference) is constant and runs through the highest peak.
- **Exam-Ready Sketches:** Always include — mean line (dotted), CCRL (solid through highest peak), profile crests/troughs, and labelled `hᵢ` arrows.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addendum Comparator Method and Base Tangent Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Addendum Comparator Method

The **Addendum Comparator Method** (also known as the **Gear Tooth Vernier Caliper Method** or **Chordal Measurement Method**) is a widely used, non-destructive gear inspection technique employed on the shop floor for the direct measurement of a gear tooth's geometric parameters. The instrument used is called a **Gear Tooth Vernier Caliper** (essentially an addendum comparator), which has two perpendicular scales: a vertical scale for the **chordal addendum (h_c)** and a horizontal scale for the **chordal tooth thickness (W)** measured at the pitch circle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *The Addendum Comparator Method is a chordal measurement technique in which the linear distance between the two involute flanks of a tooth at the pitch circle (chordal tooth thickness, W) and the perpendicular distance from the tip of the tooth to this chord (chordal addendum, h_c) are measured using a gear tooth vernier caliper.*

### 1.1.1 Real-World Analogy
Imagine you are inspecting a comb with multiple teeth. To check whether each tooth is the correct "fatness" and "height", instead of trying to wrap a flexible measuring tape around each curved tooth (which is nearly impossible), you take a special L-shaped caliper: the vertical arm of the L measures how tall the tooth is from a chosen reference line, and the horizontal arm measures how wide the tooth is at that reference line. The addendum comparator does exactly this — but for involute gear teeth, with the reference being the **pitch circle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** The Addendum Comparator Method measures two parameters simultaneously: **W** (chordal thickness) and **h_c** (chordal addendum). It is the *most common production-line inspection tool* for spur and helical gears because it is fast, repeatable, and does not require expensive fixtures.

---

## 1.2 Base Tangent Method

The **Base Tangent Method** is a precision gear measurement technique that uses a special **base tangent micrometer** (or a vernier caliper with extended parallel jaws) to measure the **Base Tangent Length (T)** of a gear. The base tangent length is the linear distance between two parallel lines, each tangent to the **base circle** of the gear on the opposite flanks of the same tooth (or a group of teeth).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** *The Base Tangent Method determines gear tooth thickness by measuring the distance over two parallel planes (or jaws) which are tangential to the base circle of a gear on opposite flanks of a tooth. This measured distance, the base tangent length, is mathematically related to the module, number of teeth, pressure angle, and the addendum modification coefficient.*

### 1.2.1 Real-World Analogy
Picture a gear as a circular dinner plate. The **base circle** is an imaginary smaller circle (concentric with the gear) inscribed within the tooth profile. Now imagine placing the gear between two perfectly straight, vertical metal plates. As you slide the plates inward, they will first touch the gear not at the tooth tips, but at the base circle on the two sides of one tooth. The distance between the two plates at this moment is the **Base Tangent Length**. If the teeth are too thick (e.g., excessive profile shift), the plates will be further apart; if the teeth are too thin, the plates will be closer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** The Base Tangent Method is highly sensitive to **profile shift (x)** and **tooth thickness errors**. It is one of the most accurate methods for verifying the **effective tooth thickness** of precision-cut gears (e.g., for automotive gearboxes, aerospace gear drives). Unlike the addendum comparator, it is **independent of the tip diameter** (which is often affected by manufacturing variations like burrs or chamfers).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theory of the Addendum Comparator Method

### 2.1.1 Working Principle
A gear tooth involute is curved. To measure its "width" and "depth" meaningfully, we project the involute onto a flat reference plane — the **pitch circle**. The intersection of the two opposite involute flanks with the pitch circle defines two points. The straight-line distance between these points is the **chordal tooth thickness (W)**, and the perpendicular distance from the tooth tip to this chord is the **chordal addendum (h_c)**.

The instrument consists of:
- **Two vertical jaws (sliding horizontally):** Contact the two involute flanks at the pitch circle. The horizontal vernier reads **W**.
- **One vertical sliding jaw:** Rests on the tooth tip. The vertical vernier reads **h_c** (with the depth rod touching the bottom of the tooth space).

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Measurement Procedure
1. **Zero the instrument** on a master gear or a known reference standard.
2. **Position the caliper** over the tooth to be measured. The two vertical jaws must contact the involute flanks at the pitch circle.
3. **Read the horizontal scale** → This is the **chordal tooth thickness (W)**.
4. **Read the vertical scale** (with the depth rod touching the tooth root) → This is the **chordal addendum (h_c)**.
5. **Repeat for multiple teeth** (typically every 3rd or 5th tooth around the gear) to detect pitch variations and tooth-to-tooth errors.

### 2.1.3 Why Two Parameters?
- **W** is sensitive to **tooth thickness** (and thus to **profile shift x** and **circular pitch errors**).
- **h_c** is sensitive to **addendum (tip height)** and combined tooth-thickness errors.
- A faulty gear may show normal W but abnormal h_c (e.g., due to a chamfer or burr on the tip), or vice versa.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Addendum Comparator is the *workhorse of gear inspection* in mass production (automotive, agricultural machinery). It is fast, requires no master gear for comparative measurements, and gives direct numerical values for the two most critical quality parameters.

---

## 2.2 Theory of the Base Tangent Method

### 2.2.1 The Base Circle
Every involute gear has a **base circle** of radius **r_b**, where:
$$r_b = r \cdot \cos(\phi) = \frac{m \cdot K}{2} \cdot \cos(\phi)$$
The involute tooth profile *originates* from this base circle. Therefore, the base circle is the **fundamental reference** for all involute geometry.

### 2.2.2 Definition of Base Tangent Length
The **base tangent length T** is the perpendicular distance between two parallel lines, one touching the base circle on the **right flank** of a tooth, and the other touching the base circle on the **left flank** of the **same tooth**. Equivalently, the two tangent points are diametrically opposite on the base circle with respect to the tooth's center plane.

### 2.2.3 Why Use the Base Tangent?
- The base circle is **machined indirectly** (it is defined by the gear cutting tool's geometry), so the base tangent length reflects the **true involute form** rather than the tip geometry.
- The measurement is **insensitive to tip diameter variations** (chamfers, burrs, runout).
- It can be performed with a standard **external micrometer** (with **knife-edge or anvils** designed for gear measurement) or a **base tangent micrometer**.

### 2.2.4 Step-by-Step Measurement Procedure
1. Mount the gear on a fixture (between centers or on a mandrel).
2. Place the base tangent micrometer's two anvils across the gear so that each anvil touches one involute flank.
3. The anvils must be **parallel** and tangent to the base circle on opposite flanks of the tooth.
4. **Read the micrometer** → This is **T**.
5. **Measure several teeth** around the gear to determine the **base tangent length variation (ΔT)**, which indicates tooth-to-tooth spacing errors.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Base Tangent Method is preferred for **high-precision gears** (e.g., aircraft engine reduction gears, machine tool gearboxes) where tooth thickness must be controlled to within a few micrometers. It is also used to **verify the profile shift coefficient (x)** after heat treatment, when tip dimensions may have changed.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value symbols inside table cells. All formulas below assume an **uncorrected (x = 0)** or **profile-shifted (x ≠ 0)** spur gear with module **m**, number of teeth **K**, and pressure angle **φ** (in radians, unless otherwise specified).

### 2.3.1 Addendum Comparator Formulas

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Chordal tooth thickness | $W$ | $W = m \cdot \cos(\phi) \cdot \left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} + 2x \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]$ | mm | Independent of K |
| Chordal addendum | $h_c$ | $h_c = m \cdot (1 + x) - \dfrac{W}{2} \cdot \sin(\phi)$ | mm | Measured from tip to pitch chord |
| Standard addendum | $h_a$ | $h_a = m \cdot (1 + x)$ | mm | Total addendum |
| Pitch circle radius | $r$ | $r = \dfrac{m \cdot K}{2}$ | mm | Reference circle |

### 2.3.2 Base Tangent Formulas

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Base circle radius | $r_b$ | $r_b = \dfrac{m \cdot K}{2} \cdot \cos(\phi)$ | mm | $r_b = r \cdot \cos(\phi)$ |
| Base tangent length (standard) | $T$ | $T = \dfrac{\pi \cdot m \cdot K \cdot \cos(\phi)}{2}$ | mm | For $x = 0$ |
| Base tangent length (modified) | $T$ | $T = m \cdot \cos(\phi) \cdot \left[\dfrac{\pi \cdot K}{2} + 2x \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]$ | mm | General form |
| Tooth thickness (arc) at P.C. | $s$ | $s = m \cdot \left[\dfrac{\pi}{2} + 2x \cdot \tan(\phi)\right]$ | mm | Arc length, not chord |
| Involute function | $\text{inv}(\phi)$ | $\text{inv}(\phi) = \tan(\phi) - \phi$ | rad | φ in radians |

### 2.3.3 Key Derived Relationships

| From | To | Relationship |
|---|---|---|
| $W$ (chordal) | $s$ (arc) | $W = s \cdot \cos(\phi) = 2r \cdot \sin\!\left(\dfrac{s}{2r}\right)$ |
| $T$ | $s_b$ (tooth thickness at base circle) | $T \approx s_b$ for many teeth |
| $\Delta T$ | $x$ | $T = T_{std} + 2xm\sin(\phi)$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->
