> For greater accuracy, step over **10 threads** and divide the total reading by 10 to average local errors.

## 2.4 Method 2 — Floating Carriage Micrometer (Pitch Measuring Machine)

This is the **gold standard** for high-precision pitch measurement. The workpiece is mounted between centres. A **microscope** is mounted on a **floating carriage** that rides on precision guideways, free of friction play.

Procedure:
1. Align the cross-hair of the microscope with the **first thread crest**.
2. Move the carriage axially — the cross-hair is **clamped** when alignment is achieved.
3. Count the number of intervals traversed.
4. Read the **precision linear scale** integrated with the carriage.

## 2.5 Method 3 — Toolmaker's / Universal Measuring Microscope

Using **incident illumination**, the thread profile is magnified (typically ×30 or ×50). The cross-hair is aligned with the **image of the thread crest**, and the stage is translated. The **micrometer drum** of the stage gives the axial pitch with a resolution of **0.001 mm**.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Pitch–Lead relation | $L = P \times n$ | mm | n = number of starts |
| 2 | Pitch from threads per inch | $P = \dfrac{25.4}{n_{TPI}}$ | mm | n_TPI = threads per inch |
| 3 | Average pitch from N intervals | $P_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i}{k}$ | mm | k = number of readings |
| 4 | Pitch error | $\Delta P = P_{\text{measured}} - P_{\text{nominal}}$ | mm | Signed deviation |
| 5 | Tolerance band of pitch | $P_{\text{nominal}} - \Delta P_{max} \le P \le P_{\text{nominal}} + \Delta P_{max}$ | mm | For ISO threads |
| 6 | Optical measurement | $P_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{image}}}{M}$ | mm | M = magnification |
| 7 | Cumulative pitch error over N threads | $\Delta P_{cum} = P_{\text{measured over N threads}} - N \cdot P_{\text{nominal}}$ | mm | Functional gauge relevant |
| 8 | Progressive pitch error (slope) | $\dfrac{dP}{dx} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{cum}}{L_{total}}$ | mm/mm | For lead-screw error grading |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Pitch error analysis is critical in **CNC lead screws**, **ball screw assemblies**, and **aerospace fasteners** where pitch deviations cause positioning errors, vibration, and fatigue failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Pitch from Multi-Interval Measurement

When the instrument steps over **N** thread intervals, random surface noise (dirt, micro-form errors) inflates the variance of any single-interval reading. The averaging process is justified as follows.

Let $P_i$ be the $i^{th}$ single-interval pitch reading, and $P_{\text{true}}$ be the true unknown pitch. Each reading is modelled as:

$$P_i = P_{\text{true}} + \epsilon_i$$

where $\epsilon_i$ is a zero-mean random error with variance $\sigma^2$.

The **arithmetic mean** of k readings is:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} P_i = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} (P_{\text{true}} + \epsilon_i)$$

Splitting the summation:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{k} \left( k \cdot P_{\text{true}} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i \right)$$

$$\bar{P} = P_{\text{true}} + \frac{1}{k} \sum_{i=1}^{k} \epsilon_i$$

The variance of the mean:

$$\sigma_{\bar{P}}^{2} = \frac{\sigma^2}{k}$$

Hence, the standard deviation (uncertainty) of the mean is:

$$\sigma_{\bar{P}} = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{k}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Increasing the number of intervals k by 4× reduces the uncertainty in the average pitch by 2×. This is precisely why KTU lab manuals mandate a **10-thread traverse** rather than a 1-thread measurement.

## 3.2 Derivation: True Pitch from Optical Magnification

In a profile projector or toolmaker's microscope, the image of a thread is enlarged by magnification **M**. If the **axial distance between two thread crests in the image plane** is $D_{\text{image}}$, then the true pitch is:

$$P_{\text{true}} = \frac{D_{\text{image}}}{M}$$

**Worked Example (KTU Style):**
A profile projector at **M = 50×** displays a thread. The distance measured between 5 consecutive thread crests on the screen is **75 mm**. Find the true pitch.

Given: $D_{\text{image, over 5 threads}} = 75$ **mm**, $M = 50$.

Pitch of 5 threads:

$$P_{5} = \frac{75}{50} = 1.5 \ \text{mm}$$

Single-thread pitch:

$$P = \frac{P_{5}}{5} = \frac{1.5}{5} = 0.3 \ \text{mm}$$

> **Answer:** The thread has a pitch of **0.3 mm** (consistent with a metric thread of nominal 0.3 mm pitch, e.g., a fine instrument thread).

## 3.3 Derivation: Cumulative Pitch Error and Functional Gauge

For a mating bolt–nut pair, what matters is not the error of one local pitch, but the **cumulative error over the engagement length**. Let the bolt have **N** engaged threads, and the pitch of the i-th interval be $P_i$.

Cumulative advance from thread 1 to thread N:

$$L_{\text{actual}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} P_i$$

Cumulative error:

$$\Delta L = L_{\text{actual}} - (N-1) \cdot P_{\text{nominal}}$$

$$\Delta L = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} (P_i - P_{\text{nominal}}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \Delta P_i$$

This **cumulative error** is what a **thread ring gauge** or **thread plug gauge** directly tests via its GO and NO-GO members.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Pitch Measurement Method

| Criterion | Steel Rule | Pitch Gauge | Thread Micrometer | Floating Carriage | Toolmaker's Microscope | Profile Projector |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resolution | 0.5 mm | Discrete | 0.01 mm | 0.001 mm | 0.001 mm | 0.005 mm |
| Accuracy class | Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High | Medium-High |
| Skill required | Minimal | Minimal | Low | Medium | High | High |
| Cost | Very Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High | High |
| Thread size range | All | Std. sizes | M3–M100 | M2–M50 | M1–M200 | All |
| Direct/Indirect | Direct | Go/No-Go | Direct | Direct | Direct | Indirect (optical) |
| KTU exam weightage | Low | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Pitch Measurement Workflow

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **decision logic and instrument flow** for selecting and executing a pitch measurement, in lieu of a physical laboratory sketch.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Thread Specimen] --> B{Size and Type?}
    B -- Coarse / Field --> C[Steel Rule or Pitch Gauge]
    B -- Standard Workshop --> D[Screw Thread Micrometer]
    B -- High Precision Lab --> E[Floating Carriage Micrometer]
    B -- Profile/Form Study --> F[Toolmaker's Microscope]
    B -- Visual Comparison --> G[Profile Projector]

    C --> C1[Engage crest with rule]
    C1 --> C2[Count N intervals]
    C2 --> C3[Divide by N equals P]
    C3 --> H[Report P and DeltaP]

    D --> D1[Set V anvil to thread flank]
    D1 --> D2[Step thimble to adjacent thread]
    D2 --> D3[Read thimble scale]
    D3 --> D4{Average over 10 threads?}
    D4 -- Yes --> D5[Divide total by 10]
    D4 -- No --> H
    D5 --> H

    E --> E1[Mount between centers]
    E1 --> E2[Align cross-hair to crest 1]
    E2 --> E3[Clamp floating carriage]
    E3 --> E4[Advance to crest N+1]
    E4 --> E5[Read linear scale]
    E5 --> E6[Compute P equals L by N]
    E6 --> H

    F --> F1[Place on stage]
    F1 --> F2[Illuminate thread]
    F2 --> F3[Focus and align cross-hair]
    F3 --> F4[Traverse stage micrometer drum]
    F4 --> F5[Record axial readings]
    F5 --> F6[Compute P equals D image by M]
    F6 --> H

    G --> G1[Project thread on screen]
    G1 --> G2[Align cross-hair to crest image]
    G2 --> G3[Traverse stage]
    G3 --> G4[Read scale]
    G4 --> H

    H --> I[Compute Pitch Error DeltaP]
    I --> J[Compare with ISO tolerance]
    J --> K[Accept / Reject Decision]
    K --> L[End]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Pitch Geometry on a Helical Thread

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Thread Crest 1] -- axial distance P --> A2[Thread Crest 2]
    A2 -- axial distance P --> A3[Thread Crest 3]
    A3 -- axial distance L equals nP --> A4[Thread Crest after 1 full turn n equals 1]
    A1 --> B[Axis of screw]
    A2 --> B
    A3 --> B
    A4 --> B
    A1 --> C[Helix angle lambda equals arctan L over pi d]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram of Floating Carriage Micrometer (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workpiece between centers] --> B[Movable Carriage on Precision Guideway]
    B --> C[Microscope with Cross-hair]
    C --> D[Cross-hair aligned to thread crest]
    D --> E[Clamping screw locks carriage]
    E --> F[Linear scale reading head]
    F --> G[Digital display or vernier micrometer]
    G --> H[Compute pitch P equals L over N]
    H --> I[Output to log or print]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "pitch" of a screw thread. How is it related to the lead for (a) a single-start thread, and (b) a double-start thread?**

**Model Answer:**
Pitch is the axial distance between two corresponding points on consecutive thread forms, measured parallel to the thread axis. 
- (a) For a single-start thread, **Lead = Pitch** (L = P).
- (b) For a double-start thread, **Lead = 2 × Pitch** (L = 2P), because the helix advances two thread intervals per full revolution.

*(3 marks — 1 mark definition + 2 marks relation)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four instruments used for the measurement of pitch of a screw thread.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Screw thread micrometer (V-anvil type)
2. Floating carriage micrometer
3. Toolmaker's / Universal measuring microscope
4. Profile projector / Optical comparator

*(3 marks — 0.75 marks each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Internal Choice Option 1) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **method of measuring pitch using a floating carriage micrometer**. Discuss its sources of error and accuracy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The axial distance measured over **20 consecutive thread intervals** on a lead screw is **30.04 mm** by a floating carriage micrometer. The nominal pitch is **1.5 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) The measured average pitch.
- (ii) The pitch error per thread.
- (iii) The cumulative pitch error.
- (iv) Comment on the functional acceptability if the ISO tolerance band is $\pm 5$ μm per thread. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Construction (2 marks):** The floating carriage micrometer consists of a heavy base with two fixed centres holding the screw thread specimen. A microscope with cross-hair is mounted on a **floating carriage** that slides on a precision linear guideway parallel to the axis. The carriage is connected to a precision micrometer head or linear scale.

2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - The screw is mounted between the centres.
   - The microscope cross-hair is aligned with the **first thread crest** (using fine focus and lateral adjustment).
   - The carriage is moved axially; as it traverses, the cross-hair follows the helix.
   - The carriage is **clamped** at the first alignment, and a reading is taken.
   - The carriage is released, advanced to the **N-th thread crest**, re-clamped, and a second reading is taken.
   - The difference in readings gives the **total traverse distance over N intervals**.

3. **Sources of error & accuracy (2 marks):**
   - Error in alignment of cross-hair to crest peak (operator dependent).
   - Lead error of the guideway (eliminated by the floating design).
   - Temperature-induced expansion (minimised by controlled lab environment).
   - Accuracy is typically **±0.001 mm** over 25 mm.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** Total traverse over N = 20 intervals = **30.04 mm**, Nominal pitch $P_n$ = **1.5 mm**.

**(i) Measured average pitch:**

$$P_{\text{measured}} = \frac{\text{Total traverse}}{\text{Number of intervals}} = \frac{30.04}{20} = 1.502 \ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Pitch error per thread:**

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{measured}} - P_{\text{nominal}} = 1.502 - 1.500 = +0.002 \ \text{mm} = +2 \ \mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Cumulative pitch error over 20 threads:**

$$\Delta P_{\text{cum}} = (N) \cdot \Delta P = 20 \times 2 = +40 \ \mu\text{m} = +0.040 \ \text{mm}$$

*(Equivalently, $\Delta P_{\text{cum}} = 30.04 - (20 \times 1.5) = 30.04 - 30.00 = +0.04$ mm)*

**(iv) Functional assessment:**
Per-thread error = +2 μm, which is **within** the $\pm 5$ μm ISO tolerance band. Hence the part is **ACCEPTABLE** at the per-thread level. However, the cumulative error of +40 μm is acceptable only if the engagement length uses $\le 2$ threads. For long engagement (e.g., 20-thread nut), this screw would be **REJECTED** for high-precision applications.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Option 2) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the **principle and procedure of pitch measurement using a Toolmaker's Microscope**. Mention the formulae used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metric thread is projected on a profile projector at a **magnification of 50×**. The axial distance between the images of 5 successive thread crests is measured as **76 mm**. If the nominal pitch is 1.5 mm, determine:
- (i) The measured pitch.
- (ii) The pitch error.
- (iii) State whether the thread passes the ISO pitch tolerance of $\pm 10$ μm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

1. **Principle (2 marks):** The Toolmaker's Microscope uses **incident illumination** to project an enlarged image of the thread profile. The thread axis is aligned with the **X-axis of the stage micrometer**. The microscope's cross-hair is aligned visually with successive thread crests, and the stage micrometer drum reading gives the axial pitch.

2. **Procedure (3 marks):**
   - Mount the thread specimen on the V-blocks on the stage.
   - Align the thread axis parallel to the **X-direction** of the stage (using a comparator or a precision flat).
   - Adjust the **incident illumination** for clear crest imaging.
   - Focus the microscope (typically 30× or 50× magnification).
   - Align the cross-hair with the **first thread crest** and note the X-drum reading $x_1$.
   - Translate the stage to bring the **N-th thread crest** under the cross-hair and note $x_N$.
   - The true pitch is:
   $$P = \frac{x_N - x_1}{N \cdot M_{\text{stage}}}$$
   *(If the drum itself is calibrated for a stated magnification, the calculation simplifies.)*

3. **Formula summary (2 marks):**
   $$P_{\text{true}} = \frac{\Delta x_{\text{drum}}}{M \cdot N} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad P_{\text{true}} = \frac{D_{\text{image}}}{M \cdot N}$$
   where $M$ is the total magnification and $N$ is the number of intervals traversed.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** M = 50×, $D_{\text{image, 5 threads}} = 76$ **mm**, $P_{\text{nominal}} = 1.5$ **mm**.

**(i) Measured pitch:**

Total axial distance in 5 intervals = $\dfrac{76}{50} = 1.52$ **mm**

Single-interval pitch:

$$P_{\text{measured}} = \frac{1.52}{5} = 0.304 \ \text{mm}$$

**(ii) Pitch error:**

$$\Delta P = 0.304 - 1.500 = -1.196 \ \text{mm}$$

**(iii) Functional assessment:**
The measured pitch of 0.304 mm differs from the nominal 1.5 mm by **1196 μm**, which is **enormously greater** than the $\pm 10$ μm tolerance. The thread is **REJECTED** — likely the operator miscounted the intervals or the thread under inspection is not the 1.5 mm thread specified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. **Unit confusion in magnification equations** — always confirm whether the drum reading is in mm or in 0.1 mm units before dividing by magnification.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by N** — students often report the *5-thread cumulative distance* as the pitch, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing pitch with lead** — for multi-start threads, the instrument actually measures **axial pitch** (not lead), but the examiner may ask for lead. State the assumption explicitly.
> 4. **Sign of pitch error** — always state the sign (+ or −) and unit. A bare number loses the dimensional mark.
> 5. **Cumulative vs per-thread error** — when the question specifies "over 20 intervals", report both per-thread and cumulative errors.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pitch (P)** = axial distance between two consecutive thread forms; **Lead (L)** = axial distance per revolution; **L = nP** where n = number of thread starts.
- For **single-start** threads (most common): **L = P**.
- **Pitch error** = $P_{\text{measured}} - P_{\text{nominal}}$; sign convention must be stated.
- **Per-thread error** governs local fit; **cumulative error** governs functional fit over the engagement length.
- **Averaging over multiple intervals** (typically 10 or 20) reduces random error by $\sqrt{k}$.
- **Screw thread micrometer** — workshop standard, V-anvil, direct reading.
- **Floating carriage micrometer** — highest workshop accuracy (±0.001 mm), used for lead screws and precision fasteners.
- **Toolmaker's microscope** — laboratory standard, optical method, accuracy ±0.001 mm; works on **incident illumination**.
- **Profile projector** — visual comparison method, magnification typically 10×, 20×, 50×, 100×.
- **Pitch gauge** — a go/no-go, not a measuring instrument; identifies the thread but does not certify it.
- **Optical pitch formula:** $P_{\text{true}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{image}}}{M \cdot N}$.
- ISO metric thread pitch $P$ and threads-per-mm $n$ are reciprocals: $P \cdot n = 1$ **mm**.
- In multi-threaded parts, the **helix angle** $\lambda = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{L}{\pi d}\right)$ governs pitch measurement difficulty — large $\lambda$ makes the thread "flat" and easier to step; small $\lambda$ makes it "steep" and harder to follow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Run Out
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Run Out

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RUN OUT** is defined as the *cumulative deviation of a surface or element from a true rotational reference axis*, measured as the **Full Indicator Movement (FIM)** of a dial indicator probe while the workpiece is rotated through a complete revolution (360°), without traversing the indicator.

Mathematically:

$$ \text{Run Out} = R_{max} - R_{min} $$

where $R_{max}$ and $R_{min}$ are the **maximum** and **minimum** dial indicator readings recorded over one full rotation of the part about the reference axis.

**Unit:** *micrometres (µm)* or *millimetres (mm)*, depending on part tolerance.

> [!NOTE]
> Run out is a **composite tolerance** — it simultaneously controls form, orientation, and location errors (like concentricity, cylindricity, circularity, and squareness) in a single inspection. This is why it is extremely popular in shop-floor quality control.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spinning Top" View

Imagine you are holding a **rotating spindle** of a ceiling fan. If you hold a pencil (acting as a dial indicator) close to the spinning shaft:

- If the shaft is perfectly straight and centred, the pencil tip stays at a *constant* distance.
- If the shaft is **bent, eccentric, or wobbly**, the pencil tip will *move in and out* rhythmically.

That "wobble" you feel and the distance the pencil travels in and out — **that is exactly what run out measures**. The pencil does not slide along the shaft; it only "watches" one cross-section of it spinning.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Symbol / Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Full Indicator Movement | **FIM** (µm) | Total travel of dial needle in one revolution |
| Reference Axis | — | The axis about which the part is rotated (e.g., centres, mandrel) |
| Datum Axis | — | The theoretically exact axis derived from datum features |
| TIR | Total Indicator Reading | Older but equivalent terminology to FIM |
| Indicator Rest Position | — | Dial reading *before* loading the probe |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Axis vs Datum Axis** — the **Reference Axis** is the *practical* axis of rotation (set up by centres, V-blocks, or a mandrel). The **Datum Axis** is the *theoretical* perfect axis derived from datum simulator fixtures (e.g., precision bore). Run out is fundamentally a *measurement of the deviation between these two*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Run Out

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies run out into **three** broad categories. A student must understand the difference clearly because they often appear in numerical/derivation questions.

### 2.1.1 Radial Run Out (RRO)
- Dial indicator is positioned **perpendicular** to the axis of rotation.
- Probe contacts a **cylindrical (radial) surface**.
- Indicator is **NOT** moved during measurement.
- Measurement taken at **one specific cross-section** along the length.

### 2.1.2 Axial Run Out / Face Run Out (FRO)
- Dial indicator is positioned **parallel** to the axis of rotation.
- Probe contacts a **flat face** (shoulder or flange face).
- Indicator is **NOT** moved during measurement.
- Captures the **squareness / perpendicularity** of the face relative to the axis.

### 2.1.3 Total Run Out (TR)
- Dial indicator reads over a **range of cross-sections** (probe is traversed).
- **Radial Total Run Out** → indicator traversed along the full length of a cylindrical surface (controls **cylindricity + concentricity + straightness** simultaneously).
- **Axial Total Run Out** → indicator traversed across the full face diameter (controls **flatness + perpendicularity**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Single-Point vs Traverse Reading:**
> - *Radial Run Out* and *Axial Run Out* are **single-point** readings.
> - *Total Run Out* requires **traversing** the indicator.

## 2.2 Governing Principle — Why Run Out Works

The physical principle is the **relative radial displacement** between two concentric axes:

$$ \Delta R(\theta) = R(\theta) - R_{ref} $$

where $R(\theta)$ is the instantaneous radial position of the measured surface at angular position $\theta$, and $R_{ref}$ is the radius of the reference axis. Run out is the *peak-to-valley* value of $\Delta R(\theta)$ over a full rotation:

$$ \text{Run Out} = \max_{\theta \in [0, 2\pi]} \Delta R(\theta) - \min_{\theta \in [0, 2\pi]} \Delta R(\theta) $$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **only** set of formulas you will need for any run-out problem in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Run Out (FIM) | $FIM = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | µm | Over one full 360° rotation |
| 2 | Eccentricity (2R dial method) | $e = \dfrac{\Delta R}{2}$ | µm | $\Delta R$ = run out reading |
| 3 | True Position from Run Out | $T = 2 \times e = \Delta R$ | µm | When run out = 2 × eccentricity |
| 4 | Angular Error from Face Run Out | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{FRO}{R}\right)$ | rad / deg | $R$ = radius at probe location |
| 5 | Squareness Error | $\delta = FRO_{axial}$ | µm | Directly read from dial |
| 6 | Sensitivity (Dial Indicator) | $S = 0.01$ mm (typical) | mm/rev | Standard metric DI |
| 7 | Least Count (LC) | $LC = 0.01$ mm = 10 µm | mm | Standard KTU lab instrument |

> **Engineering Utility of Run Out** — Used in: 
> - Acceptance testing of bearings, shafts, rotors, pulleys, brake discs.
> - Machine tool alignment (spindle run out check).
> - TIR inspection of crankshafts in IC engines.
> - Concentricity verification of turned components (shafts, axles).

## 2.4 Instrumentation Stack — What is Used on the Shop Floor

A typical KTU lab / industrial setup for run out measurement consists of:

1. **Surface Plate** — reference flatness base.
2. **V-Blocks** (or Dead Centres on a Centre Lathe Bed) — to define the rotation axis.
3. **Mandrel** (precision cylindrical plug) — inserted into a bore to convert a bore into a rotational reference.
4. **Dial Indicator (DI)** with magnetic stand — measures deviation.
5. **Clamping device** — to hold the part rigidly between centres.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the **dial indicator must be zeroed on the reference axis** (e.g., on a slip gauge or a master cylinder), *not* on the part itself, before rotating. If zeroed on a defective part, all subsequent readings are offset.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Run Out (FIM) and Eccentricity

This is a **classic KTU 2024 derivation** and appears in the ESE module almost every cycle.

### Setup
Consider a shaft rotating in a set of V-blocks. The shaft is **eccentric** (its geometric axis is offset from the rotation axis by a distance $e$). The dial indicator reads the radial position of the shaft at angular position $\theta$.

### Step 1 — Define Geometric Centre and Rotation Centre

Let:
- $O$ = the **rotation centre** (defined by the V-blocks or centres).
- $C$ = the **geometric centre** of the shaft's circle of cross-section.
- $e$ = eccentricity = distance between $O$ and $C$.

The shaft radius is $r$ (assumed constant for a perfectly circular cross-section).

### Step 2 — Express the Radial Position of a Point on the Shaft

Pick a point $P$ on the circumference of the shaft. The radial distance from $O$ to $P$ at angle $\theta$ (where $\theta$ is measured from the line $OC$) is given by the **law of cosines** on triangle $OCP$:

$$ R(\theta)^2 = e^2 + r^2 - 2 e r \cos(180° - \theta) $$

$$ R(\theta)^2 = e^2 + r^2 + 2 e r \cos\theta $$

### Step 3 — Simplify for $e \ll r$ (Common Engineering Case)

For most practical shaft measurements, eccentricity is much smaller than the radius. Expanding and applying the binomial approximation:

$$ R(\theta) = r + e \cos\theta + \mathcal{O}(e^2/r) $$

The first-order approximation gives:

$$ R(\theta) \approx r + e \cos\theta $$

### Step 4 — Identify Maximum and Minimum

The cosine term is maximum at $\theta = 0$ and minimum at $\theta = \pi$:

$$ R_{max} = r + e $$

$$ R_{min} = r - e $$

### Step 5 — Compute Run Out

By definition, Run Out = $R_{max} - R_{min}$:

$$ \text{Run Out} = (r + e) - (r - e) $$

$$ \boxed{\text{Run Out} = 2e} $$

### Step 6 — Final Relationship

Therefore, the **eccentricity is exactly half the run-out reading**:

$$ \boxed{e = \dfrac{\text{Run Out}}{2} = \dfrac{FIM}{2}} $$

> **Interpretation:** A dial indicator that reads a full indicator movement of, say, **0.04 mm** over one revolution means the geometric centre of the shaft is offset from the rotation axis by **0.02 mm = 20 µm**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Angular Squareness Error from Face Run Out

When the dial indicator is placed against a face (e.g., a flange) and rotated, the reading variation is related to the **angular tilt** of that face relative to the rotation axis.

### Setup
Let the face be tilted by an angle $\alpha$ about a diameter. The probe touches at radius $R$ from the axis.

### Step 1 — Maximum Elevation Difference

As the part rotates, the probe sees a varying elevation due to the tilt. The maximum elevation difference across one rotation is:

$$ \Delta h = 2 R \tan\alpha $$

### Step 2 — Approximate for Small $\alpha$

For $\alpha \ll 1$ rad, $\tan\alpha \approx \alpha$:

$$ \Delta h \approx 2 R \alpha $$

### Step 3 — Relate to Face Run Out (FRO)

Since $\Delta h$ is precisely the face run-out reading:

$$ \text{FRO} = 2 R \alpha \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{\text{FRO}}{2R} $$

In degrees:

$$ \boxed{\alpha_{deg} = \frac{\text{FRO}}{2R} \times \frac{180°}{\pi} = \frac{57.296 \times \text{FRO}}{2R} } $$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reading Patterns of Different Geometric Errors

The following table helps you **diagnose the type of error** by looking at the dial indicator reading pattern. This is a high-yield KTU concept.

| Reading Pattern | Indication | Type of Error | Run Out Category |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two high / two low per revolution | **True eccentricity** (shaft offset) | Concentricity | Radial Run Out |
| Smooth sinusoidal, 1 cycle/rev | **Bent shaft / single lobe error** | Straightness | Radial Run Out |
| Three or more lobes per revolution | **Out-of-roundness (lobed)** | Circularity | Radial Run Out |
| High–low reading (single step) | **Flat spot / dent** | Local form | Radial Run Out |
| Variation when traversing along face | **Warped / non-flat face** | Flatness | Axial Total Run Out |
| Variation when traversing along length | **Tapered or barrel-shaped** | Cylindricity | Radial Total Run Out |
| Step change after 180° | **Tilted face** | Perpendicularity | Axial Run Out |

> [!NOTE]
> The number of times the needle "peaks" per revolution directly gives the *harmonic order* of the geometric error. This is essentially a **Fourier decomposition** of the roundness profile.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style

**Problem:** A shaft of diameter **50 mm** is mounted between centres on a lathe bed. A dial indicator (LC = 0.01 mm) is placed on the cylindrical surface. The maximum reading is **+0.03 mm** and the minimum is **−0.05 mm**. Find:
1. The radial run out.
2. The eccentricity of the shaft axis.
3. If the same FIM is observed at the opposite end, what is the *Total Run Out*?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Radial Run Out**

$$ \text{Run Out} = R_{max} - R_{min} = (+0.03) - (-0.05) = 0.08 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Eccentricity**

$$ e = \frac{\text{FIM}}{2} = \frac{0.08}{2} = 0.04 \text{ mm} = 40 \text{ \textmu m} $$

**Step 3 — Total Run Out (same value assumed at both ends)**

$$ \text{Total Run Out} = 0.08 \text{ mm} = 80 \text{ \textmu m} $$

**Step 4 — Least Count Interpretation**

The number of divisions moved = $0.08 / 0.01 = 8$ divisions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Run Out Measurement System

Below is a Mermaid block diagram showing the **functional flow of a run out measurement setup**, including signal processing from physical part to operator readout.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    partA[Workpiece Shaft]
    mountA[Mounting System V-Block or Dead Centres]
    refA[Reference Axis Generator Mandrel or Centre Line]
    sensorA[Dial Indicator Probe Tip]
    sensorB[DI Movement Conversion Rack and Pinion Mechanism]
    sensorC[Dial Scale and Needle Display]
    opA[Operator Record Max Min]
    calcA[Computation FIM equals Rmax minus Rmin]
    resultA[Run Out Result in micrometres]
    errA[Error Type Diagnosis Pattern Analysis]

    partA --> mountA
    mountA --> refA
    refA --> sensorA
    sensorA --> sensorB
    sensorB --> sensorC
    sensorC --> opA
    opA --> calcA
    calcA --> resultA
    resultA --> errA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Workpiece Shaft** is rigidly mounted in a **Mounting System** that defines the **Reference Axis**.
- The **Dial Indicator Probe Tip** senses radial displacement.
- Internal **Rack-and-Pinion** converts vertical plunger motion into rotary needle motion.
- The **Dial Scale** displays the reading to the operator.
- The **Operator** records the peak and trough, then computes FIM.
- **Pattern Analysis** is performed to diagnose the type of geometric error.

---

## 4.2 Schematic — Reading Patterns vs Error Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    patternA[2 High 2 Low Pattern]
    patternB[Smooth Sine 1 Cycle per Rev]
    patternC[Multi Lobe 3 or More Peaks]
    patternD[Step Jump Pattern]
    patternE[Tapered Barrel]

    errA1[Eccentricity Error]
    errA2[Bent Shaft or Lobing]
    errA3[Out of Roundness]
    errA4[Local Form Defect]
    errA5[Taper or Cylindricity Error]

    patternA --> errA1
    patternB --> errA2
    patternC --> errA3
    patternD --> errA4
    patternE --> errA5
```

---

## 4.3 Setup Diagram — Radial vs Axial Run Out Probe Orientation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    shaftA[Shaft Cylinder]
    axisA[Axis of Rotation Z direction]
    probeA[Dial Indicator Probe Perpendicular to Axis]
    probeB[Dial Indicator Probe Parallel to Axis]
    faceA[End Face of Shaft]

    shaftA --- axisA
    axisA --- probeA
    axisA --- probeB
    probeB --- faceA
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Probe perpendicular to axis (top branch)** → measures **Radial Run Out**.
- **Probe parallel to axis against the face (bottom branch)** → measures **Axial / Face Run Out**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Run Out. How is it different from Concentricity?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Run Out** is the total deviation of a part element from a true rotational reference axis, measured as the **Full Indicator Movement (FIM)** of a dial indicator while the part is rotated through a full revolution.
>
> **Difference from Concentricity:**
> - *Run Out* is a **functional / shop-floor test**; the reference axis is the *actual* axis of rotation (set by centres, V-blocks, or a mandrel).
> - *Concentricity* is a **GD&T geometric tolerance** evaluated against a *theoretically exact* datum axis using a coordinate measuring machine (CMM).
> - Run out can be measured quickly with a dial indicator; concentricity requires complex datum establishment and calculation.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the difference between Radial Run Out and Total Run Out.**

**Model Answer:**

> | Feature | Radial Run Out | Total Run Out |
> |---|---|---|
> | Probe Movement | Stationary at one cross-section | Indicator is **traversed** along the surface |
> | Errors Captured | Eccentricity, roundness at one point | Cylindricity, taper, lobing over the **entire length** |
> | Setup | Single reading | Multiple readings along axis |
> | GD&T Control | Circular Run Out (symbol ⌰) | Total Run Out (symbol ⌰⌰) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

> **Note:** Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** *With a neat sketch, describe the procedure to measure the **radial run out** of a shaft mounted between centres on a lathe bed. List the instruments used. (7 marks)*

**(b)** A shaft of diameter 60 mm is rotated between centres. The dial indicator readings at four angular positions are: $+0.02$, $-0.04$, $+0.01$, $-0.05$ mm. Calculate (i) radial run out, (ii) eccentricity, and (iii) the angular error of the face if the same FIM is observed as face run out at a probing radius of 25 mm. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Instruments Required:**
1. Dial Indicator (LC = 0.01 mm) with magnetic stand.
2. Dead Centres or a centre lathe bed.
3. Surface plate.
4. Driving dog / lathe carrier.

**Procedure:**
1. Mount the shaft between the **dead centres** of a lathe bed. The lathe bed acts as the **surface plate**.
2. Ensure the centre lines of headstock and tailstock are **coaxial** (use a test bar if needed).
3. Mount the **dial indicator** on the magnetic stand such that the probe contacts the **cylindrical surface** of the shaft, perpendicular to the rotation axis.
4. **Zero** the dial indicator on a **slip gauge or master ring** held at the same height (do *not* zero on the part).
5. Rotate the shaft by hand through a complete **360°** using the lathe spindle.
6. Note the **maximum** and **minimum** readings on the dial.
7. Calculate: $FIM = R_{max} - R_{min}$.

**Neat Sketch — Verbal Description:**
- A horizontal shaft between two pointed centres, with a dial indicator mounted above, its probe touching the top of the shaft, oriented perpendicular to the shaft axis.

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Given:**
- $D = 60$ mm → $r = 30$ mm.
- Readings: $+0.02, -0.04, +0.01, -0.05$ mm.

**Step (i) — Radial Run Out**

$$ R_{max} = +0.02 \text{ mm}, \quad R_{min} = -0.05 \text{ mm} $$

$$ \text{Run Out} = 0.02 - (-0.05) = 0.07 \text{ mm} = 70 \text{ \textmu m} $$

**Step (ii) — Eccentricity**

$$ e = \frac{\text{FIM}}{2} = \frac{0.07}{2} = 0.035 \text{ mm} = 35 \text{ \textmu m} $$

**Step (iii) — Angular Error of the Face**

The face run out equals the FIM = 0.07 mm. Probing radius $R = 25$ mm.

$$ \alpha = \frac{\text{FRO}}{2R} = \frac{0.07}{2 \times 25} = 0.0014 \text{ rad} $$

Converting to degrees:

$$ \alpha = 0.0014 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \approx 0.0802° \approx 4.81 \text{ arc-minutes} $$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the **three types of run out** with the help of suitable diagrams. State the GD&T symbols used. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the relationship between **run out and eccentricity** for a cylindrical shaft rotating about a reference axis. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

| # | Type | Direction of Probe | Surface | GD&T Symbol | Error Controlled |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Radial Run Out | Perpendicular to axis | Cylindrical | ⌰ (single arrow) | Concentricity, circularity at one section |
| 2 | Axial / Face Run Out | Parallel to axis | Flat face | ⌰ (single arrow) | Perpendicularity of face |
| 3 | Total Run Out | Traversed along full length or face | Cylinder or face | ⌰⌰ (double arrow) | Cylindricity, straightness, flatness |

**Diagrams (described):**
- *Radial Run Out:* Probe oriented perpendicular to axis on a cylindrical surface.
- *Axial Run Out:* Probe oriented parallel to axis, touching a flange face.
- *Total Run Out:* Probe is traversed (shown with a double-headed arrow) along the full length/diameter.

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Full Derivation

**Step 1:** Define rotation centre $O$, geometric centre $C$, eccentricity $e = |OC|$, and shaft radius $r$.

**Step 2:** For a point $P$ on the shaft circumference at angle $\theta$ measured from line $OC$, apply the law of cosines in triangle $OCP$:

$$ R(\theta)^2 = e^2 + r^2 + 2er\cos\theta $$

**Step 3:** Take the square root and apply the first-order Taylor expansion for $e \ll r$:

$$ R(\theta) = r\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{2e\cos\theta}{r}\right) + \frac{e^2}{r^2}} \approx r + e\cos\theta $$

**Step 4:** Find the extrema:

$$ R_{max} = r + e \quad \text{at } \theta = 0° $$

$$ R_{min} = r - e \quad \text{at } \theta = 180° $$

**Step 5:** Subtract:

$$ FIM = R_{max} - R_{min} = 2e $$

$$ \boxed{e = \frac{FIM}{2}} $$

**Conclusion:** The run out (FIM) is exactly **twice the eccentricity**. This is the cornerstone relationship for KTU problems on run out.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Run Out Problems:**
> 1. **Confusing Run Out with Eccentricity** — Run out = *2 × eccentricity*. Many students write $FIM = e$, losing 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit conversion** — 0.01 mm = 10 µm. Examiner expects both forms.
> 3. **Zeroing the dial on the part itself** — This is a procedural error. Always zero on a *reference* (master ring/slip gauge).
> 4. **Not specifying whether the reading is radial, axial, or total run out** — Each has a *different* GD&T meaning and unit significance.
> 5. **Skipping the least count statement** — Always write "LC = 0.01 mm" before recording a reading; it is a 1-mark item.
> 6. **Not drawing the probe orientation** in sketches — KTU examiner specifically deducts marks if the *direction of the dial probe* is not clearly shown perpendicular or parallel to the axis.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Run Out = FIM = R_max − R_min** over a full 360° rotation.
- ✅ **Three Types:** Radial, Axial (Face), and Total Run Out.
- ✅ **Eccentricity e = FIM / 2** (the most-tested KTU relationship).
- ✅ **Angular squareness error α = FRO / (2R)** where $R$ is the probe radius.
- ✅ **Reference axis** = set by centres, V-blocks, or mandrel; **NOT** the same as a GD&T datum axis.
- ✅ **Standard Dial Indicator LC = 0.01 mm = 10 µm**.
- ✅ **GD&T Symbols:** Single arrow ⌰ → Circular/Face Run Out; Double arrow ⌰⌰ → Total Run Out.
- ✅ **Reading pattern gives error type** — 1 lobe = eccentricity, multi-lobe = lobing, step = local form defect.
- ✅ **Total Run Out requires probe traverse**; Radial/Axial do NOT.
- ✅ **Always zero the dial on a reference**, not on the workpiece.
- ✅ Run out is a **functional, shop-floor-friendly** test — quick and inexpensive compared to CMM-based concentricity checks.
- ✅ Applications: bearing inspection, brake disc TIR, machine tool spindle checks, shaft concentricity on shop floor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lead and Backlash Checking
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Lead and Backlash Checking

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Lead – Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Metrology (PCPET503)**, **Lead** is defined as the **axial distance advanced by a screw thread (or lead screw) parallel to its axis when the screw is rotated through exactly one complete revolution (360°)**. It is a fundamental geometric parameter of screw threads and is the most critical dimensional attribute governing the **positioning accuracy of machine tool slides, CNC axes, and precision lead screws**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPET503 – Module 2):** Lead and pitch are often confused. For a **single-start (single-threaded) screw**, **Lead = Pitch**. For a **multi-start screw**, **Lead = n × Pitch**, where *n* is the number of thread starts (helices).

The **SI unit** of lead is **millimetre (mm)**, and in the CGS/imperial system it is expressed in **inches (in)** or **threads per inch (TPI)**. The reciprocal of TPI gives the lead directly in inches.

$$L = n \times P$$

Where:
- $L$ = Lead of the screw (mm)
- $n$ = Number of starts (thread helices)
- $P$ = Pitch of the screw (mm) = axial distance between adjacent crests

---

## 1.2 Backlash – Formal Academic Definition

**Backlash** is defined as the **amount of intentional (or unintentional) clearance / lost motion** present between two mechanically mated components when the direction of relative motion is reversed. In **KTU Metrology Module 2**, backlash is studied specifically in the context of:
1. **Gear teeth pairs (spur, helical, bevel, worm)**
2. **Lead screw and nut pairs**
3. **Universal joints and kinematic linkages**

It is the **maximum relative angular or linear displacement** that one member can move with respect to its mating member without causing the latter to move.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Definition (Memorise Verbatim):**
> *"Backlash is the difference between the tooth thickness of one gear and the tooth space of its mating gear, measured along the pitch circle."* — This is the **standard KTU definition** for backlash in gears.

**Unit**: For **gears** → **mm (linear)** or **micrometres (μm)** measured on the pitch circle; for **lead screws** → **mm (linear axial play)**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – The "Door Hinge" and "Tap" Model

### Lead – The Tap Analogy
Imagine turning a **water tap (faucet)** by **one full rotation**. The spindle (axle) of the tap moves **inward or outward** by a fixed axial distance. That axial advance per full turn is the **Lead**. The same principle governs how a **bolt advances into wood** or how a **micrometer thimble advances the spindle** — one turn = one lead of axial movement. If the tap has **two helical threads (double-start)**, it will advance **twice as far per turn** — because you are effectively using two threads in parallel.

### Backlash – The Door Hinge Analogy
Imagine a **door with a worn-out hinge pin** that is slightly smaller than the hinge hole. When you push the door gently, the door **rocks back and forth** within the **clearance gap** before the hinge pin actually contacts the inner wall of the hole and starts transmitting the force. That **"free play"** before motion is actually transmitted is **backlash**. The same effect occurs in **gears** (tooth space wider than mating tooth) and **lead screws** (nut bore slightly larger than screw crest diameter).

> [!TIP]
> **Why backlash is sometimes necessary:** Zero backlash would cause **tooth jamming, overheating, and seizure** in gears due to thermal expansion and manufacturing imperfections. Therefore, **a small, controlled backlash is engineered into precision mechanisms** — but it must be **measured and kept within KTU prescribed limits**.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants, Standards & KTU Reference Values

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Range (KTU Industrial Context) |
|---|---|---|
| **Lead error tolerance (precision lead screws)** | $\Delta L$ | $\pm 5 \, \mu m$ to $\pm 25 \, \mu m$ per 300 mm travel |
| **Backlash (precision machine tools)** | $B$ | $0.02 \, mm$ to $0.05 \, mm$ |
| **Backlash (coarse industrial gears)** | $B$ | $0.1 \, mm$ to $0.3 \, mm$ |
| **IS Standard for gear backlash** | **IS 9192 / AGMA 2001** | Reference norms |
| **Standard lead screw pitch series (KTU lab)** | $P$ | $2, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 \, mm$ |
| **Class of accuracy (IS 2062 – lead screws)** | Grade | **Grade 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5** (Grade 0 = highest precision) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Confusing **Lead** with **Pitch** in the exam. If the question says *"A double-start screw has a pitch of 4 mm. Find its lead"*, the answer is **L = 2 × 4 = 8 mm**, NOT 4 mm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometry of Lead – Helical Interpretation

A screw thread is a **helix of uniform pitch** wrapped around a cylinder. The mathematical relationship between **lead (L)**, **pitch (P)**, **helix angle (α)**, and **mean diameter (d)** is given by the **fundamental helix equation**:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi \cdot d}$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = Helix angle of the thread (degrees)
- $L$ = Lead (mm)
- $d$ = Mean / pitch diameter of the screw (mm)
- $\pi$ = Universal constant $\approx 3.14159$

> [!NOTE]
> **Derivation Logic:** If you "unwrap" the cylindrical helix onto a flat surface, it becomes a **right-angled triangle** whose:
> - **Base** = circumference of mean circle $= \pi d$
> - **Height (perpendicular)** = Lead $= L$
> - **Angle at base** = Helix angle $= \alpha$
> Therefore $\tan \alpha = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \frac{L}{\pi d}$ — this is the standard KTU 2024 derivation.

---

## 2.2 Types of Lead Errors in Lead Screws (KTU High-Yield Topic)

Lead errors are deviations of the **actual lead** from the **nominal (designated) lead** over the useful length of the screw. They are broadly classified into **four types** — this classification is a **favourite KTU Part-A question**.

### (a) Progressive Lead Error
A **monotonically increasing (or decreasing) cumulative deviation** along the length of the screw. Caused by **gradual pitch variation in the parent screw used for thread cutting** or **thermal gradients** during machining.

$$\Delta L_{prog}(x) = L_{actual}(x) - L_{nominal}$$

The **total progressive error** over length $L_{total}$ is the **maximum deviation** at the terminal end.

### (b) Periodic Lead Error
A **sinusoidal / cyclic deviation** that repeats at regular intervals along the screw length. Caused by:
- **Lead screw of the parent lathe** (which also has its own periodic error)
- **Eccentricity of the work spindle**
- **Runout of the driving gear of the lead screw**

$$\Delta L_{per}(x) = A \cdot \sin\left(\frac{2\pi x}{P_{per}}\right)$$

Where $A$ = amplitude, $P_{per}$ = period of the cyclic error.

### (c) Local (or Spurious) Lead Error
**Sudden, localised deviations** within a small region (typically 25 mm or 1 inch length). Caused by:
- **Surface defects, nicks, dents**
- **Burrs, hardness variations**
- **Local thermal distortion**

### (d) Drift Error
A **continuous deviation in one direction** observed over long lengths, often attributed to **climatic drift, ambient temperature change, and elastic creep** of the screw material.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Favourite:** *"Classify the different types of lead errors. Which error is most significant in precision CNC machines and why?"* — Answer: **Progressive + Periodic errors** are the most critical; drift is usually negligible in controlled metrology labs.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = n \times P$ | Lead = Number of starts × Pitch | Multi-start thread identification |
| 2 | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi d}$ | Helix angle relation | Lead screw geometry |
| 3 | $\Delta L = L_{act} - L_{nom}$ | Total lead error | Lead checking |
| 4 | $\Delta L_{cum}(x_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \delta L_j$ | Cumulative lead error at position $x_i$ | Lead chart plotting |
| 5 | $B_{gear} = S_{tooth} - W_{space}$ | Gear backlash = tooth thickness − space width | Tangential backlash |
| 6 | $B_{ang} = \dfrac{360° \times B_{tan}}{\pi d_p}$ | Backlash converted to angular measure | Rotational backlash |
| 7 | $B_{centre} = B_{tan} \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{r_p}\right)$ | Backlash at the centre distance | Centre-distance testing |
| 8 | $\text{True reading} = \text{Measured} \pm \text{Backlash correction}$ | Backlash compensation in leadscrew instruments | Precision measurement |
| 9 | $N = \dfrac{\text{Travel of screw}}{\text{Circumference of micrometer drum}}$ | Number of drum divisions per revolution | Floating carriage method |
| 10 | $\delta L = \dfrac{\pi D}{N}$ | Least count of lead measurement using drum | Error analysis |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Table Syntax Note:** In the above table, the absolute value notation and division bars have been carefully rendered. In handwritten exams, always use parentheses or fractions to avoid examiner ambiguity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Lead and backlash checking are **not academic exercises** — they are **production-line critical**:

1. **CNC Machine Tools (Lead Checking):** A 0.01 mm lead error in a 1000 mm CNC lead screw translates to a **10 μm positioning error per metre travel** — which is catastrophic for sub-micron machining. Hence lead is checked on a **Maag gear tester** or **Prismo lead checker** before certification.

2. **Automotive Gearboxes (Backlash Checking):** Improper backlash in differential gears causes **NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness) issues**, premature wear, and **tooth breakage under torque shock**. KTU emphasises the **"minimum backlash that prevents jamming"** principle.

3. **Aerospace Lead Screws (Flap Actuators):** Lead errors directly cause **flight control positioning errors** — checked to **± 2 μm** accuracy using laser interferometers.

4. **Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMMs):** The CMM's own internal lead screws are **calibrated to Grade 0 IS 2062** — backlash checked using **Renishaw XL-80 laser system**.

5. **Watch & Clock Industry (Backlash):** The minute hand of a watch moves through a **gear train with backlash < 0.005 mm** — anything more causes the second hand to "jitter".
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Helix Angle of a Lead Screw (MOST IMPORTANT KTU DERIVATION)

### Statement
Derive the relationship $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi d}$ for the helix angle of a screw thread.

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Geometric Construction:**
Consider one complete helix of a screw thread, wound around a cylinder of mean diameter $d$. The helix, when unwrapped onto a flat plane (developing the cylinder), forms a **right-angled triangle** $ABC$.

**Step 2 — Identification of Sides:**
- The **horizontal base** $AC$ of the triangle = circumference of the mean circle of the screw
- The **vertical height** $AB$ of the triangle = axial advance in one full rotation = **Lead ($L$)**
- The **hypotenuse** $BC$ = the actual length of the helix along the thread crest
- The **angle at $A$** = helix angle ($\alpha$) measured from the axis

**Step 3 — Express the Base:**
$$AC = \pi \cdot d$$

This is the perimeter of a circle of diameter $d$.

**Step 4 — Apply Trigonometry:**
In the right-angled triangle $ABC$, with right angle at $A$:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{\text{Side opposite to } \alpha}{\text{Side adjacent to } \alpha} = \frac{AB}{AC}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Geometric Quantities:**
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{\text{Lead}}{\text{Circumference}} = \frac{L}{\pi d}$$

**Step 6 — Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A screw with a **large lead** and **small mean diameter** will have a **steep helix angle** (like a wood screw — fast advance). Conversely, a **fine-pitch screw** (small $L$) with **large diameter** will have a **shallow helix angle** (high mechanical advantage, like a bench vice screw).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Pitch Circle Diameters and Backlash in Mating Gears

### Setup
Two mating spur gears with **pitch circle diameters** $d_1$ and $d_2$ are in mesh. Let:
- $s_1$ = circular tooth thickness of gear 1 (measured on its pitch circle)
- $s_2$ = circular tooth thickness of gear 2 (measured on its pitch circle)
- $e_1$ = tooth space of gear 1 (measured on its pitch circle)
- $e_2$ = tooth space of gear 2 (measured on its pitch circle)
- $p$ = circular pitch (same for both gears since they are conjugate)

### Step 1 — Fundamental Constraint at the Pitch Point
At the pitch point of contact, the **tooth thickness of one gear** plus the **tooth space of the mating gear** must equal the **circular pitch**:

$$s_1 + e_2 = p \quad \text{and} \quad s_2 + e_1 = p$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Backlash**
Backlash is defined as the **excess of space over tooth thickness** when the gears are mounted at standard centre distance:

$$B_1 = e_2 - s_1 \quad \text{(backlash on gear 1)}$$
$$B_2 = e_1 - s_2 \quad \text{(backlash on gear 2)}$$

**Step 3 — Total Backlash at the Pitch Circle**
The total backlash on the **line of action** at the pitch point is:

$$B_{total} = B_1 + B_2 = (e_1 + e_2) - (s_1 + s_2)$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Relation at Centre Distance**
The total angular movement of one gear while the other is held fixed is converted to **arc on the pitch circle** using the formula:

$$B_{arc} = B_{total} = r \cdot \theta$$

where $r$ is the pitch radius of the gear on which the motion is measured, and $\theta$ is the angular backlash in radians.

**Step 5 — Conversion to Angular Backlash**
Since arc length $s = r \theta$, the **angular backlash** in degrees is:

$$\theta = \frac{180°}{\pi} \cdot \frac{B_{total}}{r}$$

**Step 6 — Final Compact Form**
For a pair of meshing gears with centre distance $C$ and pitch radius $r_p$ of the driven gear:

$$\boxed{\theta_{backlash} = \frac{180° \cdot B_{tan}}{\pi \cdot r_p} \quad \text{(degrees)}}$$

$$\boxed{B_{tan} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p \cdot \theta_{backlash}}{360°} \quad \text{(mm)}}$$

where $d_p$ is the pitch circle diameter of the driven gear.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU Solved Problem Style)

### Problem
A lead screw has **6 starts**, **pitch = 5 mm**, and **mean diameter = 30 mm**. Find:
1. The lead of the screw.
2. The helix angle.
3. The axial advance after 8 complete revolutions.

### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Number of starts, $n = 6$
- Pitch, $P = 5 \, mm$
- Mean diameter, $d = 30 \, mm$
- Number of revolutions, $N_{rev} = 8$

**Part (a) — Lead Calculation:**

$$L = n \times P = 6 \times 5 = 30 \, mm$$

**Part (b) — Helix Angle Calculation:**

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d} = \frac{30}{\pi \times 30} = \frac{1}{\pi} = 0.3183$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.3183) = 17.66°$$

**Part (c) — Axial Advance in 8 Revolutions:**

$$\text{Advance} = N_{rev} \times L = 8 \times 30 = 240 \, mm$$

### Final Answer Box

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| **Lead ($L$)** | **30 mm** |
| **Helix Angle ($\alpha$)** | **17.66°** |
| **Axial Advance in 8 rev** | **240 mm** |

---

## 3.4 Cumulative Lead Error Plot (Analytical Table)

For a **300 mm lead screw** checked at every **50 mm** interval, the readings are tabulated as follows. This is a **standard KTU question type**.

| Position $x_i$ (mm) | Nominal Lead (mm) | Actual Reading (mm) | Local Error $\delta L_i$ (μm) | Cumulative Error $\sum \delta L$ (μm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | 0 | 0 |
| 50 | 2.500 | 2.498 | −2 | −2 |
| 100 | 5.000 | 4.996 | −4 | −6 |
| 150 | 7.500 | 7.497 | −3 | −9 |
| 200 | 10.000 | 9.998 | −2 | −11 |
| 250 | 12.500 | 12.499 | −1 | −12 |
| 300 | 15.000 | 15.001 | +1 | **−11** |

**Total Lead Error** over 300 mm = **−11 μm** (the screw is slightly under-lead by 11 micrometres over its full length).

This data is conventionally plotted as a **lead error curve** (X-axis = position, Y-axis = cumulative error) — the resulting graph is the **"lead chart"** of the screw and is a **mandatory KTU practical exam component**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Complete Lead & Backlash Checking Workflow

The following **Mermaid block diagram** shows the **full procedure map** for performing lead and backlash checking in a KTU metrology lab, including both the **measurement sub-systems** and the **data processing path**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Workpiece: Lead Screw or Gear Pair Mounted on Bench] --> B[Pre-Checking Phase]
    B --> B1[Visual Inspection for Surface Defects]
    B --> B2[Cleaning with Solvent and Lint-free Cloth]
    B --> B3[Thermal Soak for 30 minutes at 20 deg C]
    B1 --> C[Setup Phase]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Leveling of Bench using Precision Spirit Level]
    C --> C2[Alignment of Screw Axis with Measurement Axis]
    C --> C3[Zero Setting of Dial Indicator or LVDT]
    C1 --> D{Choose Measurement Type}
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    D -->|Lead Checking| E1[Floating Carriage Method]
    D -->|Lead Checking| E2[Toolmaker Microscope Method]
    D -->|Lead Checking| E3[Maag Lead Measuring Machine]
    D -->|Backlash Checking| F1[Feeler Gauge Method for Coarse]
    D -->|Backlash Checking| F2[Dial Indicator Method for Fine]
    D -->|Backlash Checking| F3[Backlash Tester for Production]
    E1 --> G1[Record Readings at Every 50 mm Interval]
    E2 --> G1
    E3 --> G1
    F1 --> G2[Record Minimum Clearance and Maximum Clearance]
    F2 --> G2
    F3 --> G2
    G1 --> H1[Compute Local Error and Cumulative Error]
    G2 --> H2[Compute Backlash in mm and arc minutes]
    H1 --> I1[Plot Lead Error Curve on Graph Paper]
    H2 --> I2[Compare with IS 9192 Standard Specification]
    I1 --> J{Acceptance Decision}
    I2 --> J
    J -->|Within Tolerance| K1[Pass - Certify Component]
    J -->|Exceeds Tolerance| K2[Fail - Rework or Reject]
    K1 --> L[Generate Inspection Report]
    K2 --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for KTU Students:** The two diverging branches — `Lead Checking` and `Backlash Checking` — share the same **Setup Phase** but use **different transducers** (E vs F blocks) and produce **different outputs** (H1 vs H2). Memorise the **three classical methods** in each branch — these are the **most-frequently-asked KTU 2024 questions**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Instrument Architecture of a Floating Carriage Lead Checker

The **floating carriage micrometer** is the most common instrument used in KTU labs for lead checking. Its architecture is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Master Lead Screw Under Test] --> M2[Bench with Precision Ways]
    M2 --> M3[Floating Carriage Assembly]
    M3 --> M4[Precision Micrometer Head]
    M4 --> M5[Dial Indicator for Sub-Division]
    M5 --> M6[Digital Readout Display]
    M3 --> M7[Sensitivity Lever or Kinematic Seat]
    M7 --> M8[Standard End Bar of Known Length]
    M1 --> M9[Driving Handle and Index Plate]
    M9 --> M10[Angular Reference Disc with Vernier]
    M6 --> OUT[Combined Reading: Position + Sub-Division]
    M8 --> OUT
    M10 --> OUT
```

**Working Principle (in plain text for revision):**
1. The lead screw is mounted between centres on the bench.
2. A **floating carriage** slides on precision roller bearings, free of backlash in the carriage motion.
3. The carriage carries a **micrometer head** which contacts a **standard end bar** (e.g., a 25 mm Johansson block) at one end of the screw.
4. As the screw is rotated by a handle, the carriage moves; the micrometer reading is taken at every integer rotation.
5. The difference between successive micrometer readings gives the **actual lead**.
6. Cumulative error is plotted against length to obtain the **lead chart**.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Backlash Tester for Gears

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Driving Gear Mounted on Test Arbor] --> G2[Driving Shaft with Torsion Spring]
    G2 --> G3[Pre-Load Disk with Lock Pin]
    G3 --> G4[Driven Gear Mounted on Parallel Arbor]
    G4 --> G5[Sector Arm with Reference Pin]
    G5 --> G6[Precision Dial Indicator]
    G6 --> G7[Measuring Plunger against Gear Tooth Flank]
    G4 --> G8[Moment Arm of Known Length]
    G8 --> G9[Force Application Point]
    G7 --> H[Backlash Reading in Micrometres]
    G6 --> H
    H --> I[Conversion to Angular Backlash using Pitch Radius]
    I --> J[Comparison with IS 9192 Limit]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Note:** In the KTU Metrology Lab, the **Pfauter backlash tester** and the **Mahr Federal gear tester** are the two most commonly used instruments. The procedure is to **apply a known torque (using the torsion spring) to remove all slack**, then measure the **play of the driven gear** using the dial indicator positioned on a tooth flank.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Lead" of a screw thread. How is it related to the pitch? State the SI unit.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Structured for KTU Valuation):**

> Lead is defined as the **axial distance travelled by a screw thread along its axis for one complete revolution (360°)**. It is a fundamental geometric parameter of a screw thread.
>
> The relationship between lead ($L$) and pitch ($P$) is given by the equation:
> $$L = n \times P$$
> where $n$ is the **number of thread starts (helices)**. For a **single-start thread**, $L = P$, while for **multi-start threads**, lead is an integer multiple of pitch.
>
> **SI Unit:** **millimetre (mm)**.

*(Valuation tip: Award 1 mark each for definition, formula, and unit. Skipping the formula loses 1 mark.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is backlash? Why is a small amount of backlash provided in gear drives?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Backlash is the **amount of clearance or lost motion** between two meshing components (such as gear teeth, lead screw and nut) when the direction of relative motion is reversed. It is measured as the **excess of tooth space over tooth thickness** along the pitch circle.
>
> A small backlash is intentionally provided in gear drives to:
> 1. **Prevent jamming and seizure** due to thermal expansion of gears during operation.
> 2. **Accommodate manufacturing tolerances** in tooth thickness and centre distance.
> 3. **Allow formation of an oil film** between meshing teeth for lubrication.
> 4. **Compensate for deflection** under load.
>
> However, excessive backlash causes **noise, vibration, impact loads, and positioning inaccuracy**.

*(Valuation tip: KTU expects the **4 reasons** — at least 2 must be mentioned for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Pattern

### QUESTION A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **method of checking the lead of a lead screw using a floating carriage micrometer**. Discuss the various types of lead errors that can be identified from the lead chart. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A lead screw has **4 starts** and a **pitch of 6 mm**. The **mean diameter is 40 mm**.
   (i) Find the **lead** of the screw. **(2 Marks)**
   (ii) Calculate the **helix angle**. **(3 Marks)**
   (iii) If the screw is rotated through **12.5 revolutions**, find the **axial advance**. **(2 Marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Floating Carriage Method + Lead Error Types **(7 Marks)**

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
The floating carriage micrometer is a precision instrument used for checking the lead accuracy of lead screws in the KTU metrology lab. The procedure is as follows:

1. The **lead screw under test** is mounted between two **dead centres** on a precision bench.
2. A **floating carriage** carrying a precision **micrometer head** and a **dial indicator** is positioned on the bench ways. The carriage rides on **anti-friction rollers** to ensure frictionless motion.
3. A **standard end bar** (e.g., 25 mm or 50 mm Johansson block) of precisely known length is placed between the micrometer plunger and the end of the screw.
4. The screw is rotated through **one full revolution** using the **driving handle** fitted with an **index plate and vernier** for precise angular control.
5. The micrometer reading is taken. The **difference between successive readings** gives the **actual lead** over that interval.
6. Readings are taken at every **50 mm** interval along the entire length of the screw.
7. From these readings, **local errors** ($\delta L_i$) and **cumulative errors** ($\sum \delta L_i$) are calculated, and a **lead error curve** (lead chart) is plotted.

**Lead Errors Identified from the Lead Chart (4 Marks):**

1. **Progressive Lead Error:** Identified as a **monotonically increasing cumulative error curve** in the lead chart. Caused by gradual pitch variation in the parent screw.

2. **Periodic Lead Error:** Identified as a **sinusoidal oscillation** superimposed on the cumulative error curve. Caused by runout or eccentricity in the driving system.

3. **Local Lead Error:** Identified as a **sudden spike or dip** in the cumulative error curve over a small region. Caused by surface defects or local imperfections.

4. **Drift Error:** Identified as a **smooth, continuous deviation** consistent over the full length. Caused by thermal effects and elastic creep.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- Number of starts, $n = 4$
- Pitch, $P = 6 \, mm$
- Mean diameter, $d = 40 \, mm$
- Number of revolutions, $N = 12.5$

**Sub-Part (i) — Lead Calculation (2 Marks):**
$$L = n \times P = 4 \times 6 = 24 \, mm$$

**Sub-Part (ii) — Helix Angle (3 Marks):**
$$\tan \alpha = \frac{L}{\pi d} = \frac{24}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{24}{125.664} = 0.1910$$
$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.1910) = 10.81°$$

**Sub-Part (iii) — Axial Advance (2 Marks):**
$$\text{Advance} = N \times L = 12.5 \times 24 = 300 \, mm$$

---

### QUESTION B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 — Internal Choice Alternative]`
**(a)** Define backlash. With a neat sketch, describe the **method of checking backlash in a gear pair using a dial indicator**. Explain how backlash is **compensated in precision lead screws**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two spur gears in mesh have **pitch circle diameters 80 mm and 160 mm**. The **circular tooth thickness on the smaller gear is 7.85 mm** and the **tooth space on the larger gear is 7.95 mm**. The **circular pitch is 15.71 mm**. Calculate:
   (i) The **backlash at the pitch circle** of the smaller gear. **(3 Marks)**
   (ii) The **angular backlash** of the smaller gear in **degrees and arc-minutes**. **(4 Marks)**

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — Backlash Definition + Checking Method + Compensation **(7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Backlash is the **excess of tooth space over tooth thickness** of mating gears, measured along the pitch circle. It represents the **lost motion** when the direction of rotation is reversed.

**Dial Indicator Method (4 Marks):**
1. The **driven gear** is mounted on a fixed arbor; the **driving gear** is free to rotate but held in mesh by a light spring or weight to remove slack.
2. A **dial indicator** with its plunger resting on a **tooth flank of the driven gear** is set to zero.
3. The driving gear is rotated by a small angle in one direction until the driven gear just starts moving; the indicator reading is noted.
4. The driving gear is then rotated in the **opposite direction** until the driven gear just moves again; the **second reading** is taken.
5. The **difference between the two readings** is the **backlash** in micrometres.
6. The angular backlash is calculated using the pitch radius of the driven gear.

**Backlash Compensation in Lead Screws (2 Marks):**
1. **Split-nut design** — the nut is split and spring-loaded to take up slack.
2. **Anti-backlash nuts** — use a second half-nut pushed by a spring.
3. **Pre-loaded ball screws** — the recirculating ball bearing race is preloaded to eliminate internal clearance.
4. **Electronic compensation** — the controller adds a "backlash offset" in software during direction reversal.

---

#### Part (b) — Backlash Numerical **(7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $d_1$ (smaller gear PCD) $= 80 \, mm \Rightarrow r_1 = 40 \, mm$
- $d_2$ (larger gear PCD) $= 160 \, mm \Rightarrow r_2 = 80 \, mm$
- Tooth thickness on smaller gear, $s_1 = 7.85 \, mm$
- Tooth space on larger gear, $e_2 = 7.95 \, mm$
- Circular pitch, $p = 15.71 \, mm$

**Sub-Part (i) — Backlash on Smaller Gear (3 Marks):**

The backlash on the smaller gear is the **excess of space on the larger gear over the tooth thickness on the smaller gear**:

$$B_1 = e_2 - s_1 = 7.95 - 7.85 = 0.10 \, mm$$

**Sub-Part (ii) — Angular Backlash (4 Marks):**

The arc length $B_1$ on the pitch circle of the smaller gear corresponds to an angular backlash of:

$$\theta = \frac{180°}{\pi} \times \frac{B_1}{r_1}$$

$$\theta = \frac{180°}{\pi} \times \frac{0.10}{40} = \frac{180° \times 0.10}{3.14159 \times 40}$$

$$\theta = \frac{18°}{125.664} = 0.1432°$$

**Converting to arc-minutes:**
$$0.1432° \times 60 \text{ arc-min/deg} = 8.59 \, \text{arc-minutes}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Lead and Pitch (–2 marks):** For a double-start screw, lead = 2 × pitch, NOT equal to pitch. Read the question carefully — "starts" matters.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Helix Angle Derivation (–3 marks):** Many students write $\tan \alpha = L / \pi d$ without showing the geometric unwrapping of the helix. The examiner **demands the right-triangle construction**.
>
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to 2 marks):** Mixing mm and inches, or using **radians** when the question asks for **arc-minutes** (1 radian = 3438 arc-min).
>
> 4. **Skipping the Backlash Reasons (–2 marks):** The "why is backlash provided" question requires **at least 3 reasons** — jamming prevention, thermal expansion, oil film, manufacturing tolerance.
>
> 5. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Sub-Part (–2 to 3 marks):** KTU strictly deducts marks for **missing sketches** in floating carriage / dial indicator questions. Always include a labelled block diagram.
>
> 6. **Sign Convention for Lead Error:** If the screw is **under-lead**, the cumulative error is **negative**. Students often report only magnitudes and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Last-Minute KTU Exam Prep**

- [x] **Lead = $n \times P$** (memorise this on top priority)
- [x] **Helix angle formula:** $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{L}{\pi d}$ — derivation uses **right-triangle unwrapping**
- [x] **Four types of lead errors:** Progressive, Periodic, Local, Drift
- [x] **Lead checking methods:** Floating carriage, Toolmaker's microscope, Maag machine, Laser interferometer
- [x] **Lead chart:** X-axis = position (mm), Y-axis = cumulative error (μm)
- [x] **Backlash definition:** Excess of tooth space over tooth thickness
- [x] **Backlash checking methods:** Feeler gauge (coarse), Dial indicator (fine), Backlash tester (production)
- [x] **Backlash conversion:** $\theta = \dfrac{180°}{\pi} \times \dfrac{B}{r}$
- [x] **Backlash compensation:** Split nut, anti-backlash nut, preloaded ball screw, electronic offset
- [x] **Unit reminders:** Lead → mm; Lead error → μm; Backlash → mm or arc-min; Helix angle → degrees
- [x] **IS Standards to quote:** IS 9192 (gear backlash), IS 2062 (lead screws), AGMA 2001 (American reference)
- [x] **Industrial values:** Precision backlash = 0.02–0.05 mm; Coarse backlash = 0.1–0.3 mm
- [x] **Always draw a labelled diagram** for any 7-mark question — KTU valuation penalises missing sketches
- [x] **Backlash ≠ Bad**: Zero backlash causes seizure; small controlled backlash is **engineered necessity**

---

> **End of Module 2 — Lead and Backlash Checking Notes | KTU PCPET503 | 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Concentricity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Concentricity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Concentricity** is a geometric tolerance that controls the *median points* of all diametrically opposed elements (or correspondingly located elements) of a toleranced feature such that they lie within a **cylindrical tolerance zone of diameter equal to the specified tolerance**, whose axis **coincides with the datum axis**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GD&T Symbol:** ⊚ (Two concentric circles)
> **Datum Reference:** Required (Single datum axis)
> **Tolerance Zone:** Cylindrical (3D zone, unlike circular runout which uses 2D zones)

**KTU Standard Definition:**
> *Concentricity is that condition where the median points of all diametrically opposed elements of a figure (or correspondingly located elements of two or more radially disposed features) are congruent with a datum axis or center point.*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Spinning Wheel on an Axle"

Imagine a **bicycle wheel rotating on its axle**. For the wheel to spin smoothly without wobbling:

- The **axle's center line** (datum axis) must pass through the **exact geometric center** of the wheel hub.
- If the wheel hub is mounted slightly off-center, it will "wobble" when rotated.

**Concentricity = "How perfectly the center of one circle lines up with the center of another circle."**

- A **perfectly concentric** part = Both circles share the *same mathematical center point*.
- An **off-concentric** part = The two circles have *different centers*; one is shifted relative to the other.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Examples:**
> - Locating dowel pins in a gearbox housing
> - Bearing journals on a shaft
> - Valve stem and valve seat in an IC engine cylinder head
> - Drive shafts and couplings
> - Mounting holes for pulleys and gears

### 1.3 Concentricity vs. Coaxiality (High-Yield Distinction)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concentricity** and **Coaxiality** are *different* concepts — this is a frequent KTU exam question!

| Aspect | Concentricity | Coaxiality |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| **GD&T Symbol** | ⊚ | ⊚ (same symbol, ASME) |
| **Tolerance Zone** | Cylindrical (3D) | Cylindrical (3D) |
| **Tolerance Value** | Diameter (e.g., Ø 0.05) | Diameter (e.g., Ø 0.05) |
| **Reference Feature** | Datum **Axis** OR Datum **Point** | Only Datum **Axis** |
| **Application** | Features at the **same cross-section** | Features at **different cross-sections** |
| **ISO vs ASME** | Deprecated in ISO 1101; remains in ASME Y14.5 | Preferred term in modern ISO standards |
| **Measurement Basis** | Median **points** of cross-sections | Median **lines** (axes) along length |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern ISO 1101 (Geometrical Product Specifications):** The term **concentricity** has been replaced by **"concentricity and coaxiality"** combined into a single concept using the symbol ⊚. KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand both classical and modern terminology.

### 1.4 The Datum Concept — Foundation of Concentricity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Datum Axis:** A theoretically exact axis derived from a *datum feature* (e.g., a cylindrical hole, journal, or pin).
> - A datum axis is **NOT** a physical line drawn on the part.
> - It is a mathematically derived reference used as the "true center" for measurement.

**Establishing a Datum Axis:**
- For a **cylindrical hole** → datum axis = axis of the hole (assumed perfect).
- For a **cylindrical shaft** → datum axis = axis of the shaft.
- For a **flat surface** → datum axis is derived from the center of two coaxial features.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Geometric Principle Behind Concentricity

**Step-by-step Logic:**

1. **Establish the Datum Axis (D-Axis):**
   - Identify the *primary datum feature* (typically a cylinder).
   - The axis of this feature, when perfectly machined, is the *Datum Axis*.

2. **Identify the Toleranced Feature (T-Feature):**
   - This is the feature whose concentricity is being controlled.
   - Examples: a smaller bore, a spigot, a recess.

3. **Locate Median Points:**
   - At each cross-section of the toleranced feature, identify the **median point** (the geometric center of that cross-section).
   - For a circular cross-section: Median point = midpoint between diametrically opposed points.

4. **Define the Tolerance Zone:**
   - A **cylinder of diameter = tolerance value (t)** is constructed around the datum axis.
   - The center of this cylinder **must coincide with the datum axis**.

5. **Verification:**
   - All median points of the toleranced feature must lie **inside this cylindrical zone**.
   - If any median point falls outside → part is **rejected**.

### 2.2 Why "Median Points" and Not "Surface Points"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concentricity ignores form errors!** It only measures the *location* of the center, not the roundness of the feature.
> - A feature can be **egg-shaped (out-of-round)** but still be concentric.
> - A feature can be **perfectly round** but still be **off-concentric**.

**Example:** A Ø50 mm bore with ovality of 0.02 mm, but its median point is exactly on the datum axis → **Concentricity is perfect**, even though the bore is not round.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|------------------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Concentricity Tolerance Zone | $T_z = t$ (diameter) | mm | $t$ = specified tolerance value |
| 2 | Radial Displacement of Median Point | $\Delta r = \dfrac{d_{offset}}{2}$ | mm | Maximum offset of any median point from datum axis |
| 3 | Acceptance Criterion | $\Delta r_{max} \leq \dfrac{t}{2}$ | mm | All median points must lie within zone |
| 4 | Diameter of Tolerance Zone | $\varnothing_{tz} = t$ | mm | Same as tolerance value |
| 5 | Indicator Reading (Bench Method) | $\Delta r = \dfrac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{2}$ | mm | Total indicated reading (TIR) divided by 2 |
| 6 | TIR for Concentricity | $TIR = 2 \cdot \Delta r$ | mm | Twice the radial displacement |
