The drunken thread error is measured using:
- **Optical comparator** with thread profile magnification.
- **Universal Measuring Machine (UMM)** with a thread-measuring probe.
- **Thread micrometer** at multiple axial positions to detect lateral shift.
- The cumulative deviation is plotted as a function of axial position; the maximum deviation is the *drunken thread index*.

**(b)** For an ACME thread ($\alpha = 29°$) with nominal pitch $P = 5\,mm$ and nominal effective diameter $D_2 = 25\,mm$, the measured pitch is $5.04\,mm$ and the measured flank angle is $29.5°$. Determine the actual effective diameter. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
- $\alpha = 29°$, $P_{\text{nominal}} = 5\,mm$
- $P_{\text{actual}} = 5.04\,mm$, $\alpha_{\text{actual}} = 29.5°$
- $D_{2,\text{nominal}} = 25\,mm$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations:**

$$
\Delta P = 5.04 - 5 = +0.04\,mm
$$

$$
\Delta\alpha = 29.5 - 29 = +0.5°
$$

**Step 3 — Pitch error contribution (for general $\alpha$):**

For general flank angle, the pitch contribution to $D_2$ is:

$$
\delta_P = \frac{\Delta P}{\tan(\alpha/2)} = \frac{0.04}{\tan(14.5°)}
$$

Since $\tan(14.5°) \approx 0.2586$:

$$
\delta_P = \frac{0.04}{0.2586} \approx 0.1547\,mm
$$

**Step 4 — Flank angle contribution (general $\alpha$):**

$$
\delta_\alpha = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \cot(\alpha/2) \cdot \Delta\alpha_{rad}
$$

With $\Delta\alpha = 0.5° = 0.5 \cdot \pi / 180 = 0.008727\,rad$ and $\cot(14.5°) \approx 3.866$:

$$
\delta_\alpha = \frac{5}{2} \cdot 3.866 \cdot 0.008727 = 2.5 \cdot 3.866 \cdot 0.008727 \approx 0.0843\,mm
$$

**Step 5 — Total effective diameter error:**

$$
\Delta D_2 = \delta_P + \delta_\alpha = 0.1547 + 0.0843 = 0.239\,mm
$$

**Step 6 — Actual effective diameter:**

$$
D_{2,\text{actual}} = 25 + 0.239 = 25.239\,mm
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**

1. **Sign Confusion:** Students often forget that $\Delta P$ is *algebraic* (signed). A negative $\Delta P$ means the actual pitch is *smaller* than nominal, leading to an *undersize* $D_2$. Always carry the sign through all calculations.

2. **Constant 0.36 Misuse:** The constant **0.36** is valid **ONLY** for ISO metric threads ($\alpha = 60°$). For ACME or Whitworth threads, you **must derive the constant** or use the general formula with $\cot(\alpha/2)$. Using 0.36 for ACME threads is a guaranteed loss of 3–4 marks.

3. **Forgetting the Factor of 2 in Flank Contribution:** Some textbooks write $\delta_\alpha = 0.36 \cdot P \cdot \Delta\alpha$ while others use $\delta_\alpha = 0.72 \cdot P \cdot \Delta\alpha$. The discrepancy depends on whether $\Delta\alpha$ refers to *single flank* or *both flanks combined*. Read your reference carefully!

4. **Ignoring Units:** $\Delta\alpha$ must be in **degrees** (not radians) when using the 0.36 constant. A degree-radian mix-up will give a wrong answer ~57 times off.

5. **Conflating Pitch with Lead:** For single-start threads they are equal, but for multi-start threads they differ by factor $n$. Always state the number of starts explicitly.

6. **Skipping the Diagram:** In 7-mark questions on "types of errors", KTU examiners *expect* a labelled diagram of a thread profile showing where each error is located. **Always draw a sketch!**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

- **Effective Diameter ($D_2$)** is the *most critical* thread parameter controlling fit.
- **Pitch Error** = deviation of axial distance between adjacent crests.
- **Lead Error** = deviation of axial advance per revolution.
- **Flank Angle Error** = deviation of included angle from $60°$ (ISO metric).
- **Drunken Thread** = thread whose axis is not a true helix.
- For **ISO metric**: $\delta_P = 3 \cdot \Delta P$ and $\delta_\alpha = 0.36 \cdot P \cdot \Delta\alpha$.
- For **general thread**: use $\delta_P = \Delta P / \tan(\alpha/2)$.
- For **general thread**: use $\delta_\alpha = (P/2) \cdot \cot(\alpha/2) \cdot \Delta\alpha_{rad}$.
- **Cumulative pitch error** = algebraic sum of individual pitch errors.
- **Single-start threads**: $L = P$; **Multi-start**: $L = n \cdot P$.
- **Tolerance grades** (ISO metric): 6g, 6h for general; 4g6g for precision.
- **Always carry the sign** of $\Delta P$ and $\Delta\alpha$ in numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Board Exams:**
> *"Effective diameter error = Sum of (pitch contribution) + (flank angle contribution). Always draw the thread profile, label the nominal values, mark the deviations, then substitute into the formula with consistent units."*

---

**End of Module 2 — Errors in Threads — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screw Thread Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Screw Thread Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Screw Thread Gauges** are specialized *limit gauges* designed to verify the geometric accuracy of threaded components (both **external threads** on bolts/screws and **internal threads** in nuts/tapped holes) in a single, fast operation. They check the **Effective Diameter** (also called *Pitch Diameter*) and the **Crest Diameter** (Major for external, Minor for internal) against prescribed **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** and **Least Material Condition (LMC)** limits, in accordance with **Taylor's Principle of Gauging**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, screw thread gauges belong to the family of **GO/NO-GO gauges** and are governed by **IS 4218** (Indian Standard) and **ISO 1502** (International Standard) for ISO metric screw threads.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sieve and the Stopper"

Imagine a **post box slot** that is supposed to accept letters of a particular width but reject all thicker ones:
- The **GO gauge** is the *acceptance test* — *"if the part passes through easily, it has at least the minimum size required."*
- The **NO-GO (NOT-GO) gauge** is the *rejection test* — *"if the part also passes through this one, the part is too loose and must be rejected."*

For threads, the analogy is exactly reversed by the helical geometry. Because a thread is a 3-D helix, we cannot use a single cylindrical "sieve." Instead, we use a **female threaded ring (for bolts)** or a **male threaded plug (for nuts)**, and the **GO gauge has the FULL thread form** (longer engagement) while the **NOT-GO gauge is TRUNCATED** (very short engagement, only checks the pitch cylinder).

## 1.3 Why Special Gauges Are Needed for Threads

A standard cylindrical plug/ring gauge is **insufficient** because threads have **four** critical geometric parameters that must be controlled simultaneously:

1. **Major Diameter** ($D$ or $d$)
2. **Effective / Pitch Diameter** ($D_2$ or $d_2$)
3. **Minor Diameter** ($D_1$ or $d_1$)
4. **Flank Angle** (60° for ISO metric)
5. **Pitch** and **Lead** accuracy

> [!NOTE]
> **A plain ring gauge can ONLY check the major diameter — it cannot detect errors in the effective diameter or flank angle.** That is why a *threaded* gauge with proper profile is mandatory.

## 1.4 Types at a Glance

| Component to be Checked | Gauge Used | GO Type | NOT-GO Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **External thread** (Bolt) | Ring Gauge | Full-form ring (Solid / Adjustable) | NOT-GO ring or **Snap gauge** (truncated) |
| **Internal thread** (Nut) | Plug Gauge | Full-form plug (Solid / Adjustable) | NOT-GO plug (truncated) |
| **Conicity / Tapered thread** | Taper thread plug/ring | — | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Screw Thread Gauges

### A. Based on Workpiece Type

#### (i) **External Thread Gauges** (for Bolts / Studs / Screws)
- **Ring Gauges**
  - *Solid Ring Gauge* — single piece of hardened gauge steel, threaded internally. Long life but cannot be reset after wear.
  - *Adjustable Ring Gauge* — split ring, set by an adjusting screw. Cheaper, can be reset, but slightly less accurate.
- **Snap (Calliper) Gauges** — used as the **NOT-GO** check on the **minor diameter (root)** of the bolt; occasionally used to check the major diameter as a GO/NO-GO pair.

#### (ii) **Internal Thread Gauges** (for Nuts / Tapped Holes)
- **Plug Gauges**
  - *Solid Plug Gauge* — both ends integral; one end GO, other end NOT-GO.
  - *Adjustable Plug Gauge* — threaded bushings are mounted on a handle; each end can be replaced or reset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 2024:** The examiner expects students to **draw the sketch** of a Solid Plug Gauge showing the **GO end (longer, full form)**, the **NOT-GO end (shorter, 3–4 threads, truncated)**, and the **handle**.

### B. Based on the Gauge Design Philosophy

1. **Full Form Gauges** — have the complete thread profile (flanks + crest + root) and check *effective diameter* primarily.
2. **Truncated Gauges (NOT-GO)** — crests and roots are removed (chamfered) so that the gauge contacts the thread **only on the flanks**, isolating the *effective diameter* check from errors in *crest diameter*.

## 2.2 Taylor's Principle Applied to Screw Threads

**Original Taylor's Principle (1897):**
> *"A limit gauge should check **only one dimension at a time** — the GO gauge checks the **MMC** (max metal) and the NOT-GO gauge checks the **LMC** (min metal)."*

**Modified Application to Threads (Acharya and Bryer):**

Because a thread has multiple dimensions, the unmodified Taylor principle cannot be applied directly. The **modified rule** is:

- For **External Threads**:
  - **GO Ring Gauge** — full form, checks **effective diameter** at MMC.
  - **NOT-GO Ring Gauge** — full form OR truncated, checks **effective diameter** at LMC.
  - **Major diameter** of the bolt is implicitly limited by the bore of the GO ring.
  - **Minor diameter** is *not* checked by the NOT-GO ring — it is checked by a **NOT-GO snap gauge**.
- For **Internal Threads**:
  - **GO Plug Gauge** — full form, checks **minor diameter** + **effective diameter** at MMC.
  - **NOT-GO Plug Gauge** — truncated, checks **effective diameter** at LMC only.
  - **Major diameter** is not checked by the NOT-GO plug.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why truncated NOT-GO?** If the NOT-GO plug had a full crest, it would *jam* on the crest of the nut thread. Truncation ensures the gauge contacts the **flanks only**, giving a true *effective diameter* check.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Relation | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Effective (Pitch) Diameter of thread | $D_2$ | $D_2 = D - 0.6495\,P$ | mm |
| 2 | Minor Diameter of internal thread | $D_1$ | $D_1 = D - 1.2269\,P$ | mm |
| 3 | Depth of thread (fundamental triangle) | $H$ | $H = 0.8660\,P$ | mm |
| 4 | Truncation at crest | $C$ | $C = 0.125\,H = 0.10825\,P$ | mm |
| 5 | **GO Ring Gauge** — Effective Ø lower limit | $D_2^{GO-}$ | $D_2^{GO-} = D_2^{\min} - W_{G2}$ | mm |
| 6 | **GO Ring Gauge** — Effective Ø upper limit | $D_2^{GO+}$ | $D_2^{GO+} = D_2^{GO-} + T_{R2}$ | mm |
| 7 | **NOT-GO Ring Gauge** — Effective Ø lower limit | $D_2^{NG-}$ | $D_2^{NG-} = D_2^{\max} - T_{R2}$ | mm |
| 8 | **NOT-GO Ring Gauge** — Effective Ø upper limit | $D_2^{NG+}$ | $D_2^{NG+} = D_2^{\max}$ | mm |
| 9 | Wear allowance on GO gauge (typical) | $W_{G2}$ | $\approx 0.10 \times T_{R2}$ to tabulated value | μm |
| 10 | Tolerance on Effective Ø of gauge | $T_{R2}$ | From IS 4218 / ISO 1502 table (Grade 4, 6, 8) | μm |
| 11 | Tolerance on GO Plug (Eff. Ø) | $D_2^{GO+}$ | $D_2^{GO+} = D_2^{GO-} + T_{R2}$ | mm |
| 12 | NOT-GO Plug — Eff. Ø limits | $D_2^{NG}$ | $D_2^{NG-} = D_2^{\max} - T_{R2}$, $D_2^{NG+} = D_2^{\max}$ | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wear allowance $W_{G2}$ is given **only on the GO gauge**, NOT on the NOT-GO gauge. The NOT-GO is *not* expected to engage fully, so wear is negligible.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Mass production of fasteners** (automotive, aerospace, structural steel): thread ring/plug gauges are the only practical 100% inspection tool.
- **Rejection of undersized fasteners** prevents joint loosening under vibration.
- **Rejection of oversized fasteners** prevents jamming in tapped holes.
- **Interchangeability** of parts made in different countries is guaranteed by adhering to ISO 1502 gauge limits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Effective Diameter Formula $D_2 = D - 0.6495\,P$

The ISO metric thread has a **fundamental triangle** with apex angle 60°. The depth of engagement from crest to root (the fundamental height $H$) is:

$$
H = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,P = 0.866025\,P
$$

The **effective (pitch) diameter** $D_2$ lies at a height of $\frac{5}{8}\,H$ above the minor diameter (this is the standard truncation allowance for ISO threads: $\frac{H}{8}$ at root and $\frac{H}{4}$ at crest).

### Step-by-step derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= D - 2 \times \frac{5}{8}\,H = D - \frac{5}{4}\,H \\
\text{Substituting } H &= 0.866025\,P: \\
D_1 &= D - 1.25 \times 0.866025\,P = D - 1.0825\,P \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Now the **effective diameter** $D_2$ is offset from the minor diameter by $\frac{3}{8}\,H$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= D_1 + 2 \times \frac{3}{8}\,H = D_1 + \frac{3}{4}\,H \\
D_2 &= (D - 1.0825\,P) + 0.75 \times 0.866025\,P \\
D_2 &= D - 1.0825\,P + 0.649519\,P \\
D_2 &= D - 0.433\,P \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Wait — correction.** The standard relationship $D_2 = D - 0.6495\,P$ is derived from $D_2$ being **0.32476P below the major diameter** (i.e., the offset is $\frac{3}{8}H = \frac{3}{8} \times 0.866P = 0.32476P$ below the major diameter, **NOT** $\frac{3}{8}H$ above the minor diameter). 

### Correct Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= D - 2 \times \frac{3}{8}\,H = D - \frac{3}{4}\,H \\
\text{Substituting } H &= 0.866025\,P: \\
D_2 &= D - 0.75 \times 0.866025\,P \\
D_2 &= D - 0.649519\,P \\
D_2 &\approx D - 0.6495\,P \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

And the minor diameter becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= D - 2 \times \frac{5}{8}\,H = D - \frac{5}{4}\,H \\
D_1 &= D - 1.25 \times 0.866025\,P \\
D_1 &= D - 1.0825\,P \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalently, $D_1 = D - 1.2269\,P$ when measured from the **truncated** crest — this accounts for the crest truncation $\frac{H}{8}$.)

## 3.2 Worked Example — Design of Gauges for an M20 × 1.5 Internal Thread

> **Problem Statement:** Design GO and NOT-GO plug gauges for an internal thread of nominal size **M20 × 1.5** (ISO metric, medium fit). Use **Grade 6** tolerance.

### Given Data
- Nominal diameter $D = 20$ mm
- Pitch $P = 1.5$ mm
- Tolerance Grade = 6

### Step 1 — Read Standard Tables (IS 4218 / ISO 1502, Grade 6, Pitch 1.5)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Tolerance on Effective Ø of gauge, $T_{R2}$ | **8 μm** = 0.008 mm |
| Wear allowance on GO gauge, $W_{G2}$ | **5 μm** = 0.005 mm |
| Tolerance on Major Ø of GO plug, $T_{R1}$ | 8 μm |
| Tolerance on Minor Ø of NOT-GO plug, $T_{R3}$ | 5 μm |

### Step 2 — Calculate the Basic Effective Diameter of the Internal Thread

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= D - 0.6495\,P \\
D_2 &= 20 - 0.6495 \times 1.5 \\
D_2 &= 20 - 0.97425 \\
D_2 &= 19.02575 \text{ mm} \approx 19.026 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a medium fit internal thread (tolerance position H), the tolerance on the effective diameter is allocated equally about the basic size (assuming symmetric tolerance for simplification in this problem). Therefore:

$$
D_2^{\min} = 19.020 \text{ mm}, \quad D_2^{\max} = 19.026 \text{ mm}
$$

### Step 3 — GO Plug Gauge Effective Diameter Limits

The GO plug represents the **MMC** of the internal thread. It must enter the nut freely.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^{GO-} &= D_2^{\min} - W_{G2} = 19.020 - 0.005 = 19.015 \text{ mm} \\
D_2^{GO+} &= D_2^{GO-} + T_{R2} = 19.015 + 0.008 = 19.023 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The **GO plug** has Effective Ø between **19.015 mm** and **19.023 mm**.

### Step 4 — Minor Diameter of GO Plug

The minor diameter of the GO plug (the cylindrical "core" of the plug) is at the **maximum minor Ø of the nut** minus the wear allowance and within the gauge tolerance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1^{GO-} &= D_1^{\max} - W_{G1} \\
D_1^{GO+} &= D_1^{GO-} + T_{R3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $D_1^{\max} = D - 1.0825\,P = 20 - 1.62375 = 18.376$ mm.

(For Grade 6, $W_{G1} \approx 4$ μm, $T_{R3} = 5$ μm, giving $D_1^{GO-} = 18.372$ mm, $D_1^{GO+} = 18.377$ mm.)

### Step 5 — NOT-GO Plug Gauge Effective Diameter Limits

The NOT-GO plug must NOT enter the nut. Its effective diameter is set at the **LMC** of the nut's effective diameter.

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^{NG-} &= D_2^{\max} - T_{R2} = 19.026 - 0.008 = 19.018 \text{ mm} \\
D_2^{NG+} &= D_2^{\max} = 19.026 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The **NOT-GO plug** has Effective Ø between **19.018 mm** and **19.026 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note the absence of a wear allowance on the NOT-GO gauge.** This is a deliberate design feature because the NOT-GO is not supposed to engage fully, so it experiences negligible wear.

### Step 6 — Summary of Designed Gauges

| Gauge | Function | Effective Ø Limits (mm) | Length of Engagement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **GO Plug** | Checks MMC of nut | 19.015 — 19.023 | Full form, 5–8 threads |
| **NOT-GO Plug** | Checks LMC of nut | 19.018 — 19.026 | Truncated, 3–4 threads |

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Design Parameter

| Design Decision | Effect on Gauge | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid vs Adjustable** | Solid = rigid, fixed; Adjustable = flexible, resettable | Solid is more accurate but expensive; Adjustable is cheaper but drifts |
| **Wear Allowance on GO only** | GO gauge can wear without immediate rejection of good parts | Extends service life of expensive GO gauge |
| **Truncation of NOT-GO** | Isolates effective Ø check from crest errors | Prevents jamming, gives flank-only contact |
| **Tolerance Grade 4, 6, or 8** | Grade 4 = tightest (for fine pitches); Grade 6 = standard; Grade 8 = loosest | Tighter grade = higher cost of gauge manufacture |
| **Length of engagement** | GO plug engages fully (5–8 threads); NOT-GO engages 3–4 threads | GO must self-centre; NOT-GO must be self-guiding |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->


## 4.3 Schematic Description of a Solid Thread Plug Gauge (ASCII)

> **Note:** Mermaid cannot easily render the helical thread profile. The following **annotated ASCII schematic** conveys the geometry a student must draw in the exam.

```
        GO END (Full Form)                          NOT-GO END (Truncated)
   |----------------------------------|        |------------------------|
   |  [Helical threads, 5-8 long]    |        |  [3-4 threads only,     |
   |  [Full crests + roots]          |        |   crests chamfered off]|
   |________________________________|        |________________________|
                \                                                  /
                 \   Wear allowance region (small)               /
                  \   ___________  ___________                 /
                   \_/  Handle  \/  Handle  \________________/
                                  (Knurled)
```

| Feature | GO End | NOT-GO End |
|---|---|---|
| Thread length | Long (5–8 pitches) | Short (3–4 pitches) |
| Crest form | Sharp, full profile | Truncated / chamfered |
| Function | Self-centring, flank contact | Flank-only contact, prevents jamming |
| Diameter | Set at MMC, includes wear allowance | Set at LMC, no wear allowance |

> [!NOTE]
> **In the exam**, students should draw the plug gauge with a **centre line, dimension lines** for GO and NOT-GO lengths, and **label** the truncated and full-form regions clearly. Mark distribution typically gives **2 marks** for the sketch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Series 1]**

**Q1. State Taylor's principle of gauging. How is it modified for screw thread gauging?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Taylor's Principle (1 mark):* A limit gauge should check only one dimension at a time. The GO gauge checks the **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** and the NOT-GO gauge checks the **Least Material Condition (LMC)**.
- *Modification for threads (2 marks):* Because a thread has multiple dimensions (major Ø, effective Ø, minor Ø, flank angle), the unmodified principle is applied in a **modified form**:
  - For an **external thread**: GO **ring** checks effective Ø at MMC; NOT-GO **snap gauge** checks the minor Ø (root diameter).
  - For an **internal thread**: GO **plug** checks the minor Ø + effective Ø at MMC; NOT-GO **truncated plug** checks the effective Ø at LMC only.
- The major Ø is implicitly controlled by the gauge's crest diameter; the flank angle is controlled by the gauge's profile accuracy.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why is the NOT-GO plug gauge for an internal thread made with a truncated (chamfered) profile?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. A full-form NOT-GO plug would **jam** on the crests of the nut thread rather than give a true effective Ø check. **(1 mark)**
2. Truncation removes the crests and roots, leaving only the **flanks** of the thread to make contact. **(1 mark)**
3. This ensures the NOT-GO plug checks the **effective diameter only**, in accordance with Taylor's principle — the NOT-GO gauge must check *one* dimension (effective Ø) and not be influenced by errors in the crest form. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of neat sketches, classify screw thread gauges. Explain the difference between **solid** and **adjustable** types of ring gauges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Classification (3 marks):**
Thread gauges are classified as:
- **External thread gauges**: Ring gauges (Solid / Adjustable), Snap gauges.
- **Internal thread gauges**: Plug gauges (Solid / Adjustable).

**Solid vs Adjustable Ring Gauge (4 marks):**

| Feature | Solid Ring Gauge | Adjustable Ring Gauge |
|---|---|---|
| Construction | Single piece of hardened gauge steel | Split ring, held by adjusting screw |
| Thread cutting | Thread cut directly into the bore | Thread cut into a split bush that can be expanded |
| Wear compensation | None — worn gauge must be replaced | Adjusting screw allows re-setting to nominal size |
| Accuracy | High, no backlash | Slightly lower, can drift with use |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Master gauges, production inspection | Workshop use, when wear is expected |

**Sketch (must include in exam):**
- **Solid Ring:** A plain annular ring with internal thread, knurled outer surface.
- **Adjustable Ring:** Same as solid, but with a **slit** at one side and a **grub screw** to expand/contract the bore.

---

**(b)** Design the GO and NOT-GO **ring gauges** for an external thread of size **M16 × 2.0** (ISO metric, Grade 6). State all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Given data (1 mark):**
- $D = 16$ mm, $P = 2.0$ mm, Grade 6
- From IS 4218 table (Grade 6, pitch 2.0):
  - $T_{R2} = 8$ μm = 0.008 mm
  - $W_{G2} = 6$ μm = 0.006 mm

**Step 2 — Effective Ø of the bolt (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2 &= D - 0.6495\,P = 16 - 0.6495 \times 2.0 \\
D_2 &= 16 - 1.299 = 14.701 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Assuming medium tolerance (tolerance position 'g' or symmetric) for the bolt:
- $D_2^{\min} = 14.701$ mm
- $D_2^{\max} = 14.710$ mm (allowance shown via standard tables; here, illustrative)

**Step 3 — GO Ring Gauge (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^{GO-} &= D_2^{\min} - W_{G2} = 14.701 - 0.006 = 14.695 \text{ mm} \\
D_2^{GO+} &= D_2^{GO-} + T_{R2} = 14.695 + 0.008 = 14.703 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — NOT-GO Ring Gauge (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^{NG-} &= D_2^{\max} - T_{R2} = 14.710 - 0.008 = 14.702 \text{ mm} \\
D_2^{NG+} &= D_2^{\max} = 14.710 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Result (1 mark):**

| Gauge | Effective Ø (mm) |
|---|---|
| GO Ring | 14.695 — 14.703 |
| NOT-GO Ring | 14.702 — 14.710 |

The GO ring has a wear allowance; the NOT-GO ring has none.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **modified Taylor's principle** as applied to screw thread gauging. With a neat sketch, show how an external thread (bolt) is checked using GO and NOT-GO ring gauges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Modified Taylor's Principle (3 marks):**
- For a thread, a single gauge cannot check all four dimensions (major, effective, minor, flank angle).
- Hence the principle is *modified*: the **GO gauge** checks the **maximum material condition** of the **effective diameter** with **full thread form**, while the **NOT-GO gauge** checks the **least material condition** of the **effective diameter** with **truncated form**.
- The **major diameter** of the bolt is controlled by the bore of the GO ring; the **minor diameter** is controlled by a separate **NOT-GO snap gauge**.

**Sketch Description (4 marks — must include in exam):**

```
     GO Ring Gauge              NOT-GO Ring Gauge
   (Full form, long)            (Full or truncated, short)
  ┌──────────────────┐         ┌──────────────────┐
  │ ╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱╱  │         │ ╱╱                │
  │╱  Internal       │         │╱ Internal thread  │
  │╲  thread, 6-8    │         │╲ 3-4 threads     │
  │ ╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲╲  │         │ ╲╲                │
  └──────────────────┘         └──────────────────┘
         ▲                            ▲
         │ Slip on freely             │ Must NOT slip
         │ (MMC of bolt)              │ (LMC of bolt)
```

**Procedure (mentioned in text):**
1. Slide the GO ring over the bolt — it should pass *freely* with light finger pressure.
2. Try the NOT-GO ring — it should *not* pass beyond 1–2 threads.
3. If both conditions are met, the bolt is accepted for effective diameter.

---

**(b)** A **NOT-GO plug gauge** for an internal thread has an effective diameter of **$19.026 \pm 0.008$ mm**. The internal thread to be checked has a basic effective diameter of **19.026 mm** with a tolerance of **0.040 mm** (symmetric). Determine whether the **NOT-GO plug** correctly implements Taylor's principle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Determine the limits of the internal thread's effective Ø (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_2^{\min} &= 19.026 - \frac{0.040}{2} = 19.026 - 0.020 = 19.006 \text{ mm} \\
D_2^{\max} &= 19.026 + \frac{0.040}{2} = 19.026 + 0.020 = 19.046 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Determine the limits of the NOT-GO plug (1 mark):**

The NOT-GO plug is a *gauge for the internal thread's LMC*:

$$
D_2^{NG-} = 19.018 \text{ mm}, \quad D_2^{NG+} = 19.026 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Check the principle (3 marks):**

For a properly designed NOT-GO plug, its **minimum effective diameter** must be **greater than or equal to** the **maximum effective diameter** of the nut:

$$
D_2^{NG-} \geq D_2^{\max} \;\text{ of nut}
$$

Substituting:

$$
19.018 \text{ mm} \not\geq 19.046 \text{ mm}
$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The NOT-GO plug as specified is **INCORRECTLY designed**. The minimum effective diameter of the NOT-GO plug (19.018 mm) is **less than** the maximum effective diameter of the nut (19.046 mm). This means the NOT-GO plug would *enter* the nut even when the nut's effective Ø is at its largest acceptable value, **violating Taylor's principle**. 

**Corrected NOT-GO plug design (from Section 3.2 example):**
- The correct NOT-GO plug should have $D_2^{NG-} = 19.018$ mm **for a tolerance of 0.020 mm** on the nut (i.e., $D_2^{\max} = 19.036$ mm for M20 × 1.5 with typical medium fit).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students **confuse the formulas** for GO and NOT-GO plug gauges. Remember: **wear allowance is on the GO gauge ONLY**.
> 2. Students often **omit the truncated profile** when drawing the NOT-GO gauge. The truncation is a 1-mark item that is frequently missed.
> 3. **Units mismatch** — read table values (in μm) carefully and convert to mm before arithmetic.
> 4. The **NOT-GO plug's lower limit** must be at or above the **workpiece's upper limit** — this is the most common point of confusion in design problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- [x] **Two families** of thread gauges: **Ring** (external) and **Plug** (internal).
- [x] **Solid** gauges cannot be re-set; **Adjustable** gauges can be re-set via screw.
- [x] **GO** gauge = **MMC** (max metal) = **wear allowance included** = **full form**.
- [x] **NOT-GO** gauge = **LMC** (min metal) = **no wear allowance** = **truncated form**.
- [x] **Truncation** of NOT-GO is essential to avoid jamming and to isolate effective Ø check.
- [x] **Modified Taylor's Principle**: Effective Ø checked by both GO and NOT-GO; crest Ø checked separately.
- [x] **Effective Ø formula** (ISO metric): $D_2 = D - 0.6495\,P$.
- [x] **Minor Ø formula** (ISO metric): $D_1 = D - 1.0825\,P$ (from sharp crest) or $D_1 = D - 1.2269\,P$ (from truncated crest).
- [x] **ISO 1502 / IS 4218** are the standards; three tolerance grades: 4 (tight), 6 (standard), 8 (loose).
- [x] For an **external thread**: NOT-GO uses a **snap gauge** to check the **minor Ø** of the bolt (root diameter).
- [x] For an **internal thread**: NOT-GO plug is **truncated** and checks the **effective Ø at LMC**.
- [x] **Wear allowance** is typically 10% of gauge tolerance, but is *tabulated* in the standard for each pitch.
- [x] **TPI of pitch** $P$: Effective Ø tolerance decreases as the thread becomes coarser.
- [x] In exam, **always draw the sketch first**, label the GO and NOT-GO ends, indicate truncation, and dimension the lengths.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Elements of External and Internal Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Elements of External and Internal Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Thread? — The Engineering Definition

A **screw thread** is a continuous helical ridge of uniform section (V-shaped, Acme, square, or buttress) cut on the external or internal surface of a cylindrical or conical body. Threads serve three primary engineering functions: **fastening** (bolt-nut assemblies), **motion transmission** (lead screws), and **sealing** (pipe threads).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PCPET503 Module 2):**
> The measurement of thread parameters is critical for **interchangeability**, **fit classification**, and **quality control** in mass-produced components. KTU expects you to know both *direct functional gauging* and *analytical measurement* methods.

## 1.2 Elements of a Thread — The Six Critical Parameters

A thread is fully defined by **six geometric elements**. KTU questions typically test your ability to identify, measure, and apply tolerances to these elements.

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Definition |
|---|-----------|--------|------------|
| 1 | **Major Diameter** | $D$ (ext) / $D_1$ (int) | Largest diameter (crest-to-crest) |
| 2 | **Minor Diameter** | $D_1$ (ext) / $D$ (int) | Smallest diameter (root-to-root) |
| 3 | **Pitch Diameter (Effective Diameter)** | $D_2$ or $E$ or $D_p$ | Diameter at which thread width = space width |
| 4 | **Pitch** | $P$ | Axial distance between adjacent crests |
| 5 | **Lead** | $L$ | Axial advance per revolution ($L = nP$ for n-start thread) |
| 6 | **Thread Angle (Included Angle)** | $\alpha$ | Angle between flanks (60° ISO, 55° BSW) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Spiral Staircase" Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a **cylindrical spiral staircase** wrapped around a shaft. The *treads* of the staircase are the **crests**, the *risers* form the **flanks**, and the *floor gap* between two adjacent treads is the **pitch (P)**. The *effective diameter* (pitch diameter) is like a "ghost cylinder" passing through the staircase exactly where the **tread width equals the gap width** — this is the diameter that controls the **fit** between a bolt and nut, just as the staircase radius controls the fit between a person and the handrail.

**Why is pitch diameter the most critical measurement?** Because **a thread can have a perfect major diameter yet fail to assemble** if the pitch diameter is off by a few microns. The major/minor diameters are merely "envelopes" — the **functional mating diameter** is the pitch diameter.

## 1.4 External vs. Internal Thread — The Mirror Image Principle

External threads (on a bolt) and internal threads (in a nut/hole) are **mirror images** with diameters swapped:

- **External thread:** Major diameter ($D$) > Pitch diameter ($D_2$) > Minor diameter ($D_1$)
- **Internal thread:** Minor diameter ($D_1$) > Pitch diameter ($D_2$) > Major diameter ($D$)

This mirror relationship is why the **measurement methods for internal threads are reverse-engineered** from external thread methods (e.g., a "plug gauge" is the internal equivalent of a "ring gauge").

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Thread Geometry Constants (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **ISO Metric thread angle:** $\alpha = 60°$, flank angle = $30°$
> - **BSW/BSF Whitworth thread angle:** $\alpha = 55°$, flank angle = $27.5°$
> - **Acme thread angle:** $\alpha = 29°$ (general purpose)
> - **Thread height for 60° thread:** $H = 0.86603 \times P$
> - **Truncation:** Crest and root are truncated at $H/8$ from the sharp V-apex
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geometric Construction of the Thread Profile

For an **ideal ISO metric (60°) external thread**, the profile is constructed as follows:

- Draw the major diameter line (top horizontal line of the thread profile).
- Drop perpendiculars at every pitch distance $P$.
- From each intersection, draw flanks at $\pm 30°$ from the vertical (perpendicular to thread axis).
- The intersection of opposing flanks gives the **minor diameter** at depth $H = 0.86603\,P$.

The **pitch diameter** lies at exactly half the thread height from the crest (and half from the root):

$$D_2 = D - 0.64952 \times P$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU memory shortcut:** For a 60° ISO thread, $D_2$ lies at $\frac{0.86603 P}{2} = 0.43301 P$ below the major diameter. So $D_2 = D - 0.43301 P$. The "magic number" **0.64952** ($= 0.86603 \times 0.75$) appears for the depth to the truncated minor diameter.

## 2.2 Methods of Measuring Threads — The Master Classification

KTU expects you to know **all four major methods** and when each is used:

### A. For **External Threads**

1. **Steel Rule + Vernier / Micrometer** — Major diameter only (rough check).
2. **Floating Carriage Micrometer (Three-Wire Method)** — **Most accurate** pitch diameter measurement. KTU's flagship topic.
3. **Optical Profile Projector** — Visual inspection of profile, pitch, and angle.
4. **Toolmaker's Microscope** — Pitch and flank angle measurement.
5. **Thread Ring Gauge (GO / NO-GO)** — Functional inspection (Taylor's principle).

### B. For **Internal Threads**

1. **Thread Plug Gauge (GO / NO-GO)** — Functional inspection.
2. **Small-Hole Gauge + Outside Micrometer** — Major diameter of internal thread.
3. **Internal Measuring Instrument with Three Wires** — Pitch diameter (rare, special).
4. **Optical Projection of a Cast / Replica** — Used when internal thread is inaccessible.
5. **Tapered Pipe Thread Gauges** — For pipe threads.

## 2.3 The Three-Wire Method — Conceptual Foundation

The **three-wire method** (also called the **best-wire method**) is the **most accurate** method of measuring the pitch diameter of an external thread. Three precision cylinders ("wires") of known diameter $d_w$ are placed:

- **One wire** in the V-groove on each side of the thread being measured.
- The micrometer anvils then contact the **outermost crests of the wires** (NOT the thread crests).
- The micrometer reading $M$ is the **"measurement over wires."**

The pitch diameter is calculated from $M$ using the thread's geometry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why three wires and not two?** Two wires make a single point contact on each flank, but the thread's **helical lead error** (pitch error along the axis) tilts the measurement plane and corrupts the reading. With **three wires**, the middle wire acts as a "support" that constrains the thread axially, while the floating carriage (in a Floating Carriage Micrometer) allows the thread to find its natural position — eliminating lead error.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for KTU Module 2 questions on thread measurement. Memorize the constants and conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Constants for 60° ISO | Constants for 55° Whitworth | Units |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|----------------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Best Wire Diameter** $d_w$ | $\displaystyle d_w = \frac{P}{2 \cos(\alpha/2)}$ | $d_w = \dfrac{P}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.57735\,P$ | $d_w = 0.56369\,P$ | mm |
| 2 | **Measurement Over Wires** $M$ (3-wire) | $\displaystyle M = D_2 + d_w\!\left[1 + \csc\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right] - \frac{P}{2}\cot\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | $M = D_2 + 3d_w - 0.86603\,P$ | $M = D_2 + 3.1659\,d_w - 0.96045\,P$ | mm |
| 3 | **Pitch Diameter** $D_2$ (from 3-wire) | $\displaystyle D_2 = M - d_w\!\left[1 + \csc\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right] + \frac{P}{2}\cot\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | $D_2 = M - 3d_w + 0.86603\,P$ | $D_2 = M - 3.1659\,d_w + 0.96045\,P$ | mm |
| 4 | **Single-Wire Method** (rare) | $D_2 = M_{\text{single}} - d_w\!\left[1 + \csc\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right] + \frac{P}{2}\cot\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | Same as above but $M$ is measured over one wire + thread crest | — | mm |
| 5 | **Two-Wire Method** (small threads) | $D_2 = M_{2w} - d_w\!\left[1 + \csc\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right] + \frac{P}{2}\cot\!\tfrac{\alpha}{2} + \text{correction}$ | Correction involves $P^2 / 8M_{2w}$ | — | mm |
| 6 | **Thread Height** $H$ | $H = \dfrac{P}{2}\cot\!\dfrac{\alpha}{2}$ | $H = 0.86603\,P$ | $H = 0.96049\,P$ | mm |
| 7 | **Minor Diameter (ext)** $D_1$ | $D_1 = D - 2H + \text{clearance}$ | $D_1 = D - 1.73205\,P$ (basic) | $D_1 = D - 1.92068\,P$ | mm |
| 8 | **Pitch Diameter (ext)** $D_2$ | $D_2 = D - H$ | $D_2 = D - 0.86603\,P$ (basic, before truncation) | — | mm |
| 9 | **Lead Error Compensation (FCM)** | None (floating carriage) | — | — | — |

**NOTE on table syntax:** Mathematical absolute-value and trigonometric operators are written using `\vert`, `\csc`, and `\cot` to avoid breaking the markdown pipe-syntax of the table.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three-wire method is the **de facto industry standard** in calibration laboratories worldwide. ISO 724 (ISO Metric Threads) and ASME B1.13M both specify the three-wire method with best-size wires as the **reference (calibration-grade) technique** for certifying thread plug and ring gauges. **Every calibrated thread gauge in your college's metrology lab was certified using exactly this method.**

Other domains:

- **Aerospace** (turbine shaft threads): Pitch diameter must be measured to ±2 µm.
- **Automotive** (engine block threads): GO/NO-GO ring gauges used on 100% of production bolts.
- **Hydraulics** (NPT pipe threads): Tapered thread gauges check both diameter and taper simultaneously.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Best Wire Diameter ($d_w$)

**Statement:** For a thread of included angle $\alpha$ and pitch $P$, the **best wire diameter** $d_w$ is the wire size that contacts the thread flanks **exactly at the pitch cylinder** (i.e., at the pitch diameter $D_2$).

### Setup (Right Half of the V-Groove Cross-Section)

Consider a 2D cross-section through the thread axis. Place the **apex of the V-groove** at the origin, with the V opening upward.

- The **right flank** of the V is a line through the origin making angle $(\pi/2 - \alpha/2)$ with the positive x-axis. Its slope is $\tan(90° - \alpha/2) = \cot(\alpha/2)$, so the flank is parameterized as:

$$(x, y) = (t \sin(\alpha/2),\ t \cos(\alpha/2)), \quad t \geq 0$$

- A **wire of radius $r_w = d_w/2$** sits in the V with its center on the y-axis at $(0, h)$.
- The wire makes contact at a point on the right flank. The line from the contact point to the wire center is **perpendicular to the flank** (purely normal contact — no friction is assumed in the geometric model).
- The **outward-pointing unit normal** to the right flank, pointing into the V (toward the y-axis), is:

$$\hat{n} = \left(-\cos(\alpha/2),\ \sin(\alpha/2)\right)$$

### Geometric Equations

**Step 1 — Express contact point in terms of $r_w$ and $h$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
(0, h) &= (x_c, y_c) + r_w \cdot \hat{n} \\
       &= (x_c, y_c) + r_w \left(-\cos(\alpha/2),\ \sin(\alpha/2)\right)
\end{aligned}$$

Equating components:

$$x_c = r_w \cos(\alpha/2) \quad \text{(A)}$$

$$y_c = h - r_w \sin(\alpha/2) \quad \text{(B)}$$

**Step 2 — Contact point lies on the right flank:** the flank satisfies $y = x \cot(\alpha/2)$, hence:

$$y_c = x_c \cot(\alpha/2) = x_c \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

Substituting (A):

$$y_c = r_w \cos(\alpha/2) \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = r_w \cdot \frac{\cos^2(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)} \quad \text{(C)}$$

**Step 3 — Equate (B) and (C) to solve for $h$:**

$$h - r_w \sin(\alpha/2) = r_w \cdot \frac{\cos^2(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

$$h = r_w \left[\sin(\alpha/2) + \frac{\cos^2(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)}\right]$$

$$h = r_w \cdot \frac{\sin^2(\alpha/2) + \cos^2(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{r_w}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

$$\boxed{h = \frac{d_w}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)}}$$

This is the **height of the wire center above the V-apex**.

**Step 4 — "Best wire" condition:** the contact point must lie on the **pitch cylinder**. The pitch cylinder is at a height $H/2$ above the V-apex (i.e., halfway between the crest and the root for a symmetric V-thread), where $H$ is the thread height:

$$H = \frac{P}{2} \cot(\alpha/2)$$

So $y_c = H/2 = \dfrac{P}{4} \cot(\alpha/2) = \dfrac{P}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\cos(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$

**Step 5 — Combine with (C) and solve for $d_w$:**

$$r_w \cdot \frac{\cos^2(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)} = \frac{P}{4} \cdot \frac{\cos(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$$

Cancel $\dfrac{\cos(\alpha/2)}{\sin(\alpha/2)}$ from both sides (assuming $\alpha \neq 180°$, which is trivially true):

$$r_w \cos(\alpha/2) = \frac{P}{4}$$

$$d_w = 2 r_w = \frac{P}{2 \cos(\alpha/2)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Result for 60° ISO thread ($\alpha = 60°$):**

$$d_w = \frac{P}{2 \cos(30°)} = \frac{P}{2 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.57735\,P$$

**Result for 55° Whitworth thread ($\alpha = 55°$):**

$$d_w = \frac{P}{2 \cos(27.5°)} \approx 0.56369\,P$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Measurement Over Wires ($M$)

**Statement:** The reading $M$ of a micrometer over three best-size wires equals the pitch diameter $D_2$ plus an additive correction that depends on the wire diameter and the thread angle.

### Setup

From the cross-section, the **outermost point of the wire** is the **top of the wire** (its highest point), at height $h + r_w$ above the V-apex:

$$y_{\text{top of wire}} = h + r_w = \frac{d_w}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)} + \frac{d_w}{2} = \frac{d_w}{2}\left[\frac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)} + 1\right] = \frac{d_w}{2}\left[1 + \csc(\alpha/2)\right]$$

The **crest of the thread** (major diameter) is at height $H$ above the V-apex (since the V-apex is at the root for an external thread):

$$y_{\text{crest}} = H = \frac{P}{2} \cot(\alpha/2)$$

The **micrometer measurement** $M$ is the distance across the **outermost points** of the two outer wires (i.e., from the top of the left wire to the top of the right wire):

$$M = 2 \times y_{\text{top of wire}} - 0 + (\text{horizontal offset correction, which cancels for symmetric thread})$$

Actually, in 3D, $M$ is measured along the **diameter perpendicular to the thread axis** at the wire locations. By symmetry:

$$M = 2 \times y_{\text{top of wire, measured radially}}$$

The "outermost point" of the wire is offset from the thread axis by:

$$\text{Offset from V-apex (which is on the pitch cylinder)} = h + r_w = \frac{d_w}{2}\left[1 + \csc(\alpha/2)\right]$$

But the **diameter** $D_2$ is measured on the **pitch cylinder**, which is at $H/2$ above the V-apex (not at the V-apex itself). The **radial difference** between the top of the wire and the pitch cylinder is:

$$\Delta r = (h + r_w) - \frac{H}{2}$$

For a wire on the **right side** of the thread (at angle $0°$ from the horizontal radius), the rightmost extent is at $x = r_w \sin(\alpha/2)$ from the V-axis... 

**A simpler, classical derivation (used in KTU textbooks):**

Consider the right-half of the V-groove. Draw the pitch cylinder as a horizontal line at height $H/2$. The wire sits with its center at $(0, h)$. The **outermost point of the wire** is at horizontal distance $r_w$ from the wire center (since the wire is a circle of radius $r_w$).

The rightmost extent of the wire has x-coordinate $r_w$ (the wire is to the right of its center, with the center on the y-axis, and the rightmost point is at $x = r_w$). This is the point contacted by the micrometer anvil.

The pitch diameter is measured at the **horizontal line** $y = H/2$. At this line, the right flank is at x-coordinate:

$$x_{\text{flank at } y=H/2} = \frac{H/2}{\cot(\alpha/2)} = \frac{H}{2} \tan(\alpha/2) = \frac{P}{2} \tan(\alpha/2) \cdot \frac{\tan(\alpha/2)}{\text{(rearranging)}}$$

Actually, the radius at the pitch cylinder is $D_2/2$ (half the pitch diameter). The wire's outermost point's x-coordinate is the radius at which the micrometer reads, so $M/2 = r_{\text{wire, outermost}}$.

The radius from the thread axis to the wire's outermost point = radius to wire center + horizontal offset of outermost point from wire center.

The wire center is at $r_{\text{axis to wire center}}$ from the thread axis. The V-apex of the groove where this wire sits is at the root, so the V-apex is at radius $D_1/2$ from the thread axis (for external thread, $D_1$ is the minor diameter, and the V-apex of the groove is at the minor diameter).

So $r_{\text{wire center}} = D_1/2 + h$.

The rightmost point of the wire is at $r_{\text{wire center}} + r_w = D_1/2 + h + r_w$.

$M/2 = D_1/2 + h + r_w$

$M = D_1 + 2h + 2r_w = D_1 + 2(h + r_w)$

Now we need to express this in terms of $D_2$ (pitch diameter), not $D_1$ (minor diameter).

The pitch diameter $D_2$ is at the pitch line, which is at $H/2$ from the crest (or $H/2$ from the root). So:

$$D_2 = D_1 + H \quad \text{(for external thread, pitch diameter is between minor and major, with H being the full thread height)}$$

Wait, for a 60° thread, the pitch diameter is at $H/2$ from crest AND $H/2$ from root? Yes, for a symmetric V-thread:
- $D = D_1 + H$ (full thread height from minor to major)
- $D_2 = D_1 + H/2$ (pitch diameter is at $H/2$ from the minor)

So $D_1 = D_2 - H/2$.

Substituting:

$$M = (D_2 - H/2) + 2(h + r_w)$$

$$M = D_2 + 2(h + r_w) - H/2$$

Now substitute $h = \dfrac{d_w}{2 \sin(\alpha/2)}$ and $r_w = d_w/2$:

$$2(h + r_w) = 2\left[\frac{d_w}{2\sin(\alpha/2)} + \frac{d_w}{2}\right] = d_w\left[\frac{1}{\sin(\alpha/2)} + 1\right] = d_w[1 + \csc(\alpha/2)]$$

And $H/2 = \dfrac{P}{4} \cot(\alpha/2)$.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{M = D_2 + d_w\left[1 + \csc(\alpha/2)\right] - \frac{P}{2}\cot(\alpha/2)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**For 60° thread:**

$$M = D_2 + d_w\left[1 + \frac{1}{\sin 30°}\right] - \frac{P}{2}\cot 30°$$

$$= D_2 + d_w[1 + 2] - \frac{P}{2} \cdot \sqrt{3}$$

$$= D_2 + 3 d_w - 0.86603\,P$$

**Inverting to find $D_2$:**

$$\boxed{D_2 = M - 3 d_w + 0.86603\,P} \quad \text{(60° ISO thread)}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Typical KTU Numerical

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style problem:** An external M20 × 2.5 ISO metric thread is measured over three best-size wires using a floating carriage micrometer. The reading is **$M = 21.420$ mm**. Calculate the **pitch diameter** and the **deviation from nominal**.

**Given:**
- Thread: M20 × 2.5 → Nominal major diameter $D = 20$ mm, Pitch $P = 2.5$ mm, $\alpha = 60°$
- Measurement: $M = 21.420$ mm

**Step 1 — Best wire diameter:**

$$d_w = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2.5}{1.73205} = 1.4434 \text{ mm}$$

(Standard wire sizes nearest: 1.40 mm or 1.45 mm. Use exact calculation for KTU.)

**Step 2 — Pitch diameter:**

$$D_2 = M - 3 d_w + 0.86603 P$$

$$D_2 = 21.420 - 3(1.4434) + 0.86603(2.5)$$

$$D_2 = 21.420 - 4.3302 + 2.1651 = 19.2549 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Nominal pitch diameter:**

$$D_{2,\text{nominal}} = D - 0.64952 P = 20 - 0.64952(2.5) = 20 - 1.6238 = 18.3762 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Deviation:**

$$\Delta D_2 = 19.2549 - 18.3762 = +0.8787 \text{ mm (very large — thread is undersized in M-D sense)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** A common error is to use $D_2 = D - 0.64952 P$ as the basic pitch diameter, but this is the **truncated** (basic profile) value. For **tolerance calculations**, use the **ISO 965 tolerance tables** instead of this basic formula. The basic formula is only for **approximate checks**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods

| Method | Accuracy | Best For | Limitation |
|--------|----------|----------|------------|
| **Vernier / Micrometer (major diameter)** | $\pm 10\,\mu m$ | Quick check, field use | Cannot measure pitch diameter |
| **Floating Carriage Micrometer (3-wire)** | $\pm 2\,\mu m$ | **Calibration lab**, pitch diameter | Requires best-size wires, computation |
| **Optical Profile Projector** | $\pm 5\,\mu m$ | Profile, angle, pitch inspection | Subjective visual reading |
| **Toolmaker's Microscope** | $\pm 3\,\mu m$ | Pitch, flank angle | Slow, skilled operator |
| **Thread Ring Gauge (GO/NO-GO)** | Functional only | 100% production check | Only GO/NO-GO binary answer |
| **Internal Thread Plug Gauge (GO/NO-GO)** | Functional only | 100% production check | Same as above |
| **Internal 3-wire (special equipment)** | $\pm 5\,\mu m$ | Large internal threads | Rare equipment, complex |

## 4.2 Functional Flow of Thread Measurement — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

        A1["Identify External or Internal"] --> A2["Identify Thread Standard: ISO 60 / Whitworth 55 / Acme / Buttress"]
        A2 --> A3["Extract Pitch P and Nominal Diameter D from marking"]
    end

    subgraph SELECT["STEP 2: SELECT MEASUREMENT METHOD"]
        B1{"Component is External or Internal?"}
        B1 -- External --> B2["Best method: 3-Wire FCM for pitch diameter"]
        B1 -- Internal --> B3["Best method: GO/NO-GO plug gauge for functional check"]
        B2 --> B4["Choose best wire size dw = P divided by 2 cos alpha by 2"]
        B3 --> B5["Or use small-hole gauge plus micrometer for major diameter"]
    end

    subgraph MEASURE["STEP 3: ACQUIRE MEASUREMENT"]
        C1["Place 3 best wires in V grooves"] --> C2["Use floating carriage micrometer"]
        C2 --> C3["Read M value over wires"]
        C3 --> C4["Apply formula D2 = M minus 3 dw plus 0.866 P for 60 deg"]
    end

    subgraph VERIFY["STEP 4: VERIFY AGAINST TOLERANCE"]
        D1["Look up ISO 965 tolerance class"] --> D2["Compute deviation from nominal D2"]
        D2 --> D3{"Accept or Reject"}
        D3 -- Accept --> D4["Component passes QC"]
        D3 -- Reject --> D5["Component scrapped or reworked"]
    end

    CLASS --> SELECT
    SELECT --> MEASURE
```

## 4.3 The Three-Wire Method — Measurement Setup Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SETUP["THREE WIRE METHOD GEOMETRY"]
        direction TB

        W1["WIRE 1 --- diameter dw --- sits in V groove LEFT side"] --> CONTACT1["Contact point on left flank --- on pitch cylinder"]
        W2["WIRE 2 --- same diameter dw --- sits in middle V groove --- AXIAL SUPPORT --- eliminates lead error"] --> CONTACT2["Contact point on middle flank"]
        W3["WIRE 3 --- same diameter dw --- sits in V groove RIGHT side"] --> CONTACT3["Contact point on right flank --- on pitch cylinder"]

        CONTACT1 --> MIC["MICROMETER ANVILS"]
        CONTACT2 --> MIC
        CONTACT3 --> MIC

        MIC --> READ["Reading M = distance over outermost wire points"]

        THREAD["EXTERNAL THREAD --- nominal D = 20 mm, P = 2.5 mm, 60 deg ISO"] -.supports.-> W1
        THREAD -.supports.-> W2
        THREAD -.supports.-> W3
    end

    style W2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:3px
    style MIC fill:#87CEEB
    style READ fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **middle wire (Wire 2)** is highlighted in **gold** because it is the **key innovation** of the three-wire method. In a **Floating Carriage Micrometer (FCM)**, this middle wire sits in a "floating" anvil that moves axially — it locates the thread axially without constraining it rotationally. This **eliminates the lead-angle error** that would otherwise corrupt a two-wire measurement.

## 4.4 GO / NO-GO Gauge Concept — Taylor's Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GO["GO GAUGE --- FULL FORM --- checks all features at MAX material"]
        G1["Full thread form"] --> G2["Pitch diameter at HIGH limit"]
        G2 --> G3["Major diameter at HIGH limit for external ring"]
    end

    subgraph NOGO["NO GO GAUGE --- TRUNCATED --- checks ONE feature at MIN material"]
        N1["Truncated thread form --- relief on leading threads"] --> N2["Pitch diameter at LOW limit"]
        N2 --> N3["Checks pitch diameter ONLY for external"]
    end

    GO --> RESULT{"Both gauges pass?"}
    NOGO --> RESULT
    RESULT -- YES --> ACC["ACCEPT --- component within tolerance"]
    RESULT -- NO --> REJ["REJECT --- component out of tolerance"]

    style GO fill:#90EE90
    style NOGO fill:#FFB6C1
    style ACC fill:#006400,color:#FFFFFF
    style REJ fill:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Taylor's Principle (Full + Truncated Gauges):** The **GO gauge** has a **full thread profile** and checks the **maximum-metal condition** (all features at their tightest limit simultaneously). The **NO-GO gauge** has a **truncated profile** (chamfered/relief on the leading threads) and checks **only the pitch diameter at its loosest limit**. This separates functional checks from individual feature checks, following the **Taylor Hobson principle** that revolutionized metrology in the early 20th century.

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Definition Question `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 similar]`

**Q: Define the following terms with neat sketches: (i) Pitch diameter, (ii) Lead, (iii) Thread angle.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Pitch Diameter ($D_2$ or $E$):** The pitch diameter (also called the **effective diameter**) of a thread is the diameter of an imaginary cylinder that intersects the thread profile such that the **width of the thread and the width of the space** cut by this cylinder are **equal**. Mathematically, for an ISO 60° external thread:
>
> $$D_2 = D - 0.64952 P$$
>
> where $D$ is the major diameter and $P$ is the pitch. The pitch diameter is the **most functionally critical** dimension because it controls the **fit** between mating threaded parts.
>
> **(ii) Lead ($L$):** The lead of a thread is the **axial distance traveled by a thread** (or by a nut) in **one complete revolution** ($360°$ rotation). For a **single-start thread**, the lead equals the pitch:
>
> $$L = P$$
>
> For a **multi-start thread** with $n$ starts:
>
> $$L = n \times P$$
>
> **(iii) Thread Angle ($\alpha$):** The thread angle is the **included angle between the two flanks** of the thread profile, measured in a plane containing the thread axis. Common values:
>
> - **ISO Metric (M):** $\alpha = 60°$
> - **BSW / BSF (Whitworth):** $\alpha = 55°$
> - **Acme:** $\alpha = 29°$

### Question A2 — Short Conceptual Question `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: What is the "best wire" in the three-wire method of thread measurement? Why is it called "best"?**

**Model Answer:**

> In the three-wire method, the **"best wire"** is the wire of **optimum diameter** $d_w$ that, when placed in the thread groove, makes contact with the thread flanks **exactly at the pitch cylinder** (pitch diameter line).
>
> **Why "best"?** Any wire **larger than the best wire** will contact the flanks **above** the pitch line, giving a **larger** $D_2$ reading than the true pitch diameter. Any wire **smaller than the best wire** will contact **below** the pitch line, giving a **smaller** $D_2$. **Only the best wire** gives the true pitch diameter, and **more importantly**, the measurement $M$ becomes **insensitive to small errors in wire diameter** at this optimum size. The mathematical formula for the best wire diameter is:
>
> $$d_w = \frac{P}{2 \cos(\alpha/2)}$$
>
> For a 60° ISO thread, $d_w = P/\sqrt{3} = 0.57735\,P$. For a 55° Whitworth thread, $d_w = 0.56369\,P$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A — Floating Carriage Micrometer & Thread Parameter Calculation `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is a **Floating Carriage Micrometer (FCM)**? Explain its working principle with a neat sketch. Why is it preferred over a standard micrometer for measuring the pitch diameter of an external thread? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An external **M30 × 3.5** ISO metric thread is measured using the three-wire method. The best-size wires are used and the micrometer reading is found to be **$M = 32.815$ mm**. Calculate:
   (i) The best wire diameter $d_w$
   (ii) The actual pitch diameter $D_2$
   (iii) The deviation of the measured pitch diameter from the nominal value **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Floating Carriage Micrometer (7 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
A **Floating Carriage Micrometer (FCM)** is a specialized precision measuring instrument used to determine the **pitch diameter** of an external thread to a high degree of accuracy (typically ±2 µm). It combines the principle of the **three-wire method** with a **floating anvil (carriage)** that holds the middle wire and can move axially without constraining the thread.

**Construction (2 marks):**
- It consists of a **micrometer head** (similar to an external micrometer) but with a **special V-shaped anvil** and a **floating carriage** in place of the fixed anvil.
- The **floating carriage** holds one of the three wires and can slide axially along the thread axis.
- The micrometer spindle is replaced by a **movable anvil** that contacts the outermost (right) wire.
- **Three best-size wires** are used: one on the left flank, one in the middle (held by the floating carriage), and one on the right flank.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
1. The thread is placed between the anvils with the **three wires** resting in three V-grooves.
2. The micrometer is closed gently until the anvil contacts the outermost wire.
3. The **floating carriage** allows the middle wire to **self-locate** along the thread axis, accommodating the thread's **helix (lead) error** automatically.
4. The reading $M$ over the wires is taken from the micrometer thimble.
5. The pitch diameter is calculated as $D_2 = M - 3 d_w + 0.86603 P$ (for 60° thread).

**Why FCM is preferred (1 mark):**
- **Eliminates lead-angle error:** The floating carriage accommodates the helical geometry of the thread, which a rigid two-wire or standard micrometer cannot.
- **Highest accuracy** (±2 µm vs ±10 µm for a standard micrometer).
- **Direct calculation of pitch diameter** with no need for chart lookups.
- **Used as the reference method** in calibration labs for certifying thread gauges (per ISO 724 and ASME B1.13M).

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Thread: M30 × 3.5 → $D = 30$ mm, $P = 3.5$ mm, $\alpha = 60°$
- Measurement: $M = 32.815$ mm

**(i) Best wire diameter (2 marks):**

$$d_w = \frac{P}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{3.5}{1.73205} = 2.0207 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Actual pitch diameter (3 marks):**

$$D_2 = M - 3 d_w + 0.86603 P$$

$$D_2 = 32.815 - 3(2.0207) + 0.86603(3.5)$$

$$D_2 = 32.815 - 6.0621 + 3.0311 = 29.7840 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Deviation from nominal (2 marks):**

Nominal pitch diameter (basic):

$$D_{2,\text{nominal}} = D - 0.64952 P = 30 - 0.64952(3.5) = 30 - 2.2733 = 27.7267 \text{ mm}$$

Deviation:

$$\Delta D_2 = D_{2,\text{actual}} - D_{2,\text{nominal}} = 29.7840 - 27.7267 = +2.0573 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** The measured pitch diameter is **2.0573 mm larger** than the nominal (basic) value, indicating the thread is **severely undersized in material** (i.e., the V-grooves are cut too deep or the wire measurement is anomalous). This thread would **not assemble** with a standard M30 nut.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 0.86603 P term** in the pitch diameter formula. Many students incorrectly write $D_2 = M - 3 d_w$, missing the pitch correction. (-2 marks)
> 2. **Using nominal wire diameter** instead of the calculated best wire. If the question specifies "best-size wires," always calculate $d_w = P/\sqrt{3}$ first. (-1 mark)
> 3. **Unit errors** — keeping $d_w$ and $M$ in consistent mm units throughout. (-0.5 mark)
> 4. **Wrong interpretation of deviation** — saying "thread is oversized" when it should be "pitch diameter is larger than nominal" (the thread is undersized in functional terms). (-1 mark)
> 5. **Forgetting to identify $\alpha = 60°$** for ISO metric. (-1 mark)

---

### Question Choice B — Thread Gauges & Internal Thread Measurement `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the construction and use of **GO and NO-GO thread ring gauges** for external threads and **GO and NO-GO thread plug gauges** for internal threads. State **Taylor's principle** for gauge design. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the methods used for measuring the **major diameter**, **minor diameter**, and **pitch diameter** of an **internal thread**. Compare these methods with those used for an **external thread** of the same size. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — GO/NO-GO Thread Gauges (7 Marks)

**External Thread — Ring Gauges (3 marks):**
- A **GO thread ring gauge** has a **full-form threaded bore** with the **high limit (maximum material)** pitch diameter and the **high limit major diameter** of the external thread.
- A **NO-GO thread ring gauge** has a **truncated thread profile** (chamfered/relief on the entry side) and checks only the **pitch diameter at the low limit** (minimum material).
- The external thread is **accepted only if it passes through the GO gauge** (full engagement by hand, no forcing) **and does NOT pass through the NO-GO gauge** (rejected even by a light force).

**Internal Thread — Plug Gauges (2 marks):**
- A **GO thread plug gauge** has a **full-form threaded cylinder** with the **low limit pitch diameter** and the **low limit minor diameter** of the internal thread (these are the maximum material conditions for the internal thread, since the nut has minimum material removed).
- A **NO-GO thread plug gauge** has a **truncated thread profile** and checks only the **pitch diameter at the high limit** of the internal thread.
- The internal thread is **accepted only if the GO plug gauge enters freely** and the **NO-GO plug gauge does not enter beyond the chamfer relief**.

**Taylor's Principle (2 marks):**
**Taylor's Principle** (formulated by **William Taylor**, 1905) states that a **GO gauge** should check **all related features of the part at maximum material condition simultaneously** (full form), while a **NO-GO gauge** should check **only one feature — the most critical one — at minimum material condition** (truncated form). This principle:
- Mimics the **functional mating conditions** of the part.
- Allows **separation of form errors from size errors**.
- Prevents the "summation of tolerances" error where small individual errors combine to cause functional failure.
- Is the **basis of all modern GO/NO-GO gauging** per ISO 1502 and ASME B1.2.

### Part (b) — Internal Thread Measurement Methods (7 Marks)

| Element | Internal Thread Method | External Thread Method | Comparison |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|------------|
| **Major Diameter** | **Small-hole gauge + outside micrometer:** The small-hole gauge (a split ball) is expanded inside the internal thread's major diameter, locked, removed, and measured with a micrometer. Or a **bore gauge** with a 3-point contact. | **Outside micrometer** (direct). | Internal method is **indirect** (transfer gauge) and **less accurate** (±10–20 µm vs ±2 µm). |
| **Minor Diameter** | **Plug gauge (GO end)** checks the minimum material condition; **taper-gauge or optical projector** for precise measurement. | **Pitch micrometer** with conical anvils, or **optical projector**. | Internal measurement is more difficult because the minor diameter is at the **bottom of the V-groove** (less accessible). |
| **Pitch Diameter** | **GO/NO-GO plug gauge** (functional); for precise measurement, use **internal thread measuring wires with a special internal micrometer** (rare, expensive). | **Floating Carriage Micrometer (3-wire method)** is the gold standard (±2 µm). | Internal thread pitch diameter measurement is **less accurate** and **more time-consuming** than external. The three-wire method is impractical for internal threads due to space constraints. |
| **Pitch & Angle** | **Cast / Replica + Optical Projector:** A negative replica of the internal thread is cast using a soft material, then measured under a projector. | **Toolmaker's Microscope** or **Optical Projector** directly. | Replica method is **indirect** and adds measurement uncertainty. |
| **Lead** | **Lead-measuring machine with stylus tracing the thread helix.** | **Lead-measuring machine** with a stylus tracing the thread helix. | Similar method, similar accuracy. |

**Summary Comparison (final 1 mark):**
- **External thread measurement** is **direct, accurate, and well-established** (3-wire FCM is the gold standard).
- **Internal thread measurement** is **inherently indirect** because the V-groove is inaccessible to direct contact. The most reliable functional check is the **GO/NO-GO plug gauge**. Precise pitch-diameter measurement of internal threads remains one of the **most challenging tasks in dimensional metrology**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Thread Measurement (Module 2):**

### A. Six Thread Elements (MUST know definitions)
- [ ] Major diameter $D$ — largest diameter (outer for external, inner for internal)
- [ ] Minor diameter $D_1$ — smallest diameter (root for external, outer for internal)
- [ ] Pitch diameter $D_2$ — effective diameter, where thread = space (MOST CRITICAL)
- [ ] Pitch $P$ — axial distance between adjacent crests
- [ ] Lead $L$ — axial advance per revolution; $L = nP$ for $n$-start thread
- [ ] Thread angle $\alpha$ — $60°$ ISO, $55°$ Whitworth

### B. Three-Wire Method Formulas (MUST memorize)
- [ ] Best wire: $d_w = P / (2 \cos(\alpha/2))$ → $0.57735 P$ for 60°
- [ ] Measurement over wires: $M = D_2 + d_w[1 + \csc(\alpha/2)] - (P/2)\cot(\alpha/2)$
- [ ] For 60°: $M = D_2 + 3d_w - 0.86603 P$
- [ ] Pitch diameter: $D_2 = M - 3d_w + 0.86603 P$ (60° ISO)
- [ ] For 55° Whitworth: $D_2 = M - 3.1659 d_w + 0.96045 P$

### C. Key Methods (MUST know application)
- [ ] **External thread pitch diameter** → Floating Carriage Micrometer (3-wire)
- [ ] **External thread functional check** → GO/NO-GO Ring Gauge
- [ ] **Internal thread functional check** → GO/NO-GO Plug Gauge
- [ ] **Internal thread major diameter** → Small-hole gauge + micrometer
- [ ] **Internal thread profile** → Cast replica + optical projector
- [ ] **Pitch and flank angle** → Toolmaker's microscope / optical projector

### D. Magic Constants for 60° ISO Thread (MUST memorize)
- [ ] $\cos 30° = 0.86603$
- [ ] $1/\cos 30° = 1.15470$
- [ ] $1/\sin 30° = 2.00000$
- [ ] $\cot 30° = 1.73205 = \sqrt{3}$
- [ ] Thread height $H = 0.86603 P$
- [ ] Pitch diameter offset from major: $0.64952 P$ (truncated) or $0.86603 P$ (basic, sharp V)

### E. Taylor's Principle
- [ ] **GO gauge:** Full form, checks ALL features at maximum material
- [ ] **NO-GO gauge:** Truncated form, checks ONE feature (pitch diameter) at minimum material

### F. KTU Common Pitfalls (AVOID these mistakes)
- [ ] Don't forget the $0.86603 P$ term in pitch-diameter calculation.
- [ ] Don't confuse 60° (ISO) with 55° (Whitworth) — the constants differ.
- [ ] Don't use ring gauge terminology for internal threads (it's "plug gauge" for internal).
- [ ] When the question says "best-size wires," always calculate $d_w$ — don't assume a value.
- [ ] The pitch diameter is the **most critical** parameter for thread fit, NOT the major diameter.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread Calliper Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thread Calliper Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Thread Calliper Gauge** (also known as a **Thread Snap Gauge** or **Screw Thread Snap Gauge**) is a fixed-limit gauging instrument used for the rapid inspection of external screw threads. It consists of a rigid frame carrying two pairs of precisely shaped anvils — one pair representing the **GO** member (major diameter / pitch diameter limit) and the other representing the **NO-GO** member (effective diameter / minor flank clearance). Each anvil is contoured with the inverse profile of the thread so that contact is established only on the thread flanks at the pitch line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Thread calliper gauges belong to the family of *fixed gauges* under the **Acceptance Gauge** system defined by Taylor's Principle. They perform a **function-type** inspection (Go/No-Go) rather than a *variable-type* measurement.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you have two plastic moulds shaped like an *inverted comb* — one big comb and one small comb. To check whether a threaded bolt is "good", you simply slide the **big comb** (GO gauge) over the threads; if it fits smoothly, the major diameter is acceptable. Then you try the **small comb** (NO-GO gauge); it should **NOT** slide over more than 2–3 turns. If it does, the thread is oversized and **rejected**. The thread calliper gauge works exactly on this principle — instead of checking the pitch (comb spacing) one bolt at a time, it checks the **flank profile limits** instantly.

## 1.3 Classification of Thread Calliper Gauges

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Solid (Fixed) Thread Snap Gauge** | One-piece frame, anvils are integral, ground to a single nominal size | High-volume production of identical threaded components |
| **Adjustable Thread Snap Gauge** | Anvils held by screws, can be set to nominal limits with setting standards | Job-shop production with varying thread sizes |
| **Indicating Thread Snap Gauge** | Uses a dial indicator or air gauge for variable readout | Where actual pitch diameter value is required |
| **Thread Ring Calliper (Internal)** | Used for checking internal threads on the shop floor | Tapped holes / nuts |

## 1.4 Standard Symbols & Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values (as per ISO 965 / IS 4218):**
> - Thread angle $\alpha = \mathbf{60^{\circ}}$ (Metric ISO / Unified)
> - Whitworth angle $\alpha = \mathbf{55^{\circ}}$
> - Effective (Pitch) Diameter $\,=\, D_2$
> - Major Diameter $\,=\, D$
> - Minor Diameter $\,=\, D_1$
> - Pitch $\,=\, P$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Anatomy of a Thread Calliper Gauge

A standard **External Thread Calliper Gauge** consists of:

1. **C-shaped Frame** — Rigid, hardened steel body (typically Rc 60–62).
2. **GO Anvils** — Two anvils shaped as **complementary thread profiles** separated by the GO pitch diameter limit. The anvils bear on the **crests** of the GO thread and **flanks** of the NO-GO thread.
3. **NO-GO Anvils** — Two cylindrical or single-flank anvils separated by the minor diameter limit. They allow easy sliding over the threads without flank interference.
4. **Handle / Hub** — Provides grip and balances the gauge.
5. **Identification Stamp** — Engraved with thread designation (e.g., `M20 × 2.5 6H`).

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 → Anvil Engagement:** The thread is rolled between the two opposing anvils. The thread crests contact the roots of the gauge profile and the flanks of the thread contact the flanks of the gauge profile.

**Step 2 → Force Application:** A controlled, light hand-force (approx. **5–10 N**) is applied perpendicular to the bolt axis. Excess force causes elastic deformation of the thread, leading to false acceptance.

**Step 3 → Limit Assessment:**
- If the **GO** side slides freely over the entire thread length ⇒ **Major & Pitch diameter ≤ Upper Limit** ⇒ ACCEPT.
- If the **NO-GO** side refuses to enter beyond **2 to 3 threads** ⇒ **Pitch Diameter ≥ Lower Limit** ⇒ ACCEPT.
- Both conditions satisfied ⇒ **Thread is within tolerance band** ⇒ PASS.

**Step 4 → Decision Logic (Taylor's Principle):**
$$
\text{WORKPIECE} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{ACCEPT}, & \text{if GO passes AND NO-GO does not pass} \\
\text{REJECT}, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

## 2.3 Underlying 'Why' of the Design

> [!NOTE]
> The two anvils are NOT ground to the *nominal* pitch diameter — they are ground to the **GO** and **NO-GO** **size limits** derived from the tolerance grade. This is why a thread calliper gauge is *size-specific* and cannot be re-used for other nominal sizes without re-machining.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $D_2 = D - 0.6495\,P$ | Pitch diameter from major diameter (60° thread) | mm |
| 2 | $D_1 = D - 1.0825\,P$ | Minor diameter from major diameter | mm |
| 3 | $\text{Tol}_{D_2} = 90 \cdot P^{0.4} \cdot d^{0.1}$ | ISO Pitch diameter tolerance (Grade 6) | µm |
| 4 | $\text{Wear Allowance} = 0.05\,\sqrt{P^3}$ | Allowance on GO plug (ISO) | mm |
| 5 | $M = D_2 + d\!\left[1 + \csc\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)\right] - \tfrac{P}{2}\cot\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | Three-wire measurement over wires | mm |
| 6 | $d_0 = \tfrac{P}{2}\sec\!\left(\tfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | Best-wire size for 60° thread | mm |
| 7 | $D_2 = M - 3d_0 + 0.86603\,P$ | Pitch diameter (60° thread, best wire) | mm |
| 8 | $T_{eff} = D_{2,\max} - D_{2,\min}$ | Effective (Pitch) Diameter Tolerance | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion:** In KTU exam scripts, **never** use the bare pipe symbol `|x|` inside any table row — use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX to preserve table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** 100% inspection of cylinder-head studs on the assembly line.
- **Aerospace:** Acceptance of high-strength bolt threads (Ti–6Al–4V studs).
- **Hydraulic Fittings:** Inspection of BSP / NPT threads on valves and adaptors.
- **Fastener Manufacturing:** Inline QA at thread rolling machines.
- **Power Plant Maintenance:** Site inspection of high-pressure boiler studs where thread integrity is critical for safety.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Pitch Diameter from Major Diameter (60° ISO Metric)

We begin with the **fundamental triangle** of an ISO metric thread. The thread profile is an equilateral triangle of height $H$ removed from a cylinder of diameter $D$, leaving a pitch diameter $D_2$.

The full theoretical thread height is given by the standard relation:
$$
H = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,P \approx 0.866025\,P
$$

The **pitch diameter** lies on the cylinder that cuts the fundamental triangle such that the vertical distance from the apex of the triangle equals **$H/4$** for an ISO metric thread (since the crest truncation is $H/8$ from the major cylinder and $H/4$ from the minor cylinder).

**Derivation Steps:**

The vertical drop from the major diameter to the pitch diameter equals the distance between the apex and the pitch line in the fundamental triangle. For a 60° symmetric V-thread, this vertical drop is:
$$
\Delta = \frac{H}{2} \cdot \cos 30^{\circ}
$$

Wait — re-deriving properly. From the fundamental triangle geometry, the half-angle is $30°$. The radial distance from the crest apex (at major diameter) down to the pitch line is:
$$
\Delta = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \cot 30^{\circ} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,P
$$

But the pitch diameter sits *inside* this by a height equal to **$3H/8$** from the apex (i.e. $H/4 + H/8$ truncated region). Therefore:
$$
D_2 = D - 2 \times \frac{3H}{8}
$$

Substituting $H = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}P$:
$$
D_2 = D - 2 \times \frac{3}{8} \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} P
$$

$$
D_2 = D - \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{8}\,P
$$

$$
\boxed{\,D_2 = D - 0.64952\,P\,}
$$

**Dimensional Check:** $D$ [mm] $-$ dimensionless coefficient $\times$ $P$ [mm] $= D_2$ [mm] ✓

## 3.2 Derivation II — Best Wire Size for the Three-Wire Method (60° Thread)

This derivation is essential because it backs up the **calliper-gauge correction factor** used when verifying the gauge against a setting standard.

Let the wire of diameter $d$ sit in the thread groove. The wire centre lies on the **pitch line** (by definition of "best wire"). From the geometry of the wire resting between two flanks at angle $\alpha = 60°$:

**Step 1 — Vertical Rise of Wire Centre above Pitch Line:**
The wire touches the flanks at points that are tangent to the wire circle. The vertical rise is:
$$
h = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \csc\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \csc 30^{\circ} = \frac{d}{2} \cdot 2 = d
$$

**Step 2 — Horizontal Projection of Half-Flank:**
The horizontal offset of the contact point from the centre of the groove is:
$$
w = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \cot\!\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \cot 30^{\circ} = \frac{d}{2} \cdot \sqrt{3} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}d
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for "Best Wire":**
The contact point must lie on the **pitch line** where the flank intersects the pitch cylinder. The vertical distance from the apex of the V-groove to the pitch line is **$H/2$** for one flank (or $P/2 \cdot \cot 30°$ in axial projection). Equating vertical rise $h$ with $H/2$:
$$
\frac{d}{2} \cdot \csc 30^{\circ} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \cot 30^{\circ}
$$

$$
\frac{d}{2} \cdot 2 = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \sqrt{3}
$$

$$
d = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\,P = \frac{P}{2\,\cos 30^{\circ}} = \frac{P}{2}\sec 30^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,d_0 = 0.57735\,P\,}
$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

> **Problem [KTU University Exam - May 2024 style]:**
> An M24 × 3 external thread has its pitch diameter measured using the three-wire method. The measurement over three best-size wires reads $M = 24.872$ mm.
