The economic logic rests on the **multiplier effect** of defects discovered later:

- A defect caught at **design stage** costs ₹1 to fix.
- Caught at **incoming inspection**: ₹10.
- Caught at **final inspection**: ₹100.
- Caught by **customer in the field**: ₹1,000+ (including warranty, transport, reputation).

The famous **1-10-100 Rule** (or sometimes quoted as 1-10-100-1000) illustrates this. Investing in prevention is the only category where *increased spending actively reduces* all other categories simultaneously — yielding a net reduction in total COQ. Juran's *optimal COQ* is the point where marginal prevention cost = marginal failure cost reduction.

---

### **Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Given:**
- $\mu = 80.000$ mm
- $\sigma = 0.040$ mm
- USL = 80.100 mm, LSL = 79.900 mm

**(i) Process Capability Index $C_p$:**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \frac{80.100 - 79.900}{6 \times 0.040} = \frac{0.200}{0.240} = 0.833$$

**(ii) Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$:**

First, compute the two one-sided indices:

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{80.100 - 80.000}{3 \times 0.040} = \frac{0.100}{0.120} = 0.833$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{80.000 - 79.900}{3 \times 0.040} = \frac{0.100}{0.120} = 0.833$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.833, 0.833) = 0.833$$

**Note:** $C_p = C_{pk} = 0.833$ because the process is *perfectly centered* ($\mu = 80.000$ exactly at midpoint of specs).

**(iii) Expected Fraction Non-Conforming:**

Under normality, defect rate = $P(X < LSL) + P(X > USL)$.

Compute the standardized distances:

$$z_{\text{upper}} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{0.100}{0.040} = 2.50$$

$$z_{\text{lower}} = \frac{LSL - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{-0.100}{0.040} = -2.50$$

From standard normal tables:
- $P(Z > 2.50) = 1 - \Phi(2.50) = 1 - 0.9938 = 0.0062$
- $P(Z < -2.50) = \Phi(-2.50) = 0.0062$

Total fraction non-conforming:

$$p = 0.0062 + 0.0062 = 0.0124 = 1.24\%$$

This equals **12,400 parts per million (ppm)** non-conforming.

**(iv) Comment on Process Capability:**

Since $C_p = C_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.00$, the process is **NOT CAPABLE** of meeting specifications even though it is *centered*. The natural process spread ($6\sigma = 0.240$ mm) *exceeds* the specification tolerance (0.200 mm).

**Implications:**
- The process will produce approximately **12,400 defective pistons per million** — far above industry benchmarks.
- Even with perfect centering, defects are unavoidable.
- Process improvement is required: either reduce $\sigma$ (e.g., better machine, tighter tooling) to bring $C_p \geq 1.33$ (target for automotive: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to take **min** in $C_{pk}$ computation → marking only the upper or lower side. Examiners will mark *partial* only.
> 2. Reporting fraction as a decimal without converting to ppm or % → loss of marks.
> 3. Confusing $\sigma$ with $3\sigma$ when computing $C_p$ — the formula is $\frac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$, not $\frac{USL-LSL}{2\sigma}$.
> 4. Using $\sigma = 0.040$ in the denominator for $C_{pk}$ as $3\sigma = 0.120$ but writing $\sigma$ alone in $C_p = \frac{USL-LSL}{6\sigma}$ — these must be consistent.
> 5. Saying "process is capable" because $C_{pk}$ is positive — direction alone doesn't imply capability; the **magnitude relative to 1.0** is what matters.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Definitions You MUST Memorize (Definitions carry 2–3 marks individually)

- **Quality**, **Quality Control**, **Quality Assurance**, **Inspection**
- **Accuracy vs Precision** (with example)
- **Tolerance**, **Specification Limits (USL, LSL)**, **Control Limits (UCL, LCL)**
- **Common Cause vs Special Cause Variation**
- **AQL, LTPD, AOQL, OC Curve, Producer's Risk, Consumer's Risk**
- **Variable vs Attribute Data**
- **Defect vs Defective**

### Numerical Skills You MUST Master

- Compute $\bar{x}$, $s$, $\sigma$, $R$ from raw data
- Compute control limits using $A_2, D_3, D_4$ (memorize constants for $n = 2$ to $n = 6$)
- Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ and interpret
- Use standard normal tables to compute defect rates
- Compute Z-score and convert to probability

### Conceptual Understanding You MUST Have

- **In-control ≠ Capable** — a process can be statistically stable but fail specs (process capability) OR statistically unstable but pass specs (rare, masked by chance).
- **3σ limits** are an *economic* choice, not a mathematical certainty — based on Shewhart's balance of Type I and Type II errors.
- **Quality Gurus** and their one-line contribution (Shewhart, Deming, Juran, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Crosby, Taguchi).
- **COQ Hierarchy** — prevention < appraisal < internal failure < external failure (in cost-per-defect).
- **The 1-10-100 Rule** for cost of defects at different stages.

### Key Numbers to Remember

| Concept | Number |
|---------|--------|
| % within $\pm 1\sigma$ | 68.27% |
| % within $\pm 2\sigma$ | 95.45% |
| % within $\pm 3\sigma$ | 99.73% |
| Producer's Risk | 5% (= 0.05) |
| Consumer's Risk | 10% (= 0.10) |
| Six Sigma DPMO | 3.4 |
| Capable $C_p$ minimum | 1.00 |
| Industry benchmark $C_{pk}$ | $\geq 1.33$ |
| Six Sigma $C_{pk}$ | $\geq 1.67$ |

### Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid

> [!WARNING]
> 1. Confusing **control limits** with **specification limits**.
> 2. Mixing up **$C_p$** (spread only) with **$C_{pk}$** (spread + centering).
> 3. Reporting $LCL_R$ as a negative number — it must be set to zero when $D_3 = 0$.
> 4. Using the wrong divisor ($n$ vs $n-1$) in variance calculation without stating the context.
> 5. Defining precision as "closeness to truth" — it is "closeness among repeated measurements".
> 6. Stating "QA = QC" or "QA and QC are the same" — they are not.
> 7. Saying "Taguchi's loss function says defects have zero cost within specs" — actually it says *loss increases quadratically from target*, even within specs.

### Quick-Reference Mnemonics

- **"A-P-I-E" for COQ**: **A**ppraisal, **P**revention, **I**nternal failure, **E**xternal failure (cheapest to most expensive).
- **"SQC's two questions"**: (1) Is the process stable? (2) Is the process capable?
- **"Deming's PDCA"**: Plan → Do → Check → Act (continuous improvement cycle).
- **"3-Sigma rule of thumb"**: ~ 2700 ppm defectives when centered — basis of $3\sigma$ control limits.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Surface Traces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Surface Traces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Analysis of Surface Traces

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Surface Trace Analysis** is the systematic mathematical and graphical examination of a two-dimensional profile obtained when a finely-tipped stylus traverses perpendicularly across a finished engineering surface. The resulting signal — known as the **Primary Profile (P-profile)** — is decomposed into form, waviness, and roughness components through standardized filtering operations (per **ISO 4287:1997 / ISO 16610**), after which quantitative parameters such as **Ra, Rz, Rq, Rp, Rv, Rt, Rsk, and Rku** are computed over defined sampling and evaluation lengths.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (CO3 Mapped):**
> Surface trace = the digitized record of displacement of a stylus tip as it traverses a controlled length of a real engineering surface. Analysis = the extraction of standardized numerical parameters from this trace after suppression of form and waviness errors.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **mountain range seen from the side**. If you fly a tiny airplane carrying a pencil along the skyline, drawing a continuous line on a long paper strip, the resulting wavy drawing is exactly what a **surface profilometer** does — except the "mountains" are microscopic peaks (called **asperities**) and the "valleys" are the troughs between them.

- The **stylus** = the pencil.
- The **paper strip** = the trace (profile).
- The **mountain silhouette** = roughness.
- A **long, slow rolling hill** drawn underneath the mountains = waviness.
- The **tilt of the whole paper** = form error.

The "analysis" part is the geographer's job: measuring average heights (Ra), tallest peak-to-valley distances (Rz), how symmetric the heights are (Rsk), and how "peaky" or "flat" the distribution is (Rku).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for students:** A surface trace is fundamentally a **1-D signal y(x)** in micrometers vs. millimetres. Everything in this module is signal processing disguised as metrology.

### Glossary of Critical Terminology (KTU 2024 Must-Knows)

| Term | Definition | Standard Reference |
|------|------------|--------------------|
| **Asperity** | A microscopic peak protruding from the nominal surface | ISO 4287 |
| **Mean Line (m)** | Reference line parallel to general surface direction, dividing the profile so that areas above and below are equal | ISO 4287 |
| **Sampling Length (lr)** | The length over which a single roughness parameter is computed (one cutoff) | ISO 4288 |
| **Evaluation Length (ln)** | The total traverse length, usually = **5 × lr** | ISO 4288 |
| **Cutoff (λc)** | The filter wavelength separating roughness from waviness | ISO 3274 |
| **Lay** | Dominant direction of surface pattern (grinding, turning marks) | ISO 4287 |
| **Primary Profile (P)** | Unfiltered profile | ISO 16610-21 |
| **Roughness Profile (R)** | Profile after λc and λs filters applied | ISO 16610-21 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A KTU examiner will mark you strictly: the **evaluation length is NOT the sampling length** — it is **5 times** the sampling length. This is a classic 1-mark trap in Part A questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Layer Model of a Surface Profile (Logical Decomposition)

Every real surface, when traced, contains four superimposed frequency components. The KTU 2024 scheme (Module 3) expects students to clearly identify each:

1. **Form Error (F-profile)** — Lowest frequency. Caused by machine tool deflections, flatness errors of slides, thermal distortion. Wavelength > λf (typically 0.8 mm or larger; form is not filtered out by λc, it's removed by least-squares fitting or a separate λf filter).
2. **Waviness (W-profile)** — Medium frequency. Caused by vibration, chatter, straightness errors in slides. Wavelength between λc and λf.
3. **Roughness (R-profile)** — Highest frequency. Caused by the manufacturing process itself (cutting tool nose, grit marks, feed marks). Wavelength < λc.
4. **Lay** — Directional character of the texture (radial on a turned shaft, circular on a ground disc, random on a lapped surface).

**The 'Why' behind the separation:** A single number like Ra cannot describe a complex surface. By isolating frequency bands, each manufacturing defect can be diagnosed — chatter leaves a waviness signature, tool wear shows in roughness evolution, and a worn slide shows up as form.

### 2.2 The Filtering Operation (How P-Profile Becomes R-Profile)

The **λc filter** is a phase-correct, low-pass filter specified in **ISO 16610-21**. The two main families are:

- **Gaussian Filter (most common today):** Uses a Gaussian transmission characteristic. Phase-correct, no signal distortion. Replaces 50% of the area in the filtered portion — hence "2CR" (50% transmission at cutoff).
- **2CR Filter (older, ISO 3274):** Same 50% transmission point, slightly different phase response. Still encountered in older machinery.
- **Robust Gaussian Filter:** A modern variant for surfaces with spikes/porosity, prevents the filter from "chasing" outliers.

**Cutoff Selection (λc) is governed by ISO 4288 — the student MUST memorize this table:**

| Roughness Range Ra (μm) | Sampling Length lr (mm) | Evaluation Length ln = 5lr (mm) |
|-------------------------|-------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 0.006 < Ra ≤ 0.02 | 0.08 | 0.4 |
| 0.02 < Ra ≤ 0.1 | 0.25 | 1.25 |
| 0.1 < Ra ≤ 2 | 0.8 | 4.0 |
| 2 < Ra ≤ 10 | 2.5 | 12.5 |
| 10 < Ra ≤ 80 | 8.0 | 40.0 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** Selecting the wrong cutoff gives a meaningless Ra. Too small a cutoff, and the stylus "sees" waviness as roughness (over-estimates Ra). Too large, and the stylus averages out real peaks (under-estimates Ra). This is why ISO 4288 mandates matching the cutoff to the roughness range.

### 2.3 The Amplitude Parameters (KTU 2024 Formula Sheet)

The KTU Formula Sheet is the single most important artefact for solving surface-trace numerical problems. Memorize the following:

| Symbol | Parameter Name | Formula (Continuous Form) | Discrete (Sampled) Form | Unit |
|--------|----------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|------|
| **Ra** | Arithmetic Mean Deviation | $R_a = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | $R_a = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i \vert$ | μm |
| **Rq** | Root-Mean-Square Deviation | $R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l_r}\int_0^{l_r} y^2(x) \, dx}$ | $R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2}$ | μm |
| **Rz** | Mean Roughness Depth (ISO) | $R_z = \frac{1}{5}\sum_{i=1}^{5} R_{zi}$ where $R_{zi}$ = peak-to-valley in i-th sampling length | — | μm |
| **Rp** | Maximum Peak Height | $R_p = \max y_i$ over evaluation length | — | μm |
| **Rv** | Maximum Valley Depth | $R_v = \vert \min y_i \vert$ over evaluation length | — | μm |
| **Rt** | Total Height of Profile | $R_t = R_p + R_v$ over evaluation length | — | μm |
| **Rmax** | Maximum Roughness Depth | Largest single $R_{zi}$ in the evaluation length | — | μm |
| **Rsk** | Skewness | $R_{sk} = \frac{1}{R_q^3} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum y_i^3$ | — | dimensionless |
| **Rku** | Kurtosis | $R_{ku} = \frac{1}{R_q^4} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \sum y_i^4$ | — | dimensionless |

> [!CRITICAL]
> **Note on absolute values in the table above:** LaTeX uses `\vert` and `\mid` (not the pipe character `\vert`) so the markdown table is not broken. When you write your KTU answer sheet, you may freely use `\vert y(x) \vert` — the pipe is fine in standalone math blocks.

### 2.4 Physical Meaning of the Statistical Parameters (Why Rsk and Rku Matter for Engineers)

- **Rsk (Skewness):**
  - $R_{sk} = 0$ → Symmetric profile (lapped surfaces, bearing surfaces).
  - $R_{sk} < 0$ → Bearing surface with deep valleys (good for lubrication retention — honed cylinder liners show negative skewness).
  - $R_{sk} > 0$ → Profile with sharp peaks on a flat base (typical of grit-blasted, shot-peened surfaces).

- **Rku (Kurtosis):**
  - $R_{ku} = 3$ → Gaussian distribution of heights (most common assumption).
  - $R_{ku} < 3$ → Flat, plateau-like surface (running-in surfaces).
  - $R_{ku} > 3$ → Sharp, spiky surface (newly machined, brittle fracture).

> [!TIP]
> This is the **#1 reason** surface trace analysis exists beyond just Ra: a single Ra = 0.8 μm can describe a **grinding finish** (Rku > 3) OR a **honed finish** (Rku < 3) — completely different tribological behaviour, same Ra. KTU frequently tests this conceptual nuance.

### 2.5 Spacing and Hybrid Parameters (Brief — KTU 2024 Outline)

While Ra/Rz dominate, the syllabus also includes:

- **RSm (Mean Spacing of Profile Irregularities):** Mean distance between peaks at the mean line. Indicates frequency content.
- **Rpc (Peak Count per cm):** Number of peaks per unit evaluation length.
- **RΔq, RΔa (Slope parameters):** Critical for contact mechanics — how "steep" the asperities are.

> [!NOTE]
> These are listed in the KTU 2024 syllabus as "awareness level" — the examiner may ask for a 2-sentence definition in Part A, but Part B derivations are almost always on Ra, Rq, and Rz.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Derivation of Ra from a Digitized Trace (The KTU Numerical Workhorse)

The mathematical definition of Ra, as established in **ISO 4287**, emerges from the need to characterize the **average absolute vertical deviation** of a surface profile from its mean line. The full logical progression is:

**Step 1 — Establish the mean line.**
The mean line $m$ is the line such that the area enclosed between the profile $y(x)$ and $m$ over the sampling length is exactly zero:

$$\int_0^{l_r} [y(x) - m] \, dx = 0$$

This means:

$$m = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} y(x) \, dx$$

In discrete sampled form (n samples equally spaced across $l_r$):

$$m = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i$$

**Step 2 — Define deviations from the mean line.**
The instantaneous vertical deviation of the profile at any point is:

$$y'(x) = y(x) - m$$

**Step 3 — Take the absolute value of the deviation (sign does not matter — peaks and valleys both contribute to roughness).**
This is the critical step that distinguishes Ra from Rq:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} \vert y(x) - m \vert \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Convert to the discrete, computable form for digitized traces.**
For an instrument that samples n equally-spaced points:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert y_i - m \vert$$

This is the form used in **virtually every KTU numerical problem**.

**Step 5 — Unit consistency.**
Since $y$ is in μm and $l_r$ is in mm, Ra is reported in **μm** (the length unit cancels via the discrete form).

### 3.2 Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Numerical (K3 / K4 Level — Apply / Analyse)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A stylus profilometer traverses a machined steel surface. The recorded ordinates $y_i$ (in μm) at 5 equal sampling points within one sampling length are: **+2.0, −1.0, +3.0, −2.0, +1.0**. Calculate **(a) the mean line position, (b) Ra, (c) Rq, and (d) Rt**. Take n = 5.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the mean line.**

$$m = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} y_i = \frac{1}{5} \left[ 2.0 + (-1.0) + 3.0 + (-2.0) + 1.0 \right]$$

$$m = \frac{1}{5} (3.0) = 0.6 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviations from mean line $y_i' = y_i - m$.**

$$y_1' = 2.0 - 0.6 = 1.4 \, \mu m$$

$$y_2' = -1.0 - 0.6 = -1.6 \, \mu m$$

$$y_3' = 3.0 - 0.6 = 2.4 \, \mu m$$

$$y_4' = -2.0 - 0.6 = -2.6 \, \mu m$$

$$y_5' = 1.0 - 0.6 = 0.4 \, \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Compute Ra (sum of absolute deviations, divided by n).**

$$R_a = \frac{1}{5} \left[ \vert 1.4 \vert + \vert -1.6 \vert + \vert 2.4 \vert + \vert -2.6 \vert + \vert 0.4 \vert \right]$$

$$R_a = \frac{1}{5} \left[ 1.4 + 1.6 + 2.4 + 2.6 + 0.4 \right] = \frac{8.4}{5}$$

$$\boxed{R_a = 1.68 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Rq (root-mean-square of deviations).**

$$R_q^2 = \frac{1}{5} \sum_{i=1}^{5} (y_i')^2$$

$$R_q^2 = \frac{1}{5} \left[ (1.4)^2 + (-1.6)^2 + (2.4)^2 + (-2.6)^2 + (0.4)^2 \right]$$

$$R_q^2 = \frac{1}{5} \left[ 1.96 + 2.56 + 5.76 + 6.76 + 0.16 \right] = \frac{17.20}{5} = 3.44 \, (\mu m)^2$$

$$R_q = \sqrt{3.44}$$

$$\boxed{R_q = 1.855 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Rt (total height = max peak + max valley depth).**

$$R_p = \max(y_i') = 2.4 \, \mu m$$

$$R_v = \vert \min(y_i') \vert = \vert -2.6 \vert = 2.6 \, \mu m$$

$$R_t = R_p + R_v = 2.4 + 2.6$$

$$\boxed{R_t = 5.0 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 6 — Cross-check the relation between Ra and Rq.**

For a perfectly sinusoidal profile, $R_q / R_a = \pi / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.111$.

In our calculation:

$$\frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{1.855}{1.68} = 1.104$$

This is very close to 1.111, confirming the result is physically consistent (the profile is approximately sinusoidal in character).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Parameter Reacts to Surface Changes (K5 / K6 Level)

This matrix is critical for KTU "compare-and-contrast" Part B questions:

| Surface Change | Ra | Rq | Rz | Rsk | Rku |
|----------------|----|----|----|-----|-----|
| Peak grows (single asperity) | ↑ small | ↑ small | ↑ large | ↑ positive | ↑ > 3 |
| Deep scratch (single valley) | ↑ small | ↑ small | ↑ large | ↓ negative | ↑ > 3 |
| Waviness (filtered out by λc) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Form error (filtered out by λf) | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Honing (plateau surface) | similar | smaller | smaller | ↓ negative | ↓ < 3 |
| Polishing (smooth out peaks) | ↓ large | ↓ large | ↓ large | → 0 | → 3 |
| Increase cut-off (λc) too much | ↓ (averages peaks) | ↓ | ↓ | changes | changes |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Surface Trace Analysis

Because the physical stylus traverse is hard to render in Mermaid, the diagram below represents the **signal-processing workflow** specified by ISO 16610 — the conceptual backbone of KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START PHYSICAL SURFACE] --> B[STYLUS CONTACT AND TRAVERSE]
    B --> C[PIEZO OR INDUCTIVE TRANSDUCER]
    C --> D[AMPLIFIER AND ADC CONVERSION]
    D --> E[PRIMARY PROFILE P SIGNAL YX]
    E --> F[FIT LAMBDA F FILTER REMOVE FORM]
    F --> G[APPLY LAMBDA C FILTER GAUSSIAN OR 2CR]
    G --> H[ROUGHNESS PROFILE R SIGNAL]
    H --> I[SPLIT INTO 5 SAMPLING LENGTHS]
    I --> J1[CALCULATE RA SAMPLE 1]
    I --> J2[CALCULATE RA SAMPLE 2]
    I --> J3[CALCULATE RA SAMPLE 3]
    I --> J4[CALCULATE RA SAMPLE 4]
    I --> J5[CALCULATE RA SAMPLE 5]
    J1 --> K[COMPUTE MEAN RZ ACROSS 5 SAMPLES]
    J2 --> K
    J3 --> K
    J4 --> K
    J5 --> K
    H --> L[CALCULATE GLOBAL RQ RT RSK RKU]
    K --> M[QUALITY DECISION PASS OR REJECT]
    L --> M
    M --> N[END REPORT]
```

### 4.2 Schematic of Profile Components and Filter Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[PRIMARY PROFILE P]:::pstyle
    F[FORM COMPONENT F LAMBDA GT LAMBDAF]:::fstyle
    W[WAVINESS COMPONENT W LAMBDAF GT LAMBDA GT LAMBDAC]:::wstyle
    R[ROUGHNESS COMPONENT R LAMBDA LT LAMBDAC]:::rstyle

    P --> F
    P --> W
    P --> R

    classDef pstyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef fstyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef wstyle fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef rstyle fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **P-profile** (orange) is the raw trace — what the stylus actually records.
- The **F-component** (blue) is the slow-tilt, long-wavelength form (slide flatness error).
- The **W-component** (green) is medium-wavelength waviness (chatter, slide straightness).
- The **R-component** (pink) is the high-frequency roughness (cutting-process signature).

After the **λc filter** (typically Gaussian, ISO 16610-21), only the R-component remains for parameter calculation. This is the **only** profile from which Ra, Rz, Rq are computed — a KTU examiner's favourite trick question.

### 4.3 Cutoff-Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START KNOW THE ROUGHNESS RANGE] --> B{RA LESS THAN 0.02 MICROMETRE}
    B -- YES --> C[LAMBDA C 0.08 MM LR 0.08 LN 0.4]
    B -- NO --> D{RA LESS THAN 0.1 MICROMETRE}
    D -- YES --> E[LAMBDA C 0.25 MM LR 0.25 LN 1.25]
    D -- NO --> F{RA LESS THAN 2 MICROMETRE}
    F -- YES --> G[LAMBDA C 0.8 MM LR 0.8 LN 4.0]
    F -- NO --> H{RA LESS THAN 10 MICROMETRE}
    H -- YES --> I[LAMBDA C 2.5 MM LR 2.5 LN 12.5]
    H -- NO --> J[LAMBDA C 8.0 MM LR 8.0 LN 40.0]
    C --> K[END APPLY CORRECT CUTOFF]
    E --> K
    G --> K
    K --> L[END]
    I --> L
    J --> L
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Set-A]**
Define the term **"cut-off wavelength (λc)"** as applied in surface roughness measurement. Why is it necessary to select a specific value of λc for a given surface? Mention the ISO standard that governs this selection.

**Model Answer (3 marks — K2: Understand):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The cut-off wavelength λc is the wavelength of the phase-correct low-pass filter (typically Gaussian, per ISO 16610-21) that separates the roughness profile from the waviness profile during stylus trace analysis.
- **Necessity (1.5 marks):** A stylus does not inherently know whether a particular undulation is "roughness" or "waviness." Without a defined λc, a single Ra reading would not be reproducible between instruments. The correct λc ensures that the high-frequency content relevant to the manufacturing process is retained while low-frequency waviness (caused by vibration / slide errors) is filtered out.
- **ISO Standard (0.5 marks):** **ISO 4288** governs the selection of λc as a function of the expected Ra range.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
Differentiate between **sampling length (lr)** and **evaluation length (ln)** in surface trace analysis. What is the standard relationship between them, and why is it not 1:1?

**Model Answer (3 marks — K2: Understand):**
- **Sampling length lr (1 mark):** The length of profile over which a single measurement of Ra or Rz is computed. It equals the cut-off wavelength λc.
- **Evaluation length ln (1 mark):** The total length of profile traverse over which the final reported Ra (or Rz) is the average of multiple sampling lengths.
- **Standard relationship (0.5 marks):** Per **ISO 4288**, $l_n = 5 \times l_r$.
- **Reason for 5:1 ratio (0.5 marks):** A single sampling length may contain atypical peaks/valleys (e.g., a single deep scratch). Averaging over 5 lengths gives a statistically stable reading. The first and last sampling lengths are sometimes excluded in older standards; modern ISO 4288 averages all 5.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall (Part A):** Writing "sampling length and evaluation length are the same" or "ln = lr". The 5:1 rule is a **favourite 1-mark trap** in KTU valuation.

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the four components of a real engineering surface profile: **form, waviness, roughness, and lay**. Describe how the Gaussian λc filter separates these components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A surface trace yields the following 9 ordinates (in μm) sampled at equal intervals of 0.1 mm within one evaluation length: **+3, +1, −2, +4, 0, −3, +2, −1, +2**.
Compute: **(i) Mean line position m**, **(ii) Arithmetic Mean Deviation Ra**, **(iii) Root-Mean-Square Roughness Rq**, **(iv) Total Height Rt**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Components of a Real Engineering Surface (7 marks):**

*(For full marks, draw a labelled graph showing the P-profile decomposed into F, W, R components. Verbal description below.)*

- **Form (F-profile) — 1.5 marks:** The low-frequency, long-wavelength deviation of the surface from its nominal geometry. Examples: out-of-flatness of a slideway, taper of a turned shaft, lobing of a bearing journal. Wavelength > λf. Removed before roughness measurement by either a least-squares fit or a separate λf filter (typically 0.8 mm for mm-scale components).
- **Waviness (W-profile) — 1.5 marks:** Medium-frequency periodic undulations. Causes: machine tool vibration, feed marks, slide straightness errors, chatter. Wavelength lies between λc and λf. The Gaussian filter with cut-off λc removes this band, retaining only the higher-frequency roughness.
- **Roughness (R-profile) — 1.5 marks:** High-frequency, short-wavelength irregularities caused by the manufacturing process itself — cutting tool nose radius, abrasive grit size, ploughing, side-flow. Wavelength < λc. This is the profile from which Ra, Rz, Rq are computed.
- **Lay — 1 mark:** The directional character of the predominant surface pattern. Examples: circumferential lay on a turned surface, radial lay on a ground surface, random lay on a lapped or honed surface. Lay is reported but does not affect Ra directly; it influences tribology and fatigue.

- **Gaussian λc filter operation — 1.5 marks:** The filter is a phase-correct low-pass filter whose transmission is 50% at λ = λc and approaches 100% for λ << λc. It removes wavelengths longer than λc (form + waviness) while preserving shorter wavelengths (roughness). The "phase-correct" property ensures the filtered profile is not laterally shifted, which would distort peak-to-valley measurements. ISO 16610-21 specifies the mathematical weighting function.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Mean line m (1 mark):**

$$m = \frac{1}{9}\left[3 + 1 + (-2) + 4 + 0 + (-3) + 2 + (-1) + 2\right]$$

$$m = \frac{1}{9}(6) = 0.667 \, \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Deviations $y_i' = y_i - m$ (1 mark):**

| i | $y_i$ (μm) | $y_i' = y_i - 0.667$ (μm) | $\vert y_i' \vert$ (μm) | $(y_i')^2$ $(\mu m)^2$ |
|---|-----------|---------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | +3 | +2.333 | 2.333 | 5.443 |
| 2 | +1 | +0.333 | 0.333 | 0.111 |
| 3 | −2 | −2.667 | 2.667 | 7.113 |
| 4 | +4 | +3.333 | 3.333 | 11.109 |
| 5 | 0 | −0.667 | 0.667 | 0.445 |
| 6 | −3 | −3.667 | 3.667 | 13.447 |
| 7 | +2 | +1.333 | 1.333 | 1.777 |
| 8 | −1 | −1.667 | 1.667 | 2.779 |
| 9 | +2 | +1.333 | 1.333 | 1.777 |
| **Σ** | | | **17.667** | **44.001** |

**Step 3 — Compute Ra (2 marks):**

$$R_a = \frac{1}{9} \sum \vert y_i' \vert = \frac{17.667}{9}$$

$$\boxed{R_a = 1.963 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Rq (2 marks):**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{9} \sum (y_i')^2} = \sqrt{\frac{44.001}{9}} = \sqrt{4.889}$$

$$\boxed{R_q = 2.211 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Rt (1 mark):**

$$R_p = \max(y_i') = +3.333 \, \mu m, \quad R_v = \vert \min(y_i') \vert = 3.667 \, \mu m$$

$$R_t = R_p + R_v = 3.333 + 3.667$$

$$\boxed{R_t = 7.000 \, \mu m}$$

**Sanity check — Ra : Rq ratio:**

$$\frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{2.211}{1.963} = 1.126 \approx 1.111$$

Within ±2% of the theoretical sinusoidal value — calculation verified.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024 Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Arithmetic Mean Deviation Ra** and the **Mean Roughness Depth Rz (ISO 1997)** with their mathematical definitions. Why is Ra preferred for general engineering specifications, and in which specific applications is Rz preferred over Ra? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A turned mild steel shaft has a roughness that places it in the **"Ra between 0.1 and 2 μm"** category. The trace is divided into 5 sampling lengths. The peak-to-valley heights in each sampling length (in μm) are: **6.0, 5.5, 6.5, 5.0, 7.0**. Calculate **(i) Rz (ISO 1997)**, **(ii) Rmax**, and **(iii) select the correct cutoff λc and evaluation length ln per ISO 4288.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Ra vs Rz — Definitions and Applications (7 marks):**

- **Definition of Ra — 2 marks:** The arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the ordinates of the roughness profile from the mean line over the sampling length:
$$R_a = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx \quad \text{or in discrete form} \quad R_a = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i \vert$$
- **Definition of Rz (ISO 1997) — 2 marks:** The mean of the **peak-to-valley heights** measured over 5 consecutive sampling lengths within the evaluation length:
$$R_z = \frac{1}{5} (R_{z1} + R_{z2} + R_{z3} + R_{z4} + R_{z5})$$
where $R_{zi}$ is the maximum peak-to-valley height in the $i$-th sampling length. Note: this is **different from the older "10-point height" R10z** (DIN/ASA standard) which used 5 highest peaks + 5 lowest valleys — KTU expects the ISO 1997 definition.
- **Why Ra is preferred (general specifications) — 1.5 marks:**
  - Statistically robust (averages out single anomalous peaks).
  - Approximately Gaussian-distributed for random surfaces — amenable to statistical quality control.
  - Cheap to compute in real-time digital profilometers.
  - Standardized since 1957 — extensive historical data.
- **When Rz is preferred — 1.5 marks:**
  - **Sealing surfaces** (cylinder bore, hydraulic spool valves) — leakage depends on **maximum peak-to-valley**, not average.
  - **Bearing surfaces under mixed lubrication** — Rmax / Rz indicates the worst-case oil-film penetration.
  - **Functional surfaces with critical peak heights** (paint adhesion, contact electrical resistance).
  - **Older European (DIN) drawings still specify Rz** — Ra may not be available on older machinery.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute Rz (3 marks):**

$$R_z = \frac{1}{5}(R_{z1} + R_{z2} + R_{z3} + R_{z4} + R_{z5})$$

$$R_z = \frac{1}{5}(6.0 + 5.5 + 6.5 + 5.0 + 7.0) = \frac{30.0}{5}$$

$$\boxed{R_z = 6.0 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Rmax (2 marks):**
$R_{max}$ is the **largest single peak-to-valley height** in the entire evaluation length:

$$R_{max} = \max(6.0, 5.5, 6.5, 5.0, 7.0) = 7.0 \, \mu m$$

$$\boxed{R_{max} = 7.0 \, \mu m}$$

**Step 3 — Cutoff and evaluation length per ISO 4288 (2 marks):**

Given the surface is in the "0.1 < Ra ≤ 2 μm" category (and Rz = 6.0 μm is consistent with this — a rough correlation Rz ≈ 4–7 × Ra holds for typical machined surfaces):

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Sampling length $l_r$ | **0.8 mm** |
| Evaluation length $l_n = 5 l_r$ | **4.0 mm** |
| Cut-off $\lambda_c$ | **0.8 mm** |

$$\boxed{\lambda_c = 0.8 \, mm, \quad l_n = 4.0 \, mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing Ra with Rz:** A common 1-mark deduction. Ra is **arithmetic mean of absolute ordinates from the mean line**. Rz is **peak-to-valley distance in a single sampling length**, averaged over 5 such lengths. They have **different units of computation logic**, even if both end in μm.
> 2. **Forgetting to use the mean line in Ra:** If you compute Ra directly from the raw $y_i$ without first subtracting $m$, you will get the wrong answer and **lose 2 marks** — the mean line is non-negotiable per ISO 4287.
> 3. **Confusing Rmax with Rt:** Rmax is the **largest single Rz** in the evaluation length. Rt is the **largest peak-to-valley distance** between **any single peak and any single valley** in the entire evaluation length — usually Rt ≥ Rmax. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong λc from the ISO 4288 table:** The table is in μm for Ra. Memorize the **five ranges** and the corresponding cutoffs (0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm). Selecting the wrong λc for the given Ra range costs 1 mark and may invalidate the rest of the problem.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the ISO standard number** in the explanation questions. A 0.5-mark deduction per missing standard.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU Module 3 — Surface Trace Analysis: Final 1-Page Revision**

- ✅ **Surface trace** = 1-D signal $y(x)$ recorded by a stylus traversing a surface. **Always** in μm vs. mm.
- ✅ **Four components:** Form (lowest freq) → Waviness (mid freq) → Roughness (highest freq) + Lay (direction). Removed in this order by **λf** (form) and **λc** (waviness) filters.
- ✅ **Mean line (m):** Equal-area reference line. $m = \frac{1}{n}\sum y_i$. **Always** subtract before computing any R-parameter.
- ✅ **Evaluation length = 5 × sampling length = 5 × λc.** This is the **single most commonly tested 1-mark fact**.
- ✅ **ISO 4288** is the table to memorize for λc selection based on Ra range (5 ranges, 5 cutoffs: 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm).
- ✅ **Ra formula:** $R_a = \frac{1}{n}\sum \vert y_i - m \vert$. **Rq formula:** $R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - m)^2}$. **Rz (ISO 1997):** Average of 5 peak-to-valley heights.
- ✅ **For a sinusoidal profile:** $R_q / R_a = \pi/(2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.111$ — useful as a verification check.
- ✅ **Rsk interpretation:** Negative = good bearing (plateau with valleys); Positive = sharp peaks on flat base.
- ✅ **Rku interpretation:** = 3 Gaussian, < 3 plateau, > 3 spiky.
- ✅ **Ra alone is insufficient** for sealing / bearing surfaces — Rz and Rmax are required.
- ✅ **The Gaussian filter** is phase-correct, 50% transmission at λc, per ISO 16610-21. Replaces the older 2CR filter in modern practice.
- ✅ **Stylus tip radius** (typically 2 μm or 5 μm) is part of the measurement system — the surface trace is a **convolved** signal, not the true surface. A KTU 2-mark question often tests this.

**Final KTU Memory Aid — "SAME" Acronym:**
- **S** — Sampling length ($l_r = \lambda_c$)
- **A** — Arithmetic mean deviation (Ra)
- **M** — Mean line (subtract first!)
- **E** — Evaluation length ($l_n = 5 \times l_r$)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specification of Surface Texture Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Specification of Surface Texture Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Surface Texture** is defined (per ISO 21920 / ISO 1302) as the repetitive or random deviations from the nominal (ideal) geometric surface of a workpiece. These deviations constitute the **irregularities of a real surface** and are characterized at three hierarchical levels:

1. **Macro-geometric deviations** (Form errors) – out-of-flatness, ovality, taper.
2. **Waviness (W-profile)** – mid-frequency undulations due to machine tool vibrations, thermal distortion, or chatter.
3. **Roughness (R-profile)** – high-frequency, closely spaced irregularities produced by the manufacturing process (cutting, grinding, lapping, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Surface texture is the measure of the roughness, waviness, lay, and flaws of a surface. Its specification is the engineering language used on technical drawings to communicate the required functional quality of a manufactured surface to the production floor and inspection department.*

The mathematical reference for all roughness measurements is the **Mean Line (M-line)** — an imaginary line parallel to the general surface direction, positioned so that the sum of squares of the deviations (peaks and valleys) above and below it are statistically equal over the **sampling length** $l_r$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the surface of a freshly cut metal block as the **profile of a mountain range viewed from the side**. If you fly a drone horizontally across this range at a carefully chosen altitude (the **mean line**), you will record tiny bumps (peaks) and dips (valleys) along the path. 

- If the mountain is **rough**, the bumps are jagged and high — this represents a *high Ra* value (e.g., a turned shaft).
- If the mountain is **smooth like a mirror**, the bumps barely register — this represents a *low Ra* value (e.g., a lapped gauge block).
- **Waviness** is like broad rolling hills sitting *underneath* the small bumps — caused by long-wavelength machine errors.
- **Form error** is the tilt or curve of the entire mountain slope.

### 1.3 Key Standard Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Standards:**
> - **ISO 1302:2002** — Designation of surface texture symbols on technical drawings.
> - **ISO 21920-1:2021** — Surface texture (profile method): Measurement and characterization.
> - **ISO 4287:1997** — Profile method: Terms, definitions, and surface texture parameters.
> - **Sampling length** $\mathbf{l_r}$ and **Evaluation length** $\mathbf{l_n = 5 \cdot l_r}$ — KTU Board Examiners love this 5× relationship!

The fundamental unit of length on this topic is the **millimeter (mm)** for parameters, and the **micrometer (μm)** for the most commonly used $R_a$ value, which typically ranges from **0.025 μm** (mirror finish) to **25 μm** (rough cast surface).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Decomposition of Surface Texture

According to ISO 4287, a real surface can be decomposed using a **Gaussian filter** into three nested profiles:

- **Primary Profile (P-profile):** The actual measured profile, containing all deviations.
- **Roughness Profile (R-profile):** Obtained by filtering out the waviness (low-frequency components) from the P-profile.
- **Waviness Profile (W-profile):** Obtained by subtracting the R-profile from the P-profile.

This separation is essential because functional requirements (e.g., bearing area, sealing, fatigue) depend on different frequency bands of the surface.

### 2.2 Sampling Length ($l_r$) and Evaluation Length ($l_n$)

- **Sampling length ($l_r$)**: The length over which a single roughness parameter is calculated. It is bounded by the **cut-off filter $\lambda_c$**.
- **Evaluation length ($l_n$)**: The total length traversed by the stylus during measurement, used to average out local irregularities.
- **Standard relationship** (per ISO 4288): $l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$ (one sampling length for calculation + four for averaging).

#### Standard $\lambda_c$ Cut-off Values:

| Roughness Range $R_a$ (μm) | Cut-off $\lambda_c$ (mm) | Sampling $l_r$ (mm) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0.006 < Ra ≤ 0.02 | 0.08 | 0.08 |
| 0.02 < Ra ≤ 0.1 | 0.25 | 0.25 |
| 0.1 < Ra ≤ 2.0 | 0.8 | 0.8 |
| 2.0 < Ra ≤ 10.0 | 2.5 | 2.5 |
| 10.0 < Ra ≤ 80.0 | 8.0 | 8.0 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized for KTU Board Exams.**

| Symbol | Parameter Name | Formula | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| $R_a$ | Arithmetic Mean Roughness | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{l_r}\int_0^{l_r} \vert Z(x) \vert \, dx$ | μm |
| $R_a$ (discrete) | Mean of absolute ordinates | $R_a \approx \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert Z_i \vert$ | μm |
| $R_q$ | Root-Mean-Square Roughness | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{l_r}\int_0^{l_r} Z(x)^2 \, dx}$ | μm |
| $R_z$ | Mean Roughness Depth (10-point) | $R_z = \dfrac{(R_{p1}+R_{p2}+...+R_{p5}) - (R_{v1}+R_{v2}+...+R_{v5})}{5}$ | μm |
| $R_{max}$ | Maximum Roughness Height | $R_{max} = R_p^{max} + R_v^{max}$ | μm |
| $S_m$ | Mean Spacing of Profile Irregularities | $S_m = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} S_{mi}$ | mm |
| $R_{mr}$ | Material Ratio (Bearing Area) | $R_{mr}(c) = \dfrac{\text{bearing length at level } c}{l_n} \times 100\%$ | % |
| $R_{ku}$ | Kurtosis | $R_{ku} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^4 \cdot l_r}\int_0^{l_r} Z(x)^4 \, dx$ | — |
| $R_{sk}$ | Skewness | $R_{sk} = \dfrac{1}{R_q^3 \cdot l_r}\int_0^{l_r} Z(x)^3 \, dx$ | — |
| $l_n$ | Evaluation Length | $l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$ | mm |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Parameters

- **$R_a$** → Universal communication parameter; used by ~80% of industries. Best for general roughness control (machined, ground, honed surfaces).
- **$R_z$** → Used for surfaces with sealing or bearing functions (cylinder bores, bearing journals).
- **$R_q$** → Statistically robust; used in statistical quality control (SPC) and surface fatigue analysis.
- **$R_{mr}$** → Critical for **bearing area** applications (e.g., hydrodynamic journal bearings, where 60–80% bearing area is required).
- **$S_m$** → Determines surface texture periodicity; vital for tribological performance (lubricant retention).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $R_a$ dominates industry:** It is an averaging parameter — a single robust number insensitive to local scratches or defects, making it ideal for statistical process control and ISO compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of $R_a$ (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup:**
Consider a stylus traversed along the workpiece surface for a sampling length $l_r$. Let $Z(x)$ be the vertical deviation of the profile from the **mean line** at horizontal position $x$. The mean line is positioned such that:

$$\int_0^{l_r} Z(x) \, dx = 0$$

This condition ensures the areas above and below the line are statistically equal.

**Step 2 — Defining the absolute ordinates:**
Since deviations can be positive (peaks) or negative (valleys), the *arithmetic mean* of the absolute value of these deviations defines $R_a$:

$$R_a = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} |Z(x)| \, dx$$

**Step 3 — Discrete Sampling Approximation:**
Stylus instruments like the Talysurf sample $Z(x)$ at discrete points $Z_i$. With $n$ equispaced samples along $l_r$:

$$R_a \approx \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} |Z_i|$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example:**
Suppose 8 ordinate values are sampled (in μm): $+3, -1, +4, -2, +2, -3, +5, -1$.

Sum of absolute values: $|3| + |{-1}| + |4| + |{-2}| + |2| + |{-3}| + |5| + |{-1}| = 21$

$$R_a = \frac{21}{8} = 2.625 \, \mu m$$

This value classifies the surface in the **N6 roughness grade** (turning/medium machining).

### 3.2 Derivation of $R_q$ (Root-Mean-Square Roughness)

**Step 1 — Squaring eliminates sign:**
Because squaring makes negative values positive, no absolute operator is required:

$$R_q^2 = \frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} Z(x)^2 \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Take the square root to recover magnitude:**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l_r} \int_0^{l_r} Z(x)^2 \, dx}$$

**Step 3 — Discrete form:**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i^2}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Verification (same 8 samples: $+3, -1, +4, -2, +2, -3, +5, -1$):**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{9 + 1 + 16 + 4 + 4 + 9 + 25 + 1}{8}}$$

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{69}{8}} = \sqrt{8.625} \approx 2.937 \, \mu m$$

**Step 5 — Important Relationship:**
For a Gaussian surface, $R_q \approx 1.11 \cdot R_a$. KTU examiners frequently test this ratio.

### 3.3 Derivation of $R_z$ (10-Point Mean Roughness Depth)

**Step 1 — Identify extreme peaks and valleys:**
From the R-profile, identify the **5 highest peaks** $R_{p1}, R_{p2}, ..., R_{p5}$ and the **5 deepest valleys** $R_{v1}, R_{v2}, ..., R_{v5}$ measured from the mean line within a single sampling length.

**Step 2 — Formulate the average peak-to-valley distance:**

$$R_z = \frac{1}{5}\left[(R_{p1}+R_{p2}+R_{p3}+R_{p4}+R_{p5}) - (R_{v1}+R_{v2}+R_{v3}+R_{v4}+R_{v5})\right]$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example:**
Given five peaks (in μm): $+8, +7, +6, +5, +4$ and five valleys: $-3, -4, -5, -6, -7$.

$$\sum R_p = 8+7+6+5+4 = 30 \, \mu m$$
$$\sum R_v = (-3)+(-4)+(-5)+(-6)+(-7) = -25 \, \mu m$$

$$R_z = \frac{30 - (-25)}{5} = \frac{55}{5} = 11 \, \mu m$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Evaluation Length $l_n$

The evaluation length covers **5 sampling lengths** to suppress the influence of localized scratches:

$$l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$$

For $R_a = 1.5 \, \mu m$ (from table 2.2): $l_r = 0.8$ mm.

$$l_n = 5 \times 0.8 = 4.0 \, mm$$

Thus, the stylus travels 4 mm, but $R_a$ is calculated using only the first 0.8 mm, and the reported value is typically the **average of 5 sub-readings** along $l_n$.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Roughness Parameters

| Criterion | $R_a$ | $R_z$ | $R_q$ | $R_{max}$ |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Sensitivity to outliers | Low | High | Medium | Very High |
| Statistical robustness | High | Low | High | Very Low |
| Common in drawings | Very Common | Common | Rare | Rare |
| Functional suitability | General | Sealing/Bearing | SPC/Fatigue | Defect detection |
| Definition complexity | Simple | Medium | Medium | Simple |
| Gaussian ratio (to $R_a$) | 1.00 | ≈ 4–6 | ≈ 1.11 | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Surface Texture Symbol (ISO 1302) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: ISO 1302 Surface Texture Symbol] --> B{Basic Symbol}
    B --> B1[Plain V Symbol: No machining required]
    B --> B2[Symbol with Circle: Machining required]
    B --> B3[Symbol with Horizontal Line: Lay direction specified]
    B1 --> C[Position 1: Parameter Symbol e.g. Ra Rz]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> D[Position 2: Numerical Value e.g. 1.6]
    D --> E[Position 3: Sampling Length if non-standard]
    E --> F[Position 4: Machining Method e.g. turned, ground]
    F --> G[Position 5: Cut-off Lambda_c if non-standard]
    G --> H[End: Surface Lay Direction Symbol]
```

### 4.2 Surface Profile Decomposition Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Primary Profile P] --> F{Gaussian Filter Lambda_c}
    F --> R[Roughness Profile R: high frequency]
    F --> W[Waviness Profile W: low frequency]
    R --> P1[Mean Line Calculation]
    P1 --> P2[Ra Rz Rq Parameters]
    W --> P3[Waviness Parameters]
    P2 --> END[Surface Texture Report]
    P3 --> END
```

### 4.3 Lay Direction Symbol Reference Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Lay Direction Symbols ISO 1302] --> L1[Equals Sign: Parallel to view]
    L --> L2[Perpendicular: Perpendicular to view]
    L --> L3[X: Crosswise Two directions]
    L --> L4[M: Multidirectional Patternless]
    L --> L5[C: Circular around axis]
    L --> L6[R: Radial from center]
    L --> L7[P: Particulate Dot pattern]
```

### 4.4 Sampling Length Determination Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Given Ra Value] --> R1{Ra Range}
    R1 --> R2[0.006 to 0.02 micrometer]
    R1 --> R3[0.02 to 0.1 micrometer]
    R1 --> R4[0.1 to 2.0 micrometer]
    R1 --> R5[2.0 to 10.0 micrometer]
    R1 --> R6[10.0 to 80.0 micrometer]
    R2 --> O1[Lambda_c = 0.08 mm]
    R3 --> O2[Lambda_c = 0.25 mm]
    R4 --> O3[Lambda_c = 0.8 mm]
    R5 --> O4[Lambda_c = 2.5 mm]
    R6 --> O5[Lambda_c = 8.0 mm]
```

### 4.5 Example Technical Drawing Interpretation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Drawing Mark: V-circle, Ra 0.8, Equals sign] --> B[Parse Symbol Components]
    B --> C1[V with Circle: Machined Surface Required]
    B --> C2[Ra Parameter: Use Ra Method]
    B --> C3[0.8 micrometer: Maximum Ra Allowed]
    B --> C4[Equals Sign: Lay Parallel to View]
    C1 --> D[Manufacturing Instruction for Workshop]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[QC: Talysurf Verification at lr = 0.8 mm]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** 

**Define the term 'Mean Line' as used in surface texture measurement. Explain its role in determining $R_a$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Mean Line (M-line)** is an imaginary reference line parallel to the general direction of the surface profile, positioned such that the sum of the squares of the deviations of the profile from this line, taken over the sampling length $l_r$, is statistically equal above and below the line.

$$\int_0^{l_r} Z(x) \, dx = 0$$

It serves as the **zero reference** for measuring all ordinate values $Z(x)$. Without the mean line, positive peaks and negative valleys would distort the arithmetic mean, making $R_a$ undefined. The mean line divides the profile into a balanced geometric system on which all $R_a$, $R_q$, and $R_z$ calculations are based.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** 

**State the relationship between sampling length and evaluation length in surface roughness measurement. Why is this ratio chosen?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The relationship is:

$$l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$$

where $l_r$ is the **sampling length** (the section over which a single roughness parameter is computed) and $l_n$ is the **evaluation length** (the total traversed length).

The ratio **5:1** is chosen (per ISO 4288) because:
1. A single $l_r$ may include local scratches or defects that falsely inflate $R_a$.
2. By computing $R_a$ over each of the 5 sub-segments of $l_n$ and averaging, statistical reliability improves by a factor of $\sqrt{5}$.
3. It ensures the final reported value represents the **typical texture** rather than a local anomaly.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **surface texture symbol** as per ISO 1302. Explain the meaning of every element (positions 1 to 6) of the symbol. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between the **P-profile, W-profile, and R-profile** with suitable diagrams. How is the R-profile extracted from the P-profile using filters? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Surface Texture Symbol (ISO 1302):**

The symbol is a **V-shape** (an inverted 'V' or two lines at 60° to each other) with specific additions:

| Position | Element | Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Base V | Basic symbol | Surface treatment required (V alone = no removal, V+circle = material removal by machining) |
| 1 (Above a) | Parameter symbol | $R_a$, $R_z$, $R_q$, etc. |
| 2 (Above a) | Numerical value | Upper limit of parameter (e.g., 1.6 μm for $R_a$) |
| 3 (Above b) | Second parameter | For multiple parameters |
| 4 (Below a) | Sampling length or roughness grade number | e.g., $l_r$ or N5 grade |
| 5 (Below b/c) | Machining method | e.g., "turned," "ground," "milled" |
| 6 (Adjacent to V) | Lay direction symbol | =, ⊥, X, M, C, R, P |

```
         Ra 1.6
         ___________
         \    |    /
   ===   \   |   /
  ------  \__|__/
    \     /     \
     \___/       \____ lay direction symbol
      a
    ___________
    turned
```

**(b) Profile Differentiation:**

- **P-profile (Primary)**: The raw profile obtained from the stylus instrument; contains all geometric irregularities including roughness, waviness, and form errors.
- **W-profile (Waviness)**: The mid-frequency component extracted by applying a low-pass Gaussian filter with cut-off $\lambda_f$. It contains errors from machine vibrations, thermal distortion, and tool deflections.
- **R-profile (Roughness)**: The high-frequency component obtained by subtracting the waviness from the P-profile (or equivalently by a high-pass filter with cut-off $\lambda_c$).

**Filter Extraction Process:**
1. Stylus traverses the P-profile over $l_n$.
2. Apply a **Gaussian filter** with cutoff $\lambda_c$ (e.g., 0.8 mm).
3. Frequencies below $\lambda_c$ (long wavelength) → W-profile.
4. Frequencies above $\lambda_c$ (short wavelength) → R-profile.
5. $R_a$ is then computed over the R-profile only.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Material Ratio ($R_{mr}$)** and its significance in **bearing surface design**. Mention its relation to the **Abbott-Firestone curve**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following ordinates (in μm) are recorded at equal intervals of 0.1 mm along a sampling length of 1.0 mm: $+5, +2, -3, -1, +4, -2, +6, -3, +1, -4$. Calculate (i) $R_a$, (ii) $R_q$, and (iii) classify the surface using the standard grade number chart. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Material Ratio ($R_{mr}$) and Abbott-Firestone Curve:**

The **Material Ratio** at a depth $c$ below the highest peak is defined as:

$$R_{mr}(c) = \frac{\text{Length of profile above level } c}{\text{Evaluation length } l_n} \times 100\%$$

It is expressed as a percentage (0% to 100%) and is critical for:
- **Hydrodynamic bearings** — typically require $R_{mr} \geq 70\%$ at a specific depth.
- **Sealing surfaces** — must have high $R_{mr}$ for fluid retention.
- **Wear surfaces** — need controlled $R_{mr}$ for lubricant pockets.

The **Abbott-Firestone curve** is a plot of $R_{mr}$ (y-axis, %) versus the cutting level $c$ (x-axis, depth from highest peak). Its shape reveals:
- **Steep initial drop** → poor bearing area (bad for loaded surfaces).
- **Gradual decline** → well-distributed peaks (ideal for bearings).
- The curve is **monotonically decreasing**.

**(b) Numerical Calculations:**

Given $n = 10$ samples: $Z_i = +5, +2, -3, -1, +4, -2, +6, -3, +1, -4$ (in μm).

**(i) $R_a$ Calculation:**

$$R_a = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}|Z_i|$$

$$\sum|Z_i| = 5+2+3+1+4+2+6+3+1+4 = 31$$

$$R_a = \frac{31}{10} = 3.1 \, \mu m$$

**(ii) $R_q$ Calculation:**

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} Z_i^2}$$

$$\sum Z_i^2 = 25+4+9+1+16+4+36+9+1+16 = 121$$

$$R_q = \sqrt{\frac{121}{10}} = \sqrt{12.1} \approx 3.479 \, \mu m$$

**Verification of Ratio:** $R_q / R_a = 3.479 / 3.1 \approx 1.12$ — close to the theoretical 1.11 for a Gaussian surface ✓

**(iii) Surface Grade Classification:**

Since $R_a = 3.1 \, \mu m$ falls in the range $2.0 < R_a \leq 4.0 \, \mu m$, the surface corresponds to **Roughness Grade N7** (medium machining, e.g., rough turning).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder (–0.5 marks):** Students often forget to take absolute values for $R_a$ or square them for $R_q$. Always apply $|Z_i|$ for $R_a$ and $Z_i^2$ for $R_q$.
> 2. **Wrong $\lambda_c$ Selection (–0.5 marks):** Based on $R_a = 3.1 \, \mu m$, the correct $\lambda_c = 2.5$ mm, so $l_r = 2.5$ mm (not 0.8 mm). Forgetting this loses easy marks.
> 3. **Confusing $l_r$ vs $l_n$ (–1 mark):** Many students confuse sampling length with evaluation length. Remember: $l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$.
> 4. **Skipping the Ratio Check:** Always verify $R_q/R_a \approx 1.11$ for Gaussian surfaces. Examiners love cross-checking.
> 5. **Unit Errors:** $R_a$, $R_q$, $R_z$ are typically in **μm** while $S_m$, $l_r$, $l_n$ are in **mm**. Mixing units = guaranteed mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 Surface Texture**

- ✅ **Mean Line (M-line)** = Zero-reference line for all ordinate measurements; positioned by least-squares criterion.
- ✅ **Primary Profile (P)** = Raw measured profile (everything included).
- ✅ **Roughness Profile (R)** = High-frequency filtered component.
- ✅ **Waviness Profile (W)** = Mid-frequency filtered component.
- ✅ **$l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$** — Always state this ratio explicitly.
- ✅ **$\lambda_c$ values** = 0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm — based on $R_a$ range.
- ✅ **$R_a$** is the **most widely used parameter** — universal communication tool.
- ✅ **$R_q \approx 1.11 R_a$** for Gaussian surfaces.
- ✅ **$R_z$** uses **5 peaks and 5 valleys** averaged.
- ✅ **$R_{mr}$ (Material Ratio)** = Bearing area at depth $c$, plotted via **Abbott-Firestone curve**.
- ✅ **ISO 1302** governs surface texture **symbols on drawings**.
- ✅ **V alone** = No machining; **V + Circle** = Machining required.
- ✅ **Lay symbols** = $\equiv$ (parallel), $\perp$ (perpendicular), X (crosswise), M (multi), C (circular), R (radial), P (particulate).
- ✅ **Stylus-based instruments** (Talysurf, Perthometer) traverse $l_n = 5 \cdot l_r$.
- ✅ **For KTU Board Exam:** Always state the **standard reference** (ISO 4287 / 4288 / 1302 / 21920) wherever applicable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Measuring Surface Finish: Stylus System of Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Method of Measuring Surface Finish: Stylus System of Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Surface Finish

**Surface Finish (Surface Texture)** is the measure of the geometric irregularities present on a manufactured surface. It comprises three components: **roughness, waviness, and form errors**. In engineering metrology, surface roughness is the most critical micro-geometry parameter because it directly governs functional behavior such as friction, wear, fatigue life, sealing performance, and aesthetic appeal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Surface roughness is the deviation of a real surface from its ideal form, measured over a small sampling length where the effects of waviness and form error are negligible.*

### 1.2 What is the Stylus Method?

The **Stylus Method of Measurement** (also called the **Profilometer Method** or **Profile Method**) is a *contact-type* technique in which a finely-tipped diamond stylus is drawn across the surface under test. The vertical displacement of the stylus is converted by a transducer into an electrical signal, which is then filtered, amplified, and processed to yield quantitative roughness parameters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Governing Body:** **ISO 4287:1997** (and **ISO 21920**) defines the parameters, while **ISO 3274** specifies the instrument (stylus tip radius = **2 μm ± 0.5 μm** for standard measurements, **10 μm** for粗糙 surfaces; static measuring force typically **0.75 mN**).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Finger-Surface" Intuition

Imagine you close your eyes and slowly drag your fingertip across an old vinyl record (LP). You "feel" the microscopic grooves as gentle vibrations. Now, replace your fingertip with a **sharper, lighter, and more sensitive tool** — a diamond point sliding under controlled pressure. Replace the nerve endings in your skin with an **electrical transducer**, and feed the electrical impulses into a computer that records every peak and valley. That is exactly what a *stylus profilometer* does, but with **sub-micrometer precision** and a calibration traceable to national standards.

| Finger Analogy | Stylus Instrument Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Fingertip | Diamond Stylus (radius 2 µm) |
| Skin nerves | Piezoelectric / Inductive Pickup |
| Brain | Amplifier & Filter |
| Memory | Digital Recorder / Display |

### 1.4 Why is the Stylus Method the Industry Standard?

- **Quantitative** — gives a numerical value, unlike visual/tactile comparison.
- **Direct measurement** — the stylus physically follows the true profile.
- **Traceable** — can be calibrated against ISO reference standards.
- **Versatile** — works on most engineering surfaces (metallic, polymer, ceramic).
- **High resolution** — vertical resolution down to **nanometers** in modern instruments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Highlight for KTU 2024:** The stylus method is the *only direct method* explicitly recommended by ISO/ASME for primary calibration of roughness. Always mention this in 14-mark answers to score high.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Stylus Profilometer

A modern stylus instrument (e.g., Taylor-Hobson Talysurf, Mitutoyo SJ-series) consists of six tightly integrated functional blocks. Understanding each block's role is mandatory for KTU Module 3.

1. **Stylus (Pickup Probe)** — A diamond tip of radius **2 µm** mounted on a precision skid or shoe. The skid filters out low-frequency waviness while the stylus traces high-frequency roughness.
2. **Transducer (Pickup)** — Converts mechanical displacement of the stylus into an electrical signal.
   - *Inductive (LVDT)* — most common; high linearity, robust.
   - *Piezoelectric* — high sensitivity, suited for dynamic measurements.
   - *Capacitive* — used in laboratory-grade instruments.
3. **Amplifier** — Boosts the weak transducer signal (microvolts to volts).
4. **Filter Stage** — Splits the signal into **roughness profile (R-profile)** and **waviness profile (W-profile)** using cut-off filters (Gaussian, 2CR, phase-correct).
5. **Signal Processor / Integrator** — Computes numerical parameters (Ra, Rz, Rq, Rt, etc.) by integrating the squared or absolute deviation from the mean line.
6. **Display / Recorder** — Digital readout, graphical profile plot, and printout.

### 2.2 The Reference (Mean) Line

The **Mean Line (m-line)** is the line parallel to the general surface direction such that the sum of areas above equals the sum of areas below, over the sampling length.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematically:** The integral of the profile y(x) above the mean line equals the integral below the mean line, over the sampling length **l**.

### 2.3 Sampling Length and Cut-Off Wavelength

- **Sampling length (l)** — The length over which a single roughness parameter is evaluated. It must be longer than the longest wavelength present in the roughness.
- **Cut-off wavelength (λc)** — A standard filter cut-off selected to separate roughness from waviness. Standard values per ISO 4288:

| Cut-off λc (mm) | Sampling Length (mm) | Evaluation Length (mm) | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 0.08 | 0.08 | 0.4 | Very smooth (bearings, gauges) |
| 0.25 | 0.25 | 1.25 | Smooth machined surfaces |
| 0.8 | 0.8 | 4.0 | Standard machined surfaces |
| 2.5 | 2.5 | 12.5 | Rough surfaces (rough turning) |
| 8.0 | 8.0 | 40.0 | Very rough (grinding, milling) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *sampling length* with *evaluation length*. The evaluation length = **5 × sampling length** (per ISO 4288). Memorize this!

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Meaning |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Centreline Average | $R_a$ | $R_a = \dfrac{1}{l}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{l} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx$ | µm | Mean of absolute deviation from mean line |
| Root Mean Square | $R_q$ | $R_q = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{l}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{l} y(x)^2 \, dx}$ | µm | Statistical RMS roughness |
| Maximum Peak-to-Valley | $R_t$ | $R_t = R_p + R_v$ (max) | µm | Largest single peak-to-valley height |
| Ten-Point Mean | $R_z$ | $R_z = \dfrac{(R_1+R_2+\ldots+R_5) - (M_1+M_2+\ldots+M_5)}{5}$ | µm | Avg. of 5 highest peaks − 5 lowest valleys |
| Bearing Ratio | $t_p$ | $t_p = \dfrac{\text{length of profile above level c}}{\text{sampling length}}$ | % | Material ratio at level c |
| Stylus Tip Force | $F$ | $F \leq 0.75 \text{ mN}$ (standard) | mN | Static measuring force |
| Tip Radius | $r_{tip}$ | $2 \, \mu m$ (standard) | µm | Stylus geometry |
| ISO Ra to Rz | — | $R_z \approx 4 \text{ to } 7 \times R_a$ | — | Empirical ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use \vert for absolute value in formulas** — never use the pipe character inside the markdown table to avoid breaking the table syntax.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

- **Automotive** — Cylinder bore finish (Ra 0.4 µm) controls oil retention and piston-ring life.
- **Aerospace** — Turbine blade surface finish (Ra 0.1–0.2 µm) directly affects aerodynamic drag and fatigue.
- **Bearings** — Raceway finish (Ra ≤ 0.05 µm) governs rolling contact fatigue.
- **Seals & Valves** — Shaft finish (Ra 0.2–0.8 µm) controls leak rate in hydraulic seals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Centreline Average Roughness (R_a)

**Step 1 — Set up the coordinate system.**
Let the surface profile be represented as a function $y(x)$, where $x$ is the horizontal position along the sampling length $l$, and $y(x)$ is the instantaneous vertical deviation from the mean line.

**Step 2 — Define the mean line condition.**
The mean line is chosen such that the net signed area under the profile is zero:

$$
\int_{0}^{l} y(x) \, dx = 0
$$

This is the **zero-mean condition** that the profiler's electronics enforce during signal conditioning.

**Step 3 — Define R_a mathematically.**
The Centreline Average is the arithmetic mean of the **absolute** deviation of the profile from the mean line over the sampling length $l$:

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{l} \int_{0}^{l} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx
$$

> *Logic: We use the absolute value because peaks and valleys both contribute to roughness magnitude; their signs cancel by definition of the mean line.*

**Step 4 — Convert the continuous integral to a discrete summation.**
For a digitized profile with $N$ sampled points $y_i$ equally spaced by $\Delta x$, the integral is approximated as:

$$
R_a \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert
$$

where $N = l / \Delta x$.

**Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example.**

Suppose a stylus traces a profile and records 5 sampled heights: $y_1 = +2 \, \mu m$, $y_2 = -1 \, \mu m$, $y_3 = +3 \, \mu m$, $y_4 = -2 \, \mu m$, $y_5 = -2 \, \mu m$ over $N = 5$ points.

First, verify the mean line:

$$
\frac{1}{5}(2 - 1 + 3 - 2 - 2) = \frac{0}{5} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

The mean line condition is satisfied. Now compute $R_a$:

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{5}\left( \vert +2 \vert + \vert -1 \vert + \vert +3 \vert + \vert -2 \vert + \vert -2 \vert \right)
$$

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{5}\left( 2 + 1 + 3 + 2 + 2 \right) = \frac{10}{5} = 2 \, \mu m
$$

**Therefore, the centreline average roughness of the surface is $R_a = 2 \, \mu m$.**

### 3.2 Derivation of Root-Mean-Square Roughness (R_q)

**Step 1 — Definition.**
$R_q$ (also called RMS roughness) is the square root of the mean of the squared deviations from the mean line:

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{l}\int_{0}^{l} y(x)^2 \, dx}
$$

**Step 2 — Discrete form.**

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Using the same numerical example.**

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\left( 2^2 + (-1)^2 + 3^2 + (-2)^2 + (-2)^2 \right)}
$$

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{5}\left( 4 + 1 + 9 + 4 + 4 \right)} = \sqrt{\frac{22}{5}} = \sqrt{4.4}
$$

$$
R_q \approx 2.098 \, \mu m
$$

**Comparison:** For the same profile, $R_q > R_a$ always. This is a general theorem (by Jensen's inequality) because squaring penalizes large deviations more heavily.

### 3.3 Relationship between R_a and R_q (Gaussian Profile)

For a perfectly Gaussian surface profile (a common assumption for ground and polished surfaces), the ratio is mathematically fixed:

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} \cdot R_a \approx 1.25 \, R_a
$$

**Derivation outline:**

For a Gaussian PDF of heights with standard deviation $\sigma$:

$$
R_a = \frac{2\sigma}{\sqrt{2\pi}}, \quad R_q = \sigma
$$

Dividing:

$$
\frac{R_q}{R_a} = \frac{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}}{2\sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} \approx 1.2533
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Memorize $R_q \approx 1.25 \, R_a$. This constant appears in 3-mark and 14-mark questions almost every year.

### 3.4 Derivation of Ten-Point Height (R_z, ISO 1997)

The classical R_z parameter is the **average of the maximum peak-to-valley heights over five consecutive sampling lengths within the evaluation length**:

$$
R_z = \frac{(R_{t1} + R_{t2} + R_{t3} + R_{t4} + R_{t5})}{5}
$$

where each $R_{ti}$ is the peak-to-valley height in the $i$-th sampling section.

> [!NOTE]
> In **ISO 21920 (2021)**, the old $R_z$ has been renamed $R_z1$, and a new $R_z$ is defined per sampling length. Be precise in terminology.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison

| Parameter | Sensitivity to Outliers | Best Suited For |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| $R_a$ | Moderate | General shop-floor QA |
| $R_q$ | High (due to squaring) | Statistical/research work |
| $R_t$ | Very High (single peak) | Scratch / defect detection |
| $R_z$ | High (5 peaks) | Sealing surfaces, bearings |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Stylus Profilometer

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the signal flow from the physical surface to the digital display.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SURF[Workpiece Surface] --> STY[Diamond Stylus]
    STY --> SKID[Reference Skid]
    STY --> TRX[Transducer LVDT or Piezo]
    SKID --> TRX
    TRX --> AMP[Amplifier and Rectifier]
    AMP --> FILT[Cut-off Filter Lambda C]
    FILT --> PROC[Signal Processor Integrator]
    PROC --> DSP[Digital Display]
    PROC --> REC[Profile Recorder Printer]
    PROC --> OUT[Parameter Output Ra Rq Rz Rt]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture Flow (Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSE[Sensing Stage]
        A1[Diamond Stylus 2 um radius] --> A2[Skid Shoe Assembly]
        A2 --> A3[Displacement Transducer]
    end
    subgraph COND[Signal Conditioning]
        A3 --> B1[Pre-Amplifier]
        B1 --> B2[High-Pass Filter 0.8 mm cut-off]
        B2 --> B3[Low-Pass Filter Smooth]
    end
    subgraph PROC[Processing Stage]
        B3 --> C1[Analog to Digital Converter]
        C1 --> C2[Microcontroller DSP]
        C2 --> C3[Mean Line Calculator]
        C3 --> C4[Ra Rq Rt Rz Computor]
    end
    subgraph DISP[Output Stage]
        C4 --> D1[LCD Digital Readout]
        C4 --> D2[Profile Plot Printer]
        C4 --> D3[USB PC Interface]
    end
```

### 4.3 Stylus Geometry and Skid Arrangement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STYLUS_ASSEMBLY[Stylus Pickup Assembly]
        ARM[Pivoted Arm] --> DIA[Diamond Tip]
        DIA --> SURF2[Work Surface]
        SK[Reference Skid Radius 25 mm] --> SURF2
        ARM --> LVDT[LVDT Core]
        LVDT --> COIL[LVDT Coils]
    end
```

**Physical Specifications to Remember:**

| Specification | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Stylus tip radius | **2 µm** (or 5 µm, 10 µm for rough surfaces) |
| Static measuring force | **≤ 0.75 mN** (ISO) |
| Stylus tip angle | **60° cone** (typical) |
| Skid radius | **~25 mm** |
| Vertical resolution | **0.001 µm** (1 nm) modern |
| Traverse speed | **0.5 – 1.0 mm/s** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **skid (skate)** rides on the workpiece surface and acts as a mechanical low-pass filter, automatically removing long-wavelength waviness so the stylus is left to trace only the high-frequency roughness.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
*Define the term "Centreline Average Roughness (R_a)" and write its mathematical expression. Mention the units.*

**Model Answer:**

> Centreline Average Roughness, denoted $R_a$ (or CLA), is the arithmetic mean of the absolute values of the vertical deviations of the surface profile from the mean line, measured over a specified sampling length $l$.
>
> $$
> R_a = \frac{1}{l}\int_{0}^{l} \vert y(x) \vert \, dx
> $$
>
> It is the most widely used international roughness parameter because it is **insensitive to single large scratches** and gives a stable average value. The unit is **micrometres (µm)**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*State any three advantages of the stylus method of surface roughness measurement.*

**Model Answer:**

> 1. **Quantitative and direct** — gives a numerical value traceable to ISO standards.
> 2. **High resolution** — modern instruments can resolve down to **1 nm** vertically.
> 3. **Versatile** — applicable to a wide range of materials (metals, polymers, ceramics) and surface types.
> 4. (Bonus) The signal can be filtered to separate roughness from waviness, giving a clean profile.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module question carries 14 marks and offers an *internal choice* between two sub-questions (a) and (b), each for 7 marks.

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a stylus-type surface roughness measuring instrument. List the standard stylus tip specifications as per ISO 3274.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A stylus instrument records the following ten profile readings (in µm) over a sampling length of 2.5 mm: **+3, −2, +4, −1, +2, −3, +1, −2, +3, −1**. Calculate (i) the mean line deviation check, (ii) the centreline average roughness $R_a$, and (iii) the RMS roughness $R_q$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle & Block Diagram**

The stylus method works on the principle of **mechanical-electrical transduction**. The diamond-tipped stylus (tip radius **2 µm**, static force **0.75 mN**) is traversed horizontally across the surface at a constant speed (typically **0.5 mm/s**) over a defined evaluation length. As the stylus rides over microscopic peaks and valleys, it undergoes tiny vertical displacements. These displacements are sensed by a transducer (LVDT or piezoelectric), producing a proportional electrical signal.

**Block Diagram:**

```
Surface → Stylus + Skid → LVDT Transducer → Amplifier
       → Cut-off Filter (λc) → Integrator → Display / Recorder
```

**ISO 3274 Stylus Specifications:**

| Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Tip radius | $2 \, \mu m \pm 0.5 \, \mu m$ |
| Tip angle (cone) | $60^{\circ}$ |
| Static force | $\le 0.75 \text{ mN}$ |
| Skid radius | $\approx 25 \text{ mm}$ |

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution**

Given profile readings: $+3, -2, +4, -1, +2, -3, +1, -2, +3, -1$ (in µm), $N = 10$.

**(i) Mean line check:**

$$
\sum y_i = 3 - 2 + 4 - 1 + 2 - 3 + 1 - 2 + 3 - 1 = 4
$$

Since the sum is **not zero**, the data is referenced to an *arbitrary* zero; we must either shift the data or use absolute deviation. For typical exam simplification, we assume the data is referenced to the mean line. (If shifted by $\bar{y} = 0.4$, new values: $+2.6, -2.4, +3.6, -1.4, +1.6, -3.4, +0.6, -2.4, +2.6, -1.4$ µm.)

**(ii) Centreline Average Roughness (R_a):**

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y_i \vert
$$

$$
R_a = \frac{1}{10}\left( 3+2+4+1+2+3+1+2+3+1 \right) = \frac{22}{10}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_a = 2.2 \, \mu m}
$$

**(iii) RMS Roughness (R_q):**

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i^2}
$$

$$
R_q = \sqrt{\frac{1}{10}\left( 9+4+16+1+4+9+1+4+9+1 \right)} = \sqrt{\frac{58}{10}}
$$

$$
R_q = \sqrt{5.8} \approx 2.408 \, \mu m
$$

$$
\boxed{R_q \approx 2.41 \, \mu m}
$$

**Verification:** $R_q / R_a = 2.41 / 2.20 \approx 1.095$, close to the theoretical Gaussian ratio of 1.25, indicating a near-Gaussian profile. ✓

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **sampling length** and **evaluation length** in stylus measurement. How are they selected as per ISO 4288? What is the role of the **cut-off filter**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **R_a, R_q, R_t, and R_z** with neat sketches. Why is R_a preferred as the universal roughness parameter in industry?

**Model Solution Outline:**

**Part (a) — Sampling and Evaluation Length**

- *Sampling length* is the length over which a single measurement of a roughness parameter is made. ISO 4288 fixes it equal to the **cut-off wavelength $\lambda_c$**.
- *Evaluation length* = **5 × sampling length** (to average out local irregularities).
- The cut-off is selected based on the expected roughness grade of the surface (0.08, 0.25, 0.8, 2.5, 8.0 mm).
- The **cut-off filter** electrically/mechanically removes long-wavelength waviness (form errors and waviness) so the parameter reflects roughness only.

**Part (b) — Differentiation Table**

| Parameter | Definition | Sensitivity to Spikes | Preferred Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $R_a$ | Mean of $\vert y \vert$ | Moderate | Universal shop-floor QA |
| $R_q$ | RMS of $y$ | High | Research, statistics |
| $R_t$ | Max peak-to-valley | Very High | Scratch detection |
| $R_z$ | Avg. of 5 peak-to-valley | High | Bearings, seals |

**Why R_a is preferred:**
- Stable and repeatable.
- Insensitive to single scratches/outliers.
- Easy to compute and calibrate.
- Adopted by **80%+ of international drawings** as the primary callout.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Points — Avoid These Mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $R_a$ (parameter) with $\lambda_c$ (cut-off)** — they have different units and meanings.
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute value** in the $R_a$ integral. Marks are lost if the formula lacks $\vert \, \vert$.
> 3. **Quoting $R_z$ for the new ISO 21920** — call it $R_z1$ to be safe.
> 4. **Not mentioning the skid** in a block diagram — full marks require it, as the skid defines the reference plane.
> 5. **Wrong ratio** — saying $R_q = R_a$ is a common error; the correct theoretical ratio is $\sqrt{\pi/2} \approx 1.25$.
> 6. **Skipping units** — every numerical answer MUST carry units (µm).
> 7. **Mixing evaluation length and sampling length** — remember evaluation = 5 × sampling.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Stylus method** is the *direct, quantitative, ISO-traceable* method for measuring surface roughness.
- ✅ **Standard stylus tip radius** = **2 µm**; static force = **0.75 mN**; cone angle = **60°**.
- ✅ **Reference line** = the mean line, with zero net signed area under the profile.
- ✅ **R_a formula** (must memorize):

$$
