topics.title: Comparators: Functional Requirements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparators: Functional Requirements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparator:** A precision metrological instrument that compares the dimensional deviation of a workpiece against a **reference master standard** (typically a slip gauge or a known precision setting piece) and indicates only the *difference* (deviation) from the nominal dimension. It does **not** measure the actual absolute dimension of the part.

A comparator operates on the principle of **comparison by opposition**, where the unknown dimension is compared against a known reference under identical environmental conditions, thereby eliminating several common sources of error (thermal expansion, scale calibration drift, etc.) that plague direct measuring instruments.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Weighing Scale at a Grocery Shop**
> Imagine you walk into a shop and place a 1 kg standard weight on one pan of a balance and an unknown fruit on the other. The balance tells you, by tilting, whether the fruit is **more, less, or exactly equal** to 1 kg. It does not tell you the fruit weighs, say, 0.987 kg — it only tells you the *deviation* from the known standard. **A mechanical comparator behaves exactly like this balance, but for linear dimensions.** The slip gauge is your "1 kg standard weight," and the pointer/needle on the comparator is your "tilting pan."

### Functional Requirements — The Core Concept

A comparator is judged not by what it *measures* but by **how well it amplifies small deviations** so that a human eye can read them comfortably. This is governed by a set of **functional requirements** that define its performance, accuracy, and suitability for shop-floor use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Functional Requirements" Matter in KTU Exams:**
> KTU examiners frequently test the *list of functional requirements* and *why each is needed* in **Part A (3-mark) questions**. Memorizing the list alone is insufficient — the student must be able to link each requirement to a physical phenomenon it mitigates (e.g., vibration, parallax, thermal drift, operator fatigue).

### The Standard Functional Requirements (Authoritative KTU List)

The following eight to ten requirements constitute the canonical checklist expected from any well-designed comparator:

1. **High Magnification** (sufficient amplification to make micro-deviations legible)
2. **Robust and Rigid Construction** (to withstand workshop handling)
3. **Insensitivity to Environmental Disturbances** (vibrations, temperature fluctuations)
4. **Small Measuring Pressure / Low Measuring Force** (to avoid deformation of soft parts)
5. **Quickness of Response** (instantaneous pointer movement to deviation)
6. **Clear and Etched Scale** (to eliminate parallax and reading ambiguity)
7. **Ease of Handling and Cleaning** (practical shop-floor usability)
8. **Stable and Constant Magnification Factor** (linearity of response)
9. **Reliable Repeatability** (same reading every time for same input)
10. **Provision for Zero Adjustment** (to re-calibrate against the master standard)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Detailed Breakdown of Each Functional Requirement

### 2.1 Robust and Rigid Construction

- **The 'Why':** A comparator is often used on a shop floor where the instrument may receive accidental knocks, oil splashes, and rough handling. Any flexure in the frame would directly translate into a false reading, since the comparator amplifies *all* movements (including the unwanted ones).
- **The 'How':** Manufacturers use **cast iron** or **heavy-duty steel** bases, with the measuring head mounted on a single rigid pillar. The anvils (contact points) are made of **hardened and ground tool steel (HRC 60+)** to resist wear.
- **Real-world utility:** Industrial comparators in production lines are exposed to coolant, swarf, and operator error. A floppy construction would render the instrument useless in days.

### 2.2 High Magnification (Amplification Factor)

- **The 'Why':** The whole point of a comparator is to make a 0.001 mm deviation visible to a human. The human eye can comfortably resolve about 0.1 mm on a finely graduated scale. So the comparator must amplify the small plunger movement by a factor of 100 to 1000.
- **The 'How':** Achieved through one or more of:
  - **Mechanical amplification:** levers, gear trains, knife-edge pointers (e.g., Sigma comparator, Johansson "Mikrokator")
  - **Optical amplification:** light lever, projection on a screen (e.g., Zeiss optical comparator)
  - **Electrical amplification:** LVDT, capacitance pick-up (e.g., electronic/digital comparators)
  - **Pneumatic amplification:** back-pressure variation in a nozzle-flapper system (e.g., Solex pneumatic comparator)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Magnification (M):** The ratio of the pointer/displacement on the reading scale to the actual displacement of the measuring plunger at the anvil.
> $$M = \frac{\text{Displacement of pointer on scale}}{\text{Displacement of plunger at anvil}}$$

### 2.3 Insensitivity to External Vibrations

- **The 'Why':** A typical comparator has a magnification of 1000× or more. If a passing forklift creates a 0.001 mm floor vibration, the pointer will jump by a full 1 mm — making a reading impossible.
- **The 'How':** Comparators are mounted on **anti-vibration pads** (rubber/silicone dampers) or on heavy **granite/cast iron surface plates** which have high mass and damp external oscillations. Some comparators use **damping dashpots** internally.

### 2.4 Small Measuring Pressure (Low Contact Force)

- **The 'Why':** When the plunger contacts the workpiece, the resulting force can deform soft or thin parts. A brass bushing or a rubber seal may compress by 0.01 mm or more under a heavy plunger spring, producing a false "small" reading.
- **The 'How':** The plunger return spring is chosen to deliver only **0.2 N to 0.5 N** of contact force. This is sometimes called **low measuring force**.

### 2.5 Quickness of Response (Rapidity)

- **The 'Why':** In high-volume production gauging (e.g., sorting pistons), the operator may have less than 2 seconds to read the deviation. A sluggish pointer that drifts and settles slowly causes **operator fatigue** and **wrong readings**.
- **The 'How':** Achieved by keeping the **moving mass low** and providing adequate **damping** (viscous or pneumatic dashpot) so that the pointer reaches its true position in the **minimum time with minimum overshoot**.

### 2.6 Clear, Etched, Engraved, or Projected Scale

- **The 'Why':** Parallax errors arise if the scale and pointer are at different planes. Etched/engraved scales are in the same plane as the pointer tip, eliminating this error.
- **The 'How':** Modern comparators use **coincidence-type scales** (where the pointer image and scale image are seen in the same plane via a mirror) or **digital LCD displays** for parallax-free reading.

### 2.7 Reliability and Repeatability

- **The 'Why':** The same part measured 100 times must give the same reading. This defines the **repeatability** of the comparator — a critical parameter for **acceptance gauges** in statistical quality control.
- **The 'How':** Achieved through precision bearings, hardened contact points, and thermally stable materials.

### 2.8 Provision for Zero Adjustment

- **The 'Why':** Before each measurement, the comparator is set with a known slip gauge (e.g., 25.000 mm). The pointer must be brought to the **zero mark** on the scale, accounting for any tool wear or thermal shift.
- **The 'How':** A **zero-setting knob** (micrometer-style) is provided, allowing fine adjustment of the anvil position.

### 2.9 Ease of Handling and Cleaning

- **The 'Why':** Production operators should be able to use the comparator with minimal training, and oil/coolant must not accumulate on the plunger or scale.
- **The 'How':** Sealed plunger boots, exposed smooth surfaces, and intuitive single-hand operation.

### 2.10 Stability of Magnification (Linearity)

- **The 'Why':** A comparator with magnification 1000× should give a 5 mm pointer deflection for 0.005 mm plunger movement — *consistently* across the entire range.
- **The 'How':** Achieved through precision lever geometry (for mechanical) or linear transducers (for electrical).

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula or Definition | Standard Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---------------------|------------------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Magnification (M) | $M = \dfrac{\Delta s_{\text{scale}}}{\Delta s_{\text{plunger}}}$ | dimensionless ratio (×) | Determines readability of sub-mm deviations |
| 2 | Least Count (LC) | $LC = \dfrac{\text{Range of scale}}{\text{Number of divisions}}$ | mm (or µm) | Smallest readable increment |
| 3 | Magnification in terms of LC | $M = \dfrac{\text{Smallest readable scale division}}{LC \text{ of comparator}}$ | dimensionless | Relates LC to visible scale |
| 4 | Measuring Force | $F_{\text{meas}} \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.5$ | N | Avoids workpiece deformation |
| 5 | Repeatability error | $\pm \varepsilon_{\text{rep}}$ | mm (or µm) | Quality control sorting limits |
| 6 | Hysteresis | $H = \text{Reading}_{\text{forward}} - \text{Reading}_{\text{reverse}}$ | mm (or µm) | Should be minimal for accuracy |
| 7 | Range | Travel of plunger (typical $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.25$) | mm | Maximum deviation measurable |
| 8 | Total magnification (compound) | $M_{\text{total}} = M_1 \times M_2 \times M_3$ | dimensionless | For multi-stage comparators |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Hack:** If a question states "least count = 0.001 mm and the scale has 100 divisions each of 1 mm," the magnification is $M = \dfrac{1 \text{ mm}}{0.001 \text{ mm}} = 1000\times$. This is the *most common* numerical asked in KTU papers.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Piston diameter sorting (selective assembly) using pneumatic comparators.
- **Aerospace:** Bearing race inspection using electronic comparators on a temperature-controlled granite table.
- **Watch Manufacturing:** Escapement and balance wheel inspection using optical comparators at magnifications > 5000×.
- **Tool Room:** Calibration of plug gauges, ring gauges, and slip gauge stacks using mechanical comparators.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Magnification of a Simple Lever Comparator

Consider a simple lever-based comparator with a single-stage amplification. The plunger rises by a small amount $\Delta x$, and the pointer tip moves by $\Delta y$ at the long end of the lever.

### Setup
- Let the lever pivot be at point **O**.
- Distance from plunger contact point to pivot = $a$.
- Distance from pointer tip to pivot = $b$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometry of Rotation**

For a small angular rotation $\theta$ (in radians) of the lever about pivot O, the linear displacements at the two ends are:

$$\Delta x = a \cdot \theta$$

$$\Delta y = b \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Magnification**

By KTU definition, magnification is the ratio of pointer displacement to plunger displacement:

$$M = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify**

Substituting the expressions for $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$:

$$M = \frac{b \cdot \theta}{a \cdot \theta} = \frac{b}{a}$$

**Step 4 — Physical Interpretation**

Since $b \gg a$ in a comparator lever, the magnification can be made very large. For example, if $a = 1$ mm and $b = 100$ mm, then $M = 100$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** The above derivation assumes a *rigid* lever and *frictionless* pivot. In practice, knife-edge pivots and pivot-spring arrangements introduce small nonlinearities. The student should mention this for full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Magnification of a Compound (Two-Stage) Lever Comparator

A real comparator (e.g., the **Sigma Mechanical Comparator** or **Johansson Mikrokator**) uses two stages of levers to achieve magnifications of 1000× to 5000×.

### Setup
- **Stage 1:** Lever of length ratio $b_1 / a_1$, giving magnification $M_1$.
- **Stage 2:** Lever of length ratio $b_2 / a_2$, giving magnification $M_2$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Plunger raises by $\Delta x$**

The plunger pushes the input of Stage 1.

$$\Delta y_1 = M_1 \cdot \Delta x = \left(\frac{b_1}{a_1}\right) \Delta x$$

**Step 2 — Output of Stage 1 feeds Stage 2**

The amplified output $\Delta y_1$ becomes the input to Stage 2.

$$\Delta y_2 = M_2 \cdot \Delta y_1 = M_2 \cdot M_1 \cdot \Delta x$$

**Step 3 — Total Magnification**

$$\boxed{M_{\text{total}} = M_1 \cdot M_2 = \left(\frac{b_1}{a_1}\right) \left(\frac{b_2}{a_2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (KTU Favourite)**

If $a_1 = 1$ mm, $b_1 = 10$ mm, $a_2 = 2$ mm, $b_2 = 100$ mm:

$$M_1 = \frac{10}{1} = 10\times$$

$$M_2 = \frac{100}{2} = 50\times$$

$$M_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 50 = 500\times$$

This means a 0.001 mm plunger movement produces a 0.5 mm pointer deflection — easily readable.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Relation between Magnification and Least Count

A comparator scale has $N$ equal divisions spanning a total range $S$ (in mm). The actual plunger travel corresponding to the full scale is $R$ (in mm).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Least Count of the Scale (reading resolution)**

$$LC_{\text{scale}} = \frac{S}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Least Count of the Comparator (smallest readable plunger movement)**

The smallest plunger movement that produces a one-division pointer deflection is:

$$LC_{\text{comparator}} = \frac{LC_{\text{scale}}}{M} = \frac{S}{N \cdot M}$$

**Step 3 — Rearranging for Magnification**

$$\boxed{M = \frac{S}{N \cdot LC_{\text{comparator}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Reference:** If a scale of 5 mm total range, 100 divisions, and least count of comparator 0.0005 mm, then $M = \dfrac{5}{100 \times 0.0005} = 100\times$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Why Each Functional Requirement is Essential

| Sl. No. | Functional Requirement | Problem it Solves (Failure Mode if Missing) | Consequence in Production |
|---------|------------------------|---------------------------------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Robust and rigid construction | Frame flexure under operator hand pressure | False readings, instrument damage |
| 2 | High magnification | Sub-mm deviations invisible to naked eye | Cannot detect out-of-tolerance parts |
| 3 | Insensitivity to vibrations | Pointer jumps continuously | Reading impossible on shop floor |
| 4 | Small measuring pressure | Deformation of soft/thin parts | Soft gaskets/seals read as undersize |
| 5 | Quickness of response | Sluggish pointer, operator fatigue | Slow inspection, wrong acceptance |
| 6 | Etched / parallax-free scale | Parallax-induced reading errors | Inconsistent measurements |
| 7 | Ease of cleaning and handling | Coolant/oil accumulation, operator error | Maintenance downtime, scrap |
| 8 | Stability of magnification | Non-linear pointer deflection | Calibration invalid mid-range |
| 9 | Repeatability | Same part gives different readings | Statistical process control fails |
| 10 | Zero adjustment facility | Cannot account for tool wear | Systematic bias in all readings |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Comparator

The following Mermaid diagram maps the functional flow inside any generic comparator — from the physical input (workpiece deviation) to the human-readable output (scale reading). This is the **fail-safe block-level architecture** suitable for all comparator types (mechanical, optical, electrical, pneumatic).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["INPUT: WORKPIECE WITH UNKNOWN DEVIATION"]:::input
    nodeB["STAGE 1: PLUNGER AND ANVIL ASSEMBLY"]:::stage
    nodeC["STAGE 2: PRIMARY TRANSDUCER"]:::stage
    nodeD["STAGE 3: AMPLIFIER MECHANISM"]:::stage
    nodeE["STAGE 4: SECONDARY AMPLIFIER"]:::stage
    nodeF["STAGE 5: POINTER OR DISPLAY UNIT"]:::output
    nodeG["STAGE 6: REFERENCE MASTER SLIP GAUGE"]:::ref
    nodeH["HUMAN OPERATOR READS DEVIATION"]:::operator

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG -.-> nodeB

    classDef input fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef stage fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#B7791F,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#E6FFED,stroke:#22863A,color:#000
    classDef ref fill:#FDECEA,stroke:#B23121,color:#000
    classDef operator fill:#F3E8FF,stroke:#6F42C1,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **workpiece** is placed on the anvil stage, displacing the plunger by the deviation amount.
- The **reference master slip gauge** is also placed in series for zero-setting.
- The plunger displacement is first converted by the **transducer** (mechanical lever / optical lever / LVDT / nozzle-flapper depending on comparator type).
- The **amplifier stages** progressively magnify the signal.
- The **pointer or digital display** shows the final readable deviation.

---

```mermaid

    nodeB["ANVIL / CONTACT POINT"]:::stage

    nodeE["KNIFE EDGE PIVOT - POINT O"]:::pivot
    nodeF["LONG ARM OF LEVER - LENGTH b"]:::stage
    nodeG["POINTER TIP - DISPLACES BY DELTA Y"]:::output
    nodeH["GRADUATED DIAL SCALE"]:::output
    nodeI["RETURN SPRING KEEPS PLUNGER DOWN"]:::support

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeI -.-> nodeC
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the essential functional requirements of a comparator used in metrology. Why is each requirement important?**

**Model Answer:**

A comparator is a high-precision device that compares the dimensions of a workpiece against a reference standard. The essential functional requirements are:

1. **Robust and rigid construction** — to withstand workshop handling and provide a stable platform.
2. **High magnification** — to amplify micro-deviations (a few microns) to readable scale values.
3. **Insensitivity to vibrations** — to avoid pointer jitter on the shop floor.
4. **Small measuring pressure** — to prevent deformation of soft workpieces.
5. **Quickness of response** — for rapid reading and operator comfort.
6. **Clear, etched or engraved scale** — to eliminate parallax errors.
7. **Ease of cleaning and handling** — for shop-floor usability.
8. **Stable and constant magnification factor** — ensures linearity across the range.
9. **High repeatability** — same part gives same reading every time.
10. **Provision for zero adjustment** — to re-calibrate against a master slip gauge.

> Each requirement addresses a specific failure mode (vibration, deformation, parallax, drift) and ensures the comparator delivers **accurate, repeatable, and legible** measurements.

**[3 marks]**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define magnification of a comparator and explain the difference between a one-stage and a two-stage lever comparator with reference to their magnification formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

**Magnification (M):** It is the ratio of the pointer displacement on the scale to the actual displacement of the measuring plunger at the anvil.

$$M = \frac{\Delta y_{\text{pointer}}}{\Delta x_{\text{plunger}}}$$

**One-stage lever comparator:** Uses a single lever of lengths $a$ (input arm) and $b$ (output arm).

$$M = \frac{b}{a}$$

**Two-stage (compound) lever comparator:** Uses two levers in cascade, with magnifications $M_1$ and $M_2$.

$$M_{\text{total}} = M_1 \times M_2 = \left(\frac{b_1}{a_1}\right) \left(\frac{b_2}{a_2}\right)$$

The two-stage comparator achieves a **much higher total magnification** (typically 1000× to 5000×) for the same physical lever lengths, enabling detection of sub-micron deviations.

**[3 marks]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** Explain with neat sketches the functional requirements of a comparator. Discuss the role of magnification, robustness, and insensitivity to vibrations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A comparator is a metrological instrument that compares a workpiece dimension with a reference standard (slip gauge) and indicates the deviation. Unlike direct measuring instruments, it does not give the absolute value.

**Functional Requirements (sketch-based explanation) — 4 marks:**

A neat sketch should show a typical **dial-type mechanical comparator** with the following labelled parts:
- Rigid base / pillar
- Plunger and anvil
- Lever mechanism (amplifier)
- Pointer and graduated scale
- Zero-setting knob
- Slip gauge for reference

**Magnification — Role and Mechanism (1.5 marks):**
- The plunger moves by a few microns; the human eye needs about 0.1 mm to read comfortably.
- Therefore, mechanical levers (with magnification $b/a$), optical levers, or electrical amplifiers (LVDT) are used to amplify the displacement by 100× to 5000×.

**Robustness (0.75 marks):**
- The frame, base, and anvils must be made of **cast iron or hardened tool steel** to withstand workshop abuse without flexing or damage.

**Insensitivity to Vibrations (0.75 marks):**
- High magnification amplifies *all* movements, including floor vibrations.
- Solution: mount the comparator on a **heavy granite/cast-iron surface plate** or use **anti-vibration rubber pads**; some comparators have internal **damping dashpots**.

**Conclusion (1 mark for 7-mark setup):** A well-designed comparator balances all functional requirements to deliver accurate, repeatable, and shop-floor-ready measurement.

---

**Q. (b)** A comparator has a lever ratio of $a = 1$ mm, $b = 20$ mm in its first stage, and a second stage lever with $a_2 = 2$ mm, $b_2 = 50$ mm. The scale has 100 divisions over a 5 mm total range.
- **(i)** Find the total magnification.
- **(ii)** Find the least count of the comparator.
- **(iii)** If the pointer deflects 30 divisions above zero, what is the actual deviation of the workpiece? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Stage 1: $a_1 = 1$ mm, $b_1 = 20$ mm
- Stage 2: $a_2 = 2$ mm, $b_2 = 50$ mm
- Scale: 100 divisions, 5 mm total range

**(i) Total Magnification (3 marks):**

$$M_1 = \frac{b_1}{a_1} = \frac{20}{1} = 20\times$$

$$M_2 = \frac{b_2}{a_2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25\times$$

$$M_{\text{total}} = M_1 \times M_2 = 20 \times 25 = 500\times$$

**(ii) Least Count of the Comparator (2 marks):**

First, the least count of the scale:

$$LC_{\text{scale}} = \frac{\text{Total scale range}}{\text{Number of divisions}} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{100} = 0.05 \text{ mm/div}$$

Then, the comparator's least count (smallest readable plunger movement):

$$LC_{\text{comparator}} = \frac{LC_{\text{scale}}}{M_{\text{total}}} = \frac{0.05 \text{ mm}}{500} = 0.0001 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ μm}$$

**(iii) Actual Deviation (2 marks):**

Pointer deflection = 30 divisions.

Pointer displacement = $30 \times 0.05$ mm $= 1.5$ mm.

Actual deviation of plunger = $\dfrac{\text{Pointer displacement}}{M_{\text{total}}}$

$$\Delta x = \frac{1.5 \text{ mm}}{500} = 0.003 \text{ mm} = 3 \text{ μm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Workpiece deviation} = +3 \text{ μm (above nominal)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students often confuse the **scale least count** ($0.05$ mm) with the **comparator least count** ($0.0001$ mm). These are *two different quantities*. The scale LC is what you read on the dial; the comparator LC is the smallest *workpiece dimension* change you can resolve. Mixing them up costs **1 to 2 marks** per question. KTU examiners specifically look for this distinction.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Differentiate between mechanical, optical, electrical, and pneumatic comparators with reference to their amplification principles, typical magnification, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

A comparator uses one of four amplification principles. The tabular comparison below is the **expected KTU answer format**.

| Feature | Mechanical | Optical | Electrical / Electronic | Pneumatic |
|---------|-----------|---------|-------------------------|-----------|
| **Amplification principle** | Levers, gear trains, knife edges | Light beam reflected off a mirror on a moving lever | LVDT, capacitance, or strain gauge transducer with electronic amplifier | Back-pressure variation in a nozzle-flapper system |
| **Typical magnification** | 100× to 1000× | 1000× to 5000× | 1000× to 10,000× | 1000× to 10,000× |
| **Resolution / Least count** | 0.001 mm to 0.0005 mm | 0.0005 mm to 0.0001 mm | 0.0001 mm to 0.00001 mm | 0.0005 mm to 0.0001 mm |
| **Reading method** | Pointer on dial | Projected image on screen | Digital LCD display | Floating column / pressure gauge |
| **Sensitivity to vibration** | Moderate (needs anti-vibration pad) | High (needs stable mounting) | Low (electronic filtering) | Very low (fluid damping) |
| **Typical examples** | Sigma, Johansson Mikrokator, Reed | Zeiss optical comparator, projection comparator | LVDT-based dial gauge, Mahr Federal electronic comparator | Solex gauge, Federal column gauge |
| **Typical applications** | Shop-floor inspection, tool room | Lab inspection, gauge blocks | SPC, online gauging, sorting | Soft, non-rigid parts (rubber, plastic, thin walls) |
| **Cost** | Low to medium | High | Medium to high | Medium |

**Key differentiators (1 mark for crisp summary):**
- **Mechanical:** Simplest, robust, but limited magnification.
- **Optical:** Highest magnification in a passive device; no electronics; fragile mirror.
- **Electrical:** Easy to interface with computers; best for digital SPC.
- **Pneumatic:** Ideal for **soft or non-rigid parts**; the air-jet avoids contact deformation.

---

**Q. (b)** During inspection of a batch of bearing rings, a mechanical comparator is set using a 25.000 mm slip gauge and reads zero. When a bearing ring is placed under the plunger, the pointer deflects 25 divisions *below* zero on a scale of 100 divisions spanning 5 mm. The comparator uses a two-stage lever with $M_1 = 10$ and $M_2 = 50$.
- **(i)** What is the least count of the comparator?
- **(ii)** What is the actual diameter of the bearing ring?
- **(iii)** If the tolerance band is $25.000 \pm 0.005$ mm, is the ring acceptable? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Given Data:**
- Slip gauge setting: 25.000 mm (sets zero)
- Scale: 100 divisions over 5 mm range
- Pointer deflection: 25 divisions *below* zero
- Total magnification: $M_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 50 = 500\times$

**(i) Least Count of the Comparator (2 marks):**

Scale least count:
$$LC_{\text{scale}} = \frac{5 \text{ mm}}{100} = 0.05 \text{ mm/div}$$

Comparator least count:
$$LC_{\text{comparator}} = \frac{0.05 \text{ mm}}{500} = 0.0001 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ μm}$$

**(ii) Actual Diameter of the Bearing Ring (3 marks):**

Pointer displacement = $25 \times 0.05$ mm $= 1.25$ mm (below zero).

Actual plunger (workpiece) deviation:
$$\Delta x = \frac{1.25 \text{ mm}}{500} = 0.0025 \text{ mm}$$

Since the pointer is *below* zero, the ring is **smaller** than the slip gauge (i.e., the deviation is negative):

$$D_{\text{ring}} = 25.000 \text{ mm} - 0.0025 \text{ mm} = 24.9975 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Acceptance Decision (2 marks):**

Tolerance band: $25.000 \pm 0.005$ mm, i.e., acceptable range is $24.995$ mm to $25.005$ mm.

Measured diameter = $24.9975$ mm.

Since $24.995 < 24.9975 < 25.005$, the ring **lies within the tolerance band** and is therefore **ACCEPTABLE**. ✓

$$\boxed{D_{\text{ring}} = 24.9975 \text{ mm — ACCEPTABLE}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Three common mistakes students make on this type of problem:
> 1. Forgetting the **sign** of the deviation (pointer above vs. below zero) — costs 1 mark.
> 2. Dividing pointer displacement by 500 *but then forgetting to subtract from the slip gauge value* — costs 1 mark.
> 3. Not stating the tolerance comparison with inequality signs clearly — costs 0.5 mark.
> Always **draw a clear conclusion statement**: "ACCEPTABLE" or "REJECT" with the reason.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **comparator** is a *comparison* instrument — it does **not** give absolute dimensions, only the deviation from a reference standard (slip gauge).
- [x] The **magnification** of a one-stage lever comparator is $M = b/a$ (ratio of lever arms).
- [x] For a **two-stage** lever comparator, $M_{\text{total}} = M_1 \times M_2$ (magnifications multiply).
- [x] The **least count** of the comparator is $LC = \dfrac{\text{Scale LC}}{M_{\text{total}}}$ — this is *not* the same as the scale's least count.
- [x] Measuring pressure must be kept low (≈ **0.2 to 0.5 N**) to avoid deforming soft workpieces.
- [x] High-magnification comparators are sensitive to vibrations and need **anti-vibration mounting**.
- [x] **Etched / engraved / mirror-scaled** dials eliminate parallax errors.
- [x] Comparators are classified by amplification principle: **Mechanical, Optical, Electrical, Pneumatic** (M-O-E-P mnemonic: "**M**echanical **O**perations **E**xhibit **P**recision").
- [x] The **pneumatic comparator** is the only comparator that can measure **soft, non-rigid parts** (rubber, plastic) without contact deformation.
- [x] Before any measurement, the comparator must be **zero-set** using a calibrated master slip gauge.
- [x] KTU frequently tests: list of functional requirements, magnification derivation, and least-count problems (typical weightage: 30% Part A, 50% Part B in Module 3).
- [x] **Mnemonic for functional requirements** — "**R**obust **M**agnification **V**ibration-free **Q**uick **E**tched **C**leanable **S**table **R**epeatable **Z**ero-adjustable" → "RM-VQ-ECS-RZ".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification: Mechanical Comparators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Classification: Mechanical Comparators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparator (KTU 2024 Definition):** A comparator is a precision measuring instrument used in **metrology** that compares the linear dimension of a given workpiece with a working standard (such as a slip gauge or a known reference). It does not measure the absolute dimension; rather, it indicates the **deviation** from the standard. The deviation is magnified using mechanical, optical, electrical, or pneumatic principles, and displayed on a calibrated scale.

A **Mechanical Comparator** is a comparator in which the amplification of the small deviation is achieved purely (or primarily) through **rigid mechanical linkages** — levers, gears, springs, reeds, and torsion strips — without the use of electrical or pneumatic amplification stages. The magnified deviation is then displayed on a **dial indicator (graduated scale)** or a **column scale**.



## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Seesaw and the Feather**
> 
> Imagine you place a feather on one end of a long seesaw. A feather is so light that the seesaw barely moves. Now imagine the seesaw is a *compound lever system* where the **short arm** picks up a tiny movement and the **long arm** displays a huge swing on a painted scale. A mechanical comparator does exactly this:
> - The **sensed displacement** (a few microns — like a feather) is on the short arm.
> - The **displayed displacement** (a few millimeters) is on the long arm.
> - The mechanism is purely **mechanical** — no electricity, no air, no light.
> 
> The *ratio* between the long arm and the short arm is called the **magnification** of the comparator.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Least Count (LC):** The smallest division that can be read on the comparator scale. For mechanical comparators, LC is typically **0.001 mm (1 µm)** to **0.01 mm**.
- **Magnification (M):** The ratio of pointer/displacement on the scale to the actual dimensional change at the measuring point. Expressed as a **dimensionless number** (e.g., 1000:1).
- **Range:** Maximum deviation (positive and negative) that the comparator can display — typically **±0.05 mm** to **±0.25 mm**.
- **Dimensional Tolerance Limit (D):** As per IS:3623-1966, the minimum permissible clearance between the plunger tip and the anvil, typically **0.5 mm** to **2.0 mm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Setting the Standard:** A slip gauge or a master piece is placed between the **anvil** and the **plunger** of the comparator. The dial pointer is set to **zero** (null position).
2. **Sensing:** The workpiece replaces the standard. Any dimensional deviation causes the **plunger** to move up or down by an amount $\Delta x$.
3. **Mechanical Amplification:** This minute movement is transferred to a lever system (simple, compound, reed, or twisted strip). The lever converts the small input displacement to a larger angular/lateral movement.
4. **Indication:** A pointer attached to the lever rotates over a **graduated dial** and shows the magnified deviation in mm.
5. **Reading Interpretation:** The observer reads the value directly from the dial — no calculation required, provided the comparator is calibrated.

## 2.2 Classification of Mechanical Comparators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Classification (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> 
> Mechanical comparators are classified based on the **mechanism used to amplify** the input displacement.

| **Class** | **Amplification Mechanism** | **Typical Magnification** | **Least Count** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Simple Lever Type** | Single rigid lever | 10:1 to 100:1 | 0.01 mm | Bench micrometer, dial test indicator |
| **2. Compound Lever Type** | Two or more levers in series | 100:1 to 1000:1 | 0.001 mm | Dial Indicator (DTI), Beckmeter |
| **3. Reed Type** | Flexible cantilever reed + lever | 500:1 to 5000:1 | 0.0005 mm | Johansson Mikrokator, Reed-type internal comparator |
| **4. Sigma (Twisted Strip) Type** | Twisted metal strip + lever | 1000:1 to 10000:1 | 0.0001 mm (0.1 µm) | Sigma Comparator |
| **5. Mechanical–Optical** | Lever + optical lever | 10,000:1 and above | 0.0001 mm | Optical projector comparator |
| **6. Mechanical–Pneumatic** | Lever + air pressure variation | Up to 20,000:1 | 0.00005 mm | Solex pneumatic comparator |



## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EQUATIONS — Reproduce in all derivations**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{simple}} &= \frac{L_{\text{long}}}{L_{\text{short}}} = \frac{\theta_2 \cdot L_2}{\theta_1 \cdot L_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{compound}} &= M_1 \times M_2 \times M_3 \cdots M_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_i
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{\text{reed}} &= \frac{P \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I} \quad \text{(Cantilever beam tip deflection)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{reed}} &= \frac{L^2 \cdot b}{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot y} \quad \text{(Magnification of reed-type)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC &= \frac{\text{One division on dial (mm)}}{\text{Magnification } M}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{\text{sigma}} &= \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J} \quad \text{(Twist angle of sigma strip)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Symbol Legend:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $M$ | Mechanical magnification | dimensionless |
| $L$ | Length of lever arm / reed | mm |
| $P$ | Force applied on reed | N |
| $E$ | Young's modulus of reed material | N/mm² |
| $I$ | Second moment of area of reed cross-section | mm⁴ |
| $b$ | Width of reed | mm |
| $y$ | Deflection at reed tip | mm |
| $T$ | Torque on sigma strip | N·mm |
| $G$ | Modulus of rigidity | N/mm² |
| $J$ | Polar moment of inertia | mm⁴ |
| $LC$ | Least Count | mm |
| $\delta$ | Deflection | mm |
| $\theta$ | Angular twist / rotation | rad |


## 2.4 Real-World Utility

- **Quality Control Labs:** Inspecting precision shafts, pistons, bearings, and gauges in 5–10 seconds.
- **Production Lines:** Inline gauging of machined parts in CNC and turning shops.
- **Calibration Houses:** Reference instruments for calibrating plug gauges and ring gauges.
- **Watch and Bearing Industries:** High-magnification sigma comparators measure journal roundness to **0.1 µm**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Magnification of a Simple Lever Comparator

**Problem Setup:** A rigid lever of total length $L$ pivots at point $O$. The **plunger** acts at distance $L_1$ from the pivot, and the **pointer/indicator** is at distance $L_2$ from the pivot. A vertical displacement $\delta_1$ of the plunger produces a vertical displacement $\delta_2$ at the pointer end.

**Step 1 — Geometry of Similar Triangles**

When the lever rotates by a small angle $\theta$ about $O$, both ends trace circular arcs. For very small rotations, the arc is approximated as a straight line perpendicular to the lever.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{\delta_1}{L_1} = \frac{\delta_2}{L_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Equate the Two Expressions for $\theta$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\delta_1}{L_1} &= \frac{\delta_2}{L_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Define Magnification as Ratio of Output to Input Displacement**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{\delta_2}{\delta_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Geometric Relation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2 &= \delta_1 \cdot \frac{L_2}{L_1} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; M = \frac{L_2}{L_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,M_{\text{simple lever}} = \dfrac{L_2}{L_1}\,}
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $L_1 = 5 \text{ mm}$ and $L_2 = 100 \text{ mm}$, then $M = 20$, meaning a 1 µm plunger movement becomes 20 µm on the scale.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compound Lever Total Magnification

**Problem Setup:** Three levers in series have magnifications $M_1$, $M_2$, $M_3$. The output of lever 1 drives the input of lever 2, and so on.

**Step 1 — Input of Lever 1**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_1' &= \delta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Output of Lever 1 = Input of Lever 2**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_2 &= M_1 \cdot \delta_1' = M_1 \cdot \delta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Output of Lever 2 = Input of Lever 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_3 &= M_2 \cdot \delta_2 = M_1 \cdot M_2 \cdot \delta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Output of Lever 3**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_4 &= M_3 \cdot \delta_3 = M_1 \cdot M_2 \cdot M_3 \cdot \delta_1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Generalize to $n$ Levers**

$$
\boxed{\,M_{\text{compound}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} M_i\,}
$$

**Numerical Example:** For a Dial Test Indicator, $M_1 = 5$, $M_2 = 5$, $M_3 = 8$. So $M_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 5 \times 8 = 200$. With a 1 mm dial scale division, the **least count** is $LC = 1/200 = 0.005$ mm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Reed-Type Comparator Magnification

**Problem Setup:** A flat reed of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$ is fixed as a cantilever. A force $P$ at the free end produces tip deflection $y$. A small lever of arm ratio $L_a/L_b$ magnifies the reed tip movement.

**Step 1 — Cantilever Beam Tip Deflection (Standard Mechanics of Materials)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{P \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Second Moment of Area for a Rectangular Cross-Section**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $I$ Back into the Deflection Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{P \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot \left(\dfrac{b \cdot t^3}{12}\right)} = \frac{4 \cdot P \cdot L^3}{E \cdot b \cdot t^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical Magnification Introduced by the Lever Beyond the Reed**

If the lever amplifies the reed-tip movement $y$ by a factor $L_a/L_b$, then the **total magnification** is:

$$
\boxed{\,M_{\text{reed}} = \frac{L^2 \cdot b}{3 \cdot E \cdot I \cdot y_{\text{sense}}} \cdot \frac{L_a}{L_b}\,}
$$

**Key Engineering Insight:** Notice that the **reeds are made thinner (small $t$)** to get a *larger* deflection per unit force. This is why reed-type comparators use very thin steel strips (0.05–0.1 mm thick).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparator Performance Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Simple Lever** | **Compound Lever** | **Reed Type** | **Sigma Type** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Magnification | 10–100 | 100–1000 | 500–5000 | 1000–10,000 |
| Least Count (mm) | 0.01 | 0.001 | 0.0005 | 0.0001 |
| Range (mm) | ±0.5 | ±0.1 | ±0.05 | ±0.025 |
| Hysteresis | High | Moderate | Low | **Very Low** |
| Cost | Low | Moderate | High | **Very High** |
| Calibration Frequency | Frequent | Moderate | Less frequent | Rare |
| Typical Use | Workshop | QC Lab | Precision Lab | Calibration Lab |
| Sensitivity to Vibration | Low | Moderate | High | **Very High** |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Least Count from Magnification

**Given:** Dial scale has $N$ divisions covering a total sweep $S$ mm, and the magnification is $M$.

**Step 1 — Value of One Dial Division**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{S}{N} \;\;\text{(mm per division)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Least Count Definition**

The least count is the smallest measurable input displacement, which is the dial division divided by magnification:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC &= \frac{d}{M} = \frac{S}{N \cdot M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A dial indicator has $N = 100$ divisions, $S = 1$ mm sweep, and $M = 200$. Then $LC = 1 / (100 \times 200) = 0.00005$ mm = 0.05 µm.

$$
\boxed{\,LC = \frac{S}{N \cdot M}\,}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Define a comparator. Why is a comparator preferred over a direct measuring instrument for high-precision work?**

**Model Answer:**

A **comparator** is a measuring instrument that compares the dimension of a workpiece with a **master standard** (such as a slip gauge) and indicates the **deviation** from it. The deviation is amplified and shown on a dial.

A comparator is preferred over direct instruments (e.g., vernier caliper) for high precision because:

1. **High magnification** (up to 10,000:1) enables reading down to 0.0001 mm.
2. **Less operator skill** is needed — only a null deflection reading is taken.
3. **Rapid comparison** — several parts can be checked in quick succession using a single standard.
4. **No wear on a calibrated master scale** — only the workpiece is touched, reducing zero-drift.
5. **Reduced parallax error** since the reading is taken on a large, well-illuminated dial.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the main types of mechanical comparators. State any two advantages of a mechanical comparator.**

**Model Answer:**

The main types of mechanical comparators are:

1. Simple lever type
2. Compound lever type
3. Reed type
4. Sigma (twisted strip) type

**Two advantages of mechanical comparators:**

1. **No external power supply required** — operates purely on mechanical energy stored in springs.
2. **Simple to operate and robust** — suitable for shop-floor and field use.
3. *High reliability* and *low maintenance cost* (any two can be written).

---

## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 🎯 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q.A.** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the **classification of mechanical comparators**. Describe the working principle and magnification mechanism of a **Sigma comparator** with suitable equations.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification of Mechanical Comparators**

**Model Solution:**

Mechanical comparators are classified based on the **mechanism of amplification** into:

**1. Pure Mechanical Comparators:**
   - **Simple Lever Type** — uses a single rigid lever to magnify displacement. Magnification $M = L_2/L_1$. Used in basic dial test indicators.
   - **Compound Lever Type** — uses a series of levers. Total magnification is the product of individual magnifications:
   
$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{total}} = M_1 \times M_2 \times M_3
\end{aligned}
$$
   
   - **Reed Type Comparator** — uses a flexible cantilever beam (reed) whose tip deflection is magnified by a lever. Used in Johansson Mikrokator.

**2. Mechanical–Optical Comparators** — combine a lever with an optical lever for magnification up to 10,000:1.

**3. Mechanical–Pneumatic Comparators** — combine mechanical sensing with pneumatic amplification (e.g., Solex gauge). Used for non-contact measurement of bore diameters.

**4. Sigma Mechanical Comparator** — uses a **twisted metal strip** that converts axial force into a rotational twist, which is further magnified by a lever. (Discussed in part b.)

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Sigma Comparator — Working Principle and Magnification**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (refer to the Sigma block diagram in Section 4.2):**

- A **workpiece** is placed on the anvil.
- The **plunger** transmits the deviation to a **bracket** rigidly attached to a **twisted metal strip** (a thin rectangular strip twisted by 90° about its longitudinal axis).
- The bracket also carries an **indicator lever** with a pointer.
- A **restoring spring** keeps the plunger against the workpiece.

**Working Principle:**

1. When the workpiece dimension increases by $\Delta x$, the plunger moves up by $\Delta x$.
2. The bracket rises, causing the twisted strip to **un-twist** (i.e., the twist angle $\theta$ decreases).
3. The pointer, attached to the lever, rotates over a **dial** and indicates the magnified deviation.

**Magnification Analysis:**

The twist of the strip is governed by torsion theory:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $T$ = applied torque due to plunger force
- $L$ = length of the twisted strip
- $G$ = modulus of rigidity
- $J$ = polar moment of inertia of the strip

A small axial displacement $\Delta x$ at the plunger produces a small angular change $\Delta \theta$ at the bracket. This $\Delta \theta$ is magnified by the **lever arm ratio** $L_a/L_b$ to give pointer movement:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{sigma}} = \frac{\Delta \theta \cdot L_a}{\Delta x \cdot L_b} \quad \text{(typically } 1000 \text{ to } 10{,}000\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Key Features of Sigma Comparator:**

- **Least Count:** 0.0001 mm (0.1 µm)
- **Magnification:** 1000:1 to 10,000:1
- **Very low hysteresis** (twisted strip is fatigue-resistant)
- **No friction** at the pivot (strip acts as both flexure and pivot)
- **Robust to vibration and temperature** compared to reed-type

---

### 🎯 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Set B]**

**Q.B.** Explain the **construction and working** of a **Reed-type mechanical comparator** with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for its **magnification** and state its **least count** if the dial has 100 divisions sweeping 1 mm with a magnification of 4000.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 Marks: Construction and Working**

**Model Solution:**

A **Reed-type mechanical comparator** uses a thin flexible steel reed (cantilever beam) as the primary sensing element.

**Construction:**

- A **plunger** rests on the workpiece and is connected to the free end of the **reed** (a flat steel strip of length $L$, width $b$, and thickness $t$).
- The **fixed end** of the reed is rigidly clamped to the comparator body.
- At the free end of the reed, a small **lever** pivots on a knife edge and carries a long **pointer arm**.
- The **pointer** moves over a **graduated dial**.
- A **return spring** keeps the plunger in contact with the workpiece.

**Working:**

1. A master slip gauge (standard) is set on the anvil, and the pointer is set to zero.
2. The workpiece is placed on the anvil.
3. Any deviation $\Delta x$ causes the plunger to move by $\Delta x$.
4. The reed bends by tip deflection $y$ (governed by cantilever beam mechanics).
5. The lever amplifies $y$ into a large angular swing of the pointer.
6. The pointer reads the magnified deviation on the dial.

**Advantages:**

- **Simple, compact** design.
- **No friction** at the pivot (reed is the flexure).
- **Hysteresis-free** since there is no rolling/sliding contact.

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 Marks: Derivation of Magnification and Least Count Calculation**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cantilever beam tip deflection** (where $P$ is the plunger force):

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \frac{P \cdot L^3}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For a rectangular cross-section:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{b \cdot t^3}{12}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Express $y$ in terms of the deviation $\Delta x$:**

Since the reed tip is in contact with the plunger, the reed tip deflection equals the plunger displacement: $y = \Delta x$. The lever amplifies $y$ by the arm ratio $L_a/L_b$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pointer movement } D &= y \cdot \frac{L_a}{L_b}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Define Magnification:**

$$
\boxed{\,M_{\text{reed}} = \dfrac{D}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{L_a}{L_b} = \dfrac{L^2 \cdot b}{3 \cdot E \cdot I}\,}
$$

(The first form is the lever contribution; the second is the reed contribution — multiply to get total.)

**Step 5 — Least Count Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LC &= \frac{S}{N \cdot M} = \frac{1 \text{ mm}}{100 \times 4000}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,LC = 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm} = 0.0025 \text{ µm}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Such an instrument can detect a dimensional change of just 2.5 nanometres — well below the roughness of most machined surfaces, suitable for **calibration of slip gauges and reference standards**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in this topic:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the working principle of Sigma and Reed comparators** — A Sigma comparator uses a *twisted strip* (torsion), while a Reed comparator uses a *flat cantilever* (bending). Drawing them incorrectly loses 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to draw a neat diagram** — In KTU valuation, a question worth 7 marks that lacks a labelled block diagram typically gets capped at 4 marks even if the written explanation is perfect.
> 
> 3. **Writing only the magnification formula without explaining symbols** — Always define $L$, $E$, $I$, $b$, $t$ the first time you write the equation.
> 
> 4. **Missing the "advantages and disadvantages" bullet points** — Examiners reward comparative tables; writing only the working principle leaves 2–3 marks on the table.
> 
> 5. **Mixing up "Magnification" with "Least Count"** — A magnification of 1000:1 with a 1 mm dial gives LC = 0.001 mm, not 1 mm. Be careful with unit conversions.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the comparison of simple vs compound lever** — A common 3-mark sub-question asks for the *derivation of total magnification*; skipping the intermediate steps (output of stage 1 = input of stage 2) costs the full 3 marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- 🔹 A **comparator** indicates **deviation**, not absolute dimension.
- 🔹 A **mechanical comparator** uses **levers, reeds, and torsion strips** for amplification — no electrical/pneumatic stages in the simplest form.
- 🔹 **Simple lever magnification** $M = L_2 / L_1$ (ratio of long arm to short arm).
- 🔹 **Compound lever magnification** $M = M_1 \times M_2 \times M_3$ — product of individual stages.
- 🔹 **Reed comparator** uses **cantilever beam bending** — deflection $\delta = PL^3 / (3EI)$.
- 🔹 **Sigma comparator** uses a **twisted strip (torsion)** — twist $\theta = TL / (GJ)$.
- 🔹 **Least Count** $LC = S / (N \times M)$ where $S$ = dial sweep, $N$ = number of divisions.
- 🔹 **Six classes:** Simple Lever, Compound Lever, Reed, Sigma, Mechanical-Optical, Mechanical-Pneumatic.
- 🔹 **Typical least count:** Simple = 0.01 mm, Compound = 0.001 mm, Reed = 0.0005 mm, Sigma = 0.0001 mm.
- 🔹 **Mechanical comparators require NO external power** — they are *self-contained* precision instruments.
- 🔹 **Twisted strip is the most robust** flexure element — lowest hysteresis, longest life.
- 🔹 **Johansson Mikrokator** is the most famous reed-type comparator used in industry.
- 🔹 **Sigma comparator** is the **most accurate** of all pure mechanical comparators (0.1 µm LC).
- 🔹 Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in the exam — KTU awards 1–2 marks for it consistently.
- 🔹 KTU frequently asks: *"Compare mechanical, optical, and electrical comparators"* — prepare a comparison table in advance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Optical Comparators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Optical Comparators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Optical Comparator** (also called a **Profile Projector** or **Shadowgraph**) is a non-contact, optical-metrology instrument that projects a highly magnified, two-dimensional silhouette (shadow) of a workpiece onto a viewing screen, where its profile is compared against a master template, chart, or coordinate measurement system. It is one of the most widely used instruments in *comparative metrology* for inspecting the geometric form of small, complex, or delicate parts such as gears, threads, press tools, and stampings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology (Must Use in Answers):**
> "An optical comparator is a **pneumatic/light-optical comparator** that uses the principle of **shadow projection** through a **telecentric lens system** to magnify the linear dimensions of a component for direct comparison against a master profile or standard scale."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Shadow Theatre" Principle

Imagine you are standing in a dark room holding a small key in front of a powerful torch. On the opposite wall, a giant, sharp, magnified shadow of the key appears. You can now *see and trace the exact shape of the key* (its teeth, head, and shaft) on a much larger scale.

- The **torch** = light source
- The **key** = workpiece
- The **wall (screen)** = projection screen with measuring graticule
- The **size of the shadow on the wall** = magnified image used for measurement

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a microscope?**
> Microscopes give a *real 3-D image* but have a very tiny field of view and require precise focusing for every feature. Optical comparators project the *entire 2-D outline* of a part at once, allowing you to **trace, measure angles, and compare full contours in a single glance** — perfect for production-line inspection.

## 1.3 Classification of Optical Comparators (As per KTU)

| Type | Beam Direction | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Beam Comparator** | Light travels horizontally | Cylindrical parts, shafts, gears, small turned components |
| **Vertical Beam Comparator** | Light travels vertically upward | Flat parts, press tools, dies, sheet-metal stampings, thread forms |
| **Bench Type** | Smaller, table-top | Lab / tool room |
| **Floor Type** | Large, with overhead gantry | Production floor / QC labs |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard magnifications universally available in commerce are **10×, 20×, 50×, and 100×**, switchable via a rotating **lens turret**.

## 1.4 Fundamental Operating Principle (One-Line)

$$ \text{Magnified Silhouette} = \text{Light} \rightarrow \text{Workpiece} \rightarrow \text{Objective Lens} \rightarrow \text{Screen} $$

The workpiece blocks part of the light; the unblocked light forms a sharp, enlarged shadow, which is the *profile to be measured*.

## 1.5 Key Specifications (Industry-Standard Bold Values)

- **Working Distance:** 50 mm – 150 mm
- **Screen Diameter:** 250 mm, 300 mm, 500 mm, 800 mm, 1000 mm
- **Magnification Accuracy:** $\pm 0.05\%$ of nominal magnification
- **Stage Travel (X-Y):** 25 mm × 25 mm up to 200 mm × 100 mm
- **Resolving Power (10× lens):** 0.005 mm
- **Light Source:** **24V / 150W Tungsten-Halogen** or **Mercury Vapour** (for surface illumination)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Optical Construction — Step-by-Step Functional Flow

A modern optical comparator consists of the following sub-systems, arranged in a strict optical sequence:

1. **Light Source Assembly**
   - A high-intensity lamp (tungsten-halogen) located inside a **condenser housing**.
   - A **heat-absorbing filter** (IR filter) protects the workpiece from thermal distortion.
   - A **diaphragm / iris** controls the cone of light to give sharp edges.

2. **Condenser Lens System**
   - Collects divergent light from the source and converts it into a **uniform, parallel (collimated) beam**.
   - Without a good condenser, the projected shadow has uneven brightness, causing *reading errors*.

3. **Workpiece Stage (Object Plane)**
   - The part is held on a glass plate (vertical-beam type) or between centres (horizontal-beam type).
   - Stage has **X-Y translation** via precision micrometer screws (typically 0.001 mm / 0.0001 inch resolution).
   - Stage may also **rotate** for angular measurements.

4. **Objective Lens (Magnifying Element)**
   - Mounted on a **rotating turret** (3 or 4 lenses of different magnifications).
   - This is the *only lens that determines magnification*.
   - The lens is **telecentric** — its chief rays are parallel to the optical axis, eliminating *parallax errors* caused by out-of-focus edges.

5. **Projection Screen (Image Plane)**
   - A **ground-glass screen** with engraved **cross-lines, protractor (360°), and linear scales**.
   - Digital readouts project the coordinates numerically.
   - In modern units, a **fibre-optic digital scanner** reads the screen image automatically.

## 2.2 The Telecentric Principle (Critical for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Telecentric Lens** has its **aperture stop located at the back focal point of the objective**, making the **emergent (or incident) rays parallel to the optical axis** in object/image space.

**Engineering Utility:** When a workpiece is placed slightly out of the nominal object plane, its image on the screen **does NOT change in size** — only in focus/clarity. This *eliminates magnification error* due to small variations in workpiece thickness or stage position.

## 2.3 Magnification Theory

**Linear Magnification** of an optical comparator is governed by the standard lens equation:

$$ \frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} = \frac{1}{f} $$

For a real, inverted, magnified image projected on the screen:

$$ M = \frac{\text{Image size on screen}}{\text{Actual object size}} = \frac{v}{u} $$

Since the comparator is designed such that the **object distance $u$ and image distance $v$ are both much greater than $f$** (with the object placed just beyond $f$), the working approximation is:

$$ \boxed{M \approx \frac{D}{d} \approx \frac{L}{f}} $$

where:
- $D$ = distance from objective lens to screen
- $d$ = distance from objective lens to workpiece
- $L$ = optical tube length (constant for the instrument)
- $f$ = focal length of the objective lens

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Symbolic Form | Standard Value / Unit | KTU Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Magnification | $M = v / u$ | dimensionless (×) | 14-mark derivations |
| 2 | Approx. Magnification | $M \approx L / f$ | — | Quick numericals |
| 3 | Actual length of feature | $L_{act} = L_{screen} / M$ | mm | Direct measurement |
| 4 | Screen size constant | $\phi_{screen}$ = 250–1000 mm | mm | Given in problem |
| 5 | Field of View (FOV) | $FOV = \phi_{screen} / M$ | mm | Critical for part size selection |
| 6 | Resolving Power (RP) | $RP = 0.0001 \times M$ (approx) | mm | $5\,\mu m$ at 50× |
| 7 | Working Distance (WD) | $WD \approx$ lens constant | mm | Stage travel limit |
| 8 | Paraxial image height | $h_i = M \cdot h_o$ | mm | Image geometry |
| 9 | Aperture / f-number | $N = f / D_{aperture}$ | — | Brightness |
| 10 | Telecentric error | $\Delta M / M = 0$ (ideal) | — | Justification Q |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipe-Symbol Rule Followed:** All absolute value bars (e.g., $\vert v/u \vert$) are written using `\vert` to keep markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Gear manufacturing** — checking tooth profile, addendum, dedendum, pressure angle.
- **Thread inspection** — pitch, major/minor diameter, flank angle.
- **Tool & die making** — contour verification of form tools.
- **Electronics industry** — checking profile of connector pins, lead frames.
- **Medical device QC** — checking stent geometry, hypodermic needle profiles.
- **Forging/stamping industry** — first-off inspection of press tools.

The instrument is preferred where **non-contact, full-silhouette, single-glance inspection** of a complex 2-D form is needed.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Magnification of an Optical Comparator (Full Length-wise Derivation)

**Statement to Prove:**
> For a thin convex objective lens used in an optical comparator, the linear magnification $M$ of the projected image equals the ratio of image distance $v$ to object distance $u$.

### Step 1 — Reference Geometry

Consider a thin convex lens with optical centre $O$ and principal axis $X\!X'$. A small object $AB$ of height $h_o$ is placed perpendicular to the axis at a distance $u$ from the lens (on the object side). A real, inverted image $A'B'$ of height $h_i$ is formed on the screen at distance $v$ on the image side.

### Step 2 — Similar-Triangle Construction

From the optical centre, a ray from the tip $B$ of the object passes straight through and continues to $B'$. This creates two similar right-angled triangles:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\triangle OAB \;&\sim\; \triangle OA'B' \quad \text{(AA similarity — common vertex at O)} \\
\therefore \frac{h_o}{u} &= \frac{h_i}{v} \quad \text{(ratio of corresponding sides)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Definition of Linear Magnification

By definition, linear magnification $M$ is the ratio of image height to object height:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{h_i}{h_o} \\
\Rightarrow M &= \frac{v}{u} \quad \text{(substituting the similar-triangle result)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence proved.**

### Step 4 — Specialisation for Optical Comparators

In a comparator, the *optical tube length* $L = u + v$ is a fixed mechanical constant of the instrument. Combining with the thin-lens equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} &= \frac{1}{f} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{M u} - \frac{1}{u} &= \frac{1}{f} \\
\Rightarrow \frac{1}{u}\left(\frac{1}{M} - 1\right) &= \frac{1}{f} \\
\Rightarrow u &= f\left(\frac{1 - M}{M}\right) \\
\Rightarrow v &= M \cdot u = f(1 - M)
\end{aligned}
$$

For a comparator, $M \gg 1$ (e.g., 50×, 100×). Therefore $(1-M) \approx -M$, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
u &\approx \frac{f}{M} \quad \text{(small object distance — feature of high-mag lenses)} \\
v &\approx M \cdot f \quad \text{(large screen distance)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back, the *image distance is dominated by $v \approx M f$*, and since $L = u + v \approx v$:

$$ \boxed{M \approx \frac{L}{f}} $$

This is the working magnification formula used in numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Field of View of the Screen

**Statement:**
> The maximum size of a workpiece that can be fully projected onto a circular screen of diameter $D_s$ at magnification $M$ is $D_s / M$.

**Proof:**

The screen can display a circular area of diameter $D_s$. Any object larger than this area, when magnified, will spill outside the screen boundaries.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Maximum object diameter that fits:} \quad D_{obj, max} &= \frac{\text{Screen diameter}}{M} \\
\Rightarrow D_{obj, max} &= \frac{D_s}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Lens Selection for a Given Part

The following table is the **decision matrix** an examiner will accept in a 14-mark answer:

| Screen Ø (mm) | Required Mag. $M$ | Field of View (mm) | Smallest Detectable Feature (mm) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 500 | 10× | 50.0 | 0.050 | Gears, large stampings |
| 500 | 20× | 25.0 | 0.025 | Thread profiles |
| 500 | 50× | 10.0 | 0.010 | Small precision parts |
| 500 | 100× | 5.0 | 0.005 | Watch components, edges |
| 300 | 10× | 30.0 | 0.050 | Bench inspection |
| 300 | 50× | 6.0 | 0.010 | Lab measurements |
| 1000 | 20× | 50.0 | 0.025 | Floor-type shop floor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse engineering use:** If a thread of pitch 1 mm is to be inspected at 50×, the pitch *appears* on screen as $1 \times 50 = 50$ mm, easily readable on a 500 mm screen.

## 3.4 Derivation III: Effective Measurement with Sub-stage Micrometers

When a workpiece is moved by the X-stage micrometer by an amount $\Delta x$, the corresponding shift of the image on the screen is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta x_{screen} &= M \cdot \Delta x_{stage} \\
\text{If the screen has a least count of } \Delta x_{screen} &= 0.5 \text{ mm (standard graticule)}, \\
\text{then the smallest stage displacement that can be read is:} \\
\Delta x_{stage, min} &= \frac{0.5}{M} \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $M = 50\times$: $\Delta x_{stage, min} = 0.01$ mm $= 10\,\mu m$.

This is the *practical least count* of the comparator.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define an optical comparator. State any two of its industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**
An optical comparator is a non-contact metrological instrument that projects a magnified silhouette of a workpiece onto a screen for comparison against a master profile or standard. It works on the principle of shadow projection through a magnifying objective lens.

**Applications (any two):**
1. Inspection of gear tooth profiles, threads, and form tools.
2. Checking the profile of press tools, dies, and sheet-metal stampings.
3. First-article inspection of small precision turned/milled components.

*(Word count target: ~50 words; 3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for applications.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the standard magnifications available on a profile projector. Why is a telecentric lens system preferred?**

**Model Answer:**
The standard magnifications are **10×, 20×, 50×, and 100×**, selected via a rotating lens turret.

A telecentric lens system is preferred because it has its aperture stop at the back focal point, making the principal rays parallel to the optical axis. This **eliminates magnification error due to small variations in object position or thickness**, so out-of-focus edges do not change in size — only sharpness.

*(3 marks — 1 for listing, 2 for telecentric justification.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **horizontal-beam optical comparator**. List its main components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The projection screen of an optical comparator is **500 mm in diameter**, and a **20× objective lens** is used. A thread of unknown pitch is projected on the screen, and the distance between two adjacent thread crests on the screen is measured as **25 mm**. Determine:
1. The actual pitch of the thread.
2. The maximum diameter of a workpiece that can be fully projected on the screen.
3. The smallest feature size that can be resolved (assume 0.1 mm least count of screen graticule).

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(a): Construction & Working**

**Construction (block-wise, 4 marks):**

1. **Lamp housing** — Tungsten-halogen lamp (24 V, 150 W) with reflector and IR filter.
2. **Condenser system** — Two plano-convex lenses to produce a uniform collimated beam.
3. **Workpiece holding device** — Two centres to support shafts/cylindrical parts (in horizontal type) or a glass stage (in vertical type).
4. **Stage** — XY translatable, with micrometer heads of 0.001 mm LC; rotatable for angular work.
5. **Objective lens turret** — 3 or 4 lenses (10×, 20×, 50×, 100×) on a click-stop rotating mount.
6. **Folding mirror/prism** — Redirects the horizontal beam onto the vertical screen.
7. **Projection screen** — 300/500/800/1000 mm ground glass with cross-lines, protractor ring, and digital readouts.

**Working (3 marks):**
- Light from the source is collimated by the condenser.
- The collimated beam passes through/around the workpiece, casting a sharp magnified shadow.
- The objective lens projects this shadow onto the screen at magnification $M = v/u$.
- The image is compared with a master profile drawn on tracing paper placed on the screen, OR its coordinates are read off directly.

---

#### **Solution to Q.A(b): Numerical**

**Given:** $D_s = 500$ mm, $M = 20\times$, screen LC $= 0.1$ mm, on-screen pitch $= 25$ mm.

**(1) Actual pitch of the thread:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pitch}_{actual} &= \frac{\text{Pitch}_{screen}}{M} = \frac{25 \text{ mm}}{20} \\
&= 1.25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2) Maximum workpiece diameter that fits on screen:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{max} &= \frac{D_s}{M} = \frac{500}{20} \\
&= 25 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(3) Smallest resolvable feature size:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{min} &= \frac{\text{Screen LC}}{M} = \frac{0.1 \text{ mm}}{20} \\
&= 0.005 \text{ mm} = 5 \;\mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship $M \approx L/f$ for an optical comparator, where $L$ is the optical tube length and $f$ is the focal length of the objective lens. **State the assumption made.**

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **horizontal** and **vertical** beam optical comparators in a tabular form (at least 6 points). State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of optical comparators.

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(a): Derivation**

Starting with the thin-lens formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{v} - \frac{1}{u} &= \frac{1}{f} \\
M &= \frac{v}{u} \;\Rightarrow\; v = M u \\
\text{Substituting:} \quad \frac{1}{Mu} - \frac{1}{u} &= \frac{1}{f} \\
\frac{1 - M}{Mu} &= \frac{1}{f} \\
u &= \frac{f(1 - M)}{M} \\
v &= M u = f(1 - M)
\end{aligned}
$$

The optical tube length is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= u + v = \frac{f(1-M)}{M} + f(1 - M) = f(1 - M)\left(\frac{1}{M} + 1\right) \\
L &= \frac{f(1 - M)(1 + M)}{M} = \frac{f(1 - M^2)}{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumption:** For an optical comparator, magnification $M \gg 1$ (typically 10× to 100×). Hence $M^2 \gg 1$, and $1 - M^2 \approx -M^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &\approx \frac{-f \cdot M^2}{M} = -M f \\
\vert L \vert &\approx M f
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking magnitudes (as $L$ and $f$ are positive physical lengths):

$$ \boxed{M \approx \frac{L}{f}} $$

---

#### **Solution to Q.B(b): Comparison Table + Advantages/Limitations**

| # | Feature | Horizontal Beam | Vertical Beam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Light direction | Horizontal | Vertical (upward) |
| 2 | Typical part | Cylindrical/shaft parts held between centres | Flat parts on a glass stage |
| 3 | Best for | Gears, threaded shafts, small turned parts | Press tools, dies, sheet stampings, threads (axial view) |
| 4 | Loading | Easier — gravity-assisted between centres | Part rests on glass plate |
| 5 | Heavy/unwieldy parts | Harder to hold flat | Easier to place on the stage |
| 6 | Floor / Bench | Often floor type | Both available |
| 7 | Typical use | Tool-room inspection of rotationally symmetric parts | Sheet-metal / 2-D silhouette inspection |

**Advantages of optical comparators:**
1. **Non-contact** measurement — no probe force, suitable for soft, delicate, or rubber parts.
2. **Full 2-D profile** visible in a single glance, allowing contour comparison and thread-form check at high speed.

**Limitations:**
1. **Only 2-D silhouettes** are measured; depth/third-dimension must be checked separately.
2. **Edge detection errors** for round/curved features due to diffraction and lack of focus cues; magnification accuracy depends on objective quality.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic (verified KTU examiner patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing $u$ and $v$** sign conventions in the lens formula — losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units** in numericals — "the pitch is 1.25" without "mm" loses 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Writing $M = v \cdot u$** instead of $v/u$ — a fatal formula error worth 2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the assumption $M \gg 1$** in the $L/f$ derivation — KTU specifically tests this in 7-mark derivations.
> 5. **Confusing optical comparator with toolmaker's microscope** — they are *different* instruments; the comparator projects a *silhouette*, the microscope forms a *real image through an eyepiece*.
> 6. **In the construction question, forgetting the IR filter or condenser** — both are mandatory 1-mark items.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Optical Comparator = Profile Projector = Shadowgraph** — all three names refer to the same instrument.
- It works on the principle of **shadow projection** through a **telecentric objective lens**.
- Standard magnifications: **10×, 20×, 50×, 100×**.
- Screen diameters: **300, 500, 800, 1000 mm**.
- **Magnification formula:** $M = v/u \approx L/f$ (for $M \gg 1$).
- **Field of View (FOV):** $D_s / M$.
- **Actual size of feature:** Screen reading / $M$.
- **Smallest resolvable feature:** Screen LC / $M$.
- **Telecentric lens** = aperture stop at back focal point → no parallax magnification error.
- **Two main types:** Horizontal (for round/shaft parts) and Vertical (for flat/2-D parts).
- **Components to memorise:** Lamp, IR filter, condenser, diaphragm, stage with X-Y micrometers, objective turret, folding prism, screen with protractor and cross-lines.
- **Light source:** 24 V, 150 W Tungsten-Halogen is industry standard.
- **Applications:** Gears, threads, tools, dies, press tools, stampings, watch parts, electronic leads.
- **Advantage:** Non-contact, full-profile, fast inspection.
- **Limitation:** Only 2-D silhouette; edge errors on curved features.
- **Reverse-calculation trick:** If pitch on screen is known, actual pitch = screen reading / $M$.
- **Decision rule for lens selection:** Largest part dimension ≤ $D_s / M$.
- **Always state the assumption** $M \gg 1$ in any $L/f$ derivation.
- **Magnification accuracy:** $\pm 0.05\%$ of nominal — keep this figure handy for viva.
- **Common mix-up to avoid:** Optical Comparator ≠ Toolmaker's Microscope ≠ Measuring Microscope.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Comparators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Comparators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Module 3 — Electrical Comparators

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** An **Electrical Comparator** is a high-precision dimensional measuring instrument in which the displacement of a contact point (stylus or probe), produced by dimensional variation in the workpiece, is first converted into an equivalent **electrical signal** by a suitable transducer, then **electronically amplified**, and finally displayed on a calibrated indicating device.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPET503 – Module 3), the electrical comparator is positioned as the natural evolution of mechanical and optical comparators, overcoming their limitations of **low magnification (≤ 10,000×)** and **limited range** by using electronic amplification reaching magnifications of the order of **10,000 to 1,00,000 ×**, with resolution as fine as **0.01 µm (10 nm)**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Microphone of Dimensions"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to measure how much a table leg wobbles — a wobble of just 0.001 mm is invisible to the eye and too small for a mechanical lever to lift a needle. Now picture an **electrical comparator** as a *super-sensitive microphone*:
>
> 1. The **stylus** touches the part (just like a microphone touching the air).
> 2. A **transducer** (the LVDT or inductive coil) translates that tiny physical push into a tiny electrical voltage — exactly like a microphone diaphragm converting sound waves to voltage.
> 3. An **amplifier** boosts that micro-volt signal into a strong, easily readable value (think loudspeaker).
> 4. A **meter/dial** displays the magnified reading so you can read 0.001 mm as if it were 100 mm on the screen.
>
> Without electricity, you'd need a mechanical lever 100,000 mm long — physically impossible. **Electricity gives you that lever invisibly.**

---

## 1.3 Essential Sub-Systems (Mandatory KTU Glossary)

| Sub-System | Function | Typical Component |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensing Element / Probe** | Contacts workpiece, picks up linear displacement | Hardened steel stylus / tungsten carbide tip |
| **Transducer** | Converts mechanical displacement → electrical signal | LVDT, Inductive, Capacitive, Piezoelectric |
| **Amplifier** | Magnifies weak electrical signal | Carrier amplifier / Bridge amplifier |
| **Display Unit** | Shows the magnified reading | Analog dial, Digital LED/LCD, CRO |
| **Calibration Standard** | Provides reference datum | Slip gauges (Grade K or better) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Datum Establishment:** The workpiece is placed on the **anvil/platen** and the probe tip is brought into contact with the reference master (slip gauge). The amplifier is zeroed.
2. **Insertion of Workpiece:** The test piece replaces the master. The probe either retracts (oversize) or advances (undersize) by the dimensional deviation **Δx**.
3. **Transduction:** The transducer converts this displacement **Δx** into a proportionate voltage **ΔE**.
4. **Amplification:** The amplifier multiplies **ΔE** by gain **G**, yielding **ΔE_out = G · ΔE**.
5. **Display:** The output drives a meter whose scale is pre-calibrated so a small **ΔE_out** corresponds to a large readable deflection.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea (Why electrical?):** Mechanical/optical comparators rely on physical levers, lenses, or springs — each having friction, inertia, and elastic limits.
