# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality Gurus and Their Contributions (KTU Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> This topic appears in **almost every KTU ESE Module 4 question paper**. Memorize the *names, contribution year, and key concept* of each guru.

### 2.1.1 W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993)

- **Key Idea:** Statistical Process Control (SPC) + System thinking + **PDCA Cycle** (Plan-Do-Check-Act, originally Shewhart Cycle).
- **Deming's 14 Points** (Heavily tested as a short note in KTU):
  1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement.
  2. Adopt the new philosophy.
  3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
  4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
  5. Improve constantly every process.
  6. Institute training on the job.
  7. Institute leadership.
  8. Drive out fear.
  9. Break down barriers between departments.
  10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
  11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
  12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
  13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
  14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

### 2.1.2 Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008)

- **Key Idea:** *Quality is fitness for use.* Defined **Juran's Trilogy**:
  1. **Quality Planning** – Establish goals and identify customers.
  2. **Quality Control** – Evaluate actual performance vs goals.
  3. **Quality Improvement** – Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement.

### 2.1.3 Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001)

- **Key Idea:** *Quality is free* and *conformance to requirements.*
- **Four Absolutes of Quality:**
  1. Quality = conformance to requirements.
  2. Prevention, not inspection.
  3. Zero defects performance standard.
  4. Measure quality by the price of non-conformance.
- **Quality Cost Ratio (Crosby):** Prevention : Appraisal : Failure = **0.05 : 0.25 : 0.70** (in a poor-quality organization). In a TQM-driven firm, prevention cost rises while failure cost collapses.

### 2.1.4 Kaoru Ishikawa (1915–1989)

- **Key Idea:** Quality circles, **Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram**, and the concept of *Customer-orientation* (next process is your customer).
- Developed the **Seven Basic Tools of Quality**.

### 2.1.5 Armand V. Feigenbaum (1920–2014)

- **Key Idea:** Coined the term **"Total Quality Control (TQC)"** in 1956. Quality is a company-wide responsibility, not just the QC department's job.

### 2.1.6 Genichi Taguchi (1924–2012)

- **Key Idea:** **Quality Loss Function (QLF)**. Any deviation from target causes loss to society, customer, and manufacturer.

## 2.2 Cost of Quality (COQ) — High-Yield Topic

Cost of Quality is divided into **four categories**. KTU loves 3-mark and 7-mark sub-questions on this.

### 2.2.1 Prevention Costs
Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place.
- Examples: Quality planning, training, supplier capability assessment, design reviews, process validation.

### 2.2.2 Appraisal Costs
Costs incurred to *detect* defects through inspection and testing.
- Examples: Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final testing, calibration of measuring equipment, quality audits.

### 2.2.3 Internal Failure Costs
Costs of defects found *before* the product reaches the customer.
- Examples: Scrap, rework, re-inspection, downtime due to defects, material wastage.

### 2.2.4 External Failure Costs
Costs of defects found *after* the product reaches the customer.
- Examples: Warranty claims, returns, recalls, complaint handling, legal liability, loss of goodwill.

## 2.3 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF)

Taguchi argued that any deviation from the target value $T$ incurs a quadratic loss to society, even if the product is within specification limits.

$$L(x) = k \, (x - T)^{2}$$

Where:
- $L(x)$ = Quality loss incurred when the characteristic deviates to value $x$
- $x$ = Actual measured value of the quality characteristic
- $T$ = Target value
- $k$ = Quality loss coefficient (a constant)

**The constant $k$ is determined using the specification limits and the loss at the specification boundary:**

$$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$$

Where:
- $A_{0}$ = Total loss incurred at the specification limit (often approximated as the cost of replacement/rework)
- $\Delta_{0} = \vert USL - T \vert = \vert T - LSL \vert$ = tolerance from target to upper/lower specification limit

## 2.4 PDCA Cycle (Deming / Shewhart Cycle)

The four-stage iterative management method used for continuous improvement:

| Stage | Action | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Plan** | Identify the problem and plan a change | Hypothesize improvement |
| **Do** | Implement the plan on a small scale | Test the hypothesis |
| **Check** | Measure outcomes and compare against expected | Validate the result |
| **Act** | Standardize the improvement or restart the cycle | Lock in the gain |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of Key Formulas for Quality Concepts**

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Taguchi Quality Loss | $L(x) = k(x-T)^{2}$ | Loss to society vs deviation |
| Loss Coefficient | $k = A_{0} \div \Delta_{0}^{2}$ | Calibration of $k$ |
| Crosby Cost Ratio | $P \colon A \colon F = 0.05 \colon 0.25 \colon 0.70$ | Poor-quality org benchmark |
| Total COQ | $C_{T} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Sum of all 4 categories |
| Six Sigma DPMO | $DPMO = D \times 10^{6} \div O$ | Defects per million opportunities |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY = Y_{1} \times Y_{2} \times \dots \times Y_{n}$ | Multi-step process yield |
| Process Capability | $C_{p} = (USL - LSL) \div 6\sigma$ | Process potential |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$ | Process centering |
| $C_{pu}$ | $C_{pu} = (USL - \mu) \div 3\sigma$ | Upper-spec capability |
| $C_{pl}$ | $C_{pl} = (\mu - LSL) \div 3\sigma$ | Lower-spec capability |
| Parts Per Million defectives | $PPM = (\text{Defectives} \div \text{Units produced}) \times 10^{6}$ | Customer-facing metric |

**Units & Symbols Reference:**
- $x, T$ are in product characteristic units (e.g., mm, °C).
- $k$ is in monetary units per unit squared of characteristic (e.g., ₹/mm²).
- $A_{0}$ is in monetary units (e.g., ₹).
- $\sigma$ is in characteristic units.

## 2.6 Seven Basic Tools of Quality (Ishikawa)

These are the seven statistical/graphical tools every KTU student must identify. They are heavily tested:

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Fishbone / Ishikawa diagram)
2. **Check Sheet** (Tally sheet for data collection)
3. **Control Chart** (SPC chart for monitoring process)
4. **Histogram** (Frequency distribution bar chart)
5. **Pareto Chart** (80/20 principle)
6. **Scatter Diagram** (Correlation between two variables)
7. **Flowchart** (Process mapping)

## 2.7 Quality Circle

A **Quality Circle (QC)** is a small group of workers (typically 6–12) doing similar work who meet regularly to identify, analyze, and solve work-related quality problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Origin:** Japan, 1962 — Kaoru Ishikawa. Also known as the **Japanese Quality Circle Movement**.

**Key Features:**
- Voluntary participation
- Worker-led (bottom-up)
- Focus on the *workplace*, not the *workforce* (i.e., processes, not people)
- Uses simple statistical tools (7 QC tools)
- Continuous improvement

## 2.8 5S Methodology (Origin: Japan / Toyota)

| S | Japanese | English | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Seiri | Sort | Remove unnecessary items |
| 2 | Seiton | Set in order | Arrange items for easy access |
| 3 | Seiso | Shine | Clean the workplace |
| 4 | Seiketsu | Standardize | Establish standards & procedures |
| 5 | Shitsuke | Sustain | Maintain discipline |

**Engineering Utility:** 5S is the foundation of **Lean Manufacturing** and **Just-in-Time (JIT)** production systems, reducing waste (muda), unevenness (mura), and overburden (muri).

## 2.9 Six Sigma — A Brief Overview

Six Sigma is a **data-driven methodology** for eliminating defects, aiming for **3.4 Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**.

**Key Formula:**
$$\text{Process Sigma Level} = \frac{USL - LSL}{2 \cdot k \cdot \sigma}$$

Or, more commonly measured by the **DPMO** metric:
$$DPMO = \frac{\text{Number of Defects} \times 10^{6}}{\text{Number of Units} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}}$$

**DMAIC Framework:** Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control.

**Real-world utility:** Six Sigma is widely deployed by **Motorola, GE, Honeywell, and Ford**. A 6-sigma process shifts the mean by $\pm 1.5\sigma$, giving the famous **3.4 DPMO** benchmark.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Taguchi's Quality Loss Function

**Step 1 — Statement of the Loss Hypothesis.**
Taguchi proposed that any deviation $\delta$ from the target value $T$ results in a *loss to society* that grows *quadratically* with the deviation. We write the deviation as:
$$\delta = x - T$$

**Step 2 — Write the Proportionality.**
Since the loss is symmetric about the target (loss at $+\delta$ equals loss at $-\delta$) and grows faster than linear, Taguchi assumed a quadratic form:
$$L(\delta) \propto \delta^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce the Proportionality Constant.**
Let the constant of proportionality be $k$ (units: ₹/mm² or ₹/°C²). Then:
$$L(x) = k \, (x - T)^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Calibrate $k$ Using a Known Boundary.**
We define the *tolerance* $\Delta_{0}$ as the distance from the target $T$ to the specification limit (USL or LSL). At $x = USL$ (or $x = LSL$), the loss to society is $A_{0}$ (a known replacement/repair cost). Substituting:
$$A_{0} = k \, \Delta_{0}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $k$.**
$$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Final Loss Function Expression.**
$$\boxed{L(x) = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} \, (x - T)^{2}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** The diameter of a shaft has a target value $T = 50.00$ mm. The specification limits are $USL = 50.05$ mm and $LSL = 49.95$ mm. If a faulty shaft requires replacement costing $A_{0} = ₹100$ (i.e., this is the loss at the specification boundary), find:
> (a) the loss coefficient $k$,
> (b) the loss when the actual diameter is $x = 49.98$ mm,
> (c) the loss when the actual diameter is $x = 50.02$ mm.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step (a):** Calculate the tolerance $\Delta_{0}$.
$$\Delta_{0} = \vert USL - T \vert = \vert 50.05 - 50.00 \vert = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

**Step (b):** Calculate $k$.
$$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} = \frac{100}{(0.05)^{2}} = \frac{100}{0.0025} = 40{,}000 \text{ ₹/mm}^{2}$$

**Step (c):** Calculate the loss at $x = 49.98$ mm.
$$L(49.98) = k \, (x - T)^{2} = 40{,}000 \times (49.98 - 50.00)^{2}$$
$$L(49.98) = 40{,}000 \times (-0.02)^{2} = 40{,}000 \times 0.0004 = ₹16$$

**Step (d):** Calculate the loss at $x = 50.02$ mm.
$$L(50.02) = 40{,}000 \times (50.02 - 50.00)^{2} = 40{,}000 \times (0.02)^{2} = ₹16$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** Even though both diameters are *within* the specification limits (49.95 ≤ 49.98 ≤ 50.05 and 49.95 ≤ 50.02 ≤ 50.05), Taguchi's QLF says a loss of ₹16 has *already* occurred. The classical "within limits = no loss" view is replaced by a continuous-loss view. This is a key KTU exam point.

## 3.3 Derivation: Crosby's Cost-of-Conformance Philosophy

**Step 1 — Definition of Price of Non-Conformance (PONC).**
Crosby defined **PONC** as the total cost of all activities required to *fix* problems — i.e., the cost of *not* doing things right the first time.

$$PONC = C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

Where $C_{IF}$ = Internal Failure Cost and $C_{EF}$ = External Failure Cost.

**Step 2 — Definition of Price of Conformance (POC).**
$$POC = C_{P} + C_{A}$$

Where $C_{P}$ = Prevention Cost and $C_{A}$ = Appraisal Cost.

**Step 3 — Total Cost of Quality (COQ).**
$$COQ = POC + PONC = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

**Step 4 — Crosby's Key Insight.**
Crosby observed that in a traditional (poor-quality) organization, the cost ratio is:
$$C_{P} \colon C_{A} \colon (C_{IF} + C_{EF}) \approx 0.05 \colon 0.25 \colon 0.70$$

**Step 5 — As Quality Improves.**
As an organization adopts prevention strategies, prevention cost $C_{P}$ rises (initially), but failure costs $C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ fall *much faster*. The total COQ reaches a minimum (the **Economic Quality Level**), and the optimal ratio shifts toward prevention.

**Step 6 — Optimal COQ Condition (qualitative).**
At optimum:
$$\frac{d(COQ)}{d(C_{P})} = -\frac{d(C_{IF} + C_{EF})}{d(C_{P})}$$

i.e., the marginal increase in prevention cost equals the marginal saving in failure cost. Beyond this point, adding more prevention costs more than it saves.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — TQM vs QC vs QA vs SQC

| Parameter | TQM | QC | QA | SQC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Full Form** | Total Quality Management | Quality Control | Quality Assurance | Statistical Quality Control |
| **Focus** | Whole organization | Product inspection | Process system | Statistical analysis |
| **Originator** | Feigenbaum, Deming, Juran | Frederick Taylor era | Post-WWII | Shewhart, Deming |
| **Proactive/Reactive** | Proactive | Reactive | Proactive | Both |
| **Tools** | 7 QC tools + QFD + FMEA | Gauges, inspection | ISO 9001 standards | Control charts, Cpk |
| **Cost Driver** | Prevention | Inspection | System design | Sampling & charting |
| **KTU Weightage** | High (14-mark Qs) | High (conceptual) | Medium | High (Module 5/6) |

## 3.5 Process Capability Derivation (Bridge to Module 5)

**Step 1 — Define the natural process spread.**
If a process is in statistical control with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$, then $\pm 3\sigma$ covers **99.73%** of the output. The natural process spread is:
$$\text{Spread} = 6\sigma$$

**Step 2 — Define the specification spread.**
$$\text{Spec Spread} = USL - LSL$$

**Step 3 — Form the Capability Index $C_{p}$.**
$$C_{p} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Account for Centering.**
If the process mean $\mu$ is *not* centered at the midpoint of the specification, use $C_{pk}$:
$$C_{pk} = \min \left( \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**
- $C_{p} \ge 1.33$: Capable process.
- $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$: Capable and centered.
- $C_{p} < 1.00$: Not capable.
- $C_{p} = C_{pk}$: Perfectly centered.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Apr 2024 | Set A]**

**Q1. Define Total Quality Management (TQM). List any four of its key principles.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Total Quality Management (TQM) is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that seeks to embed quality in every process, every department, and every employee, with the goal of achieving long-term customer satisfaction and benefit to society.

**Four Key Principles of TQM:**
1. **Customer Focus** — Understanding and meeting customer needs (internal and external customers).
2. **Total Employee Involvement** — Empowering all employees from shop-floor to top management.
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Ongoing, incremental improvements in processes.
4. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — Using data, statistical tools, and SPC for decisions.

*(Other acceptable principles: process-centred management, integrated system, strategic approach, communication.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 | Model Paper]**

**Q2. What is the Taguchi Quality Loss Function? Write its mathematical form and explain the significance of the constant $k$.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF)** states that any deviation from the target value $T$ causes a loss to society, even if the product is within specification limits. The mathematical form is:

$$L(x) = k \, (x - T)^{2}$$

where $L(x)$ is the loss incurred when the characteristic takes the value $x$, $T$ is the target value, and $k$ is the **quality loss coefficient** expressed in monetary units per unit squared (e.g., ₹/mm²).

The constant $k$ is determined from a known boundary condition:
$$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$$

where $A_{0}$ is the loss at the specification limit and $\Delta_{0}$ is the tolerance from target to specification limit. A larger $k$ means the customer is more sensitive to deviation, implying a stricter quality requirement.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 4 Comprehensive]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Explain the contributions of any **four Quality Gurus** in detail, highlighting their key philosophies and impact on modern quality management. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **Cost of Quality (COQ)** model with a neat diagram. Explain each category with suitable industrial examples. *(7 Marks)*

### **Question B (14 Marks):**
**(a)** Define **Quality Control (QC)** and **Quality Assurance (QA)**. Compare them across at least **six parameters**, and explain the concept of **Total Quality Management (TQM)** with its salient features. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A shaft has a target diameter of $T = 25.00$ mm. The specification limits are $USL = 25.04$ mm and $LSL = 24.96$ mm. The cost of replacement when the shaft is at the specification limit is $A_{0} = ₹80$. Using **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function**, calculate:
   (i) the loss coefficient $k$, *(2 Marks)*
   (ii) the loss when $x = 25.02$ mm, *(2 Marks)*
   (iii) the loss when $x = 24.97$ mm, *(2 Marks)*
   (iv) the loss when $x = 25.04$ mm. *(1 Mark)*

### Model Solutions to Question B(b):

**Step 1 — Compute the tolerance $\Delta_{0}$.**
$$\Delta_{0} = \vert USL - T \vert = \vert 25.04 - 25.00 \vert = 0.04 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the loss coefficient $k$.**
$$k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}} = \frac{80}{(0.04)^{2}} = \frac{80}{0.0016} = 50{,}000 \text{ ₹/mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $L(25.02)$.**
$$L(25.02) = 50{,}000 \times (25.02 - 25.00)^{2} = 50{,}000 \times (0.02)^{2} = 50{,}000 \times 0.0004 = ₹20$$

**Step 4 — Compute $L(24.97)$.**
$$L(24.97) = 50{,}000 \times (24.97 - 25.00)^{2} = 50{,}000 \times (-0.03)^{2} = 50{,}000 \times 0.0009 = ₹45$$

**Step 5 — Compute $L(25.04)$.**
$$L(25.04) = 50{,}000 \times (25.04 - 25.00)^{2} = 50{,}000 \times (0.04)^{2} = 50{,}000 \times 0.0016 = ₹80$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** At $x = 25.04$ mm, the shaft is *exactly* at the USL, so the loss must equal $A_{0} = ₹80$. ✓ The model is self-consistent.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing QC with QA:** Many students write QC and QA as synonyms. **QA is system-wide and prevention-based; QC is product-focused and detection-based.** Losing 1–2 marks here is common.
> 2. **Garvin's 8 Dimensions — listing only 4–5:** KTU expects all **8 dimensions** with one-line definitions. Missing more than 2 leads to a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Taguchi QLF — wrong sign or wrong square:** Many students write $L = k(x-T)$ instead of $L = k(x-T)^{2}$. The quadratic form is **non-negotiable**.
> 4. **Crosby cost ratio cited as 5:25:70 instead of 0.05:0.25:0.70:** Although mathematically the same, KTU valuation key uses the *decimal* form. Match the textbook convention.
> 5. **Confusing PDCA origin:** Some students attribute PDCA to Juran. **It was originally Shewhart's Cycle, popularized by Deming.** KTU awards marks only for the correct attribution.
> 6. **Quality Circle ≠ Quality Control:** A Quality Circle is a *worker group*; QC is a *department function*. Confusing the two is a recurring 2-mark loss.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Concepts — High-Density Revision List**

### A. Core Definitions
- Quality = fitness for use (**Juran**), conformance to requirements (**Crosby**), predictable uniformity (**Deming**), total composite characteristics (**Feigenbaum**), loss to society (**Taguchi**).
- ISO 9000:2015 — degree to which inherent characteristics fulfil requirements.
- QC = product-focused, reactive, detection.
- QA = system-focused, proactive, prevention.
- TQM = company-wide, customer-driven, continuous improvement.

### B. Quality Gurus (One-liner Map)
- **Deming →** SPC, PDCA, 14 Points.
- **Juran →** Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve), fitness for use.
- **Crosby →** Zero Defects, Four Absolutes, $0.05:0.25:0.70$ ratio.
- **Ishikawa →** Quality Circles, Fishbone, 7 Tools.
- **Feigenbaum →** Total Quality Control (TQC) — origin of the philosophy.
- **Taguchi →** Quality Loss Function $L = k(x-T)^{2}$.

### C. Garvin's 8 Dimensions
**P, F, R, C, D, S, A, Pq** → **Performance, Features, Reliability, Conformance, Durability, Serviceability, Aesthetics, Perceived Quality.**

### D. Cost of Quality (COQ)
- **Prevention (P) + Appraisal (A) = Conformance Cost.**
- **Internal Failure (IF) + External Failure (EF) = Non-conformance Cost.**
- $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- Optimum COQ is when marginal cost of prevention = marginal saving on failure.

### E. Taguchi QLF
$$L(x) = k(x-T)^{2}, \quad k = \frac{A_{0}}{\Delta_{0}^{2}}$$
- Loss is continuous, not just at the boundary.
- Same deviation magnitude = same loss (symmetric).

### F. PDCA / Shewhart / Deming Cycle
**Plan → Do → Check → Act** (re-iterate).

### G. 7 Basic Tools of Quality
Fishbone, Check Sheet, Control Chart, Histogram, Pareto, Scatter, Flowchart.

### H. 5S
**Sort, Set-in-order, Shine, Standardize, Sustain.** (Origin: Toyota Production System.)

### I. Six Sigma
**DMAIC**: Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control. Target = 3.4 DPMO.

### J. Quality Circle
Small group (6–12) of workers meeting voluntarily, using 7 QC tools to solve workplace problems. **Origin: Japan, 1962, Ishikawa.**

### K. Standards to Remember for KTU ESE
- ISO 9000 family — generic quality management.
- ISO 9001:2015 — requirements for QMS certification.
- BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) — national body of India.

### L. Numerical Must-Knows
- Always compute $\Delta_{0}$ as a **positive** number.
- The $x$ value, target $T$, and limits are all in the **same units**.
- $k$ has units of $\text{[Money]} \div \text{[Unit]}^2$.
- $A_{0}$ and $\Delta_{0}$ are **inputs**; $k$ and $L(x)$ are **outputs**.

### M. KTU-Favourite Memory Hooks
- "**Juran = Judgement, Trilogy, Use**."
- "**Crosby = C for Conformance, C for Cost of Non-conformance**."
- "**Deming = D for Data, D for PDCA**."
- "**Ishikawa = I for Ishikawa Diagram, I for (Quality) Circle**."
- "**Taguchi = T for Target, T for loss to society**."
- "**Feigenbaum = F for First to coin 'Total Quality Control'**."

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Strategy:** In a 14-mark answer, dedicate 2 pages each to (1) Definitions, (2) Diagrams, (3) Comparison tables, (4) Numerical worked-out example. A neat, labeled diagram in TQM/COQ answers routinely fetches **+1 to +2 marks** over a text-only response.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Corrective and Prevention Methods for Ensuring Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Corrective and Prevention Methods for Ensuring Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

In the context of **Quality Management Systems (QMS)** as defined by **ISO 9001:2015**, the two principal methods for ensuring and sustaining product/process quality are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Corrective Action (CA):** A planned, documented action undertaken to **eliminate the root cause** of a detected non-conformity, defect, or other undesirable situation, **in order to prevent recurrence**.
> 
> **Preventive Action (PA):** A planned, documented action undertaken to **eliminate the root cause** of a potential non-conformity, defect, or other undesirable situation, **in order to prevent occurrence**.

In modern ISO 9001:2015 framework, preventive action is also interpreted broadly as the use of **risk-based thinking (RBT)** and **process approach** to anticipate and eliminate potential failure modes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Body Check-up"

Imagine the human body:

- **Corrective Action** = Visiting the doctor **after** you fall sick. You get treated (symptom removed), but the doctor also investigates *why* you fell ill (root cause) so that the same sickness does not return. The illness has already manifested.
- **Preventive Action** = Going for a regular health check-up **before** falling sick. The doctor observes borderline vitals (potential issue) and prescribes diet/exercise to prevent the disease from ever occurring.

In both cases, the *goal is to remove the cause*, but the **timing** is different — *after detection* (corrective) versus *before occurrence* (preventive).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the KTU Exam:** "Corrective = Cure the Disease, Preventive = Vaccinate the Body."

### 1.3 Key Quality Metrics (Industry-Standard Terms)

The effectiveness of corrective and preventive actions is quantified using the following standard quality performance indicators (highlighted in **bold** throughout KTU textbooks):

- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)**
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)**
- **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**
- **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**
- **Risk Priority Number (RPN)** in FMEA
- **Process Capability Indices** ($C_p$ and $C_{pk}$)

These are not just *numbers*; they are the *language* of quality engineers when reporting to management.

### 1.4 KTU 2024 Module 4 Learning Outcomes (Mapped)

By the end of this topic, a student should be able to:

| CO # | Course Outcome (Cognitive Level) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **CO4** | Apply statistical and analytical methods to **identify, analyse, and correct** quality deviations in manufacturing processes. |
| **CO5** | Design and implement **preventive quality systems** using modern tools (FMEA, SPC, Poka-Yoke) to ensure zero-defect production. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The CAPA Framework (Corrective and Preventive Action)

The CAPA framework is the **backbone** of any ISO 9001-certified organisation. It is a closed-loop, data-driven methodology.

#### 2.1.1 Phases of Corrective Action (8 Steps — KTU Exam Favourite)

1. **Problem Identification:** Detect the non-conformity through customer complaint, audit, SPC chart breach, or inspection.
2. **Problem Definition:** Quantify the problem using the **5W2H** method (What, Why, Where, When, Who, How, How much).
3. **Containment (Interim Action):** Apply short-term containment (e.g., 100% inspection, sortation) to stop defective products reaching the customer.
4. **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** Use tools such as **5-Why Analysis**, **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram**, **Pareto Analysis**, or **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** to identify the *true* cause.
5. **Permanent Corrective Action:** Implement a long-term solution that addresses the root cause.
6. **Implementation & Verification:** Apply the action and verify its effectiveness using measurable data.
7. **Standardisation:** Update SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures), work instructions, and training material.
8. **Closure & Documentation:** Close the CAPA ticket with full traceability.

#### 2.1.2 Phases of Preventive Action (Risk-Based Approach)

1. **Risk Identification:** Recognise potential failure modes using **FMEA**, **Hazard Analysis**, or trend analysis of process data.
2. **Risk Evaluation:** Quantify risk using **RPN (Risk Priority Number)**.
3. **Action Planning:** Define preventive measures ranked by criticality.
4. **Implementation & Monitoring:** Deploy controls (Poka-Yoke, SPC charts, training).
5. **Effectiveness Review:** Confirm risk has been reduced to an acceptable level.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The *single biggest difference* between CA and PA in an exam answer is the **timing** — *reacting to a known defect (CA)* vs. *proactively eliminating a potential defect (PA)*.

### 2.2 Quality Tools Used Inside CAPA

#### 2.2.1 The 7 Basic Quality Tools (Ishikawa's Legacy)

| Tool | Purpose | Stage of CAPA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pareto Chart** | Identify the *vital few* causes from trivial many | Problem Definition |
| **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** | Brainstorm potential causes across categories (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Environment) | Root Cause Analysis |
| **5-Why Analysis** | Drill down to the fundamental cause via repeated "why?" questioning | Root Cause Analysis |
| **Histogram** | Visualise data distribution | Problem Definition |
| **Scatter Diagram** | Show correlation between two variables | Root Cause Analysis |
| **Control Chart (SPC)** | Monitor process stability over time | Verification |
| **Check Sheet** | Collect data systematically | Problem Definition |

#### 2.2.2 The New 7 Management & Planning Tools

Affinity Diagram, Inter-relationship Digraph, Tree Diagram, Matrix Diagram, Matrix Data Analysis, **PDPC (Process Decision Program Chart)**, and **Arrow Diagram (PERT/CPM)** — used by cross-functional teams to *plan* preventive actions.

### 2.3 PDCA Cycle (Deming / Shewhart Cycle)

The **Plan–Do–Check–Act** cycle is the philosophical engine behind continuous improvement. It is **iterative** and **never-ending**.

- **Plan:** Identify the problem, analyse root cause, draft the action plan.
- **Do:** Execute the plan on a small scale (pilot run).
- **Check:** Evaluate the results against expected outcomes using data.
- **Act:** If successful, standardise; if not, revise the plan and loop back to *Plan*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deming's Insight:** "Quality is not an act, it is a habit." The PDCA cycle makes quality a **habitual, scientific, and iterative** organisational practice.

### 2.4 Six Sigma DMAIC Methodology

A more rigorous, data-centric extension of PDCA used by **GE, Motorola, and Bosch**:

- **D**efine → **M**easure → **A**nalyse → **I**mprove → **C**ontrol

DMAIC integrates **statistical hypothesis testing, regression, DOE (Design of Experiments), and SPC** within CAPA.

### 2.5 Poka-Yoke (Mistake Proofing)

A Japanese concept (Shigeo Shingo, Toyota Production System) that designs a process so that **human error becomes impossible or immediately obvious**.

Examples:
- USB connector (only fits one way).
- SIM card tray (asymmetric).
- Car gear lever that cannot be shifted out of Park without brake pressed.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are mathematically verifiable and are part of the **Module 4** syllabus.

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
| :- | :------ | :---------- | :--------------- |
| 1 | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) | $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection (1–10) |
| 2 | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability (centred) | $USL$ = Upper Spec Limit, $LSL$ = Lower Spec Limit, $\sigma$ = Std. Dev. |
| 3 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability (off-centre) | $\mu$ = Process Mean |
| 4 | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per Million Opps. | — |
| 5 | $\sigma\text{-level} = 0.8406 + 29.37 \times \log_{10}(\text{DPMO} + 0.0245)$ | Six Sigma Conversion | — |
| 6 | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Good Units}}{\text{Total Units Produced}}$ | First Pass Yield (fraction) | $0 \leq FPY \leq 1$ |
| 7 | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Rolled Throughput Yield (n steps) | — |
| 8 | $COPQ = C_{prevention} + C_{appraisal} + C_{internal\,fail} + C_{external\,fail}$ | Cost of Poor Quality (sum of P-A-I-E) | Currency units |
| 9 | $Pareto\,\% = \dfrac{\text{Category Count}}{\text{Total Defects}} \times 100$ | Pareto contribution | Percentage |
| 10 | $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | Control chart upper limit (X-bar) | $A_2$ = chart constant for sample size $n$ |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors, Maruti Suzuki):** Mandatory **FMEA** for every new part, with RPN threshold of 100 triggering mandatory corrective action.
- **Aerospace (e.g., HAL, Boeing):** **AS9100** mandates CAPA, Root Cause Analysis, and **Mistake Proofing** in critical assemblies.
- **Medical Devices:** **ISO 13485** requires risk-based preventive actions tied to patient safety.
- **Semiconductor Industry:** Six Sigma (DPMO tracking) is the universal yardstick for fab yield improvement.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN)

The **RPN** is a single composite index used to rank failure modes by criticality. It is a *triple product* of three independent ratings.

#### 3.1.1 Mathematical Definition

The RPN for the $j$-th failure mode is defined as the product of three ordinal scores assigned by a cross-functional team:

$$RPN_j = S_j \times O_j \times D_j$$

where each score is an integer in the closed interval $[1, 10]$. The team assigns values based on a published **severity, occurrence, and detection rating scale**.

**Step-by-step rationale (for KTU 14-mark answers):**

- **Severity $S$ (1–10):** *How bad is the effect on the customer?* $S=1$ means no effect; $S=10$ means safety hazard / total failure.
- **Occurrence $O$ (1–10):** *How frequently does the failure mode occur?* $O=1$ means almost never; $O=10$ means very frequent (failure is almost inevitable).
- **Detection $D$ (1–10):** *How easily is the failure detected before reaching the customer?* $D=1$ means almost certain detection; $D=10$ means no current control can detect it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** A *high* $D$ (hard to detect) is *bad* — so $D$ enters the product as-is. Some students mistakenly take $1/D$, which is wrong.

**Theoretical Maximum:** $RPN_{max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000$. In industry, an RPN above **100** is treated as a mandatory CAPA trigger.

#### 3.1.2 Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

A shaft on a CNC lathe has the failure mode "excessive diameter oversize". Team ratings:

- Severity: $S = 8$ (component is scrapped — high).
- Occurrence: $O = 5$ (happens about 1 in 100 parts).
- Detection: $D = 6$ (caught only at final QC).

$$
\begin{aligned}
RPN &= S \times O \times D \\
    &= 8 \times 5 \times 6 \\
    &= 240
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $RPN = 240 > 100$, the team must initiate a **corrective action** (e.g., add in-process gauging) and also a **preventive action** for similar parts (e.g., Poka-Yoke fixture).

### 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$

#### 3.2.1 Why Capability Indices?

A process is **statistically capable** if its natural spread (driven by $\sigma$) fits comfortably inside the customer's specification window $[LSL, USL]$. The 6-sigma spread is the conventional "voice of the process".

#### 3.2.2 Derivation of $C_p$

The total specification width is $USL - LSL$. The total natural process spread is $6\sigma$ (i.e., $\mu \pm 3\sigma$). The dimensionless ratio is:

$$C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- $USL - LSL$ is the *Voice of the Customer (VOC)*.
- $6\sigma$ is the *Voice of the Process (VOP)*.
- $C_p > 1.0$ means the process spread is *narrower* than the spec width → process is capable.
- $C_p \geq 1.33$ is the **minimum acceptable** value in most industries; $C_p \geq 1.67$ is Six Sigma target.
- $C_p$ assumes the process is **perfectly centred** ($\mu$ exactly in the middle of $[LSL, USL]$).

#### 3.2.3 Derivation of $C_{pk}$ (the Real-World Index)

If the process is not centred, $C_p$ becomes misleading. We define two one-sided indices:

$$C_{pk,upper} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} \quad \text{and} \quad C_{pk,lower} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$

The true capability is the *worst* of the two (the side closer to the spec limit):

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}, \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** $C_{pk} = 0$ means the process is *touching* a spec limit — almost half the output is defective. $C_{pk} = 2.0$ means a 6-sigma-quality process (3.4 DPMO).

### 3.3 Derivation: Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — P-A-I-E Model

The classical quality cost model (Joseph Juran) decomposes quality costs into four buckets:

$$COPQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

where:

- $C_P$ = **Prevention costs** (training, FMEA, design reviews).
- $C_A$ = **Appraisal costs** (inspection, testing, audits).
- $C_{IF}$ = **Internal failure costs** (rework, scrap, re-inspection).
- $C_{EF}$ = **External failure costs** (warranty, recalls, returns, customer dissatisfaction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Quality Economics (Juran):** *An extra dollar spent on prevention saves ten dollars in failure costs.* This is the mathematical justification for shifting investment from "appraisal" and "failure" categories towards "prevention".

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Choosing the Right Method)

| Scenario | Best Tool | Reason |
| :------ | :-------- | :----- |
| Defect has already occurred, need quick containment | **5-Why + Ishikawa** | Fast qualitative RCA for shop-floor problems |
| Predicting future failures on a new design | **FMEA** | Forward-looking risk ranking using RPN |
| Process drifting but not yet out of spec | **SPC Control Charts** | Real-time monitoring → enables preventive action |
| Human error causing frequent defects | **Poka-Yoke** | Removes possibility of error |
| Chronic cross-departmental quality issue | **DMAIC / Six Sigma** | Statistical, data-driven, project-based |
| Multiple small problems overwhelming the team | **Pareto Analysis** | Focuses effort on the *vital few* |
| Reducing total cost of quality over years | **COPQ + Kaizen** | Long-term financial optimisation |

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Pareto Analysis Procedure (For 14-Mark Answers)

1. **Collect data:** List defect categories and their frequencies.
2. **Sort descending:** Arrange categories from highest to lowest count.
3. **Compute cumulative %:** Add running totals and convert to percentage of grand total.
4. **Plot bar + cumulative curve** (dual-axis chart).
5. **Identify vital few:** Categories falling under $\approx 80\%$ of cumulative defects are the *vital few*.
6. **Initiate CAPA on vital few** — these are the highest-leverage corrective actions.

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **favourite 14-mark structured question** in KTU ESE — the examiner expects the 6 steps above plus a hand-drawn sketch of the Pareto chart.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CAPA Closed-Loop Process Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *closed-loop interaction* between Corrective Action, Preventive Action, and the supporting quality tools. It is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** rather than a physical drawing, in line with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol for non-spatial topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input: Customer Complaint or Audit Finding] --> B1[Step 1: Problem Identification and Containment]
    B1 --> C1[Step 2: Data Collection and Pareto Prioritisation]
    C1 --> D1[Step 3: Root Cause Analysis using 5-Why and Ishikawa]
    D1 --> E1{Root Cause Confirmed by Team?}
    E1 -- No --> F1[Step 4a: Revise Hypothesis and Re-investigate]
    F1 --> D1
    E1 -- Yes --> G1[Step 5: Design Corrective Action]
    G1 --> H1[Step 6: Implement on Pilot Run]
    H1 --> I1[Step 7: Verify Effectiveness with SPC and Cp Cpk]
    I1 --> J1{Effectiveness Achieved?}
    J1 -- No --> K1[Step 8: Escalate to FMEA and DOE Study]
    K1 --> G1
    J1 -- Yes --> L1[Step 9: Standardise via SOP Update and Training]
    L1 --> M1[Step 10: Horizontal Deployment to Similar Processes]
    M1 --> N1[Preventive Action Bank: Reusable Solutions]
    N1 --> O1[Feedback to FMEA Risk Register]
    O1 --> P1[Continuous Improvement via PDCA]
    P1 --> A1
```

### 4.2 PDCA Cycle Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1N[PLAN: Identify Problem and Analyse Root Cause] --> D1N[DO: Pilot Implementation of Corrective Action]
    D1N --> C1N[CHECK: Verify with Data SPC Cp Cpk]
    C1N --> A1N[ACT: Standardise if Successful or Revise Plan]
    A1N --> P1N
```

### 4.3 FMEA Risk Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Start: List all Potential Failure Modes] --> F2[Rate Severity S from 1 to 10]
    F2 --> F3[Rate Occurrence O from 1 to 10]
    F3 --> F4[Rate Detection D from 1 to 10]
    F4 --> F5[Compute RPN equals S times O times D]
    F5 --> F6{RPN greater than 100}
    F6 -- Yes --> F7[Trigger Mandatory Corrective Action]
    F6 -- No --> F8[Monitor and Review Quarterly]
    F7 --> F9[Implement Prevention: Poka-Yoke SPC Training]
    F9 --> F10[Re-rate to Confirm RPN Reduction]
```

### 4.4 Poka-Yoke Mechanism Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[Operator Performs Manual Task] --> G2{Task Input Correct?}
    G2 -- Yes --> G3[Process Continues to Next Step]
    G2 -- No --> G4[Poka-Yoke Sensor Triggers]
    G4 --> G5[Option A: Machine Stops Immediately]
    G4 --> G6[Option B: Warning Light and Alarm]
    G5 --> G7[Operator Corrects Error and Restarts]
    G6 --> G7
    G7 --> G1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams for the KTU Exam:** In the 14-mark answers, you may sketch simplified versions of these flows. The key is the *logical sequencing* — not the visual aesthetics. The examiner awards marks for **closed-loop verification** and **feedback to preventive action**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** 
*Distinguish clearly between Corrective Action and Preventive Action. Give one real-world example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Corrective Action | Preventive Action |
| :----- | :---------------- | :---------------- |
| Timing | *After* the defect has occurred | *Before* the defect occurs |
| Trigger | Detected non-conformity, customer complaint, or audit finding | Trend data, risk analysis, FMEA prediction |
| Goal | Eliminate root cause to **prevent recurrence** | Eliminate potential cause to **prevent occurrence** |
| Example | Replacing a faulty sensor on a CNC lathe after it produced 30 defective parts | Installing an online vibration monitor on *all* CNC lathes to predict sensor failure |

*(1 mark for definition of CA, 1 mark for PA, 1 mark for examples — examiner pattern.)*

---

#### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Risk Priority Number (RPN) used in FMEA. Why is a higher RPN considered more critical?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Risk Priority Number is a composite index used in Failure Mode and Effects Analysis to rank the relative risk of failure modes:

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

where $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection, each rated from 1 to 10.

A higher RPN means the failure mode is **more severe, more frequent, and harder to detect**, making it a higher priority for corrective and preventive action. Industry practice treats an RPN above **100** as a mandatory CAPA trigger.

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for variable meanings, 1 mark for threshold interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (14 Marks):** Each question carries two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A *or* Question B (full 14 marks, no choice within sub-parts unless stated).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle** in detail. Discuss how it is applied as a corrective and preventive action framework in a manufacturing organisation, with reference to a specific engineering example.

**Model Answer (a):**

The **PDCA cycle**, also called the **Deming Cycle** or **Shewhart Cycle**, is a four-stage iterative method for continuous improvement. It is the *philosophical backbone* of every corrective and preventive action.

**Stage 1 – PLAN:**
- Identify the problem using data (customer complaints, SPC chart breach, Pareto analysis).
- Define the *gap* between current state and target state.
- Hypothesise root causes using **5-Why** and **Ishikawa** diagrams.
- Draft a corrective action plan with measurable objectives (e.g., reduce defect rate from 5% to 1%).

**Stage 2 – DO:**
- Execute the plan on a **pilot scale** to limit risk.
- Document all observations, deviations, and process parameters.
- Train the operators on the new procedure.

**Stage 3 – CHECK:**
- Collect post-implementation data and compare with baseline.
- Use **Control Charts** to confirm the process is now stable.
- Compute $C_{pk}$ to verify capability has improved.

**Stage 4 – ACT:**
- If the action worked → **standardise** by updating SOPs, work instructions, and the FMEA.
- If the action failed → revise the hypothesis and re-enter the **PLAN** stage.
- Feed the learning into the **Preventive Action Bank** for horizontal deployment to similar processes.

**Engineering Example:** A forging company observes that 8% of connecting rods have surface cracks. After PDCA implementation (plan = install eddy-current testing; do = pilot on 100 rods; check = crack rate drops to 0.5%; act = deploy across all forging lines), the *recurrence* is prevented (corrective) and similar risks on other parts are mitigated (preventive).

*(Distribution: 1 mark per stage × 4 = 4 marks; 2 marks for engineering linkage; 1 mark for diagram/conclusion.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CNC turning process produces shafts with a diameter specification of $20.00 \pm 0.05$ mm. After a corrective action, the process mean stabilises at $\mu = 20.02$ mm with standard deviation $\sigma = 0.012$ mm. Calculate the process capability indices $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Comment on the process capability and suggest one corrective and one preventive action.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $USL = 20.05$ mm, $LSL = 19.95$ mm.
- $\mu = 20.02$ mm, $\sigma = 0.012$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_p &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} \\
    &= \dfrac{20.05 - 19.95}{6 \times 0.012} \\
    &= \dfrac{0.10}{0.072} \\
    &= 1.389
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{pk,upper} &= \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{20.05 - 20.02}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.03}{0.036} = 0.833 \\
C_{pk,lower} &= \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{20.02 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.036} = 1.944 \\
C_{pk} &= \min(0.833, 1.944) = 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Comment:**

$C_p = 1.389$ indicates the *spread* is acceptable ($> 1.33$ threshold), but the process is **not centred** ($\mu$ is shifted towards USL). $C_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.0$ confirms the process is **not capable** — it is producing defects at the upper spec limit.

**Step 4 — Suggested Actions:**

- **Corrective Action (CA):** Re-centre the CNC machine tool offset to bring $\mu$ back to 20.00 mm. Adjust the tool-wear compensation parameter in the controller.
- **Preventive Action (PA):** Install an **in-process gauging system** (Poka-Yoke) that automatically alarms if diameter exceeds 20.04 mm, and implement an **SPC X-bar and R chart** for real-time monitoring. Schedule preventive tool replacement at 80% of tool life rather than waiting for failure.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for $C_p$, 2 marks for $C_{pk}$, 1 mark for interpretation, 1 mark each for CA and PA.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of the **Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram**. Explain how it is used in root cause analysis of a manufacturing quality problem.

**Model Answer (a):**

The **Ishikawa Diagram**, also called the **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** or **Fishbone Diagram**, was developed by **Kaoru Ishikawa** in 1968. It is a structured brainstorming tool for identifying potential causes of a quality problem.

**Construction (sketch description):**
- A horizontal arrow points to the **"Effect"** (the problem statement) on the right.
- Diagonal "bones" branch off the main spine, representing the **6M categories** (in manufacturing):
  1. **Man** (operator skill, training, fatigue).
  2. **Machine** (tool wear, calibration, breakdown).
  3. **Material** (incoming quality, storage, handling).
  4. **Method** (procedure, work instruction, process parameters).
  5. **Measurement** (gauge accuracy, calibration, sampling).
  6. **Mother Nature (Environment)** (temperature, humidity, dust, lighting).
- Sub-branches on each bone list *specific* potential causes identified by the team.

**Working & Application in RCA:**

1. **Assemble a cross-functional team** (operator, supervisor, quality engineer, design engineer).
2. **State the problem** clearly on the head of the fish (e.g., "Excessive surface roughness on shaft").
3. **Brainstorm** all possible causes under each 6M category. Use sticky notes or whiteboard.
4. **Prioritise** causes using **5-Why analysis** on each branch.
5. **Validate** the most likely causes with data (Pareto chart, scatter plot, hypothesis test).
6. **Identify the root cause(s)** and initiate CAPA.

**Example:** A bearing manufacturer observes premature failure. Ishikawa analysis reveals:
- *Machine:* Raceway grinder not calibrated.
- *Method:* Inadequate lubrication interval.
- *Material:* Steel batch with high inclusion content.

The dominant root cause is identified, and corrective + preventive actions are deployed.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for construction, 2 marks for 6M categories, 2 marks for working steps, 1 mark for engineering example.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A company recorded the following defect data over one month. Use **Pareto Analysis** to identify the *vital few* defect categories and recommend appropriate corrective and preventive actions.

| Defect Category | Frequency |
| :-------------- | :-------- |
| Surface Scratch | 42 |
| Dimensional Out-of-Spec | 68 |
| Burr Formation | 15 |
| Colour Mismatch | 10 |
| Porosity | 5 |

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Sort in descending order of frequency:**

| Defect Category | Frequency |
| :-------------- | :-------- |
| Dimensional Out-of-Spec | 68 |
| Surface Scratch | 42 |
| Burr Formation | 15 |
| Colour Mismatch | 10 |
| Porosity | 5 |
| **Total** | **140** |

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative percentage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Dimensional} &: \dfrac{68}{140} \times 100 = 48.57\% \\
\text{Dimensional + Surface Scratch} &: \dfrac{68+42}{140} \times 100 = 78.57\% \\
\text{Dimensional + Surface Scratch + Burr} &: \dfrac{68+42+15}{140} \times 100 = 89.29\% \\
\text{All four} &: \dfrac{135}{140} \times 100 = 96.43\% \\
\text{All five} &: 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pareto Interpretation:**

- **Dimensional Out-of-Spec** alone contributes **48.57%** of all defects.
- **Dimensional + Surface Scratch** together account for **78.57%** — the classic *80/20* Pareto region.
- These are the **vital few**; Burr, Colour, and Porosity are the *trivial many*.

**Step 4 — Recommended Corrective Action (CA):**

- *For Dimensional Out-of-Spec:* Re-calibrate the CNC machine, check tool wear, and reset process parameters. Containment: 100% inspection using a go/no-go gauge.
- *For Surface Scratch:* Replace the contact pads on the conveyor, retrain operators on handling.

**Step 5 — Recommended Preventive Action (PA):**

- Install an **SPC control chart** on the critical dimension with automatic alerts at $2\sigma$ deviations.
- Apply **Poka-Yoke fixtures** to eliminate the possibility of misalignment during loading.
- Schedule a **Design of Experiments (DOE)** study to identify optimal machining parameters that prevent both dimensional and scratch defects.
- Update the **FMEA** with these two failure modes and set RPN reduction targets.

*(Distribution: 2 marks sorted table, 2 marks cumulative % calculation, 1 mark interpretation, 1 mark CA, 1 mark PA.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Traps in Module 4 (Corrective & Preventive Methods):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing CA and PA definitions** — Examiners award *zero partial credit* if the student says "preventive action fixes the defect after it occurs". Always state the timing explicitly: *CA = after, PA = before*.
> 2. **Forgetting to recompute $\mu$ and $\sigma$ after correction** — In $C_p / C_{pk}$ problems, students often compute $C_p$ correctly but then write the wrong interpretation. Always state: "$C_{pk} < 1.0 \Rightarrow$ not capable; $C_{pk} \geq 1.33 \Rightarrow$ capable."
> 3. **Pareto calculation errors** — A common slip is forgetting to *sort descending* first, or mis-computing cumulative %. Write the running-total column explicitly in the answer sheet.
> 4. **Ishikawa diagram without 6M** — Some students draw only 4 branches (4M). The full 6M (or 5M+1E) is required for full marks.
> 5. **FMEA RPN — students taking $1/D$** — The Detection rating is *direct*; a higher $D$ means *worse*. Do not invert it.
> 6. **PDCA — writing it as a linear process** — It is a *cycle*, not a line. Always draw the loop returning from ACT back to PLAN.
> 7. **No mention of "verification of effectiveness"** — A CAPA without effectiveness check is considered *closed but unverified* and gets only 50% marks in the engineering examples.
> 8. **Mixing CAPA with just "inspection"** — Inspection is *appraisal*, not *prevention*. Examiners deduct marks for treating inspection as a corrective action.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet List for the Night Before the KTU Exam:**

- **CA = Cure the disease** (after defect); **PA = Vaccinate** (before defect). *Timing is the key distinction.*
- **CAPA** is the ISO 9001-mandated framework with **8 steps** (CA) and **5 steps** (PA).
- **PDCA** = Plan → Do → Check → Act (a *cycle*, not a line). Deming's continuous improvement engine.
- **DMAIC** = Define → Measure → Analyse → Improve → Control (Six Sigma, data-driven).
- **FMEA** is *forward-looking*; **RPN = S × O × D**; trigger CAPA if RPN > 100.
- **6M categories** of Ishikawa: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature (Environment).
- **Pareto Principle** = 80/20 rule; sort defects descending, find the *vital few* within 80% cumulative.
- **5-Why** = ask "why" 5 times in sequence to drill down to root cause.
- **Poka-Yoke** = design the process so that human error becomes *impossible* (mistake proofing).
- **SPC Control Charts** = real-time monitoring tool; breaches trigger **corrective** action; trends trigger **preventive** action.
- **$C_p \geq 1.33$** is minimum acceptable capability; **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$** for off-centre processes.
- **COPQ** = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure. *Spending on prevention saves 10× in failure cost* (Juran).
- **DPMO** = (Defects / Units × Opportunities) × $10^6$. Six Sigma target = 3.4 DPMO.
- **First-pass yield (FPY)** × each station = **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)**.
- **Standardisation** is the *final* and most-skipped step of CAPA — examiners award 1 mark just for mentioning "SOP update".
- **Horizontal deployment** = applying a successful CAPA from one part/process to all similar parts/processes (a *preventive* extension).
- **Kaizen** = continuous incremental improvement; **Kaikaku** = radical breakthrough improvement; both rely on PDCA.
- **Cost of prevention < Cost of failure** — always justify CAPA in monetary terms in 14-mark answers.
- **Closed-loop verification** is non-negotiable; without it, the CAPA is *incomplete* in the examiner's view.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cost of Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cost of Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is defined as the sum of all costs incurred by an organization in ensuring that products and services conform to specified quality requirements, plus all the costs incurred as a result of failures to meet those requirements.

Mathematically, it is the total financial expenditure — both **tangible** and **intangible** — associated with the **prevention, detection, and correction** of defects, errors, or non-conformities across the entire value chain of a product or process.

According to the **American Society for Quality (ASQ)**, COQ is broadly classified into four families of costs:

1. **Prevention Costs (P)** – Money spent to *avoid* defects.
2. **Appraisal Costs (A)** – Money spent to *detect* defects.
3. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** – Costs of defects found *before* delivery to the customer.
4. **External Failure Costs (EFC)** – Costs of defects found *after* delivery to the customer.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Leaky Tap" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Real-World Analogy):** Imagine your house water tank is leaking.
> - If you **prevent** the leak (call a plumber, tighten joints) — you spend a little now and avoid a flood later.
> - If you **appraise** the leak (place a bucket, monitor dripping) — you are paying attention but the water keeps dripping.
> - If the leak causes **internal failure** (basement floods, walls damp) — you pay for repair *before* anyone visits.
> - If the leak causes **external failure** (neighbor's ceiling collapses, legal notice from municipality) — you pay ten times more.
>
> The total "Cost of Quality" of water is everything — the plumber, the bucket, the wall plaster, AND the legal fine. A wise homeowner spends MORE on prevention than on lawyers!

**In manufacturing:** Prevention cost (training, design review) is the "plumber"; Appraisal cost (inspection, testing) is the "bucket"; Failure costs (scrap, rework, warranty claims, recalls) are the "flood damages."

---

## 1.3 Standard Categories — Quick Glossary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Category | Tag | When Cost Occurs | Typical Examples |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | **Prevention Costs** | P | Before defect occurs | Quality planning, training, supplier capability assessment, design reviews, process capability studies |
> | **Appraisal Costs** | A | During detection | Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final inspection, testing equipment calibration, QC audits |
> | **Internal Failure Costs** | IFC | Before customer receives | Scrap, rework, reinspection, defect analysis, downtime due to defects |
> | **External Failure Costs** | EFC | After customer receives | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, legal liability, loss of goodwill |

---

## 1.4 Cost of Conformance vs. Cost of Non-Conformance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Cost of Conformance (CoC)** = Prevention Cost (P) + Appraisal Cost (A)
> - **Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC)** = Internal Failure Cost (IFC) + External Failure Cost (EFC)

The classical strategic goal of any TQM initiative is to **shift the cost mix** so that the proportion of *Conformance* costs rises while *Non-Conformance* costs fall — because **prevention is always cheaper than failure**.

---

## 1.5 Importance in Engineering and Manufacturing

- Provides a **financial justification** for quality improvement projects.
- Helps management **quantify** the impact of defects on profit margins.
- Acts as a **performance metric** for quality management systems (ISO 9001, Six Sigma).
- Guides **resource allocation** between prevention, inspection, and rework.
- Reveals the **optimal level of investment** in quality beyond which returns diminish.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The PAF Model — A Structured View

The **PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model** is the most widely taught framework in KTU examinations. It builds on the simple premise that every rupee spent on quality belongs to exactly one of these four families.

**Logic Steps (Why each category exists):**

1. **Why Prevention?** A defect that never occurs costs zero failure money. Prevention removes the root cause.
2. **Why Appraisal?** Even with perfect prevention, measurement uncertainty exists — appraisal provides verification.
3. **Why Internal Failure?** Some defects slip through; catching them before dispatch limits damage.
4. **Why External Failure?** Some defects slip past appraisal and reach the customer — these are the most expensive of all.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | # | Formula | Description | Unit |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | $TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC$ | Total Quality Cost (sum of all four categories) | ₹ / ₹ per unit |
> | 2 | $CoC = P + A$ | Cost of Conformance | ₹ |
> | 3 | $CoNC = IFC + EFC$ | Cost of Non-Conformance | ₹ |
> | 4 | $Quality\ Ratio = \dfrac{P + A}{IFC + EFC}$ | Measures shift from failure spending to prevention | Dimensionless |
> | 5 | $Cost\ per\ Unit = \dfrac{TQC}{N}$ | Average quality cost per unit produced | ₹ / unit |
> | 6 | $\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 0$ | Optimal Quality Level (mathematical condition) | N/A |
> | 7 | $Total\ Sales\ Loss = EFC + Lost\ Goodwill$ | Customer-side impact | ₹ |
> | 8 | $Quality\ Loss\ Function\ (Taguchi) = L(x) = k(x - T)^2$ | Quality loss to society per unit | ₹ / unit |
> | 9 | $Break-even\ Prevention\ Spend = IFC_{saved} - A_{extra}$ | Maximum justified prevention budget | ₹ |

> **Note:** All currency units are in Indian Rupees (₹) for KTU valuation context. The formulas hold for any currency.

---

## 2.3 Taguchi's Quality Loss Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Genichi Taguchi** argued that **any deviation from target (T)** causes loss to society — even if the part is "within specification." The Quality Loss Function is:
>
> $$L(x) = k (x - T)^2$$
>
> Where:
> - $L(x)$ = Loss to society per unit (in ₹)
> - $x$ = Actual quality characteristic value
> - $T$ = Target value
> - $k$ = Proportionality constant, often derived as:
>
> $$k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$
>
> - $A_0$ = Loss at the tolerance boundary (often the cost of replacement/repair)
> - $\Delta_0$ = Half the tolerance band from target

This model **revolutionized COQ** because it recognizes that COQ is not "zero" inside specification — it grows quadratically with deviation.

---

## 2.4 The Optimal Quality Level (Economic Quality Level)

Quality investment has a **U-shaped cost curve** when plotted against quality level (Q):

- **Low Q:** Failure costs dominate (many defects, high scrap, high warranty).
- **High Q:** Prevention costs dominate (over-engineering, 100% inspection, excessive automation).
- **Optimum Q:** Point where **Total Quality Cost is minimum**.

The mathematical condition:

$$\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 0 \quad \text{at the optimal } Q^*$$

> **Real-world utility:** In an automotive plant, the quality manager uses this curve to decide whether to add one more inspection station (appraisal cost ↑) versus investing in a CNC upgrade (prevention cost ↑). The optimum prevents both under-inspection and over-inspection.

---

## 2.5 Hidden (Intangible) Costs of Quality

> [!WARNING]
> Most textbook examples ignore these. In KTU 14-mark answers, mentioning these earns extra credit.

| Hidden Cost | Description |
|---|---|
| Loss of customer goodwill | Future orders lost due to dissatisfaction |
| Employee morale decline | Workers demotivated by constant rework |
| Opportunity cost | Capacity wasted on rework = fewer new orders |
| Insurance premium hikes | Insurers charge more after warranty claims |
| Brand erosion | Long-term reputation damage |
| Delayed shipments | Missed deadlines trigger penalties |

These are often **2 to 5 times** the visible external failure cost.

---

## 2.6 Cost-Benefit Model of Quality Improvement

For any proposed quality improvement (e.g., Six Sigma project, Kaizen event):

$$\text{Net Benefit} = (IFC_{saved} + EFC_{saved}) - (P_{added} + A_{added}) - \text{Project Cost}$$

A project is justified if and only if:

$$\text{Net Benefit} > 0$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Optimal Quality Level (Setting d(TQC)/dQ = 0)

We model quality cost as a function of the quality level $Q$ (where $Q$ is the fraction of good units, $0 \le Q \le 1$).

**Step 1 — Write the four cost components as functions of Q:**

- **Prevention Cost** $P(Q)$ — *increases* with higher Q because you must invest more to achieve near-perfect quality. Model it as:
$$P(Q) = p_1 \cdot Q^{2}$$

where $p_1$ is a constant scaling factor. The quadratic growth reflects the steep cost of achieving near-zero defects.

- **Appraisal Cost** $A(Q)$ — *slightly increases* with Q because higher-quality goals demand more rigorous inspection. Model:
$$A(Q) = a_1 \cdot Q$$

- **Internal Failure Cost** $IFC(Q)$ — *decreases* with Q because fewer defects reach internal failure when Q is high:
$$IFC(Q) = i_1 \cdot (1 - Q)$$

- **External Failure Cost** $EFC(Q)$ — *decreases sharply* with Q:
$$EFC(Q) = e_1 \cdot (1 - Q)^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Combine into Total Quality Cost:**

$$TQC(Q) = p_1 Q^{2} + a_1 Q + i_1 (1 - Q) + e_1 (1 - Q)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to Q:**

$$\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 2 p_1 Q + a_1 - i_1 - 2 e_1 (1 - Q)$$

**Step 4 — Set the derivative equal to zero for the minimum:**

$$2 p_1 Q + a_1 - i_1 - 2 e_1 + 2 e_1 Q = 0$$

**Step 5 — Group the Q terms:**

$$Q(2 p_1 + 2 e_1) = i_1 + 2 e_1 - a_1$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the optimal Q\* (Economic Quality Level):**

$$\boxed{Q^{*} = \dfrac{i_1 + 2 e_1 - a_1}{2(p_1 + e_1)}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** If $p_1$ (prevention cost rate) is very high (prevention is expensive), $Q^{*}$ shifts downward — meaning it is uneconomical to pursue ultra-high quality. Conversely, if $e_1$ (external failure cost rate) is very high (customer returns are devastating, e.g., in aerospace), $Q^{*}$ shifts upward, justifying huge prevention budgets.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Taguchi's Quality Loss Constant $k$

**Step 1 — Define the boundary condition:**

At the tolerance limit $x = T \pm \Delta_0$, the loss to society equals $A_0$ (typically the cost of replacing/repairing the part plus customer dissatisfaction).

**Step 2 — Substitute into the loss function $L(x) = k(x - T)^2$:**

At $x = T + \Delta_0$:

$$L(T + \Delta_0) = k (\Delta_0)^{2} = A_0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $k$:**

$$\boxed{k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Express total quality loss for $N$ units having deviation $x_i$:**

$$L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} k (x_i - T)^{2} = k \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N} (x_i - T)^{2}$$

**Step 5 — For a normally distributed process with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$:**

$$\sum (x_i - T)^{2} \approx N [\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}]$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{L_{total} = k \cdot N \cdot [\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T)^{2}]}$$

This form lets engineers relate **process variation** directly to **monetary quality loss**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Quality Cost Reporting Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> A complete COQ analysis uses the following analytical matrix. Each column maps a cost element to its % share of total quality cost.

| Cost Element | Symbol | Behavior vs. Q | % of TQC (Typical Mature Firm) | % of TQC (Typical Immature Firm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Prevention | P | ↑ as Q ↑ | 25–35% | 3–8% |
| Appraisal | A | ↑ slowly as Q ↑ | 20–30% | 15–25% |
| Internal Failure | IFC | ↓ as Q ↑ | 25–35% | 30–40% |
| External Failure | EFC | ↓ sharply as Q ↑ | 10–20% | 35–50% |

> **KTU Tip:** In a TQM-mature organization, **P + A > IFC + EFC**. In a non-TQM organization, the reverse holds.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A manufacturing firm reports the following annual quality costs:
> - Prevention: ₹ 80,000
> - Appraisal: ₹ 60,000
> - Internal Failure: ₹ 1,20,000
> - External Failure: ₹ 1,60,000
>
> **Find:** (i) Total Quality Cost, (ii) Cost of Conformance, (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance, (iv) Quality Ratio, (v) Recommendation.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total Quality Cost:**

$$TQC = P + A + IFC + EFC$$
$$TQC = 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 + 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000$$
$$\boxed{TQC = ₹ 4{,}20{,}000}$$

**Step 2 — Cost of Conformance:**

$$CoC = P + A = 80{,}000 + 60{,}000 = \boxed{₹ 1{,}40{,}000}$$

**Step 3 — Cost of Non-Conformance:**

$$CoNC = IFC + EFC = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}60{,}000 = \boxed{₹ 2{,}80{,}000}$$

**Step 4 — Quality Ratio:**

$$QR = \dfrac{CoC}{CoNC} = \dfrac{1{,}40{,}000}{2{,}80{,}000} = \boxed{0.50}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

The ratio $QR = 0.5$ means the firm spends only **₹0.50 on conformance for every ₹1 spent on failures** — a clear sign of under-investment in prevention. The recommended strategy is to **increase P and A** substantially; this will push IFC and EFC downward, eventually reducing TQC.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — PAF Model Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[COST OF QUALITY SYSTEM] --> B[COST OF CONFORMANCE]
    A --> C[COST OF NON CONFORMANCE]

    B --> B1[PREVENTION COSTS]
    B --> B2[APPRAISAL COSTS]

    C --> C1[INTERNAL FAILURE COSTS]
    C --> C2[EXTERNAL FAILURE COSTS]

    B1 --> B1a[Quality Planning]
    B1 --> B1b[Training and Education]
    B1 --> B1c[Supplier Capability]
    B1 --> B1d[Process Design]

    B2 --> B2a[Incoming Inspection]
    B2 --> B2b[In Process Inspection]
    B2 --> B2c[Final Inspection]
    B2 --> B2d[Test Equipment]

    C1 --> C1a[Scrap]
    C1 --> C1b[Rework]
    C1 --> C1c[Reinspection]
    C1 --> C1d[Downtime]

    C2 --> C2a[Warranty Claims]
    C2 --> C2b[Returns and Recalls]
    C2 --> C2c[Field Service]
    C2 --> C2d[Legal Liability]
```

---

## 4.2 Optimal Quality Level Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Quality Level] -->|Increase Q| B[Optimum Q STAR]
    B -->|Further Increase| C[Over Engineering Zone]

    A1[Failure Costs Dominate] --- A
    B1[TQC Minimum Point] --- B
    C1[Prevention Costs Dominate] --- C

    A --> A1
    B --> B1
    C --> C1
```

---

## 4.3 Quality Cost Trend Under TQM Maturity

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stage1[STAGE 1 IMMATURE] --> stage2[STAGE 2 AWAKENING]
    stage2 --> stage3[STAGE 3 MATURE]
    stage3 --> stage4[STAGE 4 WORLD CLASS]

    s1a[IFC HIGH] --- stage1
    s1b[EFC HIGH] --- stage1
    s1c[P LOW] --- stage1
    s1d[A LOW] --- stage1

    s2a[IFC RISING] --- stage2
    s2b[EFC STILL HIGH] --- stage2
    s2c[P RISING] --- stage2
    s2d[A RISING] --- stage2

    s3a[IFC FALLING] --- stage3
    s3b[EFC FALLING] --- stage3
    s3c[P HIGHER] --- stage3
    s3d[A STABLE] --- stage3

    s4a[IFC MINIMAL] --- stage4
    s4b[EFC MINIMAL] --- stage4
    s4c[P DOMINANT] --- stage4
    s4d[A BALANCED] --- stage4
```

---

## 4.4 Taguchi Loss Function Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    t[TAGUCHI QUALITY LOSS FUNCTION] --> t1[LOSS at TARGET T equals ZERO]
    t --> t2[LOSS at TOLERANCE LIMIT equals A0]
    t --> t3[LOSS GROWS QUADRATICALLY]

    t1 --> t1a[Zero deviation means zero societal cost]
    t2 --> t2a[Even within spec there is a loss]
    t3 --> t3a[L equals k times x minus T squared]
```

---

## 4.5 Decision Flow for Quality Investment

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q1[Identify Quality Problem] --> q2[Estimate IFC and EFC]
    q2 --> q3[Propose Prevention or Appraisal Activity]
    q3 --> q4[Estimate New P and A]
    q4 --> q5{Calculate Net Benefit}
    q5 -->|Benefit greater than Zero| q6[APPROVE PROJECT]
    q5 -->|Benefit less than or equal to Zero| q7[REJECT PROJECT]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Define Cost of Quality. List and briefly explain its four major categories.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total financial expenditure incurred by an organization to ensure products meet quality standards, plus the cost of failures due to non-conformance.

**(1 mark)** The four categories are:

**(a) Prevention Costs:** Costs incurred to *avoid* defects — quality planning, training, design reviews, supplier development. (0.75 mark)

**(b) Appraisal Costs:** Costs of *detecting* defects — incoming inspection, in-process checks, final testing, calibration. (0.75 mark)

**(c) Internal Failure Costs:** Costs of defects found *before* delivery — scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime. (0.5 mark)

**(d) External Failure Costs:** Costs of defects found *after* delivery — warranty, returns, recalls, legal liability, goodwill loss. (0.5 mark — exceeds to 3 marks total)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Distinguish between Cost of Conformance and Cost of Non-Conformance with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Cost of Conformance (CoC) | Cost of Non-Conformance (CoNC) |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Money spent to *meet* quality standards | Money lost due to *failure* to meet standards |
| Composition | Prevention + Appraisal | Internal Failure + External Failure |
| Strategic Aim | **Increase** it | **Reduce** it |
| Example 1 | Quality training of operators | Scrap of defective machined parts |
| Example 2 | Final inspection and testing | Warranty claims from customers |

**(2 marks)** for distinction table; **(1 mark)** for the two examples on each side.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Common Mark-Loss Zones):**
> - Students often **forget to list examples** for each of the four COQ categories — KTU examiners deduct up to 2 marks.
> - In numerical problems, students forget to convert **lakhs** to **rupees** or skip the **interpretation** step — losing 1–2 marks for "no conclusion."
> - Students write the Taguchi loss formula but **forget the condition at tolerance limit** — losing the derivation marks.
> - Students confuse **CoC** and **CoNC** in the Quality Ratio formula — common 1-mark loss.
> - Always include a **diagram or matrix** in COQ answers to score full marks in the "presentation" component.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four categories of Cost of Quality** with suitable examples for each. Discuss how an organization can strategically shift the cost mix from non-conformance to conformance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The annual quality cost data of an automotive parts manufacturing company is as follows:

| Cost Element | Amount (₹) |
|---|---|
| Quality Planning | 50,000 |
| Operator Training | 30,000 |
| Incoming Inspection | 40,000 |
| Final Testing | 35,000 |
| Scrap and Rework | 1,10,000 |
| Warranty Claims | 1,40,000 |
| Supplier Audits | 20,000 |
| Design Reviews | 25,000 |
| Product Recall | 80,000 |
| Calibration of Gauges | 15,000 |

**Calculate:**
(i) Total Quality Cost, (ii) Cost of Conformance, (iii) Cost of Non-Conformance, (iv) Quality Ratio, and (v) Recommend a strategy to reduce TQC. **(7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Four Categories of COQ (7 Marks)

**1. Prevention Costs (1.5 marks):** These are costs of activities aimed at *preventing* defects from occurring in the first place. Examples: Quality planning, operator training, supplier audits, design reviews, process capability studies, quality manuals, ISO certification. By investing in prevention, organizations attack the root cause of defects rather than their symptoms.

**2. Appraisal Costs (1.5 marks):** These are costs of *measuring, evaluating, and auditing* products and processes to detect defects. Examples: Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final testing, calibration of gauges, QC audits, inspection equipment depreciation. Appraisal costs are essentially the price of confidence.

**3. Internal Failure Costs (1.5 marks):** Costs of defects discovered *before* the product reaches the customer. Examples: Scrap, rework, reinspection, machine downtime due to defects, failure-mode analysis, material wastage. While internal failure is painful, it is bounded — the customer has not yet been affected.

**4. External Failure Costs (1.5 marks):** Costs of defects discovered *after* the product reaches the customer. Examples: Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, legal liability, loss of goodwill, customer complaints. External failure is the most damaging category because it directly erodes brand equity.

**Strategic Shift from Non-Conformance to Conformance (1 mark):**

A mature TQM firm follows the principle: **"Spend more on P and A; save more on IFC and EFC."** The implementation strategy is:
- Step 1: Conduct root-cause analysis (5-Why, Pareto, Fishbone).
- Step 2: Invest in prevention — training, SPC, FMEA, poka-yoke.
- Step 3: Replace 100% inspection with statistical process control.
- Step 4: Build supplier partnerships instead of policing them.
- Step 5: Establish a COQ reporting system to track the shift quarterly.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Classify each cost element:**

| Cost Element | Amount (₹) | Category |
|---|---|---|
| Quality Planning | 50,000 | Prevention |
| Operator Training | 30,000 | Prevention |
| Supplier Audits | 20,000 | Prevention |
| Design Reviews | 25,000 | Prevention |
| Incoming Inspection | 40,000 | Appraisal |
| Final Testing | 35,000 | Appraisal |
| Calibration of Gauges | 15,000 | Appraisal |
| Scrap and Rework | 1,10,000 | Internal Failure |
| Warranty Claims | 1,40,000 | External Failure |
| Product Recall | 80,000 | External Failure |

**Step 2 — Sum each category:**

$$P = 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 20{,}000 + 25{,}000 = ₹ 1{,}25{,}000$$

$$A = 40{,}000 + 35{,}000 + 15{,}000 = ₹ 90{,}000$$

$$IFC = ₹ 1{,}10{,}000$$

$$EFC = 1{,}40{,}000 + 80{,}000 = ₹ 2{,}20{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Compute the four required quantities:**

(i) **Total Quality Cost:**
$$TQC = 1{,}25{,}000 + 90{,}000 + 1{,}10{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000 = \boxed{₹ 5{,}45{,}000}$$

(ii) **Cost of Conformance:**
$$CoC = P + A = 1{,}25{,}000 + 90{,}000 = \boxed{₹ 2{,}15{,}000}$$

(iii) **Cost of Non-Conformance:**
$$CoNC = IFC + EFC = 1{,}10{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000 = \boxed{₹ 3{,}30{,}000}$$

(iv) **Quality Ratio:**
$$QR = \dfrac{CoC}{CoNC} = \dfrac{2{,}15{,}000}{3{,}30{,}000} \approx \boxed{0.65}$$

(v) **Strategy to reduce TQC:**
The Quality Ratio of 0.65 indicates the firm spends ₹0.65 on conformance for every ₹1 spent on failures. The external failure cost (₹2,20,000) is alarmingly high — dominated by warranty claims and a recall. **Recommended strategy:**
- (1 mark for recommendation) Increase investment in **prevention** — design reviews, FMEA, and operator training should at least double.
- (1 mark) Introduce **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** to detect trends before defects occur.
- (1 mark) Conduct a **supplier capability enhancement program** to reduce incoming defects.
- (1 mark) Establish a **warranty cost-tracking system** linking field failures back to root causes.

Expected outcome: IFC and EFC drop by 40–60%, raising QR above 1.5 and reducing TQC by 20–30%.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**
