**(a)** Explain the **Taguchi Quality Loss Function**. Derive the expression for the loss constant $k$ in terms of tolerance $\Delta_0$ and boundary loss $A_0$. Discuss how this concept challenges the traditional "within-spec-is-acceptable" view of quality. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process produces shafts with target diameter $T = 50.00$ mm. The process standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.05$ mm and the process is centered ($\mu = T$). The tolerance band is $T \pm 0.20$ mm, and the replacement cost at the tolerance boundary is $A_0 = ₹ 400$ per unit. If the company produces $N = 10{,}000$ shafts per month, calculate:

(i) The loss constant $k$, (ii) The quality loss per shaft at $x = 50.10$ mm, (iii) The expected monthly total quality loss. **(7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Taguchi Quality Loss Function (7 Marks)

**Concept (2 marks):** Genichi Taguchi proposed that **any deviation from the target value causes loss to society** — even if the part is within specification limits. Traditional quality thinking said: "If a part passes inspection, the loss is zero." Taguchi argued: "Even an acceptable part has some loss; the further from target, the larger the loss."

**The Loss Function (2 marks):**
$$L(x) = k(x - T)^{2}$$
where $L(x)$ = loss in ₹, $x$ = actual value, $T$ = target value, $k$ = loss constant.

**Derivation of k (2 marks):** At the tolerance limit $x = T + \Delta_0$, the loss equals $A_0$:
$$L(T + \Delta_0) = k(\Delta_0)^{2} = A_0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}$$

**Challenge to Traditional View (1 mark):** Taguchi's model contradicts the binary "pass/fail" mindset. It proves that the cost of poor quality is a *continuous, quadratic* function of deviation, not a step function. This justifies investments in tighter process control even when products are within specifications — because society still bears loss.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$T = 50.00$ mm, $\sigma = 0.05$ mm, $\mu = T$, $\Delta_0 = 0.20$ mm, $A_0 = ₹ 400$, $N = 10{,}000$.

**(i) Loss constant k (2 marks):**
$$k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}} = \dfrac{400}{(0.20)^{2}} = \dfrac{400}{0.04}$$
$$\boxed{k = ₹ 10{,}000 \text{ per mm}^{2}}$$

**(ii) Loss per shaft at $x = 50.10$ mm (2 marks):**

Deviation: $x - T = 50.10 - 50.00 = 0.10$ mm.

$$L(50.10) = k(x - T)^{2} = 10{,}000 \times (0.10)^{2} = 10{,}000 \times 0.01$$
$$\boxed{L = ₹ 100 \text{ per shaft}}$$

**(iii) Expected monthly total quality loss (3 marks):**

For a centered process, $\mu = T$, so $\mu - T = 0$. The expected loss per shaft:
$$E[L] = k \cdot \sigma^{2} = 10{,}000 \times (0.05)^{2} = 10{,}000 \times 0.0025$$
$$E[L] = ₹ 25 \text{ per shaft}$$

Total monthly loss:
$$L_{total} = N \cdot k \cdot \sigma^{2} = 10{,}000 \times 25$$
$$\boxed{L_{total} = ₹ 2{,}50{,}000 \text{ per month}}$$

> **Interpretation (for 1 mark):** Even though every shaft may be "within spec," the firm suffers a hidden quality loss of ₹ 2.5 lakhs per month. Reducing $\sigma$ from 0.05 to 0.025 (using better CNC or SPC) would cut this loss by 75%, saving ₹ 1.875 lakhs/month.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **COQ = P + A + IFC + EFC** — the master equation. Memorize it cold.
- ☐ **Prevention** = before defect occurs; **Appraisal** = during detection; **Internal Failure** = before customer; **External Failure** = after customer.
- ☐ **CoC = P + A**; **CoNC = IFC + EFC**. Never confuse them.
- ☐ **Quality Ratio = CoC / CoNC**. A TQM-mature firm has QR > 1; an immature firm has QR < 1.
- ☐ **Taguchi Loss Function:** $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$ and $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$.
- ☐ **Optimal Quality Level** is the minimum of the TQC curve — found by setting $\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 0$.
- ☐ **Hidden costs** (goodwill, morale, opportunity) can be 2–5× visible EFC — always mention them in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ **In every numerical problem**, end with a **strategic recommendation** — KTU examiners reward this.
- ☐ **Mature firm:** P + A > IFC + EFC. **Immature firm:** reverse.
- ☐ **Six Sigma philosophy:** Push defects below 3.4 per million, dramatically shifting cost mix toward prevention.
- ☐ **In a 14-mark answer**, always include: (1) classification table, (2) one numerical example, (3) a graph or matrix, (4) interpretation + recommendation.
- ☐ **Avoid these common errors:** wrong units, missing formula derivation steps, no examples for each category, no concluding remark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Organization for Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Organization for Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Organization for Quality"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Organization for Quality* refers to the **structured deployment of human resources, policies, procedures, and responsibilities** within a manufacturing or service enterprise to ensure that products and processes consistently meet customer and regulatory requirements. It defines the **chain of authority, communication channels, and documentation hierarchy** required to plan, control, assure, and continually improve quality across all functional departments.

In simple terms, it is the **"operating system of a company for quality"** — without it, quality is just an accident; with it, quality is an engineered outcome.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Think of a hospital treating a patient:
- The **Brain (Top Management)** decides the treatment policy.
- The **Nervous System (Quality Manual & Procedures)** carries instructions.
- The **Specialists (Quality Engineers, Inspectors)** perform checks.
- The **Immune System (Quality Circles & Audits)** continuously detects and destroys defects.
- The **Heart (Customer Focus)** keeps the entire organism alive.

If any organ fails, the body fails. Similarly, **every department — Design, Purchase, Production, Sales, Service — must own a piece of the quality pie**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> | Term | Meaning |
> |------|---------|
> | **Quality Policy** | Top management's documented commitment to quality |
> | **Quality Objectives** | Measurable goals aligned with the policy |
> | **Quality Manual** | Master document describing the QMS |
> | **QMS** | Quality Management System |
> | **QA** | Quality Assurance (planned, systematic activities) |
> | **QC** | Quality Control (operational techniques like inspection) |
> | **QCC** | Quality Control Circle — small worker groups for improvement |
> | **TQM** | Total Quality Management |
> | **SPC** | Statistical Process Control |

## 1.4 Why Study It?

The KTU 2024 scheme **emphasizes Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. Any graduate engineer must understand *who does what* in a quality-driven organization — this maps directly to **CO4** of PCPET503: *"Apply quality management principles and modern organizational tools for continuous improvement in manufacturing."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Organization for Quality

### Pillar 1 — Quality Policy, Objectives & Manual

The **quality policy** is a short, written statement issued by **top management**. It must be:
1. **Appropriate** to the organization's purpose.
2. **Include a commitment** to comply with requirements and continually improve.
3. Provide a **framework** for setting quality objectives.
4. Be **communicated, understood, and reviewed**.

The **Quality Manual** contains:
- Scope of the QMS
- Documented procedures
- Interaction between processes (process map)

### Pillar 2 — Responsibility & Authority Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **RACI** concept (Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed) is widely used to define who does what:
> - **Responsible (R)**: Does the work
> - **Accountable (A)**: Ultimately answerable (one person only — usually a manager)
> - **Consulted (C)**: Provides input before action
> - **Informed (I)**: Told after the fact

A typical engineering organization structure for quality:

| Hierarchy Level | Quality Function |
|-----------------|------------------|
| Managing Director | Sets Quality Policy, approves Quality Manual |
| Quality Manager (MR) | Management Representative, QMS implementation |
| Department Heads | Achieve quality objectives in their domains |
| Quality Engineers | Process audits, supplier audits, CAPA |
| Inspectors / QC Staff | Incoming, in-process, final inspection |
| Shop-floor Workers | Self-inspection, Quality Circles |
| Internal Auditors | Conduct periodic system audits |

### Pillar 3 — Quality Circles (QCCs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A *Quality Circle* is a small group of **6 to 12 employees** from the same work area who meet **voluntarily and regularly** (usually weekly) to identify, analyze, and solve quality and productivity problems in their own work environment.

**Origin:** Pioneered in **Japan (1962)** by **Kaoru Ishikawa** at the Toyota plant.

**Structure of a QCC:**
1. **Facilitator** — Senior person (often a supervisor) who guides the circle
2. **Leader** — Chosen from among members
3. **Members** — Workers from the shop floor
4. **Recorder** — Documents meetings and progress

**QCC Problem-Solving Steps (Standardized):**
1. Identify the problem
2. Analyze the problem (cause-and-effect diagram, Pareto)
3. Generate solutions
5. Test solutions on a small scale
6. Standardize successful solutions
7. Present to management for adoption

## 2.2 Total Quality Management (TQM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TQM** is a **management philosophy** that seeks to integrate all organizational functions (marketing, finance, design, engineering, production, customer service) to focus on meeting customer needs and organizational objectives.

### TQM Core Principles
1. **Customer Focus** — internal and external
2. **Total Employee Involvement** — empowerment
3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — small, incremental changes
4. **Process Approach** — manage activities as processes
5. **Integrated System** — all departments linked
6. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — use SPC, data
7. **Supplier Partnership** — long-term relationships

### TQM Gurus (KTU Frequently Asked)

| Guru | Famous Contribution |
|------|---------------------|
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management, PDCA cycle |
| **Joseph Juran** | Quality Trilogy (Planning, Control, Improvement) |
| **Philip Crosby** | "Quality is Free"; Zero Defects concept |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram; Quality Circles |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Loss Function, Robust Design |
| **Armand Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control concept |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Statistical Control Charts (father of SPC) |

### Deming's PDCA Cycle (Most Tested in KTU)

> [!NOTE]
> **PDCA = Plan – Do – Check – Act**
> - **Plan**: Establish objectives and processes (ISO clause 6).
> - **Do**: Implement the plan (execute on small scale).
> - **Check**: Measure results against expected outcomes.
> - **Act**: Standardize if successful; if not, revise and repeat.

This is the **engine of continual improvement**.

## 2.3 ISO 9000 Family (KTU Board Hot Topic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **ISO 9000 family** is a globally recognized framework for QMS. As per KTU 2024 syllabus, focus is on **ISO 9001:2015**.

### The Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015)
1. Customer Focus
2. Leadership
3. Engagement of People
4. Process Approach
5. Improvement
6. Evidence-Based Decision Making
7. Relationship Management

### ISO 9001:2015 — High-Level Structure (10 Clauses)
1. Scope
2. Normative References
3. Terms and Definitions
4. Context of the Organization
5. Leadership
6. Planning
7. Support
8. Operation
9. Performance Evaluation
10. Improvement

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** ISO 9000 ≠ ISO 9001.
> - **ISO 9000** = Vocabulary & fundamentals (definitions).
> - **ISO 9001** = Requirements standard (the one used for certification).
> - **ISO 9004** = Guidance for performance improvements (not certifiable).

## 2.4 Six Sigma

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma** is a data-driven methodology that seeks to reduce process variation so that **no more than 3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** occur — i.e., a process operating at **±6σ** from the mean.

### DMAIC Methodology (the Six Sigma problem-solving framework)
- **D — Define**: Identify the problem, customer (CTQ = Critical-to-Quality)
- **M — Measure**: Quantify current performance, gather baseline data
- **A — Analyze**: Find root causes (regression, hypothesis tests, Ishikawa)
- **I — Improve**: Implement solutions (DOE = Design of Experiments)
- **C — Control**: Sustain improvements (control plans, SPC charts)

### KTU Formula Sheet — Six Sigma DPMO

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{\text{Number of Defects Detected}}{\text{Number of Units Produced} \times \text{Number of Defect Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6
$$

**Example KTU Calculation:**
> A batch of 1,000 units has 25 defects across 4 defect categories. Find DPMO.

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{25}{1000 \times 4} \times 10^6 = \frac{25}{4000} \times 10^6 = 6250 \text{ DPMO}
$$

**Process Sigma Level** can be obtained from DPMO tables or calculated using the inverse normal distribution. A DPMO of 3.4 corresponds to **4.5 sigma** (the famous "1.5 sigma shift").

## 2.5 The 5S Methodology (Workplace Organization)

| 5S (Japanese) | English | Purpose |
|---------------|---------|---------|
| **Seiri** | Sort | Remove unnecessary items |
| **Seiton** | Set in Order | Arrange items for easy access |
| **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace |
| **Seiketsu** | Standardize | Establish standards & procedures |
| **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Maintain discipline |

5S is the **foundation of lean manufacturing** and a prerequisite for Kaizen, Six Sigma, and TQM.

## 2.6 Bench Marking

> [!NOTE]
> **Benchmarking** is the systematic process of measuring one's products, services, and processes against those of *best-in-class* organizations to identify improvement areas.

**Types:**
- **Internal** — Compare branches/departments within the same organization.
- **Competitive** — Direct comparison with competitors.
- **Functional** — Compare similar functions in different industries.
- **Generic** — Best processes regardless of industry.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{D}{U \times O} \times 10^6$ | Six Sigma |
| **Yield (Y)** | $Y = 1 - \dfrac{\text{DPMO}}{10^6}$ | Six Sigma |
| **Process Capability Index $C_p$** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | SPC |
| **$C_{pk}$** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | SPC |
| **PDCA** | Plan → Do → Check → Act | Deming, ISO |
| **DMAIC** | Define → Measure → Analyze → Improve → Control | Six Sigma |
| **Juran's Trilogy** | Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement | TQM |
| **Cost of Quality (CoQ)** | CoQ = Prevention + Appraisal + Internal Failure + External Failure | TQM Economics |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of DPMO and Conversion to Sigma Level

**Problem Setup (KTU Standard Pattern):**
A manufacturing line produces **2,000 components per shift** over **30 shifts/month**. Quality data shows **150 defects/month** across **5 defect opportunities per unit**. Calculate:
1. Total opportunities per month
2. DPMO
3. Process Sigma (using the standard 1.5-sigma-shift table)

### Step 1 — Total Units Produced

$$
U = 2000 \times 30 = 60{,}000 \text{ units/month}
$$

### Step 2 — Total Defect Opportunities

$$
O_{\text{total}} = U \times O_{\text{per unit}} = 60{,}000 \times 5 = 300{,}000
$$

### Step 3 — DPMO Calculation

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{D}{U \times O_{\text{per unit}}} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = \frac{150}{60{,}000 \times 5} \times 10^6 = \frac{150}{300{,}000} \times 10^6
$$

$$
\text{DPMO} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6 = 500 \text{ DPMO}
$$

### Step 4 — Yield

$$
Y = 1 - \frac{500}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.9995 = 99.95\%
$$

### Step 5 — Sigma Level (using Z-table with 1.5σ shift)

For DPMO = 500, the corresponding short-term sigma ≈ **4.9**.  
Including the **1.5σ shift** used by Six Sigma practitioners:

$$
\text{Long-term Sigma} = \text{Short-term Sigma} - 1.5 = 4.9 - 1.5 = 3.4\,\sigma
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This process operates at **3.4σ**, which is below the world-class 6σ benchmark. The organization should initiate a DMAIC project to improve.

## 3.2 Derivation of Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$

Given:
- $\text{USL} = 25.00 \text{ mm}$
- $\text{LSL} = 24.00 \text{ mm}$
- Sample mean $\bar{x} = 24.45 \text{ mm}$
- Sample standard deviation $\sigma = 0.10 \text{ mm}$

### Step 1 — Compute the Upper-Side Capability

$$
C_{p,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.00 - 24.45}{3 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.55}{0.30} \approx 1.833
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Lower-Side Capability

$$
C_{p,\text{lower}} = \frac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.45 - 24.00}{3 \times 0.10} = \frac{0.45}{0.30} = 1.500
$$

### Step 3 — Take the Minimum

$$
C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right) = \min(1.833,\; 1.500) = 1.500
$$

### Step 4 — Interpretation Matrix

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Capability | KTU Inference |
|:---:|:---:|---|
| $< 1.00$ | Incapable | Process must be improved before production |
| $1.00$ to $1.33$ | Marginal | Tight control needed |
| $1.33$ to $1.67$ | Capable | Acceptable |
| $> 1.67$ | Excellent | World-class |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_{pk} = 1.50$ ⇒ the process is **capable**, but the **mean has shifted toward USL**, signalling a bias that must be corrected.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Problem Type | Best Tool |
|---|---|
| A defect rate has slowly crept up over months | SPC Control Chart (X̄-R or p-chart) |
| Need to reduce defects to < 3.4 DPMO | **Six Sigma (DMAIC)** |
| A new ISO certification is needed | **ISO 9001:2015 QMS** |
| Workers are disengaged from quality issues | **Quality Circles + TQM** |
| Repeated rework in a department | **Pareto + Ishikawa + 5-Why Analysis** |
| Want to match a competitor's delivery time | **Benchmarking** |
| Need to organize the workplace first | **5S** before any improvement tool |
| Calculate cost of poor quality | **CoQ model** |

## 3.4 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — Analytical Expansion

$$
\text{Total CoQ} = C_{\text{prevention}} + C_{\text{appraisal}} + C_{\text{internal failure}} + C_{\text{external failure}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{\text{prevention}}$: Training, quality planning, design reviews
- $C_{\text{appraisal}}$: Inspection, testing, calibration
- $C_{\text{internal failure}}$: Scrap, rework, re-inspection
- $C_{\text{external failure}}$: Warranty, returns, recalls, lawsuits

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden rule of CoQ:** Investing more in *prevention* reduces *failure costs* drastically. This is the **Crosby "Quality is Free"** principle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Quality-Driven Organization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef top fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a47,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mid fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef ops fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#2e75b6,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:1px
    classDef grnd fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235,color:#1f4e79,stroke-width:1px
    classDef doc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:1px

    A[Board of Directors and Managing Director]:::top
    B[Quality Manager and Management Representative MR]:::top
    C[Department Heads - Design Production Purchase Sales Service]:::mid
    D[Quality Assurance QA Team]:::mid
    E[Quality Control QC Inspectors]:::ops
    F[Quality Circles QCC and Shop Floor Workers]:::ops
    G[Internal Auditors]:::ops
    H[Suppliers and External Partners]:::grnd
    I[Customers Internal and External]:::grnd

    A -- Sets Quality Policy and Approves QMS --> B
    B -- Issues Quality Manual and Audits --> C
    B -- Reports QMS Performance --> A
    C -- Implements Quality Procedures --> D
    C -- Allocates Inspection Resources --> E
    D -- Plans and Audits Processes --> E
    E -- Performs Incoming In Process Final Inspection --> F
    F -- Identifies Problems and Participates --> G
    G -- Conducts System and Process Audits --> B
    C -- Manages Supplier Quality --> H
    C -- Captures Customer Voice CTQ --> I
    I -- Feedback Returns and Service Data --> C

    J[Quality Manual QMS Documentation]:::doc
    K[ISO 9001 2015 Standards]:::doc
    L[Control Plans FMEAs SOPs]:::doc

    A -.Governed by.-> K
    B -.Documented in.-> J
    C -.Uses.-> L

    A --- J
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Top tier** (blue) sets policy and leads.
- The **Middle tier** plans, audits, and supports.
- The **Operational tier** executes inspection, audits, and self-control.
- The **Ground tier** represents the *real world* — suppliers and customers — which feed information **upward**.
- The **Yellow tier** represents the *documentation backbone* — ISO 9001 standards, the Quality Manual, and supporting work instructions.

## 4.2 PDCA and DMAIC Integration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef pdca fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#073763,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px
    classDef dmaic fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#274e13,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px

    P1[Plan - Establish objectives and process]:::pdca
    D1[Do - Implement on a small scale]:::pdca
    C1[Check - Measure results against plan]:::pdca
    A1[Act - Standardize or revise]:::pdca

    PD{P Deming PDCA Cycle}:::pdca
    P1 --> D1 --> C1 --> A1 --> P1

    DM[D Define CTQ and project scope]:::dmaic
    MM[M Measure baseline data]:::dmaic
    AM[A Analyze root causes]:::dmaic
    IM[I Improve pilot solutions]:::dmaic
    CM[C Control sustain improvements]:::dmaic

    DMAIC{DMAIC Six Sigma}:::dmaic
    DM --> MM --> AM --> IM --> CM --> DM

    A1 -.Initiates when opportunity found.-> DM
    CM -.Feeds back lessons learned.-> PD
```

## 4.3 Process Map for ISO 9001:2015 Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#a04000,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px
    classDef doc fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#0b2a47,stroke-width:2px

    S1[Step 1 Context Analysis and Scope]:::step
    S2[Step 2 Leadership Commitment and Quality Policy]:::step
    S3[Step 3 Risk Based Planning and Objectives]:::step
    S4[Step 4 Support Resources Competence Awareness Communication]:::step
    S5[Step 5 Operational Planning and Control]:::step
    S6[Step 6 Performance Evaluation Internal Audit Management Review]:::step
    S7[Step 7 Improvement Nonconformity and Corrective Action]:::step

    D1[Quality Manual]:::doc
    D2[Procedures and SOPs]:::doc
    D3[Records and Evidence]:::doc

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7
    S2 -.Outputs.-> D1
    S5 -.Outputs.-> D2
    S7 -.Outputs.-> D3
    D3 -.Inputs.-> S6
```
