# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Quality Audit. State any two principles of auditing as per ISO 19011.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
A Quality Audit is a **systematic, independent, and documented examination** of a quality management system, process, or product to determine whether it conforms to specified requirements (audit criteria such as ISO 9001, customer specifications, or regulatory standards) and to evaluate the effectiveness of the implemented system. The process involves collecting objective audit evidence and comparing it against audit criteria to generate audit findings.

**Any Two Principles (1 mark = 0.5 × 2):**

1. **Integrity:** Auditors must perform their work with honesty, diligence, and responsibility. They must be fair, truthful, and discreet.

2. **Independence:** Auditors must be independent of the activity being audited to ensure objectivity. They should not be involved in the design, implementation, or maintenance of the system they audit.

*(Acceptable: Fair Presentation, Due Professional Care, Confidentiality, Evidence-Based Approach, Risk-Based Approach)*

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between First-Party and Third-Party Quality Audits.
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | First-Party Audit | Third-Party Audit |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Conducted By** | The organization itself (own trained internal auditors) | An independent, external certification body (e.g., TÜV SÜD, Bureau Veritas, DNV) |
| **Independence** | Low to moderate (auditor is from the same company) | High (auditor has no commercial relationship) |
| **Purpose** | Self-assessment to identify gaps before external audit | Granting/maintaining ISO 9001 or other QMS certification |
| **Frequency** | Typically 1–2 times per year | Surveillance: annually; Recertification: every 3 years |
| **Result/Certificate** | Internal audit report | External certificate (e.g., ISO 9001:2015) |
| **Cost** | Borne internally | Borne by the organization seeking certification |

*(Any 3 distinguishing points = 3 marks; full table = full marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions on this module **always** offer a choice. Below are two fully worked-out alternatives covering distinct aspects of Quality Audits.

---

### PART B — QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### **(a) Explain in detail the various types of quality audits with suitable examples.** (7 marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks, Part a]`

**Model Answer:**

Quality audits are classified in multiple ways based on **party, scope, and nature**.

**1. Classification by Party:**

**i) First-Party Audit (Internal Audit):**
Conducted by the organization on itself. The organization trains its own employees as internal auditors who check whether the QMS conforms to ISO 9001 and internal procedures. Example: A car manufacturing plant's quality team audits its welding shop to verify WPS/PQR compliance every 6 months.

**ii) Second-Party Audit (Supplier Audit):**
Conducted by a customer on its supplier. The purpose is to ensure the supplier can consistently meet quality, delivery, and contractual requirements. Example: Boeing auditing Spirit AeroSystems for fuselage components; Tata Motors auditing its Tier-1 fastener supplier.

**iii) Third-Party Audit (Certification Audit):**
Conducted by an independent, accredited certification body such as TÜV SÜD, DNV, Bureau Veritas, or LRQA. It results in the grant of a formal certificate (e.g., ISO 9001:2015). These are legally and commercially recognized.

**2. Classification by Scope:**

**i) System Audit:** Examines the entire QMS — documentation, processes, leadership, support, performance evaluation, and improvement. Mandatory for ISO 9001 certification.

**ii) Process Audit:** Examines a specific process (e.g., heat treatment, CNC turning, assembly). Checks work instructions, operator competence, machine calibration, process parameters.

**iii) Product Audit:** Examines a specific product against drawings and specifications. Compares finished goods to design intent.

**iv) Compliance Audit:** Verifies adherence to regulatory/legal requirements (e.g., OSHA, environmental laws, CE marking).

**3. Classification by Nature:**

**i) Vertical Audit:** Deep dive into a single process across all its levels.
**ii) Horizontal Audit:** Tracks a single product or service across multiple departments.
**iii) Performance Audit:** Evaluates the *efficiency* and *effectiveness* of the QMS, not just conformance.

*(Diagrams like the one in Section 4.2 may be drawn for additional marks.)*

---

#### **(b) Describe the procedure for conducting a quality audit as per ISO 19011:2018 guidelines. List the 7 principles of auditing.** (7 marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks, Part b]`

**Model Answer:**

**Audit Procedure (ISO 19011:2018) — 6 Phases:**

**Phase 1: Initiating the Audit**
- Identify the audit need (regulatory, customer, risk).
- Define audit **objectives** (what is to be achieved).
- Define audit **scope** (boundaries: departments, processes, sites, time).
- Define audit **criteria** (ISO 9001, customer specs, procedures).
- Select the audit team (Lead Auditor, Auditors, Technical Experts).
- Confirm **feasibility** (resources, time, auditee cooperation).

**Phase 2: Preparing Audit Activities**
- Review documented information: QMS manual, procedures, prior audit reports, customer complaints.
- Prepare the **Audit Plan** (date, time, locations, auditors, clauses).
- Assign tasks to team members based on competence.
- Prepare working documents: audit checklists, sampling plans, NCR forms.

**Phase 3: Conducting the Audit (On-Site)**
- **Opening Meeting:** Introductions, scope confirmation, methodology, logistics, safety.
- **Information Collection:** Through interviews, document review, observation of processes, and physical inspection.
- **Verification:** Triangulate evidence (cross-check with multiple sources).
- **Generate Findings:** Categorize as Conformance, Major NC, Minor NC, Observation, or OFI.
- **Closing Meeting:** Present findings, agree on timelines for corrective action, clarify misunderstandings.

**Phase 4: Preparing Audit Report**
- Document findings with objective evidence and clause reference.
- Include recommendations and follow-up dates.
- Distribute to top management and relevant auditees.

**Phase 5: Completing the Audit**
- Archive all records (checklists, evidence, report).
- Distribute final report per confidentiality requirements.

**Phase 6: Conducting Audit Follow-Up**
- Verify that the auditee has implemented effective corrective action.
- Confirm root cause analysis was done.
- Close the NCR formally if verified.

**7 Principles of Auditing (ISO 19011:2018):**

1. **Integrity** – Foundation of professionalism
2. **Fair Presentation** – Truthful and accurate findings
3. **Due Professional Care** – Diligent judgment
4. **Confidentiality** – Information security
5. **Independence** – Free from bias
6. **Evidence-Based Approach** – Verifiable reasoning
7. **Risk-Based Approach** – Focus on high-risk areas

*(Total: 6 phases × ~0.7 mark = ~4.2 marks + principles list 0.5 each × 7 = 3.5 marks → ≈ 7 marks)*

---

### PART B — QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### **(a) What are Nonconformities? Explain the types of nonconformities with examples. How are they classified by severity?** (7 marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks, Part a]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **Nonconformity (NC)** is the *non-fulfillment of a requirement* (ISO 9000:2015). It is a finding raised by the auditor when the audit evidence does not match the audit criteria (standard, procedure, or specification).

**Types of Nonconformities:**

**1. Major Nonconformity:**
The absence of, or total breakdown in, a system/process to meet a requirement. It may also be a condition that raises significant doubt about the ability of the QMS to achieve its intended outcomes. Example: A foundry has no calibration records for the spectrometer used to verify chemical composition of molten metal — this is a major NC because every heat's chemistry is suspect.

**2. Minor Nonconformity:**
A single observed lapse or isolated incident that does not indicate a systemic failure. Example: One of 50 work instructions is not signed by the department head, while all others are properly approved.

**3. Observation:**
A statement of fact, based on objective evidence, that does not constitute a nonconformity but may lead to one if not addressed. Example: A machine maintenance log shows a trend of increasing breakdown frequency, though not yet a documented NC.

**4. Opportunity for Improvement (OFI) / Best Practice:**
A positive finding that recognizes a strong practice. Example: A CNC shop has implemented predictive maintenance using IoT vibration sensors — auditor records this as a best practice.

**Severity Classification Logic:**

| Type | When to Use | Action Required |
|------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Major NC** | Systemic failure or product safety risk | Root cause analysis + corrective action within 30 days; re-audit |
| **Minor NC** | Isolated non-fulfillment | Root cause analysis + corrective action within 60 days |
| **Observation** | Trend or concern | Recorded; no formal action required |
| **OFI** | Strength to share | Documented and shared with management |

---

#### **(b) Discuss the role of the Lead Auditor. Also explain the corrective action process with a suitable flowchart.** (7 marks)
**Tag:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks, Part b]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of the Lead Auditor:**

The **Lead Auditor** is responsible for the overall management of the audit. Key responsibilities include:

1. **Audit Planning:** Prepares the audit plan in consultation with the audit client, defining objectives, scope, criteria, and resource requirements.

2. **Team Selection and Briefing:** Selects audit team members based on competence, and briefs them on the audit plan, their roles, and confidentiality.

3. **Communication:** Acts as the single point of contact between the audit team and the auditee. Resolves any disputes or conflicts during the audit.

4. **Managing the Audit:** Conducts the opening and closing meetings, leads on-site activities, ensures the audit stays on schedule, and assigns work to team members.

5. **Decision Making:** Reviews all findings for accuracy, objectivity, and consistency before presentation.

6. **Report Preparation:** Drafts, reviews, and approves the final audit report, ensuring it meets the audit client's requirements.

7. **Follow-Up:** Coordinates verification of corrective actions and decides whether NCs are effectively closed.

**Corrective Action Process:**

A nonconformity is closed through the **PDCA cycle**:

**1. Identify the NC** (audit finding)
**2. Containment** (prevent recurrence or further impact)
**3. Root Cause Analysis** (using 5-Why, Ishikawa/Fishbone, or FMEA)
**4. Plan Corrective Action** (define what, who, when)
**5. Implement the Action** (Do)
**6. Verify Effectiveness** (Check — measure results)
**7. Standardize** (Act — update procedures, train staff)
**8. Close the NC** (formally document closure)

**Effectiveness Verification Equation:**

$$
\text{Effectiveness} = \frac{\text{Verified Closed NCs}}{\text{Total NCs Raised}} \times 100\%
$$

If ≥ 95%, the audit is **closed**. Otherwise, a follow-up audit is triggered.

*(Flowchart similar to Section 4.4 should be drawn for additional marks.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Audit" with "Inspection":** Students often write that auditors *inspect products*. **No.** Auditors audit the *system* that produces the product. Inspections are product checks; audits are system checks. (Loses 1–2 marks)
>
> 2. **Mishandling the Three-Party Classification:** Some students write "internal" and "external" only, missing **second-party** (supplier) audits. (Loses 1 mark)
>
> 3. **Omitting the 7 Principles of ISO 19011:** Examiners often give 1–2 marks specifically for this list. Students remember only 2–3 principles.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Opening/Closing Meetings:** In the audit procedure, omitting these critical events is treated as an incomplete answer.
>
> 5. **Major vs Minor NC Confusion:** A single isolated slip is **Minor NC**, NOT Major. Major NCs are systemic failures.
>
> 6. **No Diagram / Flowchart:** KTU examiners reward diagrams generously (1–2 bonus marks for clear, labeled flowcharts).
>
> 7. **Confusing CAPA:** *Corrective Action* addresses the **root cause**; *Preventive Action* (in older ISO) addressed **potential** nonconformities. ISO 9001:2015 merged these into a single "risk-based thinking" clause.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quality Audits**

- **Definition:** Systematic, independent, documented process comparing evidence vs. criteria (ISO 19011:2018).
- **Three-Party Classification:**
  - **First-Party** = Internal/Self-audit
  - **Second-Party** = Customer audits supplier
  - **Third-Party** = Independent Certification Body (CB)
- **Scope Types:** System, Process, Product, Compliance audits.
- **Nature Types:** Vertical, Horizontal, Performance.
- **7 Principles of Auditing (ISO 19011):** Integrity, Fair Presentation, Due Professional Care, Confidentiality, Independence, Evidence-Based, Risk-Based.
- **6-Phase Audit Process:** Initiate → Prepare → Conduct On-Site → Report → Complete → Follow-Up.
- **Audit Plan Includes:** Objectives, scope, criteria, team, dates, locations, confidentiality, reporting.
- **NC Severity:** Major (systemic) > Minor (isolated) > Observation > OFI.
- **Root Cause Tools:** 5-Why Analysis, Ishikawa Fishbone Diagram, FMEA.
- **Corrective Action Cycle:** Follows **PDCA** (Plan-Do-Check-Act).
- **Lead Auditor's Role:** Plans, manages, reports, and follows up on the audit.
- **Key Standards:** ISO 9001:2015 (QMS), ISO 19011:2018 (Audit Guidelines), IATF 16949 (Automotive), AS9100 (Aerospace), ISO 13485 (Medical).
- **Statistical Sampling Formula for Audits:**
$$
n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p(1-p)}{e^2}
$$

- **Golden Rule:** *"Audits are not about finding fault; they are about finding facts."* — Every finding must be backed by **objective evidence**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Quality Audits (Module 4)**
> *PCPET503 — Mechanical Measurements and Quality Control | KTU 2024 Scheme*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality by Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality by Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality by Design (QbD)** is a **systematic, science-based, and risk-driven approach** to product and process development that begins with **predefined objectives** (the Quality Target Product Profile), emphasizes **product and process understanding**, and establishes a robust **Control Strategy** within a defined **Design Space** to consistently deliver the intended product quality.

According to the **ICH Q8(R2)** pharmaceutical guideline (adopted globally as the gold standard for QbD):

> *"A systematic approach to development that begins with predefined objectives and emphasizes product and process understanding and process control, based on sound science and quality risk management."*

### Expanding the KTU-Mandated Definition:

The term breaks down into three operative words:

- **Quality** — The inherent suitability of a product to satisfy a customer's stated or implied needs. (KTU accepts the ISO 9000 / Juran / ICH definition.)
- **By** — Achieved *through*, not *inspected-in after*.
- **Design** — A *planned*, *documented*, *scientific* approach to engineering a product and its manufacturing process from the very first conceptual sketch.

Thus, QbD shifts quality assurance **upstream** — from end-of-line inspection to the **drawing board and process design stage**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "House Construction" Analogy** 🏠
> Imagine you are commissioning a house. **Traditional Approach** = Build the entire house first, then hire an inspector to bang on walls with a hammer and reject the "defective ones." Costly, wasteful, and embarrassing.
> **Quality by Design** = Before laying a single brick, you decide: "The house must withstand 200 km/h winds, remain at 22 °C, use sustainable materials." You then design the foundation depth, beam strength, and window orientation accordingly. If during construction the engineer sees cloud cover changing, they can adjust cement curing time *within* an approved range without asking the "regulatory inspector" — because the design space is pre-approved.

This is precisely what QbD does in manufacturing — it **pre-approves a flexible operating region** (the *Design Space*) so that engineers can adapt in real-time, without regulatory re-approval.

## 1.3 Origin and Historical Context

| Year | Milestone | Significance for KTU |
|------|-----------|----------------------|
| **2004** | FDA launches "Pharmaceutical cGMPs for the 21st Century — A Risk-Based Approach" | Birth of QbD as a regulator-driven initiative |
| **2005** | ICH Q8 issued (first time "Quality by Design" coined) | Defines pharmaceutical QbD |
| **2006** | ICH Q9 (Quality Risk Management) | Adds FMEA, HACCP, fault trees |
| **2008** | ICH Q10 (Pharmaceutical Quality System) | Closes loop with knowledge management |
| **2009** | ICH Q8(R2) revision | Adds Design Space, Real-Time Release |
| **2010+** | QbD adopted in automotive, semiconductor, food (ISO 22000), medical devices | KTU extends QbD to **mechanical / manufacturing engineering** |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Mechanical Engineering Context**: While QbD originated in the pharmaceutical sector, KTU's *Mechanical Measurements and Quality Control* course extends the philosophy to **manufacturing process design, tolerance stack-up, GD&T, Six Sigma design phases, and robust engineering**. The principles — predefined objectives, risk analysis, design space, control strategy — are identical.

## 1.4 Why QbD? The Problem with Traditional Quality

> [!WARNING]
> The traditional **"Quality by Inspection (QbI)"** or **"Quality by Testing (QbT)"** paradigm suffers from three critical flaws that KTU examiners expect you to list:
> 1. **Reactive** — Defects are detected *after* they occur.
> 2. **Costly** — Inspection adds 5–30 % to total manufacturing cost without adding value.
> 3. **Inconsistent** — Sample-based testing statistically *accepts* some defective units (Producer's Risk α, Consumer's Risk β).

QbD eliminates these by building quality **into the design**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Quality by Design

The ICH Q8(R2) framework — universally accepted as the QbD template — is built on **five structural pillars**. KTU 2024 examiners routinely ask students to *list and explain* these.

### Pillar 1 — Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QTPP** is a prospective summary of the **quality characteristics** of a drug (or product) that ideally will be achieved to ensure the desired quality, taking into account safety and efficacy.

For a mechanical component, the QTPP translates into a **Design Brief**:

- Functional requirements (load, speed, life)
- Regulatory requirements (BIS, ISO, ASTM standards)
- Performance requirements (tolerance, surface finish, fatigue life)

**Example (KTU-style):**
> *"QTPP for an automotive brake disc: Hardness 180–220 BHN, Run-out ≤ 0.05 mm, Rₐ ≤ 1.6 μm, fatigue life ≥ 10⁶ cycles under 1.2 × rated load."*

### Pillar 2 — Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs)

CQAs are the **measurable physical, chemical, biological, or microbiological properties** that must remain within approved limits to ensure product quality.

**Mathematical Statement:**
$$
CQA_i \in [LCL_i, \; UCL_i]
$$

where LCL = Lower Control Limit and UCL = Upper Control Limit.

**Mechanical Example CQA set:**
- Dimensional accuracy (mm)
- Surface roughness Rₐ (μm)
- Hardness (BHN / HRC / HV)
- Tensile strength (MPa)
- Fatigue limit (cycles)

### Pillar 3 — Critical Process Parameters (CPPs) & Critical Material Attributes (CMAs)

| Parameter Type | Definition | Mechanical Example |
|----------------|------------|---------------------|
| **CPP** | Process variables whose variability impacts CQAs | Cutting speed (V), feed (f), depth of cut (d), forging temperature, cooling rate |
| **CMA** | Material input variables whose variability impacts CQAs | Chemical composition (%C, %Cr, %Ni), grain size, inclusion content |

### Pillar 4 — Design Space (DS) ⭐

> [!NOTE]
> The **Design Space** is the **multidimensional combination and interaction of input variables (CMAs and CPPs) that has been demonstrated to provide assurance of quality**. Movement *within* the DS is **not considered a change** by regulatory authorities.

Mathematically, it is a **feasible region in N-dimensional input space**:
$$
DS = \{ \mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n \; : \; CQA_i(\mathbf{x}) \in [LCL_i, UCL_i] \;\; \forall i \}
$$

where $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ represents the vector of CPPs and CMAs.

- **Edges of the Design Space** are NOT regulatory boundaries; they are **knowledge boundaries** proven by experimentation (DoE).
- Working *within* DS = regulatory flexibility (a huge benefit).

### Pillar 5 — Control Strategy (CS)

A planned set of **controls, derived from current product and process understanding**, that assures process performance and product quality.

$$
CS = f(QTPP, CQA, CPP, CMA, DS, Risk\;Analysis)
$$

Components include:
- Input material controls (incoming inspection)
- Process controls (SPC, real-time monitoring)
- Output controls (end-of-line testing)
- Lifecycle management (CAPA, change control)

## 2.2 Risk Assessment Tools in QbD

| Tool | Full Form | When Used | KTU Exam Favourite? |
|------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| **FMEA** | Failure Mode and Effects Analysis | Component / process level | ✅ Very common |
| **HACCP** | Hazard Analysis & Critical Control Points | Food, pharma | ✅ Common |
| **FTA** | Fault Tree Analysis | Complex system failure | Moderate |
| **Pareto Analysis** | 80/20 rule | Prioritization | Often |
| **Ishikawa (Fishbone)** | Cause-and-Effect | Root cause | Often |

### Risk Priority Number (RPN) — FMEA's Key Metric

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where:
- $S$ = **Severity** (1–10): How serious is the failure?
- $O$ = **Occurrence** (1–10): How frequently does it occur?
- $D$ = **Detection** (1–10): How easily can it be detected?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **higher RPN** indicates **higher priority for mitigation**. Threshold values (e.g., RPN > 100) trigger mandatory corrective action in most QbD implementations.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following cheat-sheet compiles every equation KTU 2024 examiners can ask for on the QbD topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning / Application |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $CQA_i \in [LCL_i,\; UCL_i]$ | CQA specification range |
| 2 | $DS = \{ \mathbf{x} : f(\mathbf{x}) \in [\mathbf{LCL},\mathbf{UCL}] \}$ | Design Space definition |
| 3 | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number (FMEA) |
| 4 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process capability index |
| 5 | $P_d = 1 - (1-p)^n$ | Probability of detection after n samples |
| 6 | $\sigma^2_{total} = \sigma^2_{matl} + \sigma^2_{proc} + \sigma^2_{meas}$ | Total variance decomposition |
| 7 | $Yield = \prod_{i=1}^{k}(1-p_i)$ | Rolled throughput yield across k stages |
| 8 | $DOE: \mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{X}\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ | Linear model for Design of Experiments |

**Units commonly used in mechanical QbD:**
- Hardness: **BHN**, **HRC**, **HV**
- Surface roughness: **μm** (Rₐ, R_z)
- Tolerance: **mm**, **μm**
- Capability indices $C_p$, $C_{pk}$: **dimensionless**
- Fatigue life: **cycles (N)**
- Stress: **MPa**

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of QbD in Mechanical Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners award marks when students *apply* QbD to real engineering scenarios, not merely define it. Memorize these applications:

1. **Automotive engine block casting** — QTPP includes dimensional tolerance, porosity, hardness. Design Space defined by cooling rate, pouring temperature, inoculant %.
2. **CNC turned shaft** — CPPs: spindle speed, feed, depth of cut. CQAs: diameter, roundness, surface roughness.
3. **Welding** — Design Space for voltage, current, travel speed to achieve required weld bead profile.
4. **Heat treatment** — Design Space defined by austenitizing temperature, soaking time, quenching medium.
5. **Sheet metal forming** — Blank holder force, lubrication, draw die radius define the Design Space for defect-free drawing.

## 2.5 Difference Table: QbD vs Traditional (QbT) Approach

| Aspect | Traditional (QbT) | QbD |
|--------|-------------------|-----|
| Approach | Reactive | Proactive |
| Quality ensured by | Inspection / testing | Design |
| Regulatory flexibility | Low — every change needs approval | High — movement within DS allowed |
| Process knowledge | Empirical / limited | Deep / scientific |
| Risk management | Implicit / informal | Formal (FMEA, HACCP) |
| Variability | Fought against | Understood and controlled |
| Cost of poor quality (COPQ) | High (scrap, rework, warranty) | Low (designed-out) |
| Patient / customer safety | By testing samples | By design assurance |
| Continuous improvement | After-the-fact | Built-in (continuous verification) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: From Tolerance Stack-Up to Design Space

> [!NOTE]
> A favourite 14-mark KTU question: *"Derive how a Design Space is mathematically constructed from process capability constraints."*

### Step 1 — Define the Functional Relationship

Suppose the CQA of a turned shaft is its diameter $D$. Through regression or physical modelling, $D$ is related to two critical process parameters (CPPs): feed rate $f$ and tool wear $w$:

$$
D = \beta_0 + \beta_1 f + \beta_2 w + \beta_{12} f \cdot w + \varepsilon
$$

where $\beta_0, \beta_1, \beta_2, \beta_{12}$ are regression coefficients and $\varepsilon$ is experimental error.

### Step 2 — Define the Specification Limits

Customer (KTU exam question stem) specifies:

$$
D_{LSL} \leq D \leq D_{USL}
$$

where LSL = Lower Specification Limit (e.g., 19.95 mm) and USL = Upper Specification Limit (e.g., 20.05 mm) for a nominal Ø20 mm shaft.

### Step 3 — Substitute the CQA Model

$$
D_{LSL} \leq \beta_0 + \beta_1 f + \beta_2 w + \beta_{12} f \cdot w \leq D_{USL}
$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange into Two Inequalities

$$
\beta_0 + \beta_1 f + \beta_2 w + \beta_{12} f \cdot w - D_{USL} \leq 0
$$

$$
D_{LSL} - \beta_0 - \beta_1 f - \beta_2 w - \beta_{12} f \cdot w \leq 0
$$

### Step 5 — The Design Space is the Feasible Region

The Design Space is the **set of all (f, w) pairs satisfying both inequalities**:

$$
DS = \left\{ (f, w) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \;\middle|\; D_{LSL} \leq \beta_0 + \beta_1 f + \beta_2 w + \beta_{12} f \cdot w \leq D_{USL} \right\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: The DS is a **band-shaped region** in (f, w) space. Any point inside it guarantees the CQA specification is met. This is the *geometric* picture of QbD.

## 3.2 Derivation: Process Capability Index $C_{pk}$ in QbD

> [!NOTE]
> KTU repeatedly asks: *"Explain how $C_{pk}$ validates the Design Space."*

### Step 1 — Definition

$$
C_{pk} = \min(C_{pU}, \; C_{pL})
$$

### Step 2 — Expand Each Term

Upper capability:
$$
C_{pU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}
$$

Lower capability:
$$
C_{pL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}
$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example (worked for 7 marks)

Given:
- Specification: $LSL = 19.95$ mm, $USL = 20.05$ mm
- Process mean: $\mu = 20.00$ mm
- Standard deviation: $\sigma = 0.012$ mm

**Calculation:**

$$
C_{pU} = \frac{20.05 - 20.00}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.05}{0.036} \approx 1.389
$$

$$
C_{pL} = \frac{20.00 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.05}{0.036} \approx 1.389
$$

$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.389,\; 1.389) = 1.389
$$

### Step 4 — QbD Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ is the **industry-accepted threshold** indicating a "capable" process. QbD demands **$C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for all CQAs across the entire Design Space** — not just at the centre point. This is a fundamental departure from traditional QbT, where capability was checked only at the operating point.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping QTPP → CQA → CPP/CMA → DS → CS

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most important table** for a 14-mark KTU question. Reproduce from memory.

| Stage | Element | Question Answered | Output Document | Mechanical Example |
|-------|---------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **QTPP** | "What does the customer need?" | Target Product Profile Document | Brake disc: hard, wear-resistant, dimensionally accurate |
| 2 | **CQA** | "Which measurable properties ensure the QTPP?" | CQA List (ranked) | Hardness, run-out, surface finish |
| 3 | **Risk Assessment** | "Which CQAs are most critical?" | FMEA report (RPN) | Run-out failure (RPN = 180) → high priority |
| 4 | **CMA / CPP** | "Which inputs affect the critical CQAs?" | DoE plan, Ishikawa | Casting temperature, mould sand type |
| 5 | **Design Space** | "What combinations of CPPs/CMAs guarantee quality?" | Response surface, contour plots | T = 1450–1500 °C, cooling = 30–60 min |
| 6 | **Control Strategy** | "How do we keep operating inside DS?" | Control plan, SPC charts | Real-time thermocouple monitoring, alarms |
| 7 | **Lifecycle Mgmt** | "How do we improve continuously?" | CAPA, knowledge management | Annual DoE re-validation |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step QbD Implementation Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently frames a 7-mark sub-question: *"Explain the step-by-step implementation of QbD."*

**Step 1** — Define the QTPP through customer needs and regulatory requirements.

**Step 2** — Identify potential CQAs from the QTPP through prior knowledge and risk analysis.

**Step 3** — Conduct initial risk assessment (FMEA) to rank CQAs and identify critical ones.

**Step 4** — Identify CMAs and CPPs that influence critical CQAs using Ishikawa diagrams.

**Step 5** — Develop a **Design of Experiments (DoE)** to study the CPP–CQA relationship scientifically.

**Step 6** — Analyse DoE data using ANOVA and regression to construct the **Design Space**.

**Step 7** — Establish the **Control Strategy**: real-time monitoring, SPC, PAT (Process Analytical Technology).

**Step 8** — Validate the entire system through pilot and scale-up batches.

**Step 9** — Implement **Continued Verification / Process Performance Monitoring** throughout the product lifecycle.

**Step 10** — Update the design space and control strategy through the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle.

## 3.5 Mathematical Statement of Total Variability

In QbD, total observed variability in a CQA is decomposed into orthogonal contributions:

$$
\sigma^2_{total} = \sigma^2_{CMA} + \sigma^2_{CPP} + \sigma^2_{interaction} + \sigma^2_{measurement} + \sigma^2_{unknown}
$$

**Engineering significance:** QbD explicitly requires the manufacturer to **identify, quantify, and minimize each term**. This is the *Six Sigma* philosophy expressed mathematically — drive $\sigma_{total} \to 0$ so that $C_{pk} \to \infty$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The ICH Q8(R2) QbD Framework — Master Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Customer Needs and Regulatory Requirements"] --> node2["Define QTPP Quality Target Product Profile"]
    node2 --> node3["Identify Potential CQAs Critical Quality Attributes"]
    node3 --> node4["Initial Risk Assessment FMEA / HACCP"]
    node4 --> node5["Identify CPPs and CMAs via Ishikawa"]
    node5 --> node6["Design of Experiments DoE"]
    node6 --> node7["Response Surface and Design Space Construction"]
    node7 --> node8["Define Control Strategy SPC and PAT"]
    node8 --> node9["Pilot Scale Validation"]
    node9 --> node10["Commercial Manufacturing within DS"]
    node10 --> node11["Continued Process Verification CPV"]
    node11 --> node12["Knowledge Management and CAPA"]
    node12 -.feedback.-> node5
    node12 -.feedback.-> node7

    classDef stepBox fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
    classDef entryBox fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loopBox fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000

    class node1,node2 entryBox
    class node3,node4,node5,node6,node7,node8,node9,node10,node11 stepBox
    class node12 loopBox
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The blue boxes are the **linear QbD flow** (one-time development). The green box represents the **continuous improvement loop** that *feeds back* into process understanding — this is what distinguishes QbD from a static development plan.

## 4.2 Design Space — Geometric Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["CPP1 Feed Rate f mm per rev"] --> nodeDS["Design Space Multidimensional Feasible Region"]
    nodeB["CPP2 Cutting Speed V m per min"] --> nodeDS
    nodeC["CPP3 Depth of Cut d mm"] --> nodeDS
    nodeD["CMA1 Workpiece Hardness BHN"] --> nodeDS
    nodeE["CMA2 Tool Material Grade"] --> nodeDS
    nodeDS --> nodeF["CQA Diameter D within LSL to USL"]
    nodeDS --> nodeG["CQA Surface Roughness Ra within limits"]
    nodeDS --> nodeH["CQA Roundness within limits"]
    nodeDS -.any point inside.-> nodeI["PRODUCT QUALITY GUARANTEED"]
    nodeDS -.point outside.-> nodeJ["QUALITY FAILURE Investigation Required"]

    classDef inputBox fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000
    classDef dsBox fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputBox fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef failBox fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeC,nodeD,nodeE inputBox
    class nodeDS dsBox
    class nodeF,nodeG,nodeH,nodeI outputBox
    class nodeJ failBox
```

## 4.3 FMEA Risk Grid (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeF1["List all Failure Modes"] --> nodeF2["Assign Severity S"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeF3["Assign Occurrence O"]
    nodeF3 --> nodeF4["Assign Detection D"]
    nodeF4 --> nodeF5["Compute RPN equals S times O times D"]
    nodeF5 --> nodeF6{"RPN Greater Than 100"}
    nodeF6 -- Yes --> nodeF7["HIGH RISK Implement Corrective Action"]
    nodeF6 -- No --> nodeF8{"RPN Between 50 and 100"}
    nodeF8 -- Yes --> nodeF9["MEDIUM RISK Monitor and Reduce"]
    nodeF8 -- No --> nodeF10["LOW RISK Accept and Document"]

    classDef procBox fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef critBox fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef medBox fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef lowBox fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    class nodeF1,nodeF2,nodeF3,nodeF4,nodeF5 procBox
    class nodeF6 critBox
    class nodeF7,nodeF8,nodeF9 medBox
    class nodeF10 lowBox
```

## 4.4 QbD vs Traditional — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TRAD["TRADITIONAL APPROACH QbT"]
        nodeT1["Make Product"] --> nodeT2["Test Product"]
        nodeT2 --> nodeT3{"Pass or Fail"}
        nodeT3 -- Pass --> nodeT4["Release"]
        nodeT3 -- Fail --> nodeT5["Reject or Rework"]
    end

    subgraph QBD["QUALITY BY DESIGN QbD"]
        nodeQ1["Predefined QTPP"] --> nodeQ2["Risk Based Design"]
        nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3["DoE to Build DS"]
        nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4["Control Strategy"]
        nodeQ4 --> nodeQ5["Continuous Verification"]
        nodeQ5 -.knowledge.-> nodeQ2
    end

    classDef tradBox fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef qbdBox fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000

    class nodeT1,nodeT2,nodeT3,nodeT4,nodeT5 tradBox
    class nodeQ1,nodeQ2,nodeQ3,nodeQ4,nodeQ5 qbdBox
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question below is a **2–3 sentence model answer** that fetches full marks under KTU valuation.

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Quality by Design (QbD). Mention any two elements of QbD.**

**Model Answer:**
Quality by Design (QbD) is a systematic, science- and risk-based approach to product and process development that begins with predefined objectives and emphasizes product/process understanding and control, based on sound science and quality risk management (as per ICH Q8).

**Two elements:**
1. **Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP)** — a prospective summary of quality characteristics intended for the product.
2. **Design Space** — the multidimensional combination of CPPs and CMAs that assures quality.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the Risk Priority Number (RPN) in FMEA? State its significance in QbD.**

**Model Answer:**
RPN is the product of three ratings (each on a 1–10 scale):

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

**Significance in QbD:** It quantifies and prioritizes process risks so that high-RPN failure modes can be addressed first through design changes or additional controls, thereby reducing patient/customer risk and supporting the QbD principle of *building quality into design*.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **answer ANY ONE** full question from the Part B module. Both alternatives provided below carry equal marks and are equally weighted.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A — 14 Marks — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **five key elements** of Quality by Design as per ICH Q8(R2) framework. Illustrate each with a mechanical engineering example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The five key elements of QbD are:

**1. Quality Target Product Profile (QTPP):**
A prospective summary of the quality characteristics that the product must possess to ensure its intended performance, safety, and efficacy. *Mechanical example:* For an automotive connecting rod, the QTPP includes fatigue strength ≥ 550 MPa, hardness 28–32 HRC, dimensional tolerance ± 0.02 mm, and weight ≤ 250 g.

**2. Critical Quality Attributes (CQAs):**
Measurable physical/chemical properties that must remain within specification limits. *Mechanical example:* Hardness, surface roughness (Rₐ ≤ 0.8 μm), and dimensional accuracy of the rod's big-end bore.

**3. Critical Process Parameters (CPPs):**
Process variables whose variability directly impacts CQAs. *Mechanical example:* Forging temperature (1100–1200 °C), quenching medium (oil), and tempering time (90 min).

**4. Design Space:**
The multidimensional region of CPPs/CMAs that assures quality. *Mechanical example:* The acceptable range of (austenitizing temperature × soaking time × quenching medium temperature) combinations that yield the desired hardness.

**5. Control Strategy:**
A planned set of controls ensuring the process operates within the Design Space. *Mechanical example:* Real-time furnace thermocouple monitoring with automatic alarms; SPC charts on hardness sampled every hour.

**(b)** A turned shaft has specification $D_{LSL} = 19.95$ mm and $D_{USL} = 20.05$ mm. The process mean is $\mu = 20.00$ mm with $\sigma = 0.015$ mm. **(i)** Calculate $C_{pk}$. **(ii)** Comment on whether the process satisfies QbD capability requirements. **(iii)** What corrective action is needed if $C_{pk} = 0.8$? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Calculate $C_{pk}$:**

Upper capability:
$$
C_{pU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{20.05 - 20.00}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.05}{0.045} \approx 1.111
$$

Lower capability:
$$
C_{pL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{20.00 - 19.95}{3 \times 0.015} = \frac{0.05}{0.045} \approx 1.111
$$

Since the process is centred:
$$
C_{pk} = \min(1.111,\; 1.111) = 1.111
$$

**(ii) QbD Compliance Comment:**

The QbD threshold is $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. The current $C_{pk} = 1.111 < 1.33$, so the process **does not satisfy QbD capability requirements**. The process is "marginally capable" but not robust enough to handle normal variation without producing occasional out-of-specification parts.

**(iii) Corrective Action if $C_{pk} = 0.8$:**

A $C_{pk} = 0.8$ indicates an **incapable process** producing ~6.7 % defects (≈ 67,000 PPM). Corrective actions:
1. **Variability reduction** — improve machine rigidity, replace worn tooling, refine feed/optimize speed.
2. **Centring** — re-calibrate machine to bring mean closer to nominal (20.00 mm).
3. **Re-engineer Design Space** using DoE to identify settings that achieve $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$.
4. **Add real-time monitoring** (in-process gauging) with automatic feedback control.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — 14 Marks — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **step-by-step implementation of QbD** in a manufacturing process. Use a CNC turning operation as your example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the QTPP for the turned shaft:**
Customer requires: diameter Ø20 ± 0.05 mm, surface roughness Rₐ ≤ 1.6 μm, roundness ≤ 0.02 mm, batch size 10,000 units/month.

**Step 2 — Identify potential CQAs:**
Diameter, roundness, surface roughness, hardness.

**Step 3 — Conduct initial FMEA:**
RPN for *diameter variation* = S(8) × O(5) × D(4) = 160 (highest risk → critical).

**Step 4 — Identify CPPs and CMAs via Ishikawa:**
CPPs: spindle speed (V), feed rate (f), depth of cut (d), tool wear.
CMAs: workpiece material grade, tool insert grade, cutting fluid type.

**Step 5 — Develop DoE plan:**
Use a 3-level Box-Behnken Design (V × f × d) with 15 experimental runs.

**Step 6 — Analyse DoE and build Design Space:**
Apply regression and ANOVA. Identify the (V, f, d) region satisfying all CQAs. Result: Design Space is approximately V ∈ [120, 180 m/min], f ∈ [0.1, 0.2 mm/rev], d ∈ [0.3, 0.6 mm].

**Step 7 — Establish Control Strategy:**
In-process diameter gauge every 10 parts, SPC X̄-R chart, tool wear compensation loop, alarm if any point exits DS.

**Step 8 — Pilot and scale-up validation:**
Run 50 batches; verify $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ across the full DS.

**Step 9 — Continued Process Verification:**
Quarterly capability re-assessment; update DS if raw material supplier changes.

**Step 10 — Knowledge management:**
Document all DoE data, FMEA, and control strategy for future product transfers.

**(b)** With respect to a forging process, list **five potential failure modes** in FMEA format. For each, assign realistic S, O, D values and compute the RPN. Suggest a control action for the highest-RPN failure. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Failure Mode | Effect | S | O | D | RPN | Control Action |
|---|--------------|--------|---|---|---|-----|----------------|
| 1 | Improper forging temperature (low) | Poor die filling, low strength | 8 | 5 | 4 | 160 | Install IR pyrometer with interlock |
| 2 | Excessive die wear | Dimensional out-of-spec, surface defects | 7 | 6 | 5 | 210 | Scheduled die-life tracking; SPC on dimensions |
| 3 | Inadequate lubrication | Die scoring, high pickup | 6 | 5 | 5 | 150 | Auto-lubricant dispenser; operator checklist |
| 4 | Improper billet heating time | Non-uniform temperature, cracks | 8 | 4 | 6 | 192 | Furnace thermocouple log every batch |
| 5 | Scale formation | Surface inclusions, fatigue failure | 9 | 3 | 7 | 189 | Inert atmosphere / descaling pre-forge |

**Highest RPN: Failure Mode #2 — Excessive die wear (RPN = 210).**

**Recommended control action:**
- Implement **predictive die-life tracking** based on stroke count.
- Apply **SPC X̄-R charts** on critical dimensions after forging.
- Replace die at fixed intervals regardless of visual appearance (preventive policy).
- Use **hard-facing** or **PVD-coated dies** to extend die life.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls** — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:
> 1. **Confusing QTPP with CQA** — QTPP is *what customer wants*; CQA is *the measurable property that ensures it*. This is the most common 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Defining Design Space as a *point*** — it is a *multidimensional region*, not a single operating point. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in $C_{pk}$ calculations — final answer may be correct but examiners *will* deduct if $\sigma$ is in mm and tolerances are also mm without explicit mention.
> 4. **Skipping FMEA step** when describing QbD — FMEA/Risk Assessment is mandatory; an answer without it loses at least 1 mark in any 7-mark sub-question.
> 5. **Not linking QbD back to continuous improvement** — the *feedback loop* (knowledge management, CAPA, CPV) is a KTU-mandated closure. Omitting it = losing 1–2 marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📌

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for QbD (Module 4)**

- ✅ **Full form**: Quality by Design (QbD), defined by **ICH Q8(R2)** as a systematic, science- and risk-based approach.
- ✅ **Five pillars (in order)**: QTPP → CQA → CPP/CMA → Design Space → Control Strategy. Memorize this exact sequence.
- ✅ **Design Space** is a *multidimensional region* (not a point); movement within it is **not a regulatory change**.
- ✅ **RPN** = $S \times O \times D$ (each 1–10). RPN > 100 is the typical action threshold.
- ✅ **Process capability** for QbD: $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ across the **entire Design Space**, not just the centre point.
- ✅ **Total variance** decomposition: $\sigma^2_{total} = \sigma^2_{CMA} + \sigma^2_{CPP} + \sigma^2_{interaction} + \sigma^2_{meas} + \sigma^2_{unknown}$.
- ✅ **Origin**: 2004 FDA + 2005/2008 ICH Q8/Q9/Q10. Originally pharma; now extended to mechanical manufacturing.
- ✅ **QbD vs QbT**: Proactive vs Reactive; designed-in vs inspected-in; high vs low regulatory flexibility.
- ✅ **DoE** is the *scientific engine* of QbD — produces the data that defines the Design Space.
- ✅ **Continuous verification** (Plan-Do-Check-Act) closes the QbD loop — without it, QbD degenerates into one-time project work.
- ✅ **Tools**: FMEA, HACCP, FTA, Ishikawa, Pareto, SPC, PAT — match the tool to the layer (component vs process vs system).
- ✅ **Mechanical examples to recall**: CNC turning, forging, heat treatment, casting, welding, sheet-metal forming.
- ✅ **Common KTU traps**: confusing QTPP/CQA; treating Design Space as a point; missing FMEA step; forgetting the feedback loop.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Culture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Culture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Culture (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> **Quality Culture** is a *value-driven, organization-wide system* of shared beliefs, attitudes, norms, behaviors, and management practices that collectively shape how every stakeholder — from top management to shop-floor operators — perceives, commits to, and executes quality in all products, processes, and services. It is the *intangible psychological and sociological foundation* upon which all measurable Quality Management Systems (QMS) like ISO 9001, TQM, Six Sigma, and 5S are built and sustained.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Quality Culture is positioned as the **fourth pillar** of modern quality management, sitting alongside:
1. Quality Control (QC) – detection-oriented
2. Quality Assurance (QA) – prevention-oriented
3. Quality Management (QM) – system-oriented
4. **Quality Culture (QCt)** – people-and-values-oriented

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Healthy Family" Model:**
> Think of a company as a *large joint family*. **Quality Culture is the family's value system** — the unspoken rules like "we don't lie," "we respect elders," "we help each other in studies." Just as a family with strong values automatically produces disciplined, successful children, a company with a strong Quality Culture automatically produces defect-free products, satisfied customers, and engaged employees.

If Quality Control is the **thermometer** (detects fever), and Quality Assurance is the **medicine** (prevents fever), then **Quality Culture is the lifestyle and immunity** of the family that makes fever rare in the first place. No medicine works if the family refuses to eat healthy food, sleep on time, and exercise — similarly, no QMS works without a strong Quality Culture.

## 1.3 Core Distinguishing Metrics

- **Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)** — typically maintained at **4% – 8%** of total sales revenue in culturally mature firms.
- **First Pass Yield (FPY)** — culturally strong organizations target **≥ 95%**.
- **Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO)** — Six Sigma cultures target **≤ 3.4 DPMO**.
- **Employee Suggestion Rate** — benchmark of **≥ 5 suggestions/employee/year** in TQM-driven cultures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** For KTU examinations, the examiner expects you to (a) define Quality Culture, (b) list its elements, (c) differentiate it from QC/QA/QM, and (d) explain the steps to build a quality culture. These four sub-areas carry the **maximum weightage in Module 4**.

---

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Concept Map

| Term | Focus | Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| **Quality Control (QC)** | Product/Process output | *"Did we make it right?"* |
| **Quality Assurance (QA)** | Process discipline | *"Are we making it the right way?"* |
| **Quality Management (QM)** | System & documentation | *"Do we have a system to do it right always?"* |
| **Quality Culture (QCt)** | People & values | *"Does everyone WANT to do it right, even when no one is watching?"* |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Foundational Pillars of Quality Culture

A robust Quality Culture is built upon **four interdependent pillars**. Each pillar must be intentionally nurtured — a single weak pillar collapses the entire structure.

### Pillar 1 — Leadership Commitment & Vision
- The **single most critical pillar**. Studies (Deming's 14 Points, Juran's Trilogy) show that **> 80% of quality culture failures** originate from weak leadership commitment.
- Leaders must "walk the talk" — allocate budgets, attend training, and publicly reward quality-driven behavior.
- A vision statement must explicitly include quality as a **non-negotiable strategic pillar**, not a side activity.

### Pillar 2 — Employee Involvement & Empowerment
- Every employee, from the **CEO to the housekeeping staff**, is treated as a *quality stakeholder*.
- Implemented through **Quality Circles (QCs)**, **Kaizen Teams**, **Suggestion Schemes**, and **Autonomous Maintenance groups**.
- Empowered employees are given the **authority to stop the line** if they detect a quality risk (the *Jidoka* principle from Toyota).

### Pillar 3 — Continuous Improvement (CI) Mindset
- A belief that **every process can be improved every day** — there is no "perfect end-state."
- Tools: **PDCA Cycle (Plan-Do-Check-Act)**, **DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control)**, **Kaizen** (small incremental changes), **Poka-Yoke** (mistake-proofing).
- The Japanese term **"Kaizen"** literally means *change for the better* and is the cultural engine behind Toyota, Sony, and Komatsu.

### Pillar 4 — Customer Focus & Supplier Partnership
- **Voice of Customer (VoC)** drives every design and process decision.
- Internal customers (next process, next department) are treated with the same respect as external customers.
- Long-term supplier partnerships replace short-term transactional relationships, ensuring **incoming quality** is culturally aligned.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"L-E-C"** = **L**eadership, **E**mployee involvement, **C**ontinuous improvement + **C**ustomer focus.

---

## 2.2 The Deming Chain Reaction (Cultural Blueprint)

Dr. W. Edwards Deming proposed a *chain reaction* that mathematically and logically links Quality Culture to business survival. The chain is **culturally driven**, not procedurally driven.

$$\text{Improve Quality} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Decrease Costs} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Productivity Improves} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Market Share Grows} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stay in Business}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chain reaction works **only when the cultural foundation (people, values, leadership) supports it**. Without Quality Culture, improving quality often *increases* short-term costs — which is why many organizations abandon quality programs prematurely.

---

## 2.3 Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) — The Cultural Thermometer

Quality Culture can be **measured indirectly** by tracking the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)**, which is the total cost incurred because things are *not* done right the first time. It is the most commonly asked numerical concept in KTU 2024 quality modules.

### 2.3.1 The Four Sub-Categories of COPQ

| Category | Type | Examples | Cultural Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** | *Cost of failure before delivery* | Rework, scrap, re-inspection, failure analysis | High IFC = poor employee training & weak process discipline |
| **External Failure Costs (EFC)** | *Cost of failure after delivery* | Warranty claims, returns, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill | High EFC = weak customer focus pillar |
| **Appraisal Costs (AC)** | *Cost of detecting defects* | Inspection, testing, calibration, audits | High AC = over-reliance on detection, not prevention |
| **Prevention Costs (PC)** | *Cost of preventing defects* | Training, quality planning, process capability studies, supplier capability | High PC = strong Quality Culture |

### 2.3.2 Master Formula for COPQ

$$\text{COPQ} \;=\; \underbrace{(IFC + EFC)}_{\text{Failure Costs (FC)}} \;+\; AC \;+\; PC$$

And the **Failure Cost Ratio (FCR)** — a cultural maturity indicator — is:

$$\text{FCR} \;=\; \frac{IFC + EFC}{IFC + EFC + AC + PC} \;\times\; 100\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cultural Benchmark:** A mature Quality Culture shows **FCR ≤ 25%**. A reactive, inspection-driven organization shows **FCR ≥ 70%**.

### 2.3.3 Optimal Investment Ratio in Quality

Research by Juran and Gryna established the *rule of thumb* for a culturally healthy organization:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Prevention Costs} \;:\; \text{Appraisal Costs} \;:\; \text{Failure Costs} \;\approx\; 10\% \;:\; 25\% \;:\; 65\%\; \text{(reactive org)} \;\rightarrow\; 50\% \;:\; 35\% \;:\; 15\% \;\text{(cultural org)}\;}$$

A *shift in spending* from failure costs (right) to prevention costs (left) is the **single most visible signature** of a maturing Quality Culture.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cost of Poor Quality | $COPQ = IFC + EFC + AC + PC$ | Quantifies cultural waste |
| 2 | Failure Cost Ratio | $FCR = \dfrac{IFC + EFC}{COPQ} \times 100\%$ | Measures cultural maturity |
| 3 | Quality Cost as % of Sales | $QCR = \dfrac{COPQ}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100\%$ | Industry benchmark (~4–8% in mature firms) |
| 4 | First Pass Yield | $FPY = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first time}}{\text{Total units produced}} \times 100\%$ | Cultural process discipline metric |
| 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} FPY_i$ | Multi-stage cultural quality |
| 6 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Units} \times \text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Six Sigma cultural target ≤ 3.4 |
| 7 | Sigma Level | $\sigma = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{s} \quad \text{or} \quad \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{s}$ | Process capability culture |
| 8 | Employee Suggestion Index | $ESI = \dfrac{\text{Implemented suggestions}}{\text{Total employees}} \times 100$ | Employee involvement metric |
| 9 | Customer Satisfaction Score | $CSAT = \dfrac{\text{Satisfied customers}}{\text{Total respondents}} \times 100\%$ | Customer focus metric |
| 10 | Deming Chain Reaction | Quality $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Cost $\downarrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Productivity $\uparrow$ $\Rightarrow$ Market $\uparrow$ | Strategic cultural logic |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Automotive (Toyota Production System):** Quality Culture is the reason Toyota can run with **inventory days of supply < 2** and *still* deliver world-class quality. The cultural pillars of *Kaizen, Jidoka, and Genchi Genbutsu* are not written in manuals — they live in the employees' daily behavior.
2. **Aerospace (Boeing, Airbus):** A weak Quality Culture at Boeing (e.g., the 737 MAX crisis) directly translates to **massive EFC** — recalls, lawsuits, and brand erosion costing **billions of dollars**.
3. **Medical Devices (Medtronic, Philips):** Strong Quality Culture ensures **regulatory compliance (FDA 21 CFR Part 820)** is a *cultural habit*, not a paperwork burden.
4. **Indian Manufacturing (L&T, Tata Steel, Maruti):** Quality Culture initiatives like **Tata Business Excellence Model (TBEM)** and **Maruti's Suggestion Scheme** (3+ suggestions per employee annually) have demonstrably improved FPY and reduced COPQ.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Worked Numerical Examples

> [!NOTE]
> Quality Culture is a *management-and-behavior* topic, so this section does not contain algebraic derivations of physics laws. Instead, it provides **exhaustive analytical frameworks, worked numerical COPQ problems, and step-by-step implementation models** — exactly the type of structured analysis KTU examiners reward.

---

## 3.1 Framework I — The 10-Step Model for Building a Quality Culture

This is the **highest-yield framework** for KTU 14-mark essay questions. Each step must be remembered in order.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** **"A-L-E-C-T-P-S-M-R-F"** = Awareness, Leadership, Education, Communication, Training, Participation, Structure, Measurement, Recognition, Feedback.

### Step 1 — Top Management Commitment
- The CEO/Board must *publicly and financially* commit to quality as a **strategic survival issue**, not a slogan.
- A written **Quality Policy** signed by the CEO is published and translated into local languages.

### Step 2 — Awareness Campaign
- All employees are sensitized to the *cost of poor quality* through case studies of customer complaints, warranty data, and competitor failures.
- Posters, town halls, and plant-floor meetings communicate the *why* before the *what*.

### Step 3 — Quality Education
- Structured **training programs** at three levels: (a) basic quality awareness for all, (b) tools training (7 QC tools, 7 new tools) for engineers, (c) leadership training for managers.

### Step 4 — Communication Infrastructure
- Quality **bulletin boards**, daily-shift huddles, monthly quality review meetings, and digital dashboards.
- Communication is **two-way**: employees are *heard*, not just instructed.

### Step 5 — Quality Circles (QCs)
- Small groups of **6 – 12 employees** from the same work area meet *voluntarily* every week to identify and solve quality problems in their own domain.
- Each QC is led by a *facilitator* and presents solutions to management.

### Step 6 — Suggestion Scheme
- Formal mechanism where any employee can submit a written suggestion for improvement.
- **Implemented suggestions** are rewarded (cash, certificates, public recognition). Benchmark: **≥ 5 suggestions/employee/year** in Japanese firms.

### Step 7 — Recognition & Reward
- **Quality stars** are identified monthly/annually. Public ceremonies, bonuses, promotions, and even naming facilities after quality champions are common.
- Recognition must be **specific, timely, and sincere** — generic praise fails.

### Step 8 — Measurement & Feedback
- Culture is *tracked* using **FPY, COPQ, DPMO, ESI, CSAT** dashboards.
- Monthly Quality Council meetings review metrics, identify gaps, and re-allocate resources.

### Step 9 — Supplier Quality Culture Integration
- Suppliers are *audited, trained, and rewarded* for adopting the buyer's quality culture.
- Long-term partnerships replace transactional purchasing — this is *cultural extension upstream*.

### Step 10 — Continuous Reinforcement
- Culture is **never "achieved"**; it is *continuously nurtured*. New employee inductions include quality culture modules; senior leaders retake refresher courses annually.

---

## 3.2 Framework II — Ishikawa's Cultural Diagnosis (Fishbone Application)

To diagnose *why* a Quality Culture is failing, Dr. Kaoru Ishikawa's fishbone (cause-and-effect) diagram is applied specifically to cultural problems across **6M categories** (extended to 8M for culture):

| Category | Cultural Question | Typical Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Man (People)** | Do employees feel *empowered*? | Fear of blame, no stop-the-line authority |
| **Machine** | Do machines support quality? | Old, uncalibrated equipment, no TPM |
| **Material** | Is incoming material culturally aligned? | Suppliers not trained in buyer's quality standards |
| **Method** | Are processes *standardized and audited*? | No SOPs, undocumented tribal knowledge |
| **Measurement** | Are cultural metrics tracked? | Only output measured, not culture |
| **Mother Nature (Environment)** | Is the workplace physically conducive? | Unsafe, dirty, disorganized (5S failures) |
| **Management** | Do leaders *walk the talk*? | Slogans without budgets, no consequence for poor quality |
| **Money** | Is prevention spending adequate? | PC budget < 10% of COPQ |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — COPQ Calculation (KTU Typical Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Pattern):**
> A medium-scale manufacturing company produces **50,000 components per month**. The following monthly data is available:
> - Internal Failure Costs (Rework + Scrap) = **₹ 2,50,000**
> - External Failure Costs (Warranty + Returns) = **₹ 4,00,000**
> - Appraisal Costs (Inspection + Testing) = **₹ 1,50,000**
> - Prevention Costs (Training + Quality Planning) = **₹ 80,000**
> - Monthly Sales Revenue = **₹ 80,00,000**
>
> **Calculate:**
> (a) Total Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)
> (b) Failure Cost Ratio (FCR)
> (c) Quality Cost Ratio (QCR) as % of sales
> (d) Comment on the cultural maturity of the organization.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step (a) — Calculate COPQ**

$$COPQ \;=\; IFC + EFC + AC + PC$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= \text{₹ } 2{,}50{,}000 \;+\; \text{₹ } 4{,}00{,}000 \;+\; \text{₹ } 1{,}50{,}000 \;+\; \text{₹ } 80{,}000 \\
COPQ &= \text{₹ } 8{,}80{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (b) — Calculate Failure Cost Ratio (FCR)**

$$
FCR = \frac{IFC + EFC}{COPQ} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCR &= \frac{2{,}50{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000}{8{,}80{,}000} \times 100\% \\
FCR &= \frac{6{,}50{,}000}{8{,}80{,}000} \times 100\% \\
FCR &= 73.86\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (c) — Calculate Quality Cost Ratio (QCR)**

$$
QCR = \frac{COPQ}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
QCR &= \frac{8{,}80{,}000}{80{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
QCR &= 11.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (d) — Cultural Maturity Commentary**

> **Interpretation:**
> - **FCR = 73.86%** is far above the cultural maturity benchmark of **≤ 25%**. This indicates a **highly reactive, inspection-driven organization** with a weak Quality Culture.
> - **QCR = 11%** is also above the cultural benchmark of **4–8%**, confirming excessive waste.
> - **Prevention spending (₹ 80,000) is only ~9% of COPQ** — well below the 50% target for culturally mature firms.
> - **External Failure Costs (₹ 4,00,000) exceed Internal Failure Costs (₹ 2,50,000)** — a *red flag* indicating that defects are escaping to customers, eroding brand trust.
> - **Recommendation:** The firm must (i) **double its Prevention Cost budget** (training, process FMEA, supplier development), (ii) implement **Quality Circles** to capture shop-floor knowledge, and (iii) **empower employees to stop the line** to convert EFC into IFC (cheaper to catch internally).

---

## 3.4 Framework III — Cost-of-Culture Simulation (Before vs. After)

A common 14-mark question asks: *"If the company invests ₹ 2,00,000 in Quality Culture building (training, QCs, Kaizen), how will COPQ change?"* Below is the **analytical template** you should reproduce in the exam.

| Cost Category | *Before* Culture (₹) | *After* Culture (₹) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Prevention Cost (PC) | 80,000 | 2,80,000 | + ₹ 2,00,000 |
| Appraisal Cost (AC) | 1,50,000 | 1,30,000 | – ₹ 20,000 |
| Internal Failure Cost (IFC) | 2,50,000 | 1,20,000 | – ₹ 1,30,000 |
| External Failure Cost (EFC) | 4,00,000 | 80,000 | – ₹ 3,20,000 |
| **Total COPQ** | **8,80,000** | **6,10,000** | **– ₹ 2,70,000** |
| **Net Savings** | — | — | **₹ 2,70,000 – ₹ 2,00,000 = ₹ 70,000** |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always show the **net savings** calculation, because examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for the *cost-benefit* conclusion.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quality Culture vs. Other Quality Concepts

| Dimension | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) | Quality Management (QM) | **Quality Culture (QCt)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Era / Origin** | 1920s (Shewhart) | 1950s (Juran, Feigenbaum) | 1980s (ISO 9000) | **1990s–present (TQM, Six Sigma)** |
| **Focus** | Product inspection | Process discipline | System & documentation | **People, values, behavior** |
| **Approach** | Detective (find defects) | Preventive (stop defects) | Systemic (manage defects) | **Transformative (change mindset)** |
| **Driver** | Inspectors & gauges | Engineers & SOPs | Managers & systems | **Everyone, led by leadership** |
| **Measurement** | % Defective, AQL | Process capability (Cp, Cpk) | Audit scores, ISO certification | **FPY, COPQ, DPMO, ESI, CSAT** |
| **Failure Mode** | Inspection errors | Process non-conformance | Documentation gaps | **Cultural erosion, employee disengagement** |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | Low | Medium | High | **Highest in Module 4** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Four Pillars of Quality Culture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    QCt["QUALITY CULTURE"]
    P1["PILLAR 1: LEADERSHIP COMMITMENT"]
    P2["PILLAR 2: EMPLOYEE INVOLVEMENT"]
    P3["PILLAR 3: CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT"]
    P4["PILLAR 4: CUSTOMER FOCUS"]

    QCt --> P1
    QCt --> P2
    QCt --> P3
    QCt --> P4

    P1 --> P1a["Quality Policy signed by CEO"]
    P1 --> P1b["Budget for prevention activities"]
    P1 --> P1c["Walk the talk behaviour"]

    P2 --> P2a["Quality Circles 6 to 12 members"]
    P2 --> P2b["Suggestion Scheme minimum 5 per employee per year"]
    P2 --> P2c["Stop the line authority Jidoka"]

    P3 --> P3a["PDCA Cycle"]
    P3 --> P3b["Kaizen small daily improvements"]
    P3 --> P3c["DMAIC for Six Sigma projects"]

    P4 --> P4a["Voice of Customer VoC"]
    P4 --> P4b["Internal customer respect"]
    P4 --> P4c["Long term supplier partnerships"]

    OUT["OUTCOME: Reduced COPQ, Higher FPY, Sustainable Excellence"]
    P1 --> OUT
    P2 --> OUT
    P3 --> OUT
    P4 --> OUT
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — The Deming Chain Reaction (Cultural Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["IMPROVE QUALITY"] --> B["DECREASE COSTS"]
    B --> C["PRODUCTIVITY IMPROVES"]
    C --> D["MARKET SHARE GROWS"]
    D --> E["STAY IN BUSINESS"]
    E --> F["CREATE JOBS"]
    F --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrow from F back to A represents the **virtuous cycle** — sustained business success reinvests into culture, which in turn improves quality further.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — COPQ Cost Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    COPQ["COPQ: TOTAL COST OF POOR QUALITY"]
    COPQ --> FC["FAILURE COSTS FC"]
    COPQ --> AC["APPRAISAL COSTS AC"]
    COPQ --> PC["PREVENTION COSTS PC"]

    FC --> IFC["INTERNAL FAILURE COST: Rework, Scrap, Re-inspection"]
    FC --> EFC["EXTERNAL FAILURE COST: Warranty, Returns, Lawsuits, Lost Goodwill"]
    AC --> AC1["Inspection and Testing"]
    AC --> AC2["Calibration and Audits"]
    PC --> PC1["Training and Education"]
    PC --> PC2["Quality Planning and FMEA"]
    PC --> PC3["Supplier Capability Development"]
```

## 4.4 Process Flow — 10-Step Quality Culture Implementation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["STEP 1: Top Management Commitment"]
    S2["STEP 2: Awareness Campaign"]
    S3["STEP 3: Quality Education"]
    S4["STEP 4: Communication Infrastructure"]
    S5["STEP 5: Quality Circles Formation"]
    S6["STEP 6: Suggestion Scheme Launch"]
    S7["STEP 7: Recognition and Reward"]
    S8["STEP 8: Measurement and Feedback"]
    S9["STEP 9: Supplier Culture Integration"]
    S10["STEP 10: Continuous Reinforcement"]

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10
    S10 -.Reinforces.-> S1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted feedback arrow from Step 10 to Step 1 signifies that **culture-building is a closed-loop, never-ending cycle** — a critical KTU point worth 2–3 marks in descriptive questions.

## 4.5 Block Diagram — Cultural Maturity Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1["LEVEL 1: REACTIVE - Inspection driven, FCR above 70 percent, No PC budget"]
    L2["LEVEL 2: AWARENESS - QC tools introduced, FCR 50 to 70 percent"]
    L3["LEVEL 3: PREVENTIVE - QMS ISO 9001 deployed, FCR 25 to 50 percent"]
    L4["LEVEL 4: PROACTIVE - Quality Circles, Kaizen, FCR below 25 percent"]
    L5["LEVEL 5: CULTURAL - Six Sigma culture, DPMO below 3.4, FCR below 15 percent"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5
    L5 -.Benchmark.-> L4
```
