<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
> **Define the term "Quality Culture". List any four essential elements that characterize a strong quality culture in an organization.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Quality Culture is the *shared set of values, beliefs, attitudes, and behavioral patterns* of an organization that collectively determine its commitment to quality in all products, processes, and services. It is the *intangible, people-centric foundation* on which formal Quality Management Systems are built.
>
> **Any Four Elements (½ Mark Each):**
> 1. **Leadership Commitment** at the top management level.
> 2. **Employee Involvement & Empowerment** at all levels.
> 3. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** mindset.
> 4. **Customer Focus** (internal & external).
> 5. **Effective Communication** of quality goals.
> 6. **Recognition & Reward** for quality contributions.
> 7. **Supplier Partnership** aligned with quality values.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
> **Differentiate between Quality Control (QC), Quality Assurance (QA), and Quality Culture (QCt) in any three dimensions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Dimension | Quality Control (QC) | Quality Assurance (QA) | **Quality Culture (QCt)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Detecting defects in finished products | Preventing defects in the process | **Transforming people's mindset & behavior** |
| **Approach** | Reactive & inspection-based | Preventive & process-based | **Value-based & people-oriented** |
| **Driver** | Inspectors, gauges, and AQL sampling | Engineers, SOPs, and process audits | **Leaders, employees, and shared values** |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **write a generic "TQM essay"** instead of a focused **Quality Culture essay** — this loses 3–4 marks. *Always anchor* your answer to the *people-and-values* dimension.
> - Students **forget to list and explain** the four pillars (Leadership, Employee Involvement, CI, Customer Focus) — examiners allocate **2 marks specifically for the pillar framework**.
> - In numerical COPQ questions, students **omit the cultural interpretation** at the end — this costs the **2 application marks**. Always end with a "comment on cultural maturity" paragraph.
> - Students confuse **Quality Circle (a group)** with **Quality Culture (an organization-wide value system)** — do not interchange these terms.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Define Quality Culture. Explain in detail the **four foundational pillars** upon which a strong Quality Culture is built, with suitable engineering industry examples. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The following data is collected from a pump-manufacturing unit producing **10,000 pumps per month**:
>
> | Cost Category | Monthly Cost (₹) |
> |---|---|
> | Internal Failure Cost (Rework + Scrap) | 1,80,000 |
> | External Failure Cost (Warranty + Field Returns) | 3,20,000 |
> | Appraisal Cost (Inspection + Testing) | 1,40,000 |
> | Prevention Cost (Training + Quality Planning) | 60,000 |
> | Total Monthly Sales | 50,00,000 |
>
> Calculate (i) Total COPQ, (ii) Failure Cost Ratio, (iii) Quality Cost Ratio, and (iv) **comment on the cultural maturity** of the organization with two specific recommendations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Definition and Four Pillars (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Quality Culture is the **organization-wide set of shared values, beliefs, attitudes, and behaviors** that determine how every member of the organization thinks about, commits to, and practices quality — independent of formal systems or supervision.

**The Four Pillars (1½ Marks Each):**

**Pillar 1 — Leadership Commitment:**
The CEO and senior management must *visibly and financially* commit to quality. For example, in **Tata Steel**, the Chairman personally chairs the monthly Quality Council meeting, and every plant's quality metrics are reviewed at the Board level. Leadership commitment converts quality from a department's job into a *strategic organizational priority*.

**Pillar 2 — Employee Involvement & Empowerment:**
Every employee is a *quality stakeholder*. **Toyota's Quality Circles** — small groups of 6–12 shop-floor workers meeting weekly to solve local quality problems — have generated **over 1 million suggestions annually**, with **> 95% implementation rate**. Empowerment means employees can *stop the line* (Jidoka) if they detect a defect risk.

**Pillar 3 — Continuous Improvement (CI):**
The belief that every process can be improved *every day*. **Maruti Suzuki's Kaizen program** records an average of **3.2 implemented suggestions per employee per year**, directly reducing COPQ by an estimated **₹ 40 crores annually**. Tools include PDCA, DMAIC, and Poka-Yoke.

**Pillar 4 — Customer Focus & Supplier Partnership:**
Quality is defined by the **Voice of Customer (VoC)**, and suppliers are treated as *cultural partners*, not vendors. **Boeing's "Partnering for Success" program** integrates tier-1 and tier-2 suppliers into Boeing's quality culture through shared training, common metrics, and joint audits.

---

**(b) COPQ Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Step 1 — Total COPQ (2 Marks):**

$$COPQ = IFC + EFC + AC + PC$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
COPQ &= 1{,}80{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}40{,}000 + 60{,}000 \\
COPQ &= \text{₹ } 7{,}00{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Failure Cost Ratio (2 Marks):**

$$FCR = \frac{IFC + EFC}{COPQ} \times 100\%$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
FCR &= \frac{1{,}80{,}000 + 3{,}20{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
FCR &= \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{7{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
FCR &= 71.43\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Quality Cost Ratio (1 Mark):**

$$QCR = \frac{COPQ}{\text{Total Sales}} \times 100\%$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
QCR &= \frac{7{,}00{,}000}{50{,}00{,}000} \times 100\% \\
QCR &= 14.00\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Cultural Maturity Commentary & Recommendations (2 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Commentary:** With **FCR = 71.43%** and **QCR = 14%**, the organization is in the **"Reactive" (Level 1)** stage of cultural maturity. Failure costs dominate (₹ 5,00,000 out of ₹ 7,00,000), and prevention spending is a mere **8.6% of COPQ**, far below the 50% benchmark for mature cultures. **External Failure Costs (₹ 3,20,000) exceeding Internal Failure Costs (₹ 1,80,000)** is a red flag — defects are escaping to customers, eroding brand trust and warranty budgets.
>
> **Recommendation 1 — Boost Prevention Spending (1 Mark):** Increase the Prevention Cost budget to at least **₹ 3,50,000 (50% of COPQ)** by funding (a) Quality Circles, (b) employee training in 7 QC tools, and (c) supplier development programs. This will reduce both IFC and EFC over 6–12 months.
>
> **Recommendation 2 — Implement Jidoka and Stop-the-Line (1 Mark):** Empower shop-floor workers with the authority and accountability to *halt production* the moment a defect is suspected. This converts External Failure Costs into the cheaper Internal Failure Costs, reducing warranty claims and brand damage.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> **(a)** Explain the **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** concept in detail. Describe its **four sub-categories** with two examples for each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **step-by-step model (10 steps)** for building a Quality Culture in an organization. Why is **Step 1 (Top Management Commitment)** considered the most critical step? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution Outline

**(a) COPQ Concept and Four Sub-Categories (7 Marks)**

**Concept (2 Marks):**
COPQ is the *total financial penalty* an organization pays because its products, processes, and services are *not* right the first time. It is the **monetary mirror of organizational culture**: a strong Quality Culture *minimizes* COPQ; a weak culture *maximizes* it.

**Four Sub-Categories (5 Marks):**

1. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC) – Costs incurred BEFORE delivery:**
   - *Example 1:* **Rework** — re-machining a defective shaft to bring it within tolerance.
   - *Example 2:* **Scrap** — discarded aluminum castings rejected at final inspection.

2. **External Failure Costs (EFC) – Costs incurred AFTER delivery:**
   - *Example 1:* **Warranty claims** — free replacement of a failed compressor under warranty.
   - *Example 2:* **Product recalls** — automotive industry recalls costing crores.

3. **Appraisal Costs (AC) – Costs of DETECTING defects:**
   - *Example 1:* **In-process inspection** using Coordinate Measuring Machines (CMM).
   - *Example 2:* **Final acceptance testing** of finished products.

4. **Prevention Costs (PC) – Costs of PREVENTING defects:**
   - *Example 1:* **Quality training programs** for employees on SPC, FMEA.
   - *Example 2:* **Process capability studies** to verify Cp and Cpk before launch.

**Master Equation (½ Mark):** $COPQ = IFC + EFC + AC + PC$

**FCR Formula (½ Mark):** $FCR = \dfrac{IFC + EFC}{COPQ} \times 100\%$

---

**(b) 10-Step Model & Importance of Step 1 (7 Marks)**

**The 10 Steps (5 Marks — ½ Mark Each):**

1. Top Management Commitment
2. Awareness Campaign
3. Quality Education & Training
4. Communication Infrastructure
5. Quality Circles Formation
6. Suggestion Scheme Launch
7. Recognition & Reward
8. Measurement & Feedback
9. Supplier Culture Integration
10. Continuous Reinforcement

**Why Step 1 is the Most Critical (2 Marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Resources follow leadership:** Without CEO/Board-level commitment, no budget is allocated for prevention activities, training, or Quality Circles. Culture cannot be built with slogans alone — it requires *financial* and *emotional* investment from the top.
> - **Behavior cascades downward:** Employees mimic what leaders do, not what they say. If the CEO skips quality reviews, the shop-floor will skip quality checks.
> - **Empirical evidence:** Deming's 14 Points and Juran's research both identify *leadership* as the single largest differentiator between culturally successful and culturally failing organizations. **> 80% of culture-transformation failures** trace back to weak or absent top management commitment.
> - **Strategic anchoring:** Leadership commitment converts quality from a *tactical activity* (handled by the QC department) into a *strategic pillar* (handled by the entire C-suite), ensuring longevity beyond any single manager's tenure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Quality Culture:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Quality Culture = *shared values + beliefs + behaviors* that drive everyone to do quality right, even when unsupervised.
- ✅ **Four Pillars (LECC):** **L**eadership, **E**mployee involvement, **C**ontinuous improvement, **C**ustomer focus.
- ✅ **COPQ Formula:** $COPQ = IFC + EFC + AC + PC$.
- ✅ **FCR Formula:** $FCR = \dfrac{IFC + EFC}{COPQ} \times 100\%$; benchmark for mature culture **≤ 25%**.
- ✅ **Optimal Spending Shift:** Reactive org (PC 10% : AC 25% : FC 65%) $\rightarrow$ Cultural org (PC 50% : AC 35% : FC 15%).
- ✅ **Quality Circle:** Group of **6–12 voluntary employees** from the same work area solving local quality problems.
- ✅ **Kaizen:** Japanese term for *continuous, incremental improvement*; the cultural engine of Toyota.
- ✅ **Jidoka:** *Stop-the-line authority* given to any employee who detects a quality risk.
- ✅ **Deming Chain Reaction:** Improve Quality $\rightarrow$ Decrease Costs $\rightarrow$ Improve Productivity $\rightarrow$ Grow Market Share $\rightarrow$ Stay in Business.
- ✅ **Difference from QC/QA/QM:** QCt is *people-and-values* oriented, while QC is product-oriented, QA is process-oriented, and QM is system-oriented.
- ✅ **Benchmark Metrics to memorize:** FPY ≥ 95%, DPMO ≤ 3.4 (Six Sigma), QCR = 4–8% of sales, ESI ≥ 5/employee/year.
- ✅ **Exam Mnemonic for 10 steps:** **"A-L-E-C-T-P-S-M-R-F"** = Awareness, Leadership, Education, Communication, Training, Participation, Structure, Measurement, Recognition, Feedback.
- ✅ **Gurus to quote in exams:** Deming (14 Points, Chain Reaction), Juran (Quality Trilogy, COPQ), Ishikawa (Fishbone, QC Circles), Feigenbaum (Total Quality Control), Crosby (Zero Defects, Quality is Free).
- ✅ **Always end numerical answers** with a *cultural maturity interpretation* — this fetches the **2 application marks** in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality from the Customer Perspective
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality from the Customer Perspective

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality from the Customer Perspective** is the degree to which a product or service meets or exceeds the **expectations, needs, and perceived value** of the customer. In modern Total Quality Management (TQM) frameworks adopted by KTU, quality is no longer an internal engineering attribute alone — it is an **external, customer-driven construct** evaluated against the customer's voice.

Mathematically, the foundational **expectation–perception gap** model (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988 — SERVQUAL framework) defines customer-oriented quality as:

$$
Q_{customer} \;=\; P \;-\; E
$$

Where:
- $Q_{customer}$ = Customer-perceived service/product quality
- $P$ = Customer's **Perception** of actual performance received
- $E$ = Customer's **Expectation** of desired performance

If $Q_{customer} > 0$, the customer is **delighted**; if $Q_{customer} = 0$, the customer is **satisfied**; if $Q_{customer} < 0$, the customer is **dissatisfied**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Coffee Shop Visit**
> Imagine you walk into a café and order a cappuccino for ₹150.
> - If the coffee is hot, smooth, aromatic, and the waiter is polite and quick → You are **satisfied** ($P \geq E$).
> - If the coffee arrives cold, tastes like water, and the staff is rude → You are **dissatisfied** ($P < E$).
> - If a tiny heart-shaped latte art is drawn on top (which you did NOT expect) → You are **delighted** ($P \gg E$).

Notice three things:
1. **Quality is in the eye of the customer**, not the barista.
2. The same product (a ₹150 cappuccino) gives different satisfaction to different people because **expectations differ**.
3. **Surprise value** (latte art) creates loyalty, not just the base product.

This is the essence of *Quality from the Customer Perspective* — **understanding what the customer values, measuring it, and engineering the gap $P - E$ to be positive**.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors

| Term | Meaning | KTU Highlight |
|---|---|---|
| **VOC** | Voice of the Customer — raw customer inputs, complaints, feedback | Drives CTQ (Critical to Quality) |
| **CTQ** | Critical to Quality — measurable features derived from VOC | Bridging VOC to engineering specs |
| **Kano Model** | Classification of customer preferences into Basic, Performance, Excitement | High-yield KTU topic |
| **SERVQUAL** | 22-item, 5-dimension instrument to measure service quality gap | Standard TQM framework |
| **CSAT** | Customer Satisfaction Score | KPI of customer quality |
| **NPS** | Net Promoter Score | Loyalty indicator |
| **QFD** | Quality Function Deployment — House of Quality | Translates VOC to engineering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In the Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic maps to **CO4** (Apply quality management tools for customer-focused product/service design) and is tested both as a 3-mark definition and as a 14-mark framework explanation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Customer Expectation Pyramid (Hierarchical View)

Customer expectations exist on **three escalating levels**:

1. **Explicit Expectations** — What the customer explicitly asks for.
   *Example:* "I want a car with mileage of at least 20 kmpl."
2. **Implicit Expectations** — Unspoken, assumed standards.
   *Example:* "The car should not break down in the first year."
3. **Dynamic Expectations** — Expectations that evolve with time, technology, and exposure.
   *Example:* "Now I expect a touchscreen infotainment system."

> **Why this matters:** A firm that delivers only on *explicit* expectations risks being replaced; one that anticipates *dynamic* expectations builds **loyalty**.

### 2.2 The Five SERVQUAL Dimensions (Parasuraman Model)

| Dimension | Description | Mechanical Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance | Clean factory floor, modern CNC machines, neat uniforms |
| **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably | On-time delivery of machined parts |
| **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | Quick RMA processing |
| **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of staff, ability to inspire trust | Certified quality engineers |
| **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention to customers | Customised solutions for low-volume clients |

### 2.3 The Kano Model of Customer Satisfaction (High-Yield)

The **Kano Model** (Professor Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies product/service attributes into **three categories** based on how they affect satisfaction:

| Category | Customer Reaction if Present | Customer Reaction if Absent | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Must-Be (Basic / Threshold)** | Neutral (taken for granted) | Extremely dissatisfied | Non-negotiable baseline — must be 100% |
| **One-Dimensional (Performance)** | More satisfaction | Less satisfaction | Linear improvement — invest proportionally |
| **Attractive (Excitement / Delighter)** | Extremely satisfied | Neutral | Differentiator — competitive edge |

> **Mathematical insight:** If we let $S$ = satisfaction and $x$ = level of attribute, the three categories can be represented by distinct functional forms:
>
> - **Must-Be:** $S(x) = S_{max}$ for $x > x_{critical}$, else $S \to -\infty$
> - **One-Dimensional:** $S(x) = a + b \cdot x$ (linear)
> - **Attractive:** $S(x) = S_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{-kx})$ (saturating exponential)

### 2.4 Customer Satisfaction Metrics (Formula Sheet)

| Metric | Formula | Range / Target | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CSAT (Customer Satisfaction Score)** | $\displaystyle CSAT = \frac{N_{satisfied}}{N_{total}} \times 100\,\%$ | 0–100%, target $\geq 90\%$ | High |
| **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** | $\displaystyle NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | -100 to +100, target $\geq 50$ | High |
| **CES (Customer Effort Score)** | $\displaystyle CES = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{7 - x_i}{6} \right) \times 100$ | 0–100%, target $\geq 80\%$ | Moderate |
| **CSI (Customer Satisfaction Index)** | $\displaystyle CSI = \frac{P}{E} \times 100$ | $\geq 100\%$ implies delight | Moderate |
| **SERVQUAL Gap** | $\displaystyle Gap_k = P_k - E_k$ for $k = 1 \dots 5$ | $\geq 0$ for each dimension | High |
| **Customer Retention Rate** | $\displaystyle CRR = \frac{C_{end} - C_{new}}{C_{start}} \times 100\,\%$ | Maximise | Moderate |

### 2.5 Voice of Customer (VOC) to Critical-to-Quality (CTQ) Flow

The standard KTU-recognised translation pipeline is:

$$
\text{VOC} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Affinity Diagram}}\; \text{Affinity Clusters} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Tree Diagram}}\; \text{Needs Hierarchy} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Kano}}\; \text{Categorised Needs} \;\xrightarrow{\text{CTQ Tree}}\; \text{CTQs}
$$

> **Real-world engineering utility:** In an automotive firm, VOC like *"I want a quieter cabin"* is translated via this pipeline into measurable CTQs like *"NVH level $\leq 40$ dB at 60 kmph"* — which can then be designed, manufactured, and tested.

### 2.6 Service-Quality Gap Model (Five Gaps)

The SERVQUAL framework identifies **five gaps** that a quality-conscious firm must close:

1. **Gap 1:** Consumer expectation $\neq$ Management perception of expectations
2. **Gap 2:** Management perception $\neq$ Service-quality specifications
3. **Gap 3:** Service-quality specifications $\neq$ Service delivery
4. **Gap 4:** Service delivery $\neq$ External communication
5. **Gap 5:** Expected service $\neq$ Perceived service (the customer-side gap, $P - E$)

> **Closure of Gap 5** is the ultimate KTU objective — it is the *quality from the customer perspective* gap.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the CSAT Confidence Interval (Exhaustive)

The **Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)** is a proportion $p$ estimated from a finite sample. For a KTU-style 14-mark question, students are often asked to compute CSAT **along with its 95% confidence interval**.

**Step 1 — Define the sample proportion.**

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{N_{satisfied}}{N_{total}}
$$

**Step 2 — State the standard error using the binomial-to-normal approximation.**

For large $N$, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal with standard error:

$$
SE(\hat{p}) \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}\,(1-\hat{p})}{N_{total}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Z-multiplier for the chosen confidence level.**

For a 95% confidence interval, $z_{\alpha/2} = 1.96$.

$$
z_{crit} \;=\; 1.96
$$

**Step 4 — Construct the confidence interval.**

$$
CI_{95\%} \;=\; \hat{p} \,\pm\, z_{crit} \cdot SE(\hat{p})
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} \;=\; \hat{p} \,\pm\, 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}\,(1-\hat{p})}{N_{total}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

A factory surveys 400 customers; 340 report satisfaction.

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{340}{400} \;=\; 0.85
$$

$$
SE \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.85 \cdot 0.15}{400}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.1275}{400}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.000319} \;\approx\; 0.01785
$$

$$
CI_{95\%} \;=\; 0.85 \,\pm\, (1.96)(0.01785) \;=\; 0.85 \,\pm\, 0.03498
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} \;=\; [\,0.815\,,\; 0.885\,] \;=\; [\,81.5\%\,,\; 88.5\%\,]}
$$

> **Interpretation for KTU answer:** We are 95% confident that the true population CSAT lies between 81.5% and 88.5%. Since the lower bound (81.5%) is below the corporate target of 90%, the firm **has not yet achieved** its quality goal — corrective action is required.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the NPS Classification Logic

The **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** requires customers to rate, on a 0–10 scale, their likelihood to recommend the product. The classification rule is:

$$
\text{Customer Class} =
\begin{cases}
\text{Promoter}, & \text{if score} \in \{9, 10\} \\
\text{Passive}, & \text{if score} \in \{7, 8\} \\
\text{Detractor}, & \text{if score} \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6\}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 1 — Count promoters, passives, detractors.**

If out of 1000 customers, 600 are promoters, 250 are passives, and 150 are detractors:

$$
\%Promoters \;=\; \frac{600}{1000} \times 100 \;=\; 60\%
$$

$$
\%Detractors \;=\; \frac{150}{1000} \times 100 \;=\; 15\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute NPS.**

$$
NPS \;=\; \%Promoters - \%Detractors \;=\; 60 - 15 \;=\; \boxed{45}
$$

> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** NPS $\geq 50$ → Excellent; $0 \leq NPS < 50$ → Good; NPS $< 0$ → Critical. The above firm is *Good* but not yet *Excellent*.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Quality Frameworks

| Framework | Origin | Dimensions / Categories | Best Used When | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SERVQUAL** | Parasuraman et al., 1988 | 5 dimensions, 22 items | Service industries (banks, hospitals) | 7–10 marks |
| **SERVPERF** | Cronin & Taylor, 1992 | Performance only (no expectation) | Quick customer surveys | 3–5 marks |
| **Kano Model** | N. Kano, 1984 | Must-be / One-Dim / Attractive | Product feature prioritisation | 7–14 marks |
| **QFD (House of Quality)** | Akao, 1972 | Customer needs vs technical specs | New product development | 7–14 marks |
| **NPS** | Reichheld, 2003 | Single 0–10 scale | Loyalty benchmarking | 3–7 marks |
| **CSAT** | Industry practice | 1–5 / 1–10 satisfaction scale | Transactional feedback | 3–5 marks |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When the question asks *"Discuss how quality is viewed from the customer's perspective"* — start with the **$Q = P - E$** gap model, then mention **SERVQUAL's 5 dimensions**, then anchor the answer with the **Kano model** for full marks.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Kano Classification Survey

A manufacturer surveys 50 customers on whether they want an *"automatic tyre-pressure indicator"* in their car. Responses:

| Response | Count |
|---|---|
| I like it (functional) | 25 |
| I expect it (must-be) | 5 |
| I am neutral | 5 |
| I can tolerate it | 5 |
| I dislike it (functional) | 10 |

**Step 1 — Use the Kano evaluation table** (standard cross-tabulation).

A "functional" (positive) and "dysfunctional" (negative) response pair maps each respondent to a category.

**Step 2 — Tabulate and identify the majority category.**

Mapping (functional vs dysfunctional):
- (Like, Dislike) → **Attractive**
- (Like, Tolerate) → **One-Dimensional**
- (Like, Neutral) → **Attractive**
- (Expect, Dislike) → **Must-Be**
- etc.

Assuming the cross-tab yields: Attractive = 30, One-Dimensional = 12, Must-Be = 5, Indifferent = 3.

**Step 3 — Conclusion.**

$$
\text{Majority Category} = \text{Attractive} \quad (\text{60\% of respondents})
$$

> **Engineering decision:** Add the tyre-pressure indicator — it is a **delighter** that differentiates the car in the market, not a baseline expectation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Customer Perspective Quality Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["CUSTOMER\nEXPECTATIONS E"]
    node2["FIRM LISTENS\nVOC CAPTURE"]
    node3["TRANSLATE TO\nCTQ METRICS"]
    node4["DESIGN\nAND PRODUCE"]
    node5["CUSTOMER\nPERCEIVES P"]
    node6["CALCULATE\nQ = P MINUS E"]
    node7["IF Q POSITIVE\nDELIGHTED"]
    node8["IF Q NEGATIVE\nDISSATISFIED"]
    node9["FEEDBACK\nTO VOC"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node5
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node9
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node2
```

```mermaid
    k1 --> k2
    k2 -- YES --> k3
    k2 -- NO --> k4
    k4 -- YES --> k5
    k4 -- NO --> k6
```


<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define **Quality from the Customer Perspective**. State the **expectation–perception gap model** with its equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Quality from the customer perspective is the customer's judgement of a product or service based on the gap between what they **expect** and what they **perceive** they receive. The model, formalised by Parasuraman et al. (1988) in the SERVQUAL framework, is given by:

$$
Q_{customer} \;=\; P \;-\; E
$$

where $P$ = perceived performance and $E$ = expected performance. If $Q > 0$, the customer is delighted; if $Q < 0$, the customer is dissatisfied. This model places the **customer, not the producer**, as the final arbiter of quality. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for interpretation.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** List and briefly explain the **three categories of the Kano Model** of customer satisfaction.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Kano Model (Noriaki Kano, 1984) classifies product/service attributes into:

1. **Must-Be (Basic / Threshold):** These are baseline expectations. Their presence causes no satisfaction, but their absence causes extreme dissatisfaction. *Example:* Antilock brakes in a modern car.
2. **One-Dimensional (Performance):** Satisfaction increases linearly with the level of the attribute. *Example:* Fuel efficiency — better mileage gives more satisfaction.
3. **Attractive (Excitement):** Unexpected features that delight the customer but whose absence does not cause dissatisfaction. *Example:* Auto-parking assist in an economy car.

*(1 mark each for the three categories, with example.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Choose this OR Question B.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **SERVQUAL framework** for measuring service quality from the customer's perspective. List its five dimensions and describe each with an industrial example.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A firm receives **800 customer responses** on a 1–5 satisfaction scale. The results are: 1★ = 40, 2★ = 60, 3★ = 100, 4★ = 300, 5★ = 300. Compute the **CSAT score** and the **95% confidence interval**. Comment on whether the firm has met a target of 85%.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — SERVQUAL Framework**

The SERVQUAL (Service Quality) model, developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml and Berry (1988)**, measures service quality as the gap between customer expectations and perceptions. It uses a 22-item questionnaire across five dimensions:

| Dimension | Meaning | Industrial Example (Mechanical) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangibles** | Physical evidence — equipment, facilities, personnel appearance | Modern CNC shop floor, neat uniforms of quality inspectors |
| **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately | On-time delivery of precision-machined components as per PO |
| **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | Quick response to a customer's request for a replacement batch |
| **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust | Certified Six Sigma Black Belts handling customer audits |
| **Empathy** | Caring, individualised attention provided to customers | Customised PPM tracking reports for a strategic OEM client |

The gap for each dimension $k$ is:

$$
Gap_k \;=\; P_k - E_k
$$

If all five gaps are positive ($Gap_k \geq 0$), the firm delivers service quality from the customer's perspective. SERVQUAL is the most widely cited instrument in TQM literature and is a **KTU-recommended** framework. *(Full marks for naming all five dimensions, giving examples, and writing the gap equation.)*

**(b) [7 Marks] — CSAT Computation**

**Step 1 — Define satisfaction.** Convention: 4★ and 5★ are "satisfied" (top-2-box method).

$$
N_{satisfied} \;=\; 300 + 300 \;=\; 600
$$

$$
N_{total} \;=\; 40 + 60 + 100 + 300 + 300 \;=\; 800
$$

**Step 2 — Compute CSAT.**

$$
\hat{p} \;=\; \frac{600}{800} \;=\; 0.75
$$

$$
CSAT \;=\; 75\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute standard error.**

$$
SE \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.75 \cdot 0.25}{800}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{0.1875}{800}} \;=\; \sqrt{0.0002344} \;\approx\; 0.01531
$$

**Step 4 — 95% Confidence Interval.**

$$
CI_{95\%} \;=\; 0.75 \,\pm\, 1.96 \cdot (0.01531) \;=\; 0.75 \,\pm\, 0.0300
$$

$$
\boxed{CI_{95\%} \;=\; [\,0.720\,,\; 0.780\,] \;=\; [\,72.0\%\,,\; 78.0\%\,]}
$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

The firm has a CSAT of **75%** with 95% CI **[72.0%, 78.0%]**. Since the entire interval lies **below** the 85% target, the firm has **NOT** met its quality target. Recommended actions: root-cause analysis of 1★–3★ responses and a corrective-action plan. *(Full marks: 2 for CSAT, 3 for CI, 2 for conclusion and recommendation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice.

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Kano Model** of customer satisfaction. With the help of a labelled diagram, classify the three categories and explain how an engineer should use this classification in product design.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **Voice of Customer (VOC) to Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** translation process. How does the House of Quality in QFD support customer-driven quality?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 Marks] — Kano Model**

The **Kano Model**, proposed by Professor **Noriaki Kano** (1984), is a theory of product development and customer satisfaction that classifies customer preferences into three distinct categories based on non-linear satisfaction behaviour:

1. **Must-Be (Threshold / Basic) Attributes:**
   - Taken for granted when present; their absence causes extreme dissatisfaction.
   - Mathematical behaviour: $S(x) \to S_{max}$ for $x \geq x_{crit}$; $S \to -\infty$ otherwise.
   - *Mechanical Example:* Brake functionality in a passenger car.

2. **One-Dimensional (Performance) Attributes:**
   - Satisfaction increases proportionally with the level of the attribute.
   - Mathematical behaviour: $S(x) = a + b \cdot x$ (linear).
   - *Mechanical Example:* Fuel efficiency in km/litre.

3. **Attractive (Excitement / Delighter) Attributes:**
   - Unexpected features that delight customers; their absence causes no dissatisfaction.
   - Mathematical behaviour: $S(x) = S_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{-kx})$ (saturating exponential).
   - *Mechanical Example:* Self-driving mode in a mid-segment car.

**Engineering use in product design:**
- Step 1: Conduct Kano questionnaire survey on customers.
- Step 2: Classify each feature into one of the three categories using cross-tabulation.
- Step 3: Allocate design effort — Must-Be (100% reliability), One-Dimensional (proportional investment), Attractive (strategic differentiator).
- Step 4: Re-classify annually since Attractive features become One-Dimensional over time (Kano lifecycle).

*A labelled sketch would show three curves: an exponential-decay-type Must-Be, a straight line for One-Dimensional, and a saturating exponential for Attractive.* *(Full marks: 2 for definition, 3 for categories with examples and behaviour, 2 for engineering use.)*

**(b) [7 Marks] — VOC to CTQ and House of Quality**

**VOC → CTQ Process:**

Voice of Customer (VOC) is the raw, qualitative expression of customer needs gathered through interviews, surveys, complaints, focus groups, and social-media analytics. Converting these vague statements into measurable engineering specifications requires the following pipeline:

$$
V_1[\text{VOC Capture}] \;\to\; V_2[\text{Affinity Diagram}] \;\to\; V_3[\text{Customer Need Clusters}] \;\to\; V_4[\text{Tree Diagram}] \;\to\; V_5[\text{Kano Classification}] \;\to\; V_6[\text{CTQ Tree}] \;\to\; V_7[\text{Measurable CTQs}]
$$

**Critical-to-Quality (CTQ)** is a measurable characteristic of a product or process that directly relates to a customer need. *Example:* VOC = *"I want a quieter cabin"* → CTQ = *"NVH $\leq 40$ dB at 60 kmph"*.

**House of Quality (HoQ) — QFD:**

The House of Quality is the central matrix of **Quality Function Deployment (QFD)**, introduced by **Yoji Akao (1972)**. It is a structured planning tool that translates customer requirements (WHATS) into engineering specifications (HOWS). Its key components are:

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Customer Requirements (WHATS)** | Top of the house — VOC-derived |
| **Technical Requirements (HOWS)** | Ceiling — engineering parameters |
| **Relationship Matrix (Roof)** | Centre — strength of WHATS–HOWS correlation |
| **Inter-correlations (Roof)** | Triangular matrix — synergy/conflict between HOWS |
| **Customer Importance Ratings** | Left wall — weighted priorities |
| **Competitive Benchmarking** | Right wall — competitor performance |
| **Technical Difficulty / Targets** | Basement — engineering targets |

**How HoQ supports customer-driven quality:**
1. Forces a **structured translation** from VOC to measurable specs — no guesswork.
2. Highlights **trade-offs** (e.g., weight reduction may reduce noise insulation) via the roof matrix.
3. Provides **prioritisation** based on customer importance weights.
4. Aligns **cross-functional teams** (marketing, design, manufacturing) on a single artefact.
5. Drives **continuous improvement** by re-running the HoQ as customer expectations evolve.

Hence, HoQ operationalises the principle that *quality must be defined by the customer* and *engineered into the product*. *(Full marks: 3 for VOC–CTQ pipeline, 4 for HoQ components and benefits.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing "$Q = E - P$" instead of "$Q = P - E$":** This reverses the sign of all gap interpretations and costs **2 marks** outright.
> 2. **Confusing SERVQUAL with SERVPERF:** SERVQUAL uses Perception **minus** Expectation; SERVPERF uses Perception **only**. Examiners expect this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the units in CSAT:** CSAT is a **percentage** (0–100%), not a ratio (0–1). State clearly.
> 4. **Skipping the confidence interval:** In numerical problems, computing CSAT alone gives only **2 out of 7 marks**; the 95% CI carries the rest.
> 5. **Mixing up Kano categories:** Students often confuse *Must-Be* with *Attractive*. Remember: **Must-Be = absence hurts**; **Attractive = presence delights**.
> 6. **Not stating the source:** Always attribute SERVQUAL to Parasuraman et al. (1988) and Kano to N. Kano (1984) — examiners reward academic rigour.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ Quality from the customer perspective = **Customer's judgement**, not the producer's.
- ✅ Foundational equation: $Q = P - E$ (Perception − Expectation).
- ✅ **Three expectation levels:** Explicit, Implicit, Dynamic.
- ✅ **SERVQUAL** has **5 dimensions**: Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- ✅ **SERVQUAL has 5 Gaps**, of which **Gap 5** is the customer-side gap.
- ✅ **Kano Model** has **3 categories**: Must-Be, One-Dimensional, Attractive.
- ✅ Attractive features follow **saturating-exponential** satisfaction behaviour.
- ✅ **CSAT** = (Satisfied ÷ Total) × 100%; **NPS** = %Promoters − %Detractors (range −100 to +100).
- ✅ **95% CI for CSAT:** $\hat{p} \pm 1.96 \cdot \sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/N}$.
- ✅ **VOC → CTQ pipeline:** Capture → Cluster → Tree → Kano → CTQ → Specs.
- ✅ **House of Quality (QFD)** translates WHATS (VOC) into HOWS (engineering specs).
- ✅ **NPS benchmark:** $\geq 50$ Excellent; $0$–$50$ Good; $<0$ Critical.
- ✅ Quality is **dynamic** — Attractive features decay into One-Dimensional over time (Kano lifecycle).
- ✅ **KTU CO mapping:** CO4 — Apply TQM tools for customer-focused design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Information Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Information Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quality Information System (QIS):** A structured, organization-wide framework comprising people, procedures, data, and technology designed to systematically **collect, store, process, analyze, and disseminate quality-related information** to all levels of management for the purpose of **planning, controlling, and improving** product and process quality.

A Quality Information System acts as the **central nervous system** of any Total Quality Management (TQM) or ISO 9001-certified organization. It transforms raw inspection and process data (input) into actionable management intelligence (output), enabling data-driven decisions across strategic, tactical, and operational hierarchies.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, QIS is treated as the **integration layer** that links measurement instruments (metrology), statistical process control (SPC), acceptance sampling, and reliability engineering with enterprise decision-making.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Patient Records System"

Imagine a **multi-specialty hospital**:
- The **reception desk** records patient entry data (raw data collection).
- The **laboratory** generates test reports (data processing & analysis).
- The **digital records database** stores every report for future reference (information storage).
- The **duty doctor** instantly retrieves history before prescribing (tactical use).
- The **hospital director** reviews monthly statistics to plan resource allocation (strategic use).

In this analogy, the **hospital information system = Quality Information System** of a manufacturing industry. Just as the doctor cannot treat a patient without history, a quality manager cannot control a process without organized quality data. The data flow, storage, retrieval, and decision linkage are identical concepts.

## 1.3 Why QIS is Critical in Modern Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (KTU Board Favourite):** Without a QIS, quality data remains scattered across inspection sheets, logbooks, and operator memory — leading to **reactive** rather than **proactive** quality control.

The modern industry 4.0 paradigm relies on **real-time QIS** integrated with:
- **MES** (Manufacturing Execution Systems)
- **ERP** (Enterprise Resource Planning)
- **IoT sensors** on shop floor
- **Cloud-based SPC dashboards**

## 1.4 Core Components of a Quality Information System

A QIS consists of **five interdependent layers**:

1. **Data Source Layer** — Inspection records, test reports, customer complaints, supplier data, machine parameters, SPC charts.
2. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Manual entry forms, barcode/RFID readers, automated CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) outputs, sensor networks.
3. **Data Processing & Storage Layer** — Quality database (SQL-based), data warehouses, cloud storage.
4. **Analysis & Decision Layer** — Statistical software (Minitab, SPSS), Pareto analysis, fishbone tools, cost-of-quality calculators.
5. **Dissemination & Feedback Layer** — Reports (daily/weekly/monthly), dashboards, alerts, e-mail triggers to concerned departments.

## 1.5 Standard Quality Metrics Used in QIS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metrics (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **DPMO** — Defects Per Million Opportunities
> - **PPM** — Parts Per Million (defective)
> - **RTY** — Rolled Throughput Yield
> - **COPQ** — Cost of Poor Quality
> - **Six Sigma Level** — 3.4 DPMO target
> - **Cpk / Ppk** — Process Capability Indices
> - **FMEA RPN** — Risk Priority Number

These metrics are the **currency** in which QIS communicates quality status to all stakeholders.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Quality Information

A QIS serves **three managerial levels**, each demanding different information:

### 2.1.1 Strategic-Level Information (Top Management)
- **Purpose:** Long-term planning, policy formulation, capital investment.
- **Data type:** Annual quality cost trends, customer satisfaction indices, market share related to quality, ISO audit summaries.
- **Frequency:** Monthly / Quarterly / Annual.
- **Format:** Executive summary dashboards, board-level presentations.

### 2.1.2 Tactical-Level Information (Middle Management)
- **Purpose:** Resource allocation, department-wise quality targets, supplier evaluation.
- **Data type:** Departmental scrap rates, supplier rejection percentages, process capability indices, training needs.
- **Frequency:** Weekly / Monthly.
- **Format:** Comparative bar charts, Pareto charts, trend graphs.

### 2.1.3 Operational-Level Information (Shop Floor)
- **Purpose:** Real-time process control, immediate corrective action.
- **Data type:** Individual measurement readings, control chart points, machine settings, tool wear data.
- **Frequency:** Real-time / Shift-wise.
- **Format:** SPC charts, alarm signals, work-instruction displays.

## 2.2 The P-A-F Model of Quality Cost (Feigenbaum's Framework)

This is a **high-yield KTU topic** — almost guaranteed to appear in either Part A or Part B.

**Prevention Costs (P):** Money spent to *prevent* defects from occurring.
- Training, quality planning, design reviews, supplier capability assessment, process validation.

**Appraisal Costs (A):** Money spent to *detect* defects after they occur.
- Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final testing, calibration of gauges, SPC implementation.

**Internal Failure Costs (IF):** Costs arising from defects found *before* delivery.
- Scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure analysis, downtime.

**External Failure Costs (EF):** Costs arising from defects found *after* delivery to customer.
- Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, legal liabilities, loss of goodwill.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal Quality Investment Principle:** As prevention investment increases, appraisal + failure costs decrease. The **Total Quality Cost (TQC)** curve has a **U-shape**, and the **optimal quality level** lies at the minimum of this U-curve.

## 2.3 Quality Information Flow Architecture

The information flow in a QIS follows a **closed-loop pattern**:

1. **Data Generation** (Shop floor) → 2. **Data Capture** (Operators/Sensors) → 3. **Data Transmission** (Network) → 4. **Data Storage** (Database) → 5. **Data Analysis** (Statistical tools) → 6. **Information Reporting** (Reports) → 7. **Decision & Feedback** (Action taken) → loops back to Step 1.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Expression** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Total Quality Cost (TQC) | $TQC \;=\; P \;+\; A \;+\; IF \;+\; EF$ | Currency (₹ / $) |
| Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ) | $COPQ \;=\; IF \;+\; EF$ | Excludes prevention + appraisal |
| Cost of Good Quality (COGQ) | $COGQ \;=\; P \;+\; A$ | Investment side |
| Quality Cost Ratio (QCR) | $QCR \;=\; \dfrac{IF+EF}{P+A}$ | Lower is better; ideal < 1 |
| Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO \;=\; \dfrac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}$ | $D$ = defects, $N$ = units, $O$ = opportunities per unit |
| Parts Per Million Defective | $PPM \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Defective units}}{\text{Total units}} \times 10^{6}$ | — |
| Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Product of individual station yields |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\;\dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = std deviation |
| Six Sigma Level | $\sigma_{level} \;=\; 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(RTY)}$ | Used to find sigma from RTY |
| FMEA Risk Priority Number | $RPN \;=\; S \times O \times D$ | $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note:** All absolute-value-style expressions inside the table use $\min(\cdot)$ notation to avoid the `vert` symbol which breaks markdown table syntax. Use `\dfrac` or `\frac` carefully within `$...$`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

A well-designed QIS enables:
- **Predictive maintenance** through Cpk trend analysis.
- **Supplier score-carding** by tracking incoming PPM.
- **Customer-centric design** by integrating voice-of-customer (VoC) data.
- **Regulatory compliance** (automotive IATF 16949, aerospace AS9100) by providing auditable digital records.
- **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** by exposing the "vital few" problems through Pareto prioritization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Quality Cost Minimization Model

This derivation shows **why** organizations should invest in prevention rather than tolerate failure costs.

**Given:** Let total quality cost $TQC$ be a function of prevention investment $P$.

**Assumption 1:** Prevention costs increase **linearly** with effort.

$$
P(x) = k_1 x
$$

Where $x$ is the level of prevention investment (normalized 0 to 1) and $k_1$ is a positive constant.

**Assumption 2:** Failure costs decrease **exponentially** as prevention increases.

$$
F(x) = k_2 \cdot e^{-k_3 x}
$$

Where $k_2$ is the initial failure cost (at $x=0$) and $k_3$ is the decay constant.

**Assumption 3:** Appraisal costs are relatively **constant** for a given system.

$$
A = \text{constant}
$$

**Total Quality Cost function:**

$$
TQC(x) = P(x) + A + F(x)
$$

Substituting:

$$
TQC(x) = k_1 x + A + k_2 e^{-k_3 x}
$$

**Step 1:** To find the **optimal prevention level**, differentiate with respect to $x$ and set to zero.

$$
\frac{d(TQC)}{dx} = k_1 - k_3 k_2 e^{-k_3 x} = 0
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the optimal $x^*$.

$$
k_1 = k_3 k_2 e^{-k_3 x^*}
$$

$$
e^{-k_3 x^*} = \frac{k_1}{k_3 k_2}
$$

**Step 3:** Take natural logarithm of both sides.

$$
-k_3 x^* = \ln\!\left(\frac{k_1}{k_3 k_2}\right)
$$

**Step 4:** Final expression for optimal prevention level.

$$
x^* = \frac{1}{k_3} \ln\!\left(\frac{k_3 k_2}{k_1}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Verify minimum by second derivative test.

$$
\frac{d^2(TQC)}{dx^2} = k_3^2 k_2 e^{-k_3 x^*}
$$

Since $k_2, k_3 > 0$ and the exponential is always positive, the second derivative is positive — confirming a **minimum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** If the ratio $\frac{k_3 k_2}{k_1} \le 1$, then $x^* \le 0$, meaning prevention is already over-invested or the system is so robust that further prevention is uneconomical.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: QIS Cost Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
A manufacturing unit reports the following annual quality costs:
- Prevention: ₹ 2,00,000
- Appraisal: ₹ 1,50,000
- Internal Failure: ₹ 3,00,000
- External Failure: ₹ 1,50,000
- Total units produced: 1,00,000
- Defective units: 800
- Each unit has 5 quality characteristics (opportunities).

**Step 1:** Compute Total Quality Cost.

$$
TQC = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
TQC = 2{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000
$$

$$
TQC = \text{₹ } 8{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Compute Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ).

$$
COPQ = IF + EF = 3{,}00{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 3:** Compute Quality Cost Ratio (QCR).

$$
QCR = \frac{IF + EF}{P + A} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{3{,}50{,}000} = 1.286
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Interpretation:** QCR > 1 means the firm is spending **more on failure than on prevention + appraisal**. This is the classic "fire-fighting" pattern — the firm should **increase prevention investment** to lower long-run failure costs.

**Step 4:** Compute Defects Per Million Opportunities (DPMO).

$$
DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{N \times O}
$$

Let total defect count $D = 800 \times 5 = 4000$ (assuming each defective has at least one defect — but using total opportunities):

$$
DPMO = \frac{4000 \times 10^{6}}{1{,}00{,}000 \times 5}
$$

$$
DPMO = \frac{4 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{5}} = 8000
$$

**Step 5:** Compute Parts Per Million Defective (PPM).

$$
PPM = \frac{800}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 10^{6} = 8000 \text{ ppm}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert DPMO to Sigma Level using the standard industry equation.

$$
\sigma_{level} = 0.8406 + \sqrt{29.37 - 2.221 \cdot \ln(4000)}
$$

Computing inside the square root:

$$
\ln(4000) = 8.294
$$

$$
2.221 \times 8.294 = 18.421
$$

$$
29.37 - 18.421 = 10.949
$$

$$
\sqrt{10.949} = 3.309
$$

$$
\sigma_{level} = 0.8406 + 3.309 = 4.15 \; \sigma
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The process operates at **4.15 sigma**, which is **below the world-class benchmark of 6 sigma (3.4 DPMO)**. The QIS will flag this as a priority for improvement projects.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Information Type vs. Decision Level

| **Information Type** | **Decision Level** | **Frequency** | **Source** | **Format** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Defect counts | Operational | Per shift | Inspection sheets | SPC chart |
| Cpk trend | Tactical | Weekly | SPC software | Run chart |
| Cost of poor quality | Strategic | Monthly | Accounts + QMS | Pareto + bar chart |
| Customer complaints | Strategic | Quarterly | CRM | Trend report |
| Supplier PPM | Tactical | Per lot | Incoming inspection | Histogram |
| Warranty claims | Strategic | Annual | Field service | Fishbone input |
| Process capability $C_{pk}$ | Tactical | Monthly | SPC database | Box plot |

## 3.4 Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY) Derivation

A product passes through $n$ sequential workstations. If station $i$ has yield $Y_i$ (fraction non-defective):

$$
Y_i = \frac{\text{Good units at station } i}{\text{Input to station } i}
$$

Since the stations are in series, the probability of a unit surviving all stations is the product of individual survival probabilities:

$$
RTY = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times \ldots \times Y_n = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i
$$

**Why RTY matters in QIS:** It reveals "hidden factories" — many individually high-yielding stations ($Y_i \approx 0.99$) compound to a very low RTY. For example, 10 stations at 0.99 yield give $RTY = 0.99^{10} = 0.904$, i.e., **9.6% of input is scrapped somewhere** — a critical QIS insight.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

```mermaid
    capture2 --> capturegrp

    db --> proc2[Pareto Analysis]
```

## 4.3 P-A-F Cost Pyramid (Schematic Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ext[External Failure Costs]:::ext
    intf[Internal Failure Costs]:::intf
    appr[Appraisal Costs]:::appr
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pyramid Reading Direction:** In an ideal QIS-optimized organization, the **weight shifts downward** — heavy on Prevention at the base, light on External Failure at the top. In a poor organization, the pyramid is **inverted** (top-heavy with failure costs).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q: Define Quality Information System (QIS). List any four components of QIS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** A Quality Information System (QIS) is a structured framework of people, procedures, data, and technology used to systematically collect, process, store, analyze, and disseminate quality-related information to all levels of an organization for planning, controlling, and improving product and process quality.

**Four Components (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Data Source Layer** — Inspection records, customer complaints, supplier data, sensor outputs.
2. **Data Acquisition Layer** — Manual forms, barcode scanners, CMM, IoT sensors.
3. **Data Storage and Processing Layer** — Central quality database, data warehouse.
4. **Analysis and Decision Layer** — SPC software, Pareto, FMEA, COPQ calculators.
5. *(Optional 5th)* — Dissemination and Feedback Layer — Reports, dashboards, alerts.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q: Differentiate between Prevention costs and Appraisal costs in the P-A-F model. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Prevention Cost** | **Appraisal Cost** |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | To *prevent* defects from occurring | To *detect* defects after occurrence |
| **Timing** | Before the product is made | During or after production |
| **Examples (2 each)** | (i) Operator training, (ii) Design review, (iii) Process validation, (iv) Quality planning | (i) Incoming inspection, (ii) Final testing, (iii) Gauge calibration, (iv) SPC implementation |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "appraisal" with "failure." Appraisal is the cost of *checking* — failure is the cost of *what you find wrong.* Examiners deduct 1 mark for this confusion.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ONE** of the following (Question A or Question B).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the P-A-F model of quality costs with a neat sketch. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **P-A-F (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) model** was developed by **A.V. Feigenbaum** to classify all quality-related expenditures in a manufacturing organization.

**1. Prevention Costs (P) (2 marks):**
Expenditure incurred to *prevent* the occurrence of defects. Examples: quality planning, training, design reviews, supplier evaluation, process capability studies.

**2. Appraisal Costs (A) (1 mark):**
Cost of *detecting* defects through inspection and testing. Examples: incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final testing, gauge calibration.

**3. Failure Costs (F) — split into two sub-categories (2 marks):**
- **Internal Failure (IF):** Costs from defects found before delivery — scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure analysis.
- **External Failure (EF):** Costs from defects found after delivery — warranty, returns, recalls, legal claims, loss of goodwill.

**Total Quality Cost (TQC) equation (1 mark):**

$$
TQC = P + A + IF + EF
$$

**Sketch — U-shaped curve (1 mark):** As prevention investment increases from left to right, failure cost drops sharply. The total cost curve is **U-shaped**, with the minimum giving the **optimal quality level**.

**(b) The following data is obtained from a manufacturing firm for one year. Calculate (i) Total Quality Cost, (ii) Cost of Poor Quality, and (iii) Quality Cost Ratio. Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**

| **Cost Element** | **Amount (₹)** |
|---|---|
| Quality planning & training | 80,000 |
| Inspection and testing | 1,20,000 |
| Scrap and rework | 2,00,000 |
| Warranty claims | 1,50,000 |
| Supplier evaluation | 40,000 |
| Re-inspection of reworked items | 30,000 |
| Calibration of instruments | 50,000 |
| Customer returns handling | 70,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Classify each cost into P, A, IF, EF.

- **Prevention (P):** Quality planning & training (80,000) + Supplier evaluation (40,000) = **₹ 1,20,000**
- **Appraisal (A):** Inspection & testing (1,20,000) + Calibration of instruments (50,000) = **₹ 1,70,000**
- **Internal Failure (IF):** Scrap & rework (2,00,000) + Re-inspection of reworked items (30,000) = **₹ 2,30,000**
- **External Failure (EF):** Warranty claims (1,50,000) + Customer returns handling (70,000) = **₹ 2,20,000**

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate Total Quality Cost.

$$
TQC = P + A + IF + EF
$$

$$
TQC = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}70{,}000 + 2{,}30{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000
$$

$$
TQC = \text{₹ } 7{,}40{,}000
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate Cost of Poor Quality.

$$
COPQ = IF + EF = 2{,}30{,}000 + 2{,}20{,}000 = \text{₹ } 4{,}50{,}000
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Calculate Quality Cost Ratio.

$$
QCR = \frac{IF + EF}{P + A} = \frac{4{,}50{,}000}{2{,}90{,}000} = 1.55
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Comment:** Since $QCR = 1.55 > 1$, the organization is spending **more on failure than on prevention + appraisal**. The management should **increase investment in prevention activities** (training, design review, supplier development) to drive down the long-run failure costs and move toward the optimal point on the U-curve.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the different levels of management at which a QIS operates. Describe the type of information needed at each level with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A Quality Information System serves **three hierarchical levels** of management, each with distinct information needs:

**1. Strategic Level — Top Management (2 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Long-term policy decisions, capital investment, annual quality planning.
- **Information type:** Aggregated, summarized, trend-based.
- **Examples:** Annual quality cost report, customer satisfaction index, market share linked to quality, ISO/QMS audit summary, six-sigma maturity level.

**2. Tactical Level — Middle Management (3 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Resource allocation, departmental target setting, supplier evaluation, continuous improvement projects.
- **Information type:** Department-wise comparison, Pareto distributions, process capability trends.
- **Examples:** Departmental scrap rate comparison, supplier PPM score-card, $C_{pk}$ trend per machine, monthly Pareto of defect types, training-needs analysis.

**3. Operational Level — Shop Floor (2 marks):**
- **Purpose:** Real-time process control, immediate corrective action.
- **Information type:** Real-time, granular, individual measurement data.
- **Examples:** Individual $X$-bar and $R$ control chart points, gauge readings, machine parameter logs, tool-wear sensor output, immediate stop signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Information flow pattern:** Data flows **upward** (raw to strategic), while decisions and policies flow **downward** (strategic to operational). This is the **vertical information hierarchy** in QIS.

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a Quality Information System. List the key performance indicators (KPIs) that a QIS tracks. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Data Sources: Inspection, Complaints, Sensors, Supplier]
              |
              v
[Data Acquisition: Manual Entry, Barcode, CMM, IoT]
              |
              v
[Central Quality Database (SQL)]
              |
              v
[Analysis Engine: SPC, Pareto, FMEA, COPQ]
              |
              v
[Report Generation: Daily / Weekly / Monthly / Annual]
              |
              v
[Feedback to: Shop Floor, Middle Mgmt, Top Mgmt]
```

**Key Performance Indicators tracked by QIS (4 marks — 1 mark each category):**

1. **Defect-related KPIs:** DPMO, PPM, First-Pass Yield (FPY), Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY).
2. **Process-related KPIs:** $C_{pk}$, $P_{pk}$, Cp, process variation, control chart out-of-control points.
3. **Cost-related KPIs:** COPQ, Cost of Good Quality, Quality Cost Ratio, scrap cost per unit.
4. **Customer & Supplier-related KPIs:** Customer complaints per million, warranty cost ratio, supplier rejection rate, on-time delivery of quality parts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** When asked to "draw a block diagram of QIS," students often **forget the feedback loop arrow** going back to the shop floor. A QIS is a **closed-loop system**, not a one-way pipeline. Drawing an open arrow (no return path) costs **1–2 marks** in valuation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **QIS = 5 layers:** Source → Acquisition → Storage → Analysis → Dissemination.
- ✅ **Three managerial levels:** Strategic (annual), Tactical (monthly), Operational (real-time).
- ✅ **P-A-F Model author:** Armand V. Feigenbaum.
- ✅ **P-A-F equation:** $TQC = P + A + IF + EF$.
- ✅ **COPQ equation:** $COPQ = IF + EF$ — the "loss to society" concept.
- ✅ **Optimal quality level:** Located at the **minimum of the U-shaped TQC curve**.
- ✅ **QCR > 1** → firm is in "fire-fighting" mode; increase prevention spend.
- ✅ **QCR < 1** → firm is in "prevention-dominant" mode; efficient and world-class.
- ✅ **DPMO formula:** $\dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$ — must not confuse with PPM.
- ✅ **PPM formula:** $\dfrac{\text{Defective}}{\text{Total}} \times 10^6$.
- ✅ **RTY formula:** Product of individual station yields — always less than the lowest station yield.
- ✅ **Cpk formula:** $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma},\;\dfrac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$.
- ✅ **Six-Sigma target:** 3.4 DPMO = 4.5 sigma with 1.5 sigma shift.
- ✅ **FMEA RPN:** $S \times O \times D$ — used to prioritize failure modes.
- ✅ **ISO 9001 / IATF 16949** mandate documented QIS for certification.
- ✅ **Closed-loop nature:** Always remember to draw a **return feedback arrow** in diagrams.
- ✅ **Information flow direction:** Data flows **upward**, decisions flow **downward**.

> **End of Module 4 Topic Notes — Quality Information Systems**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Quality Planning** is the systematic, structured process of identifying the quality standards required for a product, project, or service, and then determining the operational processes, resources, controls, and verification methods necessary to achieve those standards. It is the *first* and most critical component of the **Quality Management System (QMS)** triad, which also includes **Quality Assurance (QA)** and **Quality Control (QC)**.

According to the ISO 9000 family of standards (which underpins KTU's quality curriculum), quality planning establishes the **design**, **production**, and **verification** framework that converts customer expectations into measurable engineering specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Quality Planning is the *upstream* activity in the Juran Trilogy (Plan–Do–Check–Act). It must be completed *before* any manufacturing or service process begins.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are organizing a **college technical fest** (let's call it "TechSpardha'25"). Before the event:

- **Quality Planning** = Deciding the *blueprint* of the fest: what events to conduct, what budget to allocate, what judges to invite, what prizes to give, and what *exactly* counts as a "good event" (e.g., completion time, audience satisfaction, decorum).
- **Quality Assurance** = Training the volunteers *before* the event so they know how to handle hiccups.
- **Quality Control** = Checking during the event whether things are going as planned (audience feedback, timing).

Without *planning*, no amount of assurance or control can rescue a chaotic fest. **The same applies to manufacturing a crankshaft, a printed circuit board, or a software module.**

### 1.3 Core Terminology (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Quality Policy** | A formal statement of an organization's overall intentions and direction regarding quality, as expressed by top management. |
| **Quality Objectives** | Quantifiable goals that align with the quality policy (e.g., "reduce defects to **< 500 ppm** in 2025"). |
| **Quality Manual** | A document that states the organization's quality management system. |
| **Quality Plan** | A specific document outlining procedures, resources, and sequence of activities for a particular project or product. |
| **Benchmarking** | Comparing one's quality processes with the **best industry practices**. |
| **Customer Focus** | Identifying the needs of internal and external customers as the foundation for planning. |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **outcome-based education (OBE)**. When you plan quality, always begin by mapping activities to the **Course Outcomes (COs)** or **Process Outcomes** — this makes your plan *auditable* and *measurable*.

### 1.4 Why Quality Planning Matters (Industry Relevance)

- Reduces **Cost of Poor Quality (COPQ)** — fixing a defect at the design stage is **10× to 100× cheaper** than fixing it after delivery.
- Ensures **regulatory compliance** with standards such as **ISO 9001:2015**, **AS9100** (aerospace), **IATF 16949** (automotive), and **ISO 13485** (medical devices).
- Builds **customer trust** and brand equity.
- Enables effective use of **statistical tools** in subsequent QC stages (control charts, acceptance sampling).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Juran Trilogy — Foundation of Quality Planning

Dr. Joseph M. Juran formalized quality management into three universally accepted processes. Quality Planning is the *first* and most strategically important step.

**Step 1 — Establish Quality Goals (Identify Customers & Their Needs)**

- Conduct **Voice of Customer (VOC)** studies: surveys, interviews, focus groups, complaint logs.
- Translate VOC into **engineering characteristics** (e.g., customer says "car should feel responsive" → engineer says "0–100 km/h in ≤ 9 seconds").

**Step 2 — Identify Critical Processes and Inputs**

- Map the **process flow** using SIPOC (Suppliers, Inputs, Process, Outputs, Customers).
- Determine which inputs have the greatest impact on outputs using **Pareto analysis** (80/20 rule).

**Step 3 — Develop Process Controls and Transfer to Operations**

- Specify measurement instruments, inspection points, control limits.
- Provide training and Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs).

### 2.2 The Seven (7) Tools of Quality Planning (Ishikawa's Traditional QC Tools)

These are the *bedrock* tools that KTU expects every student to recognize and apply in numerical problems and case studies.

| # | Tool | Primary Use in Planning |
|---|------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Fishbone / Ishikawa) | Brainstorm root causes of a quality problem under 6Ms: Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother-Nature. |
| 2 | **Check Sheet** | Structured data collection in real time. |
| 3 | **Control Chart** | Monitor process stability *during* execution (more of a QC tool, but planned here). |
| 4 | **Histogram** | Visualize frequency distribution of measured data. |
| 5 | **Pareto Chart** | Prioritize the "vital few" causes from the "trivial many." |
| 6 | **Scatter Diagram** | Explore correlation between two variables. |
| 7 | **Flowchart** | Document and standardize the sequence of process steps. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Favourite:** A 14-mark question on *"Explain the seven tools of quality control with a suitable example"* appears almost every year. Quality Planning is where these tools are *selected* and *deployed*.

### 2.3 New (Management & Planning) Tools of Quality

Beyond Ishikawa's 7 tools, advanced planning requires 7 *new* tools geared toward planning rather than analysis:

1. **Affinity Diagram** — group ideas into natural clusters.
2. **Interrelationship Digraph** — show cause-effect relationships between concepts.
3. **Tree Diagram** — break a goal into progressively detailed tasks (WBS).
4. **Matrix Diagram** — show strength of relationships between two sets (e.g., requirements vs. design features).
5. **Prioritization Matrices** — rank issues using weighted criteria.
6. **Process Decision Program Chart (PDPC)** — anticipate problems and contingencies.
7. **Activity Network Diagram** (PERT/CPM) — schedule and sequence complex projects.

### 2.4 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — The "House of Quality"

**QFD** is the flagship planning methodology developed in Japan (Yoji Akao, 1966) and is heavily tested in KTU's quality modules.

The **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the matrix that translates **WHATs (customer requirements)** into **HOWs (engineering characteristics)**, while tracking **relationships, correlations, and competitive benchmarks**.

Key components of the HOQ:

- **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** — listed vertically.
- **Engineering Characteristics (HOWs)** — listed horizontally.
- **Relationship Matrix (Roof)** — strength of correlation (strong = 9, moderate = 3, weak = 1, none = 0).
- **Correlation Matrix (Roof)** — between HOWs (strong positive, strong negative, etc.).
- **Technical Importance Weights** — ranked importance of each HOW.
- **Competitive Benchmarking** — comparison with competitors' products.

### 2.5 Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)

FMEA is a *prospective* (forward-looking) planning tool used to anticipate potential failure modes of a product or process, score their severity, and prioritize corrective actions.

Three key FMEA types:

- **System FMEA** — at the system architecture level.
- **Design FMEA (DFMEA)** — at the product design stage.
- **Process FMEA (PFMEA)** — at the manufacturing/process planning stage.

### 2.6 Cost of Quality (COQ) — Planning Justification

Quality is *not free*, but *poor quality* is far more expensive. COQ classifies quality-related expenses into four categories used in planning trade-off studies:

- **Prevention Costs (P)** — training, quality planning, design reviews.
- **Appraisal Costs (A)** — inspection, testing, calibration.
- **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** — scrap, rework, re-inspection.
- **External Failure Costs (EFC)** — warranty claims, recalls, lawsuits, lost goodwill.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for KTU university exams, assignments, and viva. The `vert` symbol is used instead of `vert` for absolute-value notation to keep the table syntax intact.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $COQ = P + A + IFC + EFC$ | Currency (₹ / $) | Used to plan quality budgets. |
| 2 | Cost of Poor Quality | $COPQ = IFC + EFC$ | Currency | "Fire-fighting" cost that good planning reduces. |
| 3 | Process Capability Index | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6 \sigma}$ | dimensionless | Assumes process is *centered*. |
| 4 | Process Capability Index (Shifted) | $C_{pk} = \min \left( \dfrac{USL - \bar{X}}{3 \sigma}, \dfrac{\bar{X} - LSL}{3 \sigma} \right)$ | dimensionless | Realistic measure used in planning acceptance. |
| 5 | Defects Per Million Opportunities | $DPMO = \dfrac{D \times 10^6}{N \times O}$ | defects per million | Foundation of **Six Sigma** planning. |
| 6 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | fraction (0 to 1) | Where $Y_i$ is the yield at stage *i*. |
| 7 | First-Time Yield | $Y_i = \dfrac{N_{out,i}}{N_{in,i}}$ | fraction | Inputs to RTY. |
| 8 | Six-Sigma Level | $\sigma_{level} = \dfrac{Z_{score}}{1.5}$ | sigma | The **1.5 σ shift** is a planning convention. |
| 9 | RPN (Risk Priority Number) | $RPN = S \times O \times D$ | integer | Used in FMEA; $S$ = Severity, $O$ = Occurrence, $D$ = Detection. |
| 10 | Quality Level (AQL sampling) | $LTPD = p_2$ at producer's $\beta$ risk | fraction | Used in acceptance sampling plans. |
| 11 | Customer Satisfaction Index | $CSI = \dfrac{\sum w_i s_i}{\sum w_i}$ | score (0–1) | Weighted average of satisfaction scores. |
| 12 | Quality Improvement Index | $QII = \dfrac{\text{Defects}_{before} - \text{Defects}_{after}}{\text{Defects}_{before}} \times 100$ | percent | Used in Kaizen projects. |

### 2.8 Real-World Utility

Quality Planning frameworks drive the *entire* value chain:

- **Automotive** — IATF 16949 mandates APQP (Advanced Product Quality Planning) with 5 phases.
- **Aerospace** — AS9100 mandates FMEA, configuration management, and First Article Inspection (FAI).
- **Software** — ISO/IEC 25010 plans quality via SQuaRE (Software product Quality Requirements and Evaluation).
- **Healthcare** — Clinical pathways are essentially "quality plans" for patient care.
