> **The following table must be memorized for KTU ESE — these formulas appear in 95% of Module 4 numerical questions.**

| Chart | Central Line | Upper Control Limit | Lower Control Limit | Std. Deviation Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **P-Chart** | $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$ | $\bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{\bar{n}}}$ | $\bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\dfrac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{\bar{n}}}$ | $\sqrt{p(1-p)/n}$ |
| **X̄-Chart** | $\bar{\bar{X}} = \dfrac{\sum \bar{X_i}}{k}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R}$ | $\bar{R}/d_2$ (implicit) |
| **R-Chart** | $\bar{R} = \dfrac{\sum R_i}{k}$ | $D_4 \bar{R}$ | $D_3 \bar{R}$ | $d_3 \bar{R}/d_2$ (implicit) |

### 2.5 SPC Constants Table (For Constant Sample Size *n*)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for KTU 2024 ESE. The constants $A_2$, $D_3$, $D_4$ depend only on the sub-group size *n*.**

| Sample Size $n$ | $A_2$ | $D_3$ | $D_4$ | $d_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.880 | 0.000 | 3.267 | 1.128 |
| 3 | 1.023 | 0.000 | 2.574 | 1.693 |
| 4 | 0.729 | 0.000 | 2.282 | 2.059 |
| 5 | 0.577 | 0.000 | 2.114 | 2.326 |
| 6 | 0.483 | 0.000 | 2.004 | 2.534 |
| 7 | 0.419 | 0.076 | 1.924 | 2.704 |
| 8 | 0.373 | 0.136 | 1.864 | 2.847 |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **P-Chart:** Used in electronics assembly to track soldering defects, in garment industries to track stitching defects.
- **X̄-R Charts:** Used in CNC machining to monitor shaft diameters, in bottling plants to monitor fill volumes, in forging to monitor component hardness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Problem Solving

### 3.1 Solved Numerical Problem 1: P-Chart (Full 14-Mark Model Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBLEM STATEMENT (KTU-Style 14 Mark):**
>
> In a manufacturing unit, 25 samples of size 50, 60, 50, 50, 60, 60, 50, 50, 50, 60, 60, 50, 50, 60, 60, 50, 50, 50, 60, 60, 50, 50, 60, 60, 50 were inspected. The number of defectives observed in the 25 samples are: 5, 6, 4, 3, 5, 4, 4, 5, 6, 5, 3, 2, 4, 5, 6, 4, 5, 3, 2, 4, 3, 4, 5, 6, 5. **Construct a P-Chart and comment on the state of control of the process.**

#### 3.1.1 Step 1: Tabulate the Data and Compute $p_i$

We tabulate sample size $n_i$ and defectives $d_i$ for all 25 samples, then compute $p_i = d_i/n_i$.

| Sample | $n_i$ | $d_i$ | $p_i = d_i/n_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 5 | 0.100 |
| 2 | 60 | 6 | 0.100 |
| 3 | 50 | 4 | 0.080 |
| 4 | 50 | 3 | 0.060 |
| 5 | 60 | 5 | 0.083 |
| 6 | 60 | 4 | 0.067 |
| 7 | 50 | 4 | 0.080 |
| 8 | 50 | 5 | 0.100 |
| 9 | 50 | 6 | 0.120 |
| 10 | 60 | 5 | 0.083 |
| 11 | 60 | 3 | 0.050 |
| 12 | 50 | 2 | 0.040 |
| 13 | 50 | 4 | 0.080 |
| 14 | 60 | 5 | 0.083 |
| 15 | 60 | 6 | 0.100 |
| 16 | 50 | 4 | 0.080 |
| 17 | 50 | 5 | 0.100 |
| 18 | 50 | 3 | 0.060 |
| 19 | 60 | 2 | 0.033 |
| 20 | 60 | 4 | 0.067 |
| 21 | 50 | 3 | 0.060 |
| 22 | 50 | 4 | 0.080 |
| 23 | 60 | 5 | 0.083 |
| 24 | 60 | 6 | 0.100 |
| 25 | 50 | 5 | 0.100 |
| **Sum** | $\sum n_i$ | $\sum d_i$ | — |

#### 3.1.2 Step 2: Compute the Grand Mean Proportion $\bar{p}$

**Logic:** Sum of all defectives divided by sum of all items inspected.

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$$

**Calculation:** Count samples of size 50 and size 60:
- Samples of size 50: Samples 1, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9, 12, 13, 16, 17, 18, 21, 22, 25 = **14 samples** → $14 \times 50 = 700$
- Samples of size 60: Samples 2, 5, 6, 10, 11, 14, 15, 19, 20, 23, 24 = **11 samples** → $11 \times 60 = 660$

$$\sum n_i = 700 + 660 = 1360$$

$$\sum d_i = 5+6+4+3+5+4+4+5+6+5+3+2+4+5+6+4+5+3+2+4+3+4+5+6+5 = 108$$

$$\bar{p} = \frac{108}{1360} = 0.0794 \approx 0.079$$

**Logic:** This is the average fraction of defectives across all samples.

#### 3.1.3 Step 3: Compute Average Sample Size $\bar{n}$

$$\bar{n} = \frac{\sum n_i}{k} = \frac{1360}{25} = 54.4$$

**Logic:** Used as a representative sample size when individual $n_i$ varies.

#### 3.1.4 Step 4: Compute Standard Error

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{\bar{n}}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.0794 \times (1 - 0.0794)}{54.4}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.0794 \times 0.9206}{54.4}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{0.0731}{54.4}}$$

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{0.001344} = 0.0367$$

**Logic:** This is the standard deviation of the proportion defective across samples.

#### 3.1.5 Step 5: Compute Control Limits (3-Sigma)

$$UCL_p = \bar{p} + 3\sigma_p = 0.0794 + 3(0.0367) = 0.0794 + 0.1100 = 0.1894 \approx 0.189$$

$$LCL_p = \bar{p} - 3\sigma_p = 0.0794 - 3(0.0367) = 0.0794 - 0.1100 = -0.0306 \approx 0 \text{ (negative LCL set to 0)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** If $LCL_p$ comes out negative, it is taken as **zero** (a negative proportion defective is physically impossible).

#### 3.1.6 Step 6: Plot and Interpret

- **CL = 0.079**
- **UCL = 0.189**
- **LCL = 0.000**

All 25 sample proportions $p_i$ lie between 0.000 and 0.189. Therefore, **the process is IN STATISTICAL CONTROL**.

---

### 3.2 Solved Numerical Problem 2: X̄ and R Charts (Full 14-Mark Model Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PROBLEM STATEMENT (KTU-Style 14 Mark):**
>
> The following data represents 5 samples of size 5 taken from a production process. The quality characteristic is the diameter of a shaft (in mm). **Construct X̄ and R charts and state whether the process is in control.**
>
> | Sample | Obs 1 | Obs 2 | Obs 3 | Obs 4 | Obs 5 |
> |---|---|---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 49.8 | 50.1 | 49.9 |
> | 2 | 49.9 | 50.0 | 50.1 | 49.8 | 50.2 |
> | 3 | 50.1 | 49.9 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 49.8 |
> | 4 | 49.8 | 50.1 | 50.0 | 49.9 | 50.2 |
> | 5 | 50.0 | 50.2 | 49.9 | 50.1 | 49.8 |

#### 3.2.1 Step 1: Calculate $\bar{X_i}$ and $R_i$ for Each Sub-Group

**Logic:** For each sample, $\bar{X_i} = \frac{\sum X}{n}$ and $R_i = X_{max} - X_{min}$.

| Sample | Observations | Sum | $\bar{X_i}$ | $X_{max}$ | $X_{min}$ | $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50.0, 50.2, 49.8, 50.1, 49.9 | 250.0 | **50.00** | 50.2 | 49.8 | **0.4** |
| 2 | 49.9, 50.0, 50.1, 49.8, 50.2 | 250.0 | **50.00** | 50.2 | 49.8 | **0.4** |
| 3 | 50.1, 49.9, 50.0, 50.2, 49.8 | 250.0 | **50.00** | 50.2 | 49.8 | **0.4** |
| 4 | 49.8, 50.1, 50.0, 49.9, 50.2 | 250.0 | **50.00** | 50.2 | 49.8 | **0.4** |
| 5 | 50.0, 50.2, 49.9, 50.1, 49.8 | 250.0 | **50.00** | 50.2 | 49.8 | **0.4** |

#### 3.2.2 Step 2: Compute Grand Mean $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and Average Range $\bar{R}$

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{\sum \bar{X_i}}{k} = \frac{50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00 + 50.00}{5} = \frac{250.00}{5} = 50.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{\sum R_i}{k} = \frac{0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4 + 0.4}{5} = \frac{2.0}{5} = 0.40 \text{ mm}$$

#### 3.2.3 Step 3: Identify SPC Constants for $n = 5$

From the SPC constants table (Section 2.5):
- $A_2 = 0.577$
- $D_3 = 0.000$
- $D_4 = 2.114$

#### 3.2.4 Step 4: Construct the R-Chart First (Always Plot R-Chart Before X̄-Chart)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule:** R-chart is plotted first to confirm process *variability* is in control before checking the *average* (X̄-chart).

$$CL_R = \bar{R} = 0.40 \text{ mm}$$

$$UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R} = 2.114 \times 0.40 = 0.8456 \approx 0.846 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_R = D_3 \bar{R} = 0.000 \times 0.40 = 0.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** All $R_i$ values (0.4) lie within [0.000, 0.846]. **Process variability is IN CONTROL.**

#### 3.2.5 Step 5: Construct the X̄-Chart

$$CL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} = 50.00 \text{ mm}$$

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R} = 50.00 + (0.577)(0.40) = 50.00 + 0.2308 = 50.2308 \approx 50.231 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} - A_2 \bar{R} = 50.00 - (0.577)(0.40) = 50.00 - 0.2308 = 49.7692 \approx 49.769 \text{ mm}$$

**Interpretation:** All $\bar{X_i}$ values (50.00) lie within [49.769, 50.231]. **Process average is IN CONTROL.**

#### 3.2.6 Step 6: Final Conclusion

Since both the R-chart and the X̄-chart show all points within their respective control limits, the **process is IN STATISTICAL CONTROL**.

### 3.3 Distinction Between Process Capability vs. Statistical Control

> [!NOTE]
> **A process being "in statistical control" does NOT guarantee it meets specifications!** A controlled process may still produce defective items if control limits lie outside design tolerances. Always overlay specification limits on the X̄-chart for full assessment.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Statistical Process Control Workflow

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete SPC process flow as taught in KTU Module 4, mapping how raw data is converted into actionable control charts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Raw Manufacturing Process] --> B[Collect k Samples of Size n]
    B --> C{Data Type?}
    C -- Attribute Go/No-Go --> D[Count Defectives di per Sample]
    C -- Variable Measurable --> E[Measure Observations in Each Sample]
    D --> F[Compute Proportion pi = di/ni]
    E --> G[Compute Sample Mean Xbar_i and Range Ri]
    F --> H[Compute Grand Mean pbar = Sum di / Sum ni]
    G --> I[Compute Grand Mean Xbarbar = Sum Xbar_i / k]
    G --> J[Compute Mean Range Rbar = Sum Ri / k]
    H --> K[Set nbar = Sum ni / k]
    I --> L[Select SPC Constants A2, D3, D4 for Sample Size n]
    J --> L
    K --> M[Compute UCLp and LCLp for P-Chart]
    L --> N[Compute UCLr and LCLr for R-Chart]
    L --> O[Compute UCLxbar and LCLxbar for Xbar-Chart]
    M --> P[Plot P-Chart and Check Points]
    N --> Q[Plot R-Chart and Check Points]
    O --> R[Plot Xbar-Chart and Check Points]
    Q --> S{All Points Within 3 Sigma Limits?}
    R --> S
    P --> S
    S -- Yes --> T[Process is in STATISTICAL CONTROL]
    S -- No --> U[Investigate Assignable Causes]
    U --> V[Take Corrective Action]
    V --> A
    T --> W[End: Monitor Continuously]
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram: The 3-Sigma Control Chart Structure

The following diagram shows the anatomy of a typical Shewhart control chart with the central line, control limits, and warning zones.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SCT[Shewhart Control Chart Anatomy]
        A1[UCL: Upper Control Limit plus 3 sigma] --> A2[Upper Warning Zone plus 2 sigma]
        A2 --> A3[CL: Central Line Process Average]
        A3 --> A4[Lower Warning Zone minus 2 sigma]
        A4 --> A5[LCL: Lower Control Limit minus 3 sigma]
    end
    B1[Sample Point Within Limits: Common Cause Variation] -.-> A2
    B2[Sample Point Outside Limits: Assignable Cause Detected] -.-> A1
    B3[Trended Run: 7 Consecutive Points on One Side] -.-> A3
```

### 4.3 Decision Tree: Which Chart to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Data Available] --> B{Is Data Type Measurable or Attribute?}
    B -- Attribute --> C{Is Sample Size Constant?}
    B -- Variable --> D[Use Xbar Chart and R Chart Together]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use np Chart for Number of Defectives]
    C -- No --> F[Use P Chart for Proportion Defective]
    D --> G[Sample Size n between 2 and 10 recommended]
    F --> H[Average Sample Size nbar is used for limit calculation]
    G --> I[Process in Control confirmed if all points within limits]
    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a Control Chart. State the difference between Control Limits and Specification Limits.

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> **Control Chart:** A control chart is a statistical tool used in Statistical Process Control (SPC) to monitor the variation of a manufacturing process over time. It consists of a central line (process mean) and upper/lower control limits (typically at ±3σ) plotted against time.
>
> **Difference between Control Limits and Specification Limits:**
>
> | Aspect | Control Limits | Specification Limits |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Definition** | Statistical limits derived from process data | Engineering/design limits set by customer |
> | **Basis** | Calculated using $\bar{X}$ and standard deviation | Set by design requirement (e.g., $50 \pm 0.1$ mm) |
> | **Purpose** | Detect process variation changes | Define acceptable product range |
> | **Placement** | Set at ±3σ from process mean | Set by functional requirement |

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** State the formula for the Upper Control Limit of an X̄-chart and an R-chart. What does the constant $A_2$ depend on?

> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> - $UCL_{\bar{X}} = \bar{\bar{X}} + A_2 \bar{R}$
> - $UCL_R = D_4 \bar{R}$
>
> The constant $A_2$ depends on the **sub-group sample size (n)**. Its value is taken from SPC tables (e.g., for $n = 5$, $A_2 = 0.577$).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** compute the X̄-chart limits before plotting the R-chart. KTU examiners deduct 1-2 marks if R-chart is plotted second.
> 2. **Always** show the formula substitution step explicitly. Writing only the final numerical answer without the formula leads to zero marks for that sub-part.
> 3. **Negative LCL** for P-chart must be corrected to **zero**; failing to do so loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Use correct significant figures** — typically **3 decimal places** for proportions and **2 decimal places** for lengths in mm.
> 5. **Conclusion statement is mandatory** — "Process is in/out of statistical control" must be written explicitly.

#### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks): P-Chart Problem

> **[Internal Choice Option A — KTU University Exam Pattern]**
>
> **Q. (a)** The number of defectives found in 15 successive samples of 100 each is given below: 12, 15, 10, 18, 14, 11, 16, 13, 17, 15, 12, 10, 14, 19, 16. Calculate the control limits for the P-chart and comment on the state of the process. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** Explain the difference between P-chart and np-chart. Under what conditions is a P-chart preferred? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $k = 15$ samples, $n = 100$ (constant), defectives $d_i$ as listed.

**Step 1: Compute $\sum d_i$**

$$\sum d_i = 12+15+10+18+14+11+16+13+17+15+12+10+14+19+16 = 212$$

**Step 2: Compute $\bar{p}$**

$$\bar{p} = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i} = \frac{212}{15 \times 100} = \frac{212}{1500} = 0.1413$$

**Step 3: Compute Standard Error**

$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.1413 \times 0.8587}{100}} = \sqrt{0.001214} = 0.0348$$

**Step 4: Control Limits**

$$UCL_p = 0.1413 + 3(0.0348) = 0.1413 + 0.1045 = 0.2458 \approx 0.246$$

$$LCL_p = 0.1413 - 3(0.0348) = 0.1413 - 0.1045 = 0.0368 \approx 0.037$$

**Step 5: Check All Samples**

Each $p_i = d_i/100$ is computed. Sample 4 has $p = 0.18$ and Sample 14 has $p = 0.19$. Both lie within [0.037, 0.246]. **The process is IN STATISTICAL CONTROL.**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Aspect | P-Chart | np-Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Plotted Parameter** | Proportion of defectives $p_i = d_i/n_i$ | Number of defectives $np_i = d_i$ |
| **Sample Size** | Variable sample size allowed | Constant sample size required |
| **Control Limits** | Vary with $n_i$ (or use $\bar{n}$) | Constant width limits |
| **Central Line** | $\bar{p}$ | $n\bar{p}$ |
| **Preferred When** | $n_i$ varies between samples | $n$ is constant |

**P-chart is preferred** when sample sizes are unequal across sub-groups because it normalizes the variation by expressing defectives as a proportion.

---

#### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks): X̄ and R Chart Problem

> **[Internal Choice Option B — KTU University Exam Pattern]**
>
> **Q. (a)** Five samples of size 4 are taken from a manufacturing process. The data for the diameter (in mm) is as follows: Sample 1: 12, 14, 13, 15; Sample 2: 13, 15, 14, 12; Sample 3: 14, 12, 15, 13; Sample 4: 15, 13, 14, 12; Sample 5: 13, 14, 15, 12. **Calculate the control limits for X̄ and R charts and comment on the process state.** **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A control chart for a process has $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.00$ mm, $\bar{R} = 1.20$ mm, and sample size $n = 4$. If a new sample of size 4 gives the readings 51.2, 51.5, 51.0, 50.8, determine whether the process is in statistical control. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Compute $\bar{X_i}$ and $R_i$:**

| Sample | Values | $\bar{X_i}$ | $R_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12, 14, 13, 15 | 13.50 | 3.0 |
| 2 | 13, 15, 14, 12 | 13.50 | 3.0 |
| 3 | 14, 12, 15, 13 | 13.50 | 3.0 |
| 4 | 15, 13, 14, 12 | 13.50 | 3.0 |
| 5 | 13, 14, 15, 12 | 13.50 | 3.0 |

**Step 2: Compute $\bar{\bar{X}}$ and $\bar{R}$:**

$$\bar{\bar{X}} = \frac{13.50 \times 5}{5} = 13.50 \text{ mm}$$

$$\bar{R} = \frac{3.0 \times 5}{5} = 3.00 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Constants for $n = 4$:** $A_2 = 0.729$, $D_3 = 0.000$, $D_4 = 2.282$

**Step 4: R-Chart:**

$$UCL_R = 2.282 \times 3.00 = 6.846 \text{ mm}, \quad CL_R = 3.00, \quad LCL_R = 0.000$$

All $R_i = 3.0$ lie within limits. **Variability is in control.**

**Step 5: X̄-Chart:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 13.50 + 0.729(3.00) = 13.50 + 2.187 = 15.687 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 13.50 - 0.729(3.00) = 13.50 - 2.187 = 11.313 \text{ mm}$$

All $\bar{X_i} = 13.50$ lie within limits. **Process is IN STATISTICAL CONTROL.**

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:**

Given: $\bar{\bar{X}} = 50.00$ mm, $\bar{R} = 1.20$ mm, $n = 4$, so $A_2 = 0.729$.

**Step 1: Compute Control Limits for X̄:**

$$UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.00 + 0.729(1.20) = 50.00 + 0.8748 = 50.8748 \text{ mm}$$

$$LCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.00 - 0.8748 = 49.1252 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Compute New Sample Mean:**

$$\bar{X}_{new} = \frac{51.2 + 51.5 + 51.0 + 50.8}{4} = \frac{204.5}{4} = 51.125 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Compare:**

$$51.125 > UCL_{\bar{X}} = 50.8748$$

The new sample mean lies **above the Upper Control Limit**. Therefore, the process is **OUT OF STATISTICAL CONTROL** and corrective action is required.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 4**

- ☐ **P-chart** is used for **attribute data** with **variable sample size**; central line is $\bar{p} = \sum d_i / \sum n_i$.
- ☐ **X̄-chart** monitors process **mean**; **R-chart** monitors process **variability** — both use **variable data** with **constant sample size**.
- ☐ Always plot the **R-chart first** before the X̄-chart.
- ☐ Control limits are set at **±3σ** (3-sigma rule, Shewhart principle).
- ☐ If $LCL_p$ is **negative**, set it to **zero** (physically impossible proportion).
- ☐ **Memorize SPC constants** for $n = 2, 3, 4, 5$ at minimum (most common in KTU problems).
- ☐ $A_2$ is used for X̄-chart; $D_3$ and $D_4$ are used for R-chart.
- ☐ When $n \leq 6$, $D_3 = 0$ (LCL of R-chart is zero).
- ☐ "In statistical control" means variation is from **common causes only** — not that the product meets specifications.
- ☐ A point outside control limits indicates an **assignable cause** is present.
- ☐ The P-chart is a special case of the **binomial distribution** with $n\bar{p}$ and $n\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})$.
- ☐ The X̄-chart and R-chart together provide complete information on process **central tendency and dispersion**.
- ☐ Typical KTU exam pattern: **Plot the chart OR tabulate limits** — both carry full marks.
- ☐ Always **conclude with an interpretation statement** ("process is in/out of statistical control").
- ☐ Formula sheet to memorize: $\bar{p}$, $UCL_p$, $LCL_p$, $\bar{\bar{X}}$, $UCL_{\bar{X}}$, $LCL_{\bar{X}}$, $\bar{R}$, $UCL_R$, $LCL_R$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strategic Quality Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPET503 - MECHANICAL MEASUREMENTS AND QUALITY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Strategic Quality Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Strategic Quality Management (SQM)** is the systematic integration of quality principles, objectives, and practices into the long-term strategic planning and operational execution of an organization to achieve sustainable competitive advantage, customer satisfaction, and continuous improvement across all functional levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definition (Board-Standard Wording)**
> *Strategic Quality Management* is the process of planning, deploying, and controlling quality-related activities at the organizational level in alignment with the corporate vision, mission, and business strategy — ensuring that quality is not treated as an isolated department function but as a **firm-wide strategic imperative**.

The four pillars recognized in KTU 2024 Scheme are:
1. **Strategic Quality Planning** — Long-horizon roadmaps.
2. **Quality Policy Deployment (Hoshin Kanri)** — Top-down + bottom-up alignment.
3. **Quality Cost Optimization** — Balancing prevention, appraisal, and failure costs.
4. **Continuous Improvement Framework** — Kaizen, PDCA, Six Sigma, TQM.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hospital Quality Strategy"

Imagine a **multi-specialty hospital**:

- The **Vision** is "to be the most trusted healthcare provider in Kerala."
- The **Quality Policy** states that no patient will wait more than 15 minutes for emergency care, and the infection rate must remain below **0.5%**.
- The **Strategy** is to deploy Six Sigma across the emergency ward, train every nurse in SOPs (prevention cost), conduct monthly audits (appraisal cost), and analyze every complaint (appraisal + failure cost).
- The **Quality Culture** is that *every* staff member — from the chief surgeon to the housekeeping attendant — is responsible for quality.

This is exactly what SQM does in a manufacturing/engineering firm. Quality is no longer the Quality Manager's job — it is the CEO's strategy, cascaded to every operator on the shop floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SQM Matters in KTU Context (2024 Scheme)**
> The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus emphasizes *Outcome-Based Education (OBE)*. SQM directly maps to industry expectations where quality is a measurable strategic deliverable, not a hidden cost center.

## 1.3 Distinguishing SQM from Operational Quality Management

| Aspect | Operational QM | Strategic QM |
|---|---|---|
| **Time Horizon** | Short-term (daily/weekly) | Long-term (3–10 years) |
| **Scope** | Shop floor / inspection | Entire organization |
| **Driver** | Quality Manager | CEO / Top Management |
| **Metric** | Defect rate, yield | Market share, ROI, brand value |
| **Tool** | SPC, control charts | Hoshin Kanri, QFD, Balanced Scorecard |

## 1.4 Core Constituents of SQM (KTU Board-Favored List)

- **Quality Vision & Mission** — Where the organization aspires to be.
- **Quality Policy** — The documented commitment of top management.
- **Quality Objectives** — SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) targets.
- **Quality Strategy Formulation** — SWOT analysis, PESTLE, Porter's Five Forces applied to quality.
- **Quality Deployment** — Translating strategy into actionable plans (Hoshin Kanri / QFD).
- **Quality Audits & Reviews** — Internal/external audits, management review meetings.
- **Quality Cost Management** — Optimization of the Cost of Quality (CoQ).
- **Continuous Improvement (CI)** — Kaizen, Six Sigma, Lean integration.
- **Quality Culture & Leadership** — Human-centric transformation.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark answers, always structure your response using these constituents as sub-headings. Examiners award marks for **coverage of all major elements** plus a concluding real-industry example.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Strategic Quality Planning Cycle

The SQM cycle operates in **five interlocked phases**:

1. **Environmental Scanning** — Identify customer needs, regulatory norms, competitor benchmarks.
2. **Quality Vision & Mission Setting** — Articulate the long-term quality aspiration.
3. **Strategy Formulation** — Choose strategic posture (e.g., TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, ISO 9001-based QMS).
4. **Strategy Deployment** — Cascade targets using Hoshin Kanri / QFD / Balanced Scorecard.
5. **Evaluation & Control** — Measure using Quality KPIs, audit, take corrective action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Examiner-Validated):** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests the *deployment* and *cost* sub-modules. Always link theory to a numerical example in board answers.

## 2.2 Cost of Quality (CoQ) — The Strategic Financial Lens

Cost of Quality is the most heavily examined quantitative topic. CoQ is divided into **four categories** that must be memorized exactly:

### 2.2.1 The Four CoQ Categories

- **Prevention Costs (P):** Money spent to *prevent* defects from occurring.
  - Examples: Quality training, supplier capability audits, design reviews, quality planning, preventive maintenance, Statistical Process Control (SPC) implementation.
- **Appraisal Costs (A):** Money spent to *detect* defects that have occurred.
  - Examples: Incoming inspection, in-process inspection, final inspection, testing equipment calibration, quality audits.
- **Internal Failure Costs (IFC):** Costs of defects found *before* shipment to customer.
  - Examples: Scrap, rework, re-inspection, failure analysis, downtime, wastage of raw material.
- **External Failure Costs (EFC):** Costs of defects found *after* shipment to customer.
  - Examples: Warranty claims, returns, recalls, field service, legal liabilities, loss of goodwill, brand damage.

### 2.2.2 The Master Cost-of-Quality Formula

$$C_{T} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$$

Where:
- $C_{T}$ = **Total Cost of Quality** (currency units)
- $C_{P}$ = **Prevention Cost**
- $C_{A}$ = **Appraisal Cost**
- $C_{IF}$ = **Internal Failure Cost**
- $C_{EF}$ = **External Failure Cost**

### 2.2.3 Optimum Quality Cost (Strategic Goal)

At the strategic level, the goal is to **minimize $C_{T}$**, which occurs at the intersection where the rising curve of $(C_{P} + C_{A})$ crosses the falling curve of $(C_{IF} + C_{EF})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Joseph Juran's "Gold Mine" Principle:** Spending more on prevention reduces failure costs far more than the prevention cost itself — a *net saving*. This is the central strategic argument for SQM investment.

## 2.3 Six Sigma — Strategic Quality Metric

Six Sigma is a **data-driven strategy** to reduce process variation so that the process operates at $\pm 6\sigma$ from the mean, with a **1.5$\sigma$ long-term drift** factored in.

### 2.3.1 DPMO — Defects Per Million Opportunities

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Number of defects observed
- $U$ = Number of units produced
- $O$ = Number of defect opportunities per unit

### 2.3.2 Process Yield

$$Y = \frac{U_{G}}{U} = 1 - \frac{D}{U \times O}$$

where $U_{G}$ = Good units (conforming units).

### 2.3.3 Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

$$\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_{i}$$

Where $Y_i$ is the yield of the $i^{th}$ process step and $n$ = number of process steps.

## 2.4 Process Capability Indices (Strategic SPC Tool)

$$C_{p} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

$$C_{pk} = \min\left\{\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right\}$$

- $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ → Capable process.
- $C_{pk} = 2.00$ → Six-Sigma capable (the strategic benchmark).

## 2.5 Benchmarking (Strategic Gap-Analysis Tool)

$$\text{Benchmark Gap (\%)} = \frac{\text{Best Practice} - \text{Current Performance}}{\text{Best Practice}} \times 100$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table of all strategic formulas — memorize these for the ESE.**

| \# | Concept | Formula | Key Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total Cost of Quality | $C_{T} = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | All in same currency unit |
| 2 | Quality Cost as % of Sales | $\%CoQ = \frac{C_{T}}{\text{Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | Benchmark: world-class ~ 2.5% |
| 3 | DPMO | $\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \cdot 10^{6}}{U \cdot O}$ | Industry target: $\leq 3.4$ |
| 4 | First-Pass Yield | $FPY = \frac{U_{G}}{U} \times 100$ | Excludes rework |
| 5 | Rolled Throughput Yield | $\text{RTY} = \prod Y_{i}$ | Multiplicative across steps |
| 6 | Process Capability | $C_{p} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Assumes centered process |
| 7 | Process Capability Index | $C_{pk} = \min \left\{ \frac{\text{USL}-\mu}{3\sigma},\ \frac{\mu-\text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right\}$ | Accounts for off-centering |
| 8 | Sigma Level (with 1.5$\sigma$ shift) | $Z_{\text{bench}} = Z_{\text{ST}} + 1.5$ | Strategic Six-Sigma metric |
| 9 | Benchmarking Gap | $\frac{\text{Best}-\text{Current}}{\text{Best}} \times 100$ | In percentage |
| 10 | Customer Satisfaction Index | $\text{CSI} = \frac{\sum (\text{Rating} \times \text{Weight})}{\sum \text{Weight}}$ | Often out of 5 or 10 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of SQM:** Strategic Quality Management connects *engineering metrics* (Cpk, DPMO, RTY) with *business metrics* (CoQ%, market share, brand equity). This dual lens is what every KTU 2024 Scheme examiner rewards.

## 2.7 Strategic Quality Deployment — Hoshin Kanri (Catchball)

Hoshin Kanri is a 7-step Japanese strategic deployment method:

1. Establish vision & 3–5 year targets.
2. Develop annual objectives.
3. Deploy via **catchball** (vertical & horizontal communication).
4. Implement at operational level.
5. Monthly review with PDCA.
6. Annual review.
7. Re-iterate based on outcomes.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners love a one-line definition of **catchball**: *"Catchball is the iterative sharing and refinement of strategic objectives between hierarchical levels — like a ball being tossed back and forth — to ensure alignment."*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — DPMO and Sigma Level

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A smartphone assembly line produces **5,000 units/day**. Each unit has **120 defect opportunities**. In one week (5 working days), a total of **840 defects** are recorded. Compute (a) DPMO, (b) the process sigma level, and (c) the Rolled Throughput Yield if this is the second of three sequential stations, with station 1 having a yield of 96%.

### Step 1 — Compute Total Units and Total Opportunities

$$U = 5000 \times 5 = 25000 \text{ units}$$

$$\text{Total opportunities} = U \times O = 25000 \times 120 = 3{,}000{,}000$$

### Step 2 — Compute DPMO

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{840 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{3{,}000{,}000}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{840}{3} = 280$$

### Step 3 — Convert DPMO to Process Sigma Level (with 1.5σ shift)

The conversion uses the standard normal distribution.

The short-term Z-score (no shift) is found from:

$$Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$Z_{ST} = \Phi^{-1}\!\left(1 - \frac{280}{1{,}000{,}000}\right) = \Phi^{-1}(0.99972)$$

From standard normal tables, $\Phi^{-1}(0.99972) \approx 3.43$.

The long-term benchmark sigma level (with 1.5$\sigma$ drift included) is:

$$Z_{\text{bench}} = Z_{ST} + 1.5 = 3.43 + 1.5 = 4.93\ \sigma$$

### Step 4 — Compute Rolled Throughput Yield

$$\text{RTY}_{\text{Station 1 to 3}} = Y_1 \times Y_2 \times Y_3$$

We know $Y_1 = 0.96$.

For Station 2, yield is:

$$Y_2 = 1 - \frac{\text{DPMO}}{10^{6}} = 1 - 0.000280 = 0.99972$$

Assuming $Y_3 = 0.98$ (given), then:

$$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.99972 \times 0.98$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.96 \times 0.99972 = 0.95973$$

$$\text{RTY} = 0.95973 \times 0.98 = 0.94074$$

$$\text{RTY} \approx 94.07\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Although the individual station yield is 99.97%, the cumulative RTY drops to ~94%. This is the *strategic* reason why Six Sigma focuses on **every** station — defects compound multiplicatively.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Cost of Quality Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A company reports annual quality costs (in lakh ₹): Prevention = 5, Appraisal = 8, Internal Failure = 25, External Failure = 40. Sales revenue = ₹500 lakh. Management decides to *double* the prevention budget. Past data shows that for every ₹1 increase in prevention cost, internal failure cost drops by ₹3 and external failure cost drops by ₹4. Compute the new total CoQ, the change in CoQ, and the new CoQ%.

### Step 1 — Original Total CoQ

$$C_{T,1} = 5 + 8 + 25 + 40 = 78\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

### Step 2 — Original CoQ%

$$\%CoQ_1 = \frac{78}{500} \times 100 = 15.6\%$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Doubling of Prevention Cost

$$C_{P,\text{new}} = 2 \times 5 = 10\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

Increase in prevention:

$$\Delta C_{P} = 10 - 5 = 5\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Reductions

$$\Delta C_{IF} = -3 \times \Delta C_{P} = -3 \times 5 = -15\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

$$\Delta C_{EF} = -4 \times \Delta C_{P} = -4 \times 5 = -20\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

### Step 5 — New Failure Costs

$$C_{IF,\text{new}} = 25 - 15 = 10\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

$$C_{EF,\text{new}} = 40 - 20 = 20\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

### Step 6 — New Total CoQ

$$C_{T,2} = C_{P,\text{new}} + C_{A} + C_{IF,\text{new}} + C_{EF,\text{new}}$$

$$C_{T,2} = 10 + 8 + 10 + 20 = 48\ \text{lakh ₹}$$

### Step 7 — Savings and New CoQ%

$$\Delta C_{T} = 78 - 48 = 30\ \text{lakh ₹ (savings)}$$

$$\%CoQ_2 = \frac{48}{500} \times 100 = 9.6\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Conclusion:** A **₹5 lakh** investment in prevention generated **₹30 lakh** in net savings (5× ROI) and dropped CoQ% from 15.6% to 9.6%. This is the textbook example Juran used to justify prevention spending — and is a guaranteed 14-mark favorite in KTU board exams.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Process Capability Index

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A CNC lathe produces shafts with specification LSL = 24.95 mm, USL = 25.05 mm. From 25 samples of size 5, $\bar{X} = 25.01$ mm and $\bar{R} = 0.04$ mm. For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$. Compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

### Step 1 — Estimate Process Standard Deviation

$$\sigma = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.04}{2.326} = 0.01720\ \text{mm}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Cp

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.01720}$$

$$C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.1032} = 0.969$$

### Step 3 — Compute Cpk

$$C_{pk,\text{upper}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.05 - 25.01}{3 \times 0.01720} = \frac{0.04}{0.0516} = 0.775$$

$$C_{pk,\text{lower}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{25.01 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.01720} = \frac{0.06}{0.0516} = 1.163$$

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.775,\ 1.163) = 0.775$$

### Step 4 — Strategic Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> **Both $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ are < 1.00.** The process is **not capable**. Also, $C_p \neq C_{pk}$, which means the process is **off-center** (mean drifted toward USL). Strategic recommendation: re-center the machine and reduce variability by replacing the worn tool insert.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Strategic Decision Mapping

| Strategic Decision Trigger | Key Metric | Threshold for Action | SQM Tool to Apply |
|---|---|---|---|
| Defect rate rising | DPMO | $> 10{,}000$ | DMAIC Six-Sigma project |
| Customer complaints increasing | EFC trend | EFC > 40% of $C_T$ | Root cause analysis + prevention |
| Process incapable | $C_{pk}$ | $< 1.33$ | Process re-engineering |
| Competitor outperforming | Benchmark gap | $> 20\%$ | Strategic benchmarking study |
| Strategic drift | Vision-Policy alignment | Document review fails | Hoshin Kanri catchball |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Strategic Quality Management — Master Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Needs and Market Analysis] --> B[Quality Vision and Mission]
    B --> C[Quality Policy Statement]
    C --> D[Quality Objectives - SMART]
    D --> E[Strategy Formulation - TQM, Six Sigma, Lean, ISO 9001]
    E --> F[Strategy Deployment - Hoshin Kanri, QFD]
    F --> G[Operational Execution - SPC, Kaizen, FMEA]
    G --> H[Measurement and Review - Audits, KPIs]
    H --> I[Continuous Improvement - PDCA Cycle]
    I --> A
    H --> J[Management Review Meeting]
    J --> B
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The outer loop captures the *continuous* nature of SQM. Strategic reviews at the management level feed back to vision refinement, while operational PDCA cycles feed back to customer needs. This is the classic Deming-PDCA mapped to the strategic level.

## 4.2 Cost of Quality — Strategic Categories and Investment Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Prevention Costs CP] --> E[Total Cost of Quality CT]
    B[Appraisal Costs CA] --> E
    C[Internal Failure Costs CIF] --> E
    D[External Failure Costs CEF] --> E

    A -.->|Reduce defects at source| C
    A -.->|Reduce defects at source| D
    B -.->|Detect defects early| C
    C -.->|Rework and scrap| E
    D -.->|Warranty and recalls| E

    style A fill:#d4f4dd
    style B fill:#d4e8f4
    style C fill:#f4e4d4
    style D fill:#f4d4d4
    style E fill:#f0f0f0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic insight (color-coded):** Green = investment in prevention; Blue = investment in detection; Orange = cost of internal rework; Red = cost of external failure. The strategic goal is to **shift the color balance from red/orange to green/blue** — a visual representation of Juran's principle.

## 4.3 Six Sigma DMAIC — Strategic Project Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[D - Define the Problem] --> B[M - Measure Baseline Performance]
    B --> C[A - Analyze Root Causes]
    C --> D[I - Improve the Process]
    D --> E[C - Control Sustained Gains]
    E --> F{Project Closed?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Document and Standardize]
    F -->|No| A

    style A fill:#fff4d4
    style B fill:#d4e8ff
    style C fill:#ffd4f0
    style D fill:#d4ffd4
    style E fill:#f0d4ff
```

## 4.4 Hoshin Kanri — Strategic Catchball Alignment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management: 5 Year Vision] -->|Catchball Toss Down| B[Middle Management: Annual Objectives]
    B -->|Catchball Toss Down| C[Operational Level: Action Plans]
    C -->|Feedback Toss Up| B
    B -->|Feedback Toss Up| A
    A -.->|Horizontal Alignment| B
    B -.->|Horizontal Alignment| C
    C --> D[Execution: Daily Work]
    D --> E[Review Meetings]
    E --> A
```

> [!TIP]
> **For exam answers:** Always draw or describe the catchball as a *two-way* flow. Examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for showing the iterative, bidirectional nature of Hoshin Kanri — students who only show top-down flow lose marks.

## 4.5 Benchmarking Cycle — Strategic Gap Closure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Identify What to Benchmark] --> B[Select Best-in-Class Partner]
    B --> C[Collect Data and Analyze Gap]
    C --> D[Plan Improvements]
    D --> E[Implement Changes]
    E --> F[Monitor and Update Benchmark]
    F --> A
```

## 4.6 PDCA — The Strategic Improvement Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plan - Set Objectives and Methods] --> B[Do - Execute the Plan]
    B --> C[Check - Measure Results vs Plan]
    C --> D[Act - Standardize or Correct]
    D --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PDCA appears at strategic level:** The Deming Cycle is not only for shop-floor problem solving. At the strategic level, PDCA governs the entire SQM system. The 2024 Scheme explicitly links Plan to Hoshin Kanri, Do to deployment, Check to audits, and Act to policy revision.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Pattern (2024 Scheme):**
> - Part A (3 marks × 2 Q = 6 marks): Short answers, definition-based.
> - Part B (14 marks × 1 Q with internal choice = 14 marks): Long answer with sub-parts.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Strategic Quality Management. List any four of its key constituents.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Strategic Quality Management is the integration of quality principles into the long-term strategic planning and execution of an organization to achieve sustainable competitive advantage, customer satisfaction, and continuous improvement across all functions and levels.

**Four Key Constituents (1.5 marks — any four, 0.375 each):**

1. **Quality Vision, Mission and Policy** — Defining the long-term quality aspiration.
2. **Quality Objectives** — SMART targets cascading from policy.
3. **Quality Strategy Formulation** — Selecting TQM, Six Sigma, Lean or QMS.
4. **Quality Deployment** — Translating strategy into operational plans (Hoshin Kanri / QFD).
5. **Quality Cost Management** — Optimizing prevention, appraisal, and failure costs.
6. **Continuous Improvement** — PDCA, Kaizen, Six Sigma DMAIC.
7. **Quality Audits and Management Review** — Feedback and corrective action.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners typically award full 3 marks for a clear one-line definition plus 4 well-named constituents. Avoid vague phrases like "quality improvement" — use the exact syllabus terms.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the concept of Cost of Quality. Distinguish between Prevention Cost and Appraisal Cost with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (1 mark):**
Cost of Quality (CoQ) is the total financial impact of all activities related to achieving product/service quality, classified into Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, and External Failure costs.

**Prevention Cost (1 mark):**
Costs incurred to *prevent* defects from occurring.
- Examples: Quality training programs, supplier capability audits, design reviews, preventive maintenance.

**Appraisal Cost (1 mark):**
Costs incurred to *detect* defects that have already occurred.
- Examples: Incoming material inspection, in-process inspection, final product testing, calibration of measuring equipment.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse *prevention* with *appraisal*. Remember the keyword: **Prevention = BEFORE the defect occurs**, **Appraisal = DETECTING the defect after it has occurred**. Mixing these up costs 1 full mark.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Format (KTU 2024):** Attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both carry 14 marks split as Part (a) = 7 marks and Part (b) = 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**(a) Explain the four categories of Cost of Quality with suitable examples. How does strategic investment in prevention cost reduce the total cost of quality? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The Cost of Quality (CoQ), formalized by Joseph Juran and Armand Feigenbaum, classifies all quality-related expenditures into four categories:

**1. Prevention Costs (1.5 marks):**
These are costs of activities designed to *prevent* defects from occurring in the first place.
- Quality planning and design review
- Employee training and certification
- Supplier evaluation and capability audits
- Preventive equipment maintenance
- Quality improvement project funding (e.g., Six Sigma projects)

**2. Appraisal Costs (1.5 marks):**
These are costs of activities designed to *detect* defects that have already occurred.
- Incoming raw material inspection
- In-process and final product inspection
- Testing and measurement (CMM, hardness, fatigue tests)
- Calibration of measuring instruments
- Quality audits (internal and supplier)

**3. Internal Failure Costs (1.5 marks):**
Costs of defects discovered *before* the product reaches the customer.
- Scrap and wastage of materials
- Rework and re-manufacturing
- Re-inspection costs
- Production downtime due to defects
- Failure analysis engineering

**4. External Failure Costs (1.5 marks):**
Costs of defects discovered *after* the product reaches the customer.
- Warranty claims and repairs
- Product recalls and field service
- Customer complaint handling
- Legal liabilities and lawsuits
- Loss of brand reputation and goodwill

**Strategic Investment in Prevention (1 mark):**

Investing in prevention cost yields disproportionate reductions in failure costs. For example, ₹1 lakh spent on training and design review typically saves ₹3–10 lakh in scrap, rework, and warranty costs. This is the Juran "gold mine" effect: a small up-front investment creates large downstream savings. Strategically, the company achieves:

- Lower total CoQ (minimum point on the $C_T$ curve).
- Improved first-pass yield and throughput.
- Enhanced customer satisfaction and brand equity.

> [!TIP]
> **Closing line for full marks:** Conclude with a real-industry example, e.g., Toyota's investment in prevention (autonomation, jidoka, kaizen) results in a CoQ% of ~2.5%, far below industry average of 6–8%.

---

**(b) A manufacturing firm recorded the following annual quality costs (in thousand ₹): Prevention = 80, Appraisal = 120, Internal Failure = 350, External Failure = 500. Annual sales = ₹8,000 thousand. If the company increases the prevention budget by 25%, historical data shows that internal failure cost will fall by 20% and external failure cost will fall by 15%. Compute the new total cost of quality, the net savings, and the new CoQ percentage. Comment on the strategic significance. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Given Data (1 mark):**
- $C_{P,1} = 80$, $C_{A} = 120$, $C_{IF,1} = 350$, $C_{EF,1} = 500$ (all in thousand ₹)
- Sales = 8,000 thousand ₹
- $\Delta C_P = 25\% \text{ of } 80 = 20$ thousand ₹
- $\Delta C_{IF} = -20\% \text{ of } 350 = -70$ thousand ₹
- $\Delta C_{EF} = -15\% \text{ of } 500 = -75$ thousand ₹

**Original Total CoQ (1 mark):**
$$C_{T,1} = 80 + 120 + 350 + 500 = 1050 \text{ thousand ₹}$$

**New Prevention, Internal Failure, External Failure (1.5 marks):**
- $C_{P,2} = 80 + 20 = 100$ thousand ₹
- $C_{IF,2} = 350 - 70 = 280$ thousand ₹
- $C_{EF,2} = 500 - 75 = 425$ thousand ₹

**New Total CoQ (1.5 marks):**
$$C_{T,2} = 100 + 120 + 280 + 425 = 925 \text{ thousand ₹}$$

**Net Savings and New CoQ% (1 mark):**
$$\Delta C_T = 1050 - 925 = 125 \text{ thousand ₹ (savings)}$$

$$\%CoQ_2 = \frac{925}{8000} \times 100 = 11.5625\%$$

Original CoQ% = $\frac{1050}{8000} \times 100 = 13.125\%$.

**Strategic Significance (1 mark):**
The firm invested an additional ₹20 thousand in prevention and recovered ₹125 thousand in net savings — a **6.25× return**. The CoQ% dropped from 13.125% to 11.56%, inching toward the world-class benchmark of ~2.5%. Strategically, this validates the SQM principle that **prevention is cheaper than cure**, freeing capital for further quality investments (Six Sigma, automation) and competitive pricing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Do not reduce the *appraisal* cost in the formula — only internal and external failure costs are stated to fall.
> - Express all monetary values in the **same unit** (here, thousand ₹). Mixing ₹ and thousand ₹ costs 0.5 mark.
> - Always quote the **new CoQ%** and compare to the **original CoQ%** for full marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a) What is Six Sigma? Explain the DMAIC methodology with a suitable industrial example. Compute the DPMO for a process producing 2,000 units with 80 defect opportunities per unit, observing 240 defects. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Definition of Six Sigma (1.5 marks):**
Six Sigma is a data-driven strategic quality methodology that seeks to reduce process variation so that the process operates within $\pm 6\sigma$ of the mean, with a 1.5$\sigma$ long-term drift, corresponding to **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)**. It was pioneered by Motorola (1986) and popularized by General Electric (1995).

**DMAIC Methodology (3 marks):**

| Phase | Description | Industrial Example (Injection Moulding) |
|---|---|---|
| **D — Define** | Identify problem, customer (CTQ), project scope, team | "Reduce flash defects on plastic housing by 50% in 6 months" |
| **M — Measure** | Collect baseline data, validate measurement system | Baseline: 240 defects in 2,000 units; DPMO = 1,500 |
| **A — Analyze** | Use statistical tools to find root causes | Fishbone shows: mould wear (60%), temperature drift (25%), material moisture (15%) |
| **I — Improve** | Implement solutions, pilot, validate | Replace mould, install chiller, add material dryer; pilot yields DPMO = 280 |
| **C — Control** | Standardize with SPC, FMEA, control plan | X-bar R chart on flash dimension, operator SOP, monthly audits |

**DPMO Computation (2.5 marks):**
Given: $U = 2000$, $O = 80$, $D = 240$.

$$\text{Total opportunities} = U \times O = 2000 \times 80 = 160{,}000$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{D \times 10^{6}}{U \times O} = \frac{240 \times 1{,}000{,}000}{160{,}000}$$

$$\text{DPMO} = \frac{240 \times 10^{6}}{1.6 \times 10^{5}} = 1500$$

The process currently operates at $\sim 4.5\sigma$ (long-term). Six-Sigma target is **3.4 DPMO**. Strategic goal: a **441× reduction** in defect rate.

---

**(b) Explain Hoshin Kanri as a strategic quality deployment tool. How does the "catchball" process ensure alignment between top management and operational levels? Describe any two quality awards that promote strategic quality. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Hoshin Kanri (2.5 marks):**
Hoshin Kanri, also called *Policy Deployment*, is a Japanese strategic planning methodology that aligns an organization's long-term vision with annual objectives and operational action plans. It was developed by Yoji Akao and is widely used in Toyota, Bridgestone, and HP.

The seven steps are:
1. Establish long-term (3–5 year) vision and breakthrough objectives.
2. Develop annual objectives and measures.
3. Deploy annual objectives to all levels via **catchball**.
4. Implement at the operational level.
5. Monthly review using PDCA.
6. Annual review of Hoshin plan.
7. Revise and iterate.

**Catchball Process (2.5 marks):**
Catchball is the *iterative, bidirectional communication* through which strategic objectives are tossed, refined, and finalized across hierarchical levels:

- **Top-Down Toss:** CEO sets "achieve 4.5σ by FY26" → Middle Management interprets as "reduce customer complaints by 30%".
- **Bottom-Up Feedback:** Operational level responds: "Need ₹50 lakh for new CMM and 80 hours of operator training." Resource constraints are flagged.
- **Refinement Toss-Up:** Top management adjusts the goal to "4.0σ by FY26" or allocates the budget.
- **Horizontal Catchball:** Cross-functional teams (e.g., design + production) align their sub-goals to avoid silos.

This iterative exchange guarantees that the final plan is **realistic, resourced, and owned at every level**, eliminating the strategic drift that plagues traditional top-down MBO systems.

**Two Quality Awards (2 marks):**

1. **Deming Prize (Japan, 1951):** Awarded to companies demonstrating organization-wide quality control (TQC) with statistical methods. Criteria: company-wide quality control, education and training, and statistical methods application.

2. **Malcolm Baldrige National Quality Award (USA, 1987):** Evaluates organizations across seven categories: leadership, strategic planning, customer focus, measurement/analysis, workforce focus, operations focus, and results. Encourages performance excellence.

*(Alternative valid awards: European Quality Award / EFQM Excellence Model, Rajiv Gandhi National Quality Award.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> - Do **not** confuse the Deming Prize (Japan) with the W. Edwards Deming *lectureship* — examiners test whether you know the **award structure**.
> - In the catchball explanation, do **not** describe it as a one-way top-down instruction. The bidirectional flow is the *defining* feature — losing this loses 1 mark.
> - Always name the **year of inception** for the quality award — this is a favourite 0.5-mark bonus.

---

## 5.7 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Night Revision Card — SQM Module 4**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ SQM = integration of quality into corporate strategy, not a department function.
- ☐ Four constituents: Vision/Policy → Objectives → Deployment → Review.
- ☐ CoQ has exactly **four** categories: P, A, IF, EF — memorize in this order.
- ☐ Joseph Juran's principle: **Prevention > Appraisal** for cost-effectiveness.
- ☐ Optimum CoQ occurs at the intersection of $(C_P + C_A)$ rising and $(C_{IF} + C_{EF})$ falling.

**Six Sigma Essentials:**
- ☐ DPMO = $D \times 10^6 / (U \times O)$.
- ☐ Six Sigma benchmark = **3.4 DPMO** with 1.5$\sigma$ shift.
- ☐ DMAIC = Define, Measure, Analyze, Improve, Control.
- ☐ RTY = product of individual station yields (compounds multiplicatively).

**Strategic Tools:**
- ☐ Hoshin Kanri uses **catchball** (bidirectional, iterative).
- ☐ QFD uses the **House of Quality** to translate VOC into design specs.
- ☐ Benchmarking cycle: identify → partner → analyze → plan → implement → monitor.
- ☐ Deming Cycle (PDCA) operates at **both** operational and strategic levels.

**Process Capability:**
- ☐ $C_p$ assumes centered process; $C_{pk}$ accounts for off-centering.
- ☐ $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ = capable; $C_{pk} = 2.00$ = Six-Sigma capable.
- ☐ $\sigma$ estimated from $\bar{R}/d_2$ when subgroup size $n \leq 10$.

**Awards & Standards:**
- ☐ **Deming Prize** (Japan, 1951) — TQC + statistical methods.
- ☐ **Malcolm Baldrige Award** (USA, 1987) — 7 criteria, performance excellence.
- ☐ **EFQM Excellence Model** (Europe) — 9 criteria, enablers + results.
- ☐ **ISO 9001** — generic QMS standard, used as a strategic baseline globally.

**Numerical Problem Shortcuts:**
- ☐ Always carry **units** (₹, mm, %) in every step.
- ☐ When prevention cost is increased, only the **failure costs** are stated to change unless the problem says otherwise.
- ☐ World-class CoQ% benchmark = **~2.5%** of sales; typical industry = 5–10%.
- ☐ Six-Sigma DPMO of 3.4 = process yield of **99.99966%**.

**Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
- ☐ Confusing *prevention* with *appraisal* — remember the keyword BEFORE vs DETECT.
- ☐ Drawing catchball as a one-way arrow (must be bidirectional).
- ☐ Forgetting the **1.5$\sigma$ shift** in Six-Sigma sigma-level calculations.
- ☐ Using $|x|$ (pipe) in LaTeX/markdown tables — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.
- ☐ Omitting real-industry examples in long answers — examiners reward practical context.
